documents.title: Implementation of New Policies, Procedures, and SOPs
documents.purpose: The Riverside County Animal Services Department encourages the implementation of new policies, procedures, and SOPs, as well as amending current policies, procedures, and SOPs, in order to provide the most efficient service to citizens of Riverside County. The following procedures will be followed to implement a new, or to revise an existing, policy and procedure and/or SOP.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services (final approval and signature). SOPs do not require a policy number; policy numbers are assigned by the Executive Assistant I.
documents.scope: All Department of Animal Services employees
documents.supersedes: 000-05 Implementation of New Policies and Procedures, effective October 5, 2009 (revised from August 23, 2000); absorbs unsigned draft revision dated May 17, 2019

sections.heading: Initiation
steps: Any staff member of Animal Services can initiate, or request to revise, a policy and/or SOP.

Route the proposed new or revised changes through your immediate supervisor. The immediate supervisor will review the changes to determine whether to present the item at the Operations Chiefs' meeting (Operations or next level up).

If the item is approved by the Operations Chiefs, the item moves forward for implementation and/or revision. The Executive Assistant I will assign it to the department's Administrative Services Analyst or designee to generate or update the policy and procedure and/or SOP.

sections.heading: Review

steps.step: Once the initial draft is complete, route the policy and procedure and/or SOP to all senior and supervisory staff, as necessary, for review and comments within an allotted amount of time.
sub: Senior and supervisory staff include: Operations Chiefs, Volunteer Services Coordinator, Program Chief, Veterinarian, Administrative Services Supervisor, Lieutenants and Sergeants, and the Principal Accountant.
The review timeframe should be limited to 2 weeks for the initial quick review, to be revisited at the next Operations Chiefs' meeting.

steps: Once the draft is approved by the senior and supervisory staff identified above, the Executive Assistant I will forward or present the draft policy and procedure to the Management Team for review and comments.
Forward any comments back to the Executive Assistant I, who will make the appropriate changes or redirect them to the appropriate designee or Administrative Services Analyst.

sections.heading: Approval and Distribution
steps: Once the policy and/or SOP has been reviewed and approved by the Management Staff, construct a final version. The Executive Assistant I will assign it a policy number (if it is a new policy; SOPs do not require a number) and forward it to the Director for final approval and signature.
Upon final approval and signature by the Director, the Executive Assistant I will route the policy and procedure and/or SOP to all DAS staff via email.

Management and senior staff shall be responsible for ensuring that all department staff under their division are aware of the new policy and procedure and/or SOP, and will require acknowledgements to be signed as requested.
Add or amend the new or revised policy and/or SOP in the department's Table of Contents, the shared drive, and the DAS website if applicable.

revisions.author: RCDAS
revisions.desc: Signed revision (draft header cites Rev. September 23, 2009)

revisions.author: RCDAS
revisions.desc: Draft revision adding SOP process; never signed

revisions.author: Outcomes for Pets (draft)
revisions.desc: Migrated into standard RCDAS SOP template. Content unchanged from 2019 draft version; substantive edits pending (see edit log).

documents.title: Board of Supervisor and City Council Complaints
documents.purpose: To establish a uniform procedure for urgent handling and responding to all Board of Supervisor and City Council complaints faxed to Animal Control.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: All County of Riverside Animal Control employees
documents.supersedes: 000-09 Board of Supervisor & City Council Complaints, effective September 28, 2000

sections.heading: Receiving the Complaint
steps: All Board of Supervisors and City Council complaints will be faxed to the Administrative fax machine (909-358-7300).

steps.step: Any employee who sees an Animal Control Complaint Inquiry Form (Attachment A) or a City of Riverside Caller Log Detail (Attachment B) on the fax machine will remove the complaint from the fax machine and log it in on the City of Riverside Number Log (Attachment C) or the County of Riverside Number Log (Attachment D) as follows:
sub: Number (1): Enter the next corresponding number of the complaint preceded by an R (for City) or a C (for County). Also enter this number on item 7 of the City of Riverside Number Log (Attachment C) or the County of Riverside Number Log (Attachment D), whichever is most appropriate.
Date Recd (2): Enter the date that the complaint was received.
Referred By (3): Enter the name of the person who referred the complaint (item 1 on the Animal Control Complaint Inquiry Form and on the City of Riverside Caller Log Detail).
Referred To (4): Enter the name of the person to whom the complaint was referred by the Supervisor.

steps: Hand-deliver the complaint to either the Supervising Animal Control Officer or the Supervising Office Assistant I. If neither is available, hand-deliver it to the Executive Assistant I. Regardless of whom it is delivered to, it must be given to one of these people for immediate attention. It is not to be placed in a mailbox, on a countertop, or in an in box. Immediate action is required.

sections.heading: Responding to the Complaint
steps: Once the Supervisor has reviewed the complaint, he/she will determine to whom it is to be assigned and refer it to that person (i.e., ACO in specific area, barking dog desk, etc.). The Supervisor will be responsible for ensuring that the complaint is completed.

steps.step: Fill out the Animal Control Complaint Inquiry Form (Attachment A). The person who directly handles the complaint will complete the bottom portion of the form as follows:
sub: Investigation (2): Enter the steps taken for the investigation (if any).
Action Taken (3): Enter the final disposition of the complaint.
Investigating Officer/Staff (4): Obtain the signature and printed name of the person who handled the complaint.
Date (5): Enter the date that the complaint was completed.

steps.step: Fill out the City of Riverside Caller Log Detail (Attachment B). Enter the following information in the bottom portion (item 2) of the form:
sub: Date that the complaint was completed.
Steps taken for the investigation (if any).
Final disposition of the complaint.
Name of the person who handled the complaint.

steps: The person who directly handled the complaint will fax the completed Animal Control Complaint Inquiry Form (Attachment A) or the City of Riverside Caller Log Detail (Attachment B) back to the referring party. See items 1 and 6 on the Animal Control Complaint Inquiry Form, or the upper left corner of the City of Riverside Caller Log Detail, for the name and fax number.

sections.heading: Completing the Complaint Form

steps.step: The person who directly handled the complaint will fill out the City of Riverside Number Log (Attachment C) or the County of Riverside Number Log (Attachment D) as follows:
sub: Action Taken (5): Enter the final disposition of the complaint.
Date Closed (6): Enter the date that the complaint was completed.

Date Faxed to BOS or City Council (7): Enter the date that the complaint was faxed to the BOS or City Council member, per items 1 and 6 on the Animal Control Complaint Inquiry Form or item 2 on the City of Riverside Caller Log Detail.

steps: Three (3) hole punch the original Animal Control Complaint Inquiry Form (Attachment A) or the City of Riverside Caller Log Detail (Attachment B) and file it in the City or County High Level Complaint binder located next to the fax machine.

related.name: Animal Control Complaint Inquiry Form (Attachment A)
City of Riverside Caller Log Detail (Attachment B)
City of Riverside Number Log (Attachment C)
County of Riverside Number Log (Attachment D)

appendices: Appendix A: Animal Control Complaint Inquiry Form (attached separately)
Appendix B: City of Riverside Caller Log Detail (attached separately)
Appendix C: City of Riverside Number Log (attached separately)
Appendix D: County of Riverside Number Log (attached separately)

documents.title: Driver's Safety
documents.purpose: To ensure officers' safety when parking alongside a road or highway.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: All Animal Control Officers and License Inspectors
documents.supersedes: 000-13 Drivers Safety, effective April 4, 2002
references: California Vehicle Code (CVC) 25268

sections.heading: When Pulling Up to a Resident's Home or Business to Investigate a Call
steps: Turn on your turn signal to indicate the direction you are turning.
Turn overhead flasher lights on and leave them on for the duration of the call. NOTE: This should occur only if an unusual traffic hazard exists (CVC 25268).
Check both the driver's side and passenger's side mirrors to verify clearance.
When it is safe to pull over, park parallel with the curb.
Check the driver's side mirror and exit the vehicle when it is safe to do so.

If the animal control vehicle will need to be parked for more than 15 minutes, place one cone approximately 6 inches from the front bumper of the animal control vehicle so that it is visible to oncoming traffic. NOTE: This step must be done whenever the vehicle will be parked for more than 15 minutes (i.e., breaks, etc.).
Place a second cone approximately 6 inches away from the rear bumper of the animal control vehicle so that it is visible to oncoming traffic.
Proceed with the call.

sections.heading: When Stopping to Pick Up an Animal in a Roadway
steps: Turn on your turn signal to indicate the direction in which you are turning.
Turn overhead flasher lights on and leave them on for the duration of the call.
Check both the driver's side and passenger's side mirrors to verify clearance.

Pull off the road so that the entire animal control vehicle is completely out of traffic. Be aware of roadside hazards such as a soft shoulder, steep cliffs, and other objects that can do damage to your person and vehicle.
Put on the orange safety vest so that you are visible to nearby traffic.
Check the driver's side mirror and exit the vehicle when it is safe to do so.

When it is safe to do so, proceed into the street to retrieve the animal, keeping aware of your surroundings at all times. NOTE: If traffic is too heavy, you may need to call for additional help from another Animal Control Officer or possibly from law enforcement so that they can conduct a traffic break.
Remove the animal from the road and return to the animal control vehicle.
Load the animal into the animal control vehicle by lifting with your legs and getting assistance from another Animal Control Officer when necessary.

sections.heading: When Leaving a Call
steps: When approaching the passenger side of the animal control vehicle, check for oncoming traffic before proceeding.

Walk in front of the animal control vehicle, check traffic in both directions to ensure safety, and then pick up the cone in front of the vehicle. Make sure to check for any possible obstructions that may be in front of the animal control vehicle.

Walk to the back of the animal control vehicle, check traffic in both directions to ensure safety, and then pick up the cone in back of the vehicle. Make sure to check for any possible obstructions that may be in back of the animal control vehicle.
Check traffic in both directions and, when it is safe to do so, walk to the driver's side door. Store the cones behind the driver's seat and then proceed to get into the animal control vehicle.
If it is a hazard to stay at the present location, move to a safer location before filling out the paperwork.
When you are ready to merge back into traffic, put your turn signal on, check both mirrors, and merge when it is safe to do so.
When back in traffic, check to verify that the turn signal and overhead flasher lights are turned off.
In any situation, always remember to use common sense.

related.name: Acknowledgment of Driver's Safety Policy (retained in employee's personnel file and attached to employee's Person ID in Chameleon)

appendices: Appendix A: Acknowledgment of Driver's Safety Policy

revisions.author: Outcomes for Pets (draft)
revisions.desc: Migrated into standard RCDAS SOP template. Content unchanged from 2009 version; substantive edits pending (see edit log).

documents.title: Internal Conflict Resolution
documents.purpose: Establish a means of communication with staff utilizing conflict resolution, where an employee can report an incident they feel is in need of management and/or supervisory attention, as well as receive notification that the incident was investigated. The Department of Animal Services is a very diverse group with many units or divisions making up one organization; it is important to have a reporting procedure to address a wide variety of issues or concerns while protecting the anonymity of staff members.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: All Animal Services employees
documents.supersedes: None identified in source
references: DAS Chain of Command Memorandum, dated February 24, 2017 (Attachment B)

sections.heading: Objective
steps: The primary objective of this document is to ensure all employees communicate with each other whenever a staff conflict occurs, allowing for a team-based approach so that incidents can be resolved quickly. Collaboration among employees often results in faster resolution of the incident and builds stronger, long-term, trusting relationships.
This process establishes a method to utilize the organizational chain of command (reference the Chain of Command Memorandum) for reporting purposes.

sections.heading: Informal Internal Conflict Resolution Process
steps: Attempt to resolve issues and personal complaints at the lowest possible level. When appropriate, the parties directly involved in conflict should try to resolve the conflict between each other.

Speak only to those people whose involvement is necessary to facilitate a resolution to a complaint. At times, this might include only the Complainant(s) and Respondent(s) and/or the immediate supervisor.

Any employee who feels that there is perceived wrongdoing, and cannot resolve the conflict between the originating parties, should utilize the proper chain of command and direct all concerns to their supervisor or manager. Supervisors should use conflict resolution techniques to attempt to resolve the concern(s) brought forward.

Chain of command establishes accountability and lays out the organizational lines of authority and decision-making power. This ensures that every task, job position, and division has one person assuming responsibility for performance.
If needed, the employee reporting the incident may use the Internal Incident Form (Attachment A) or send an email documenting all known information related to the incident.

sections.heading: Formal Internal Conflict Resolution Process
steps: The manager or supervisor should make the determination as to whether a violation of any department or county policy has taken place.

Any supervisor or manager who receives a complaint of a violation of department or county policy shall follow all policy protocols, and if necessary provide the employee with a copy of the policy, complete any needed documentation, and notify the department Employee Resources Department.

The employee reporting the incident may also use the Internal Incident Form (Attachment A) to document all known information related to the incident if a specific policy does not have an attachment to complete.

Employees should only speak to those people whose involvement is necessary to facilitate a resolution to a complaint. At times, this might include only the Complainant(s) and Respondent(s) and/or the immediate supervisor.

sections.heading: Notification
steps: In certain cases, a request for case status may come from the complainant regarding the respondent. If applicable, a statement such as the following should be made: "The complaint has been investigated and appropriate action has been taken."

related.name: DAS Chain of Command Memorandum, February 24, 2017 (Attachment B)

appendices: Appendix A: Internal Incident Form
Appendix B: Chain of Command Memorandum (February 24, 2017)

revisions.author: Outcomes for Pets (draft)
revisions.desc: Migrated into standard RCDAS SOP template. Content unchanged from 2017 version; substantive edits pending (see edit log).

documents.title: Job Recruitment Notification
documents.purpose: To alert Department of Animal Services (DAS) employees of job opportunities.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: All Department of Animal Services (DAS) employees
documents.supersedes: 000-16 Job Recruitment Notification, effective September 30, 2009 and April 19, 2007

sections.heading: Policy
steps: Employees will receive notification of Department of Animal Services job opportunities via email. The notification will include a Human Resources determined cut-off date for submitting resumes to Job Gateway.

sections.heading: Procedure
steps: The Departmental Employee Resources staff will email a notification of current DAS job opportunities to each staff member who has a county email account. The email will contain the job titles, work location(s), number of open positions, the deadline to submit resumes for consideration, and instructions about how to submit a resume.

steps.step: Employees can access Job Gateway by:
sub: Visiting www.rc-hr.com.
Selecting the Careers tab.
Selecting the How to Apply tab.
Selecting the icon listed as Log In Job Gateway Current Employees.
Continuing with log in and further instructions.

steps: Employees are responsible for creating and submitting their resumes by the stated deadline and taking any and all tests for jobs that they are interested in.

revisions.author: Outcomes for Pets (draft)
revisions.desc: Migrated into standard RCDAS SOP template. Content unchanged from 2017 version; substantive edits pending (see edit log).

documents.title: Code of Ethics and Employee Conduct
documents.purpose: The Department of Animal Services (DAS) has established the Code of Ethics to provide a consistent, ethical framework for DAS business practices. The Mission Statement of the Department is "Working together to improve Riverside County for people and animals." In order to accomplish the mission, the employees and volunteers of DAS must demonstrate honesty, integrity, and respect for confidential information, our customers, volunteers, and fellow employees. We are committed to the humane treatment of animals in our care and in the communities.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: This policy provides an ethical uniform business practice for DAS employees and volunteers. All DAS employees will uphold the standards of the DAS Code of Ethics in conjunction with the County's Code of Ethics. Each employee will complete the Code of Ethics Acknowledgement form (Attachment A) during the first week of employment and during annual reviews, or when either Code of Ethics policy is revised.
documents.supersedes: 000-17 DAS Code of Ethics & Employee Conduct, effective September 9, 2013 and September 30, 2009
references: County-wide Code of Ethics (Approved January 29, 1991, Item 3.17)
Management Resolution, SEIU and LIUNA Memorandum of Understanding
BOS Policy C-35, Standards of Ethical Conduct to Address Fraud, Waste and Abuse
BOS Policy C-23
DAS Policy 000-60, Diversity Policy
DAS Policy 000-10, Public Records and Subpoenas
DAS Memorandum on Professional and Courteous Conduct
DAS Memorandum, Chain of Command
California Labor Code 1102.5 and 1106
County of Riverside Alcohol and Drug Abuse Policy
County Anti-Violence in the Workplace Policy

sections.heading: Confidentiality
steps: Respect the rights and privacy of, and safeguard information about, a customer, member of the public, animal, DAS employees, and volunteers.

Department records should contain no unnecessary reference to behavior or social condition. Personal values and judgments are not appropriate in either customer or animal related records. It may be important to describe specific aspects of problematic behavior in some cases.
Release of information on employees and customers will be in compliance with DAS Policy 000-10, Public Records and Subpoenas.

sections.heading: Professional Conduct

steps.step: Maintain high standards of professional behavior and responsibility. All employees shall comply with the LIUNA and SEIU MOUs and the Resolution for Exempt Management, Management, Confidential, and other unrepresented employees. The MOUs and Resolution provide that any of the following acts of an employee who has permanent status shall be good cause for dismissal, demotion, reduction in compensation, suspension, or any other action taken for disciplinary reasons:
sub: Dishonesty.
Incompetence.
Inefficiency or negligence in performance of duties.
Neglect of duty.
Insubordination.
Willful violation of an employee regulation prescribed by the Board of Supervisors or the head of the department in which the employee is employed.
Absence without leave.

Conviction of either a felony, or any offense, misdemeanor or felony, involving moral turpitude, or any offense in connection with or affecting the employee's duties, other than minor traffic violations. Conviction means a plea of guilty or nolo contendere or a determination of guilt in a court of competent jurisdiction.
Discourteous treatment of the public or other employees.
Political activity in violation of federal or state law.
Physical or mental unfitness to perform assigned duties.
Making a material misrepresentation in connection with obtaining or maintaining employment or position.
Conduct either during or outside of duty hours which adversely affects the employee's job performance or operation of the department/district in which they are employed.

Failure to maintain the license, registration, certificate, professional qualifications, education, or eligibility required for the employee's classification, when the failure of the employee to maintain such requirements adversely affects the employee's ability to perform the job or the performance of the department/district. The department/district shall prescribe procedures to ensure that employees affected by the requirements are informed of them.
Substance abuse in violation of the County of Riverside Alcohol and Drug Abuse Policy.
Violation of the County Anti-Violence in the Workplace Policy.

steps: Direct any criticism of another staff member's work or actions to that person, to that person's supervisor, or follow the appropriate chain of command per DAS's Chain of Command memorandum.

Treat all individuals with dignity and respect, as identified in DAS's memorandum on Professional and Courteous Conduct, the Diversity Policy, and the discourteous treatment provision above. Employees are expected to conduct themselves in a professional, courteous, responsive, respectful, and civil manner in the workplace at all times. Rude and discourteous behavior is unacceptable and will not be tolerated.

sections.heading: Quality of Service
steps: The maintenance of high standards of professional competence and quality of service is the responsibility of all DAS employees.

Each individual will be responsible for recognizing the limitations of his/her competence and will not provide services or use procedures with which he/she is not familiar. A supervisor should be sought out for assistance when needed.

sections.heading: Moral and Legal Standards
steps: Show regard for the social codes and moral expectations of the community in which you work. Violations of accepted moral and legal standards may result in personal harm and injury to reputations of DAS customers, colleagues, employees themselves, and DAS, as well as cause unnecessary risk to DAS.
Do not misrepresent your professional credentials, qualifications, affiliations, and functions, or those of colleagues, institutions, and organizations with which you are associated.

sections.heading: Employee-Customer Relationships
steps: Maintain professional and objective personal conduct between employees and the customer.
Criticism or controversy related to staff actions, County, or DAS policies, personnel, or procedures shall not be directed to customers, discussed within their hearing, or in open view of customers.

sections.heading: Fraud, Waste, and Abuse
steps: Adhere to and uphold County Policy C-35 on reporting fraud, waste, and abuse. It is the duty of every employee to report any known violation of this policy, or what would appear to a reasonable person to be a violation of this policy.

steps.step: Reporting channels:
sub: Employees can report the information to their immediate supervisor, or via the chain of command if the employee is not satisfied with the supervisor's response or when the supervisor's conduct may be in question.
The employee can report the information anonymously via the Internet by going to https://www.RivcoFraudHotline.com or www.lighthouse-services.com/rivco.
Personnel issues or complaints of lesser severity should be reported to the department's internal Employee Resources Division for handling.

Personnel issues or complaints of a serious severity should be reported to the County Human Resources Department (i.e., employee discrimination, favoritism, mistreatment, violence in the workplace, harassment, and safety concerns), as indicated in Policy C-35.

sections.heading: Supervisors and Managers
steps: Encourage and be open to employees reporting any actual or suspected fraud or other criminal, negligent, or prohibited activities occurring in the workplace.

Take reports seriously; steps will be taken immediately to address any employee report of actual or suspected fraud or other criminal, negligent, or prohibited activity. Substantiated reports will be referred to County Human Resources in accordance with BOS Policy C-23.

It is a violation of County Policy C-35 to retaliate against an employee who makes a report using the county's anonymous incident reporting system under California Labor Code 1102.5 and 1106. The CA State Attorney General's Whistleblower Hotline number is (800) 952-5225.

documents.title: Expense Reimbursement Claim Procedure
documents.purpose: This policy establishes procedures and standards for reimbursement of general travel and other actual and necessary expenses incurred by departmental employees during performance of official county business.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services. Travel approval: County Executive Officer or designee for travel not in the approved budget, costing $1,000 or more per person, or out of state; Department Head for travel under $1,000 per person.
documents.scope: Applicable to all Animal Services personnel requesting reimbursement for department expenses
documents.supersedes: 000-19 Expense Reimbursement Claim Procedure, effective October 6, 2009 and August 14, 2000
references: Board of Supervisors Policy D-1 (Reimbursement for General Travel and Other Actual and Necessary Expenses)
DAS 200-01 (Guidelines for the Operation of Motor Vehicles)
IRS Publication 1542 (high cost cities)
Government Code section 53232.4 (penalties)
Public Records Act, Chapter 3.5 (commencing with Section 6250) of Division 7, Title 1

steps.step: The following travel is authorized for reimbursement by the County:
sub: Meeting with representatives on policy positions.
Attendance at educational seminars that are designed to improve the employee's skill and information levels.
Participation in organizations whose activities affect the Department or County interests.
Attending county events.

sections.heading: Lodging
steps: Reimbursement for actual costs will not exceed $159 per night, inclusive of all occupancy, accommodation taxes, and other room related taxes and fees. Lodging in high cost cities as defined by the Internal Revenue Service (IRS Publication 1542) or by the Board of Supervisors (Sacramento) shall not exceed $239 per night, or the applicable conference rate at the conference hosting hotel.
Provide an explanation of the business purpose of the stay with all employee reimbursement claims for lodging, supported with a receipt/facility folio.

The department head may approve extended lodging if the cost is less than daily travel expenses without the extended stay. Approval of extended lodging for any location in Riverside, Orange, San Diego, Imperial, Los Angeles, and San Bernardino counties is required prior to the travel occurrence and must be less costly than a daily commute.

sections.heading: Meals
steps: Actual cost (not to exceed maximum) shall be allowed for meals related to attendance at conventions, scheduled meetings, conferences, seminars, special assignments, or an assignment that requires an overnight stay. No meal expense will be reimbursed during attendance at any single day event. All meal reimbursement requests must be accompanied by an original detailed receipt. Without a detailed receipt, County policy will not allow reimbursement for the expense.

Maximum reimbursement for meals, per day, is $51, inclusive of taxes and tip. Tips in excess of 15% of the cost of the meal will not be reimbursed. Tips made at fast food restaurants and/or convenience stores will not be reimbursed, even if the meal cost is less than the maximum reimbursement rate (e.g., meal at $6.00, tip equals $6.90).

The maximum reimbursement for meals in high cost cities as defined by the Internal Revenue Service (IRS Publication 1542) or by the Board of Supervisors (Sacramento) is $71, inclusive of taxes and tip.
All reimbursement claims are based on actual (not to exceed maximum) cost.

Reimbursement for meals may exceed the maximum amounts only if the meal is organized by a non-county entity where the established price of the meal includes facility, speaker, or other costs and is a required portion of the meeting and/or conference. A written statement explaining the necessity for incurring such expense and supporting documentation (e.g., flyer, agenda, or brochure) must be submitted with the employee reimbursement claim.
When the cost of a meal is included as part of a registration charge or fee, no additional employee reimbursement may be claimed for that meal.

Expenses for meals are limited to activities outside normal work duties. No reimbursement for meals will be made for same day travel. Reimbursement for a meal is provided when it is not reasonable for employees to provide their own meal. Special situations may be considered on a case-by-case basis. A memo from the employee to the department head is required, and the department head's concurrence must be noted before the memo is forwarded to the designated Executive Office analyst for review and final approval.
Travel to a temporary worksite does not qualify an employee for meal reimbursement.
No reimbursement shall be made for alcoholic beverages of any kind.

sections.heading: Transportation
steps: Actual cost of common carrier services, including taxicabs, car rentals, and baggage fees (overweight or excessive number of bags are excluded), when necessary, shall be allowed.

Utilize online travel services and secure the least expensive flights and car rental arrangements possible. Upon request from the Auditor/Controller, the department is to provide supporting documentation that the flights and car reservations made were the least expensive option available.

Travel in business class, first class, or any category on any flight above the coach/economy level is allowable if (1) the traveler pays the difference, or (2) the department can document that no other option exists and the selected flight is the only option for travel.

Book reservations for air transportation as early as is reasonable (minimum of 30 days before travel is to take place) to take advantage of lower cost airfares. Airline government and group rates must be used when available.

sections.heading: Rental Cars
steps: The County maintains a contract with a vehicle rental company, and every effort should be made to use the contracted company. If available, the reservation for a rental car should be made on a county Purchasing card (P-card). A P-card shall be used for any reservations that are not made with the county contracted vendor. Government and group rates must be used when available.

Submit actual costs evidenced by an original, dated receipt, inclusive of all related taxes and other rental fees, along with actual gas receipts (dated, vendor name printed on the receipt) obtained for the purchase of gas for the rental vehicle.

Only standard equipment is allowed, and no rental car reimbursement will be made for cars above the mid-range size unless four or more employees are traveling in the same vehicle and this information is documented in the reimbursement information.

Purchase the Loss Damage Waiver (LDW), as required by the county, so the employee is not held responsible for damage (under normal circumstances) to the rental vehicle; such cost will be reimbursed. The county will not reimburse employees for the cost of other optional insurance (e.g., liability, uninsured/underinsured motorist, personal accident, and personal effects), as the county is self-insured for vehicle liability and third party physical damage and provides worker's compensation coverage.

Notify Human Resources, Risk Management Division at (951) 955-3540 and the employee's supervisor as soon as possible (within 24 hours) of any event, incident, or accident related to the rental car. The employee must complete the County Vehicle Accident/Incident Report, Form 942.6 (Safety Division form). See DAS Policy 200-1 (DAS Guidelines for the Operation of Motor Vehicles); for a copy of the form, go to the Q drive at Q//ANMSHLT_Forms, Letterhead, Misc. Info/Incident Accident Report Form 942.6.

sections.heading: Private Automobile
steps: Reimbursement for use of a private vehicle shall be allowed upon authorization of the department head. The County's private vehicle mileage reimbursement rate is the same rate as the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) standard mileage rate and will be effective concurrently with the IRS' periodic establishment of such rate.

steps.step: If an employee is required to use the employee's personal vehicle while in the course and scope of employment, the employee must, prior to using said vehicle, do the following:
sub: Complete the Authorization to Drive Riverside County Vehicle or Private Vehicle for County Business, Form 30, authorizing the employee to use a personal vehicle, which must be approved by the department head. The form is located on the Department Q Drive at Q//ANMSHLT_Shared/Forms, Letterhead, Misc. Info/Authorization to drive Form 30.

Insure the vehicle to at least the minimum limits required by the State of California, or if registered out of state, the insurance must be equal to or greater than the minimum limits required by the State of California. Although not required, it is recommended that employees who use their personal vehicle while in the course and scope of employment place a business use endorsement on their personal automobile policy. The expense of adding a business use endorsement is the sole responsibility of the employee.

Maintain a valid driver's license, which is appropriate for the class of vehicle to be operated. If any restrictions apply, the employee must notify his/her supervisor of the restrictions and/or any and all changes in the license (i.e., suspended, etc.).

steps: The use of motorcycles, mopeds, and similar types of vehicles for the conduct of county business is expressly prohibited.
When a department head authorizes use of a private vehicle for the convenience of the driver, instead of more economical travel by air, reimbursement shall not exceed the cost of usual airfare.

Notify Human Resources, Risk Management Division's representative, and the employee's supervisor as soon as possible (within 24 hours) of any incident or accident. Employees must complete the County Vehicle Accident/Incident Report, Form 942.6 (Human Resources Safety Division form). See DAS Policy 200-1 (DAS Guidelines for the Operation of Motor Vehicles); for a copy of the form, go to the Q drive at Q//ANMSHLT_Forms, Letterhead, Misc. Info/Incident Accident Report Form 942.6.

sections.heading: Miscellaneous Expenses
steps: Miscellaneous expenses, including charges for business telephone calls, fax service, e-mail service, the cost of usual or necessary services and supplies, including emergency repairs, parts or towing for county vehicles, conference registration fees, vehicle parking, bridge/road tolls, and any other justifiable business expenses, shall be allowed if they represent a valid business need.

Provide a satisfactory explanation of the circumstances for these expenditures. An employee reimbursement for actual miscellaneous expenses shall be accompanied by an original detailed receipt or other original voucher. Personal telephone calls are not reimbursed.

sections.heading: Travel Authorization
steps: Reimbursement for travel expenses requires prior authorization. No reservations shall be made until approval has been secured as outlined below.

steps.step: By County Executive Officer or designee:
sub: All travel wherein the estimated total cost (including registration, transportation, lodging, and meals) is not included in the approved budget or is expected to cost $1,000 or more per person, or if the travel is out of state. Prior approval for travel estimated as costing more than $1,000 or travel out of state is required even if the travel was anticipated and approved in the department budget.

Each request should be in the form of a memorandum that details costs to be incurred and substantiates the need for said travel. Attendance must be required for purposes of maintaining a professional license, or participation in professional activities which benefit the County of Riverside, and not solely for the purpose of professional enhancement or to collect an award. Funding availability for the proposed travel is not a guarantee that the travel will be approved. The travel must provide a clear benefit to the County of Riverside.

steps.step: By Department Head:
sub: All travel wherein the estimated total cost (including registration, transportation, lodging, and meals) is less than $1,000 per person. This travel should also be requested in an email prepared by the employee, outlining all anticipated expenditures. If the travel involves participation at a conference or training venue, the proposed agenda should be included. The memorandum should explicitly detail how the proposed travel benefits Riverside County.

The Department Head's approval is an indication that the travel is included in the approved departmental budget. If the travel is not in the approved budget, the Department Head should make a recommendation and forward the memo to the designated analyst in the Executive Office.

steps: Format: All approved travel should be noted on a per trip basis in a memorandum signed by either the County Executive Officer/designee or the department head, as delineated above. A copy of the signed memorandum should be attached to any requests for payment of travel expenses, including Form 14, which is located on the Q drive at Q//ANMSHLT_Shared/Forms, Letterhead, Misc. Info/Expense Reimbursement Claim Form 14.

sections.heading: Use of Claim Form
steps: File employee expense claims on a Form 14; include date, business destination, amount, and business purpose. Claims shall be filed promptly, no longer than two weeks from the business travel or expense. Claims filed after this time will not be considered for payment without documentation of the reason for the late filing.

When requesting reimbursement, complete the Form 14 online and print it on pink paper. This helps the Finance team identify employee reimbursements so they can be prioritized as urgent. All employee reimbursement forms shall be processed as pay immediately; this should help to reduce the employee's wait time to receive the reimbursement voucher. Form 14 is located on the Q drive at Q//ANMSHLT_Shared/Forms, Letterhead, Misc. Info/Expense Reimbursement Claim Form 14.

Original detailed receipts are required for reimbursement. Original receipts must include the name of the establishment where service was provided and the date on which the service was rendered. Restaurant receipts must include items ordered as well as the total payment made. All claim forms and associated documents related to reimbursable county expenditures are considered public records and, therefore, are subject to disclosure under the Public Records Act (Chapter 3.5, commencing with Section 6250, of Division 7, Title 1).

sections.heading: Penalties
steps: Penalties for the misuse of public resources or falsifying expense reports in violation of expense reporting policies may include, but are not limited to, the penalties specified in Government Code section 53232.4.

related.name: Authorization to Drive Riverside County Vehicle or Private Vehicle for County Business, Form 30
DAS 200-01 Guidelines for the Operation of Motor Vehicles

appendices: Appendix: 000-19 Expense Reimbursement Claim Form 14.xlsm (attached separately)
Appendix: 000-19 mileage.xlsm (attached separately)

travel out of state is required even if the travel was anticipated and approved in the department budget.", "each request should be in the form of a memorandum that details costs to be incurred and substantiates the need for said travel. attendance must be required for purposes of maintaining a professional license, or participation in professional activities which benefit the county of riverside, and not solely for the purpose of professional enhancement or to collect an award. funding availability for the proposed travel is not a guarantee that the travel will be approved. the travel must provide a clear benefit to the county of riverside."]} {"step": "by department head:", "sub": ["all travel wherein the estimated total cost (including registration, transportation, lodging, and meals) is less than $1,000 per person. this travel should also be requested in an email prepared by the employee, outlining all anticipated expenditures. if the travel involves participation at a conference or training venue, the proposed agenda should be included. the memorandum should explicitly detail how the proposed travel benefits riverside county.", "the department head's approval is an indication that the travel is included in the approved departmental budget. if the travel is not in the approved budget, the department head should make a recommendation and forward the memo to the designated analyst in the executive office."]} format: all approved travel should be noted on a per trip basis in a memorandum signed by either the county executive officer/designee or the department head, as delineated above. a copy of the signed memorandum should be attached to any requests for payment of travel expenses, including form 14, which is located on the q drive at q//anmshlt_shared/forms, letterhead, misc. info/expense reimbursement claim form 14. use of claim form file employee expense claims on a form 14; include date, business destination, amount, and business purpose. claims shall be filed promptly, no longer than two weeks from the business travel or expense. claims filed after this time will not be considered for payment without documentation of the reason for the late filing. when requesting reimbursement, complete the form 14 online and print it on pink paper. this helps the finance team identify employee reimbursements so they can be prioritized as urgent. all employee reimbursement forms shall be processed as pay immediately; this should help to reduce the employee's wait time to receive the reimbursement voucher. form 14 is located on the q drive at q//anmshlt_shared/forms, letterhead, misc. info/expense reimbursement claim form 14. original detailed receipts are required for reimbursement. original receipts must include the name of the establishment where service was provided and the date on which the service was rendered. restaurant receipts must include items ordered as well as the total payment made. all claim forms and associated documents related to reimbursable county expenditures are considered public records and, therefore, are subject to disclosure under the public records act (chapter 3.5, commencing with section 6250, of division 7, title 1). penalties penalties for the misuse of public resources or falsifying expense reports in violation of expense reporting policies may include, but are not limited to, the penalties specified in government code section 53232.4. board of supervisors policy d-1 (reimbursement for general travel and other actual and necessary expenses) das 200-01 (guidelines for the operation of motor vehicles) irs publication 1542 (high cost cities) government code section 53232.4 (penalties) public records act, chapter 3.5 (commencing with section 6250) of documents.title: Cash Box Issuance
documents.purpose: To provide guidelines for the proper issuance of cash box cash to Department of Animal Services employees.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: All Department of Animal Services employees that handle money at front counters, clinics, or special events
documents.supersedes: 000-24 Cash Box Issuance, revised September 29, 2009; initial June 14, 2007

sections.heading: Cash Drawer General Procedures
steps: Department of Animal Services employees who work the front desk, clinic, or special events shall be required to check out cash box cash from the cash box cash custodian assigned to the facility where the cash drawer is issued.

Only one cash drawer shall be issued per person, with no exceptions. The person who signs out the cash drawer shall be the only person to use that cash drawer during the assigned shift, with no exceptions.
Use the cash issued to provide change to cash paying customers only.

Before any employee is authorized to sign out cash box cash, they must attend a cash handling course presented by the Finance Unit of the Department of Animal Services. Cash handling courses will be offered monthly by the Finance Unit.

Any time a shortage or overage is noted, the individual who signed the cash box cash out, and their supervisor during the shift where the overage/shortage occurred, shall be required to attend a refresher cash handling course.

During the course of business, a cash box may, on occasion, be unable to make proper change. In these instances, the shift supervisor may exchange a larger bill from the drawer that cannot make proper change for small bills from a drawer that has the capacity to make change for the larger bill. The supervisor and both employees assigned to each cash drawer will need to verify the amount being pulled from their drawer and the amount being returned to their drawer. It should be an even trade, with no exceptions.

sections.heading: Cash for Cash Drawer Sign-Out Procedures
steps: To sign out the cash drawer cash, the employee will need to present their county identification badge to the cash drawer custodian.

The employee signing out the cash drawer shall verify the amount issued in front of the cash drawer custodian. Once that is done, the cash drawer custodian shall sign the daily cash for cash drawer form. The person issuing the cash drawer and the person signing for the cash drawer shall not be the same person.

steps.step: Record the following information on the cash drawer sign-out sheet (Attachment A, B, C, or D, depending on shelter location):
sub: Date cash drawer is issued.
Time cash drawer is issued.
Cash drawer amount (hard coded by location).
Name of person issuing the cash drawer (printed neatly).
Signature of person issuing the cash drawer.
Name of person receiving the cash drawer (printed neatly).
Signature of person receiving the cash drawer.
County Employee ID number (E-Number).

steps: Each shelter location is responsible for filing the cash drawer sign-out sheet once completed. These will be secured in a file and kept for a period of seven (7) fiscal years. Each shelter manager must set up a file for each fiscal year. This will allow for easy disposal at the end of the seventh fiscal year.

sections.heading: Cash Box/Register Procedures
steps: When an employee signs out cash for their cash box, two five dollar ($5.00) bills shall be issued, with the remaining amount being one dollar ($1.00) bills. The total of the one dollar bills may change depending on the location where the cash box cash is issued (see Cash Drawer Authorized Amount).

Once the employee signs out the cash for their cash box, they are responsible for the funds. If they leave the cash box cash custodian with an incorrect amount and come up with an overage or shortage at the end of the shift, they will be held responsible for the discrepancy and will be required to attend the next cash handling refresher course with their supervisor. In addition, the supervisor or shift supervisor of the person reporting the cash discrepancy shall document the incident in the form of a formal write-up, per the County Disciplinary Process.
Cash boxes/registers shall be inaccessible to the general public.
Cash boxes/registers shall be locked and keys removed when more than an arm's length away or out of direct line of sight of the authorized user.
Cash is to be deposited at the end of each shift (see Policy 000-25, Daily Deposit Procedures).
The authorized user shall be held responsible for any overage/shortage while the cash box is entrusted to their care, whether due to negligence or failure to follow prescribed procedures.

Any misuse or misappropriation of collected fees shall be reported to local law enforcement and reported as theft in accordance with County policy. The Department of Animal Services shall pursue prosecution to the full extent of the law.

related.name: Cash Drawer Cash Sign-out Sheet (Attachments A, B, C, and D, by shelter location)

appendices: Appendix: 000-24 Attachment A, B, C & D.xlsx (attached separately)

documents.title: Daily Deposit Procedures
documents.purpose: Establish a consistent deposit procedure. For Fiscal use only.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: Applicable to all Fiscal Team members that handle deposits for Animal Services
documents.supersedes: 000-25 Daily Deposit Procedures, revised September 29, 2009; initial June 14, 2007
references: County retention policy (7-year retention of deposit documentation)

sections.heading: Receipt/Revenue Code Balancing
steps: Collect all cash boxes with individual Daily Deposit Batch Sheets and calculator tapes created by the cash box custodian. Ensure the Daily Deposit Batch Sheet and the calculator tape match. All cash boxes will be collected from the safe in the break room.
Ensure all revenue codes are accurate. If any corrections are needed, ensure they are done before moving to the next step.

steps.step: Run a calculator tape for each cash box. The calculator tape will have the following on it:
sub: Receipt total.
Cash total.
Credit card total.
Check total.
Total of cash, credit card, and checks.

steps: Ensure the receipt totals match the cash, credit card, and check totals.
Run the Daily Deposit Batch for all cash boxes in your location (Riverside, CVAC, San Jacinto, and Blythe).
Run a calculator tape that adds up all clerks' cash box receipts for the day.
Verify the calculator tape and the Daily Deposit Batch Slip match.

Run one calculator tape for all deposits. Add all credit card totals to get a grand total, and add all credit card settlement reports to get a total; both should match. Add receipt cash and check totals to get a grand total. Add credit card and cash/checks to get a grand total. This should balance against the Daily Deposit Batch Slip for all users and the Account Code Report.

sections.heading: Preparing Receipts for Deposit
steps: Verify all checks have a current date and designated signatures.
Verify all checks are stamped with the "for deposit only" stamp and the bank's account number.
Make photocopies of any check that did not get run through and approved by the check verification machine.
Input cash and checks (that did not go through and get approved in the check verification machine) into the Excel Cash Reconciliation form.

Subtract the total amount of credit card receipts from the total amount of receipts; ensure the total receipts, minus web licensing and credit card totals, matches the total on the Excel Cash Reconciliation form.
Print, sign, and date the Excel Cash Reconciliation form.
The cash and check total from the previous calculator tape (Receipt/Revenue Code Balancing, step 8) should match the Excel Cash Reconciliation form.

Have a second person verify the cash and checks. They will run a calculator tape of the cash and checks. Once completed, they will initial and date the calculator tape and the Excel Cash Reconciliation form.

sections.heading: Preparing Cash and Checks for Deposit and Transport to Bank
steps: Prepare the plastic deposit bag provided by the bank.
Fill out the three part deposit ticket (White, Pink, and Yellow).
Place the white deposit ticket, Excel Cash Reconciliation form, and checks in the bottom pouch of the deposit bag. Ensure you seal the bottom pouch of the bag before moving on to the next step.
Place the pink deposit ticket, Excel Cash Reconciliation form, and cash in the top pouch of the deposit bag. Ensure you seal the top pouch of the bag before moving on to the next step.
On the inside lip of the top pouch, a bank bag number is listed. Attach this number to the yellow deposit ticket.

Include the yellow deposit ticket and Excel Cash Reconciliation form with all documentation of the daily deposit. These are immediately filed in a cardboard banker's box that will be stored for 7 years, per county retention policy.

sections.heading: Armored Transport Pick-Up
steps: Before giving the cash/check deposit to the armored transport person, verify the employee's credentials are valid for pickup of the deposit.
Once identified as an authorized transport person, the authorized transport personnel shall sign the deposit book before Animal Services relinquishes the deposit to the driver.

documents.title: Restraint and Care of Personal Pets
documents.purpose: To provide consistent guidelines and safety practices for DAS employees and volunteers permitted to bring their personal pets to work.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: DAS employees and volunteers permitted to bring their personal pets to work at all County shelter locations.
documents.supersedes: Policy 000-40, effective May 11, 2016 (source is an undated, unsigned draft revision of the 2016 policy)

sections.heading: Policy
steps: It is the intention of the Department to permit personal pets to accompany employees and volunteers to work when their job functions allow such an opportunity.
To ensure the safety of employees, personal pets, and customers of the Department, personal pets must not interfere with day to day work activities or customer interactions.

Animal shelters contain disease and safety hazards. Employees and volunteers electing to bring their personal pets to work acknowledge the potential hazards and risks and understand that the department is not liable for any issues that may arise.
Mutual consent and consideration to fellow staff is required when bringing personal pets into a shared office space.
Obtain approval from your supervisor to ensure the pet's presence does not impact business needs.

Failure to comply with this policy could result in a denial to violators. Widespread failure could result in elimination of this policy and prevention of employees bringing their personal pets to the shelter.

sections.heading: Dogs
steps: Keep dogs on a leash (standard 6-foot leash, no retractable leads) and maintain control over the dog at all times when in public and non-public areas. Exceptions include having the dog contained in a closed play yard, office, or cubicle. Dogs may not run free in open office areas.

steps.step: Dogs should be well-behaved. Bad behavior examples include:
sub: Excessive barking.
Tendency to snarl, growl, or snap to protect food or other resources.
Fearfulness in new situations or around strangers.
Severe attacks on other animals such as cats.
Attempts to mount people's legs.
Snapping and snarling when petted, groomed, or lifted.
Frequent attempts to chase moving objects such as bicycles, skateboards, cars, and trucks.
Biting, regardless of size.

steps: Dogs reported to supervision to be in violation of the well-behaved requirement must complete a Behavioral Assessment completed by a department approved and assigned Animal Behaviorist. The Animal Behaviorist's assessment will be reviewed by the Director of Animal Services or their designee to determine if the dog may return.

Keep dogs a minimum of 25 feet from kennels, corrals, or other areas where shelter animals are being housed outdoors or with outdoor access. Designated play yards and shelter lobbies are exempt from this rule. No personal dogs are permitted inside a pod, critter corner, cat room, or puppy room.

Dogs must be parasite free and not currently sick or suffering from an auto-immune disease or other ailment which may be worsened by exposure at an animal shelter. It is up to the pet owner to decide the best type of flea or tick program for their dog, and treatment is required.

The Department allows only three bathroom accidents per dog. After that the pet must stay home. The dog may return once it has been successfully potty-trained. Puppies up to 4 months in age are exempt from this rule.

Pick up pet waste always and immediately, whether inside or outside. No exceptions. The Department provides garbage cans located outside for owners to easily throw away any feces. Failure to do so could result in lost privileges.

Introduce a personal dog to other dogs only in secured play yards and not in an office or shared area. The owner or custodian of all animals involved must agree to the interaction prior to the interaction taking place. Those introducing personal pets to unfamiliar pets must take great care to ensure all animals are safe and comfortable.
Dogs must be currently licensed, vaccinated, well-groomed, and clean.

sections.heading: Cats
steps: Confine cats in a carrier or in a closed office or cubicle.
Cats should be well-behaved (not aggressive towards people or animals).

Cats must be parasite free and not currently sick or suffering from an auto-immune disease or other ailment which may be worsened by exposure at an animal shelter. It is up to the pet owner to decide the best type of flea or tick program for their cat.

The Department allows only three bathroom accidents per cat. After that the pet must stay home. The owner must provide a litter pan for the cat during its visit and must keep it clean for the duration of the stay.
Cats must be currently vaccinated, well-groomed, and clean.

sections.heading: All Personal Pets
steps: Business needs of the department are first priority, followed by employee safety.

Too many animals in a work area can cause hazards to staff and stress to the animals. This may require staff to coordinate and plan ahead to ensure too many animals or incompatible animals are not in the same area. Supervisors will review and approve all requests.

Animals such as reptiles, arachnids, or others may frighten some staff or volunteers. Take special care to ensure co-workers are comfortable when handling any personal pet; consent is required during all interactions.
Pets must have water available at all times.
All pets must come with appropriate collars, leashes, and carriers for the size and type of pet.

Some work areas may require some animals to be kept in a crate, kennel, playpen, tank, or other appropriate enclosure (for example, public areas). Employees are responsible for providing proper confinement when needed.
Pets are not allowed in any break room or eating area.
Pets requiring a tank must have clean water and proper habitat. Cleaning of tanks must be done on the employee's personal time.
When animals are loose in an office or work area, post a sign stating there is a loose animal where it is visible before entering the office or work area.

steps.step: Pets, including foster pets, are not to be left overnight in an office or cubicle.
sub: Exemption: with a supervisor's permission, any pets that live in tanks or terrariums (reptiles, fish, turtles) may be left overnight.
Exemption: with a supervisor's permission, pets that live in specifically designed habitats (mice, guinea pigs, hamsters, rabbits) may be left overnight.

steps: In the event a pet is injured or gets sick, it is the owner's responsibility to seek veterinary care at their own expense.

related.name: Restraint and Care of Personal Animals at County Shelter Acknowledgement Form (retained in the employee's personnel file)

appendices: Appendix A: Acknowledgement Form

documents.title: Proper Procedure for the Operation of the Rhino 660
documents.flag: Numbered (=Policy per rule) but reads as a step-by-step protocol
documents.purpose: To establish a uniform procedure for the proper and safe use of the Rhino 660. All applicable staff must view the Basic Orientation DVD attached with the policy.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: This procedure is applicable to all Animal Services employees.
documents.supersedes: None identified; original policy effective April 10, 2012
references: T-CLOC Pre-Ride Inspection Checklist (ASI)
Rhino 660 owner's manual
Rhino 660 Basic Orientation DVD

definitions.def: Pre-ride inspection checklist covering Tires and Wheels, Controls and Cables, Lights and Electrics, Oil and Fuel, and Chain/Driveshaft and Chassis.

sections.heading: Basic Safety Requirements
steps: Limit the Rhino to two (2) people only, the driver and one passenger, and require both people to keep their entire body inside the enclosure.
Never allow more than two (2) people in the vehicle, and never allow passengers in the cargo bed.
Wear seat belts at all times, both driver and passenger.
Operate the vehicle at speeds that are safe for the terrain and visibility conditions.
Keep the wheels on the ground at all times; attempting jumps or stunts increases the risk of an accident with severe injuries.
Never operate the vehicle while under the influence of drugs or alcohol.

sections.heading: Controls
steps: Remember that although the Rhino has controls and features similar to a car, its handling characteristics will not be the same as an automobile.
Locate the brake pedal on the left and the gas pedal on the right, as in a car.

Turn the ignition key, located on the right side of the steering wheel, clockwise 45 degrees to put the electrical system in the ON position; turn the key 90 degrees and briefly hold it in the start position to engage the starter.
Use the digital meter on the dash, which displays the speedometer, odometer, two trip meters, clock, and an hour meter showing total key-on time.

steps.step: Control off-road handling with the drive select lever.
sub: Stop the vehicle and press the brake pedal firmly before using the lever.
Shift to select Low Range Forward, High Range Forward, Neutral, or Reverse; each position displays on the digital meter.

steps.step: Adjust handling with the On-Command 4WD/Differential Gear Lock Switch, which selects between 2WD and 4WD, or between 4WD and 4WD with fully locked differential.
sub: 2WD operates the rear wheels only; 4WD delivers power to the front wheels as needed through the limited slip differential; fully locked 4WD distributes power evenly to all four wheels so they turn at the same speed regardless of traction.
Bring the vehicle to a complete stop before switching drive modes; the difference in handling between 2WD and 4WD can be unexpected and can cause loss of control if you switch while in motion.
Confirm the indicator light comes on when the vehicle is in 4WD.

Slide the movable selector cover to the right to expose the Differential Gear Lock Switch; placing the switch in the locked position lights the Differential Gear Lock Indicator, which stays on while operating in fully locked 4WD.

steps: Stop the engine and let it cool for about ten (10) minutes if the coolant temperature warning light comes on; continuing to operate an overheating engine can cause severe engine damage.

Set the parking brake, located to the right of the driver's seat, by pulling up on the lever; release it by pulling the lever up slightly while pressing the release button and pushing the lever back down. An indicator light shows when the parking brake is applied. Do not drive the vehicle with the parking brake engaged.
Use the light switch located to the left of the steering wheel: the L position turns on the low beams and tail lights, and the H position turns on the high beams and the lights.

Pull out the choke knob, located above the key to the right of the steering wheel, to provide a rich fuel mixture for cold engine starting; push it back in before riding once the engine idles smoothly.

sections.heading: Operation
steps: Apply the brakes and shift into neutral before starting the engine.
Use the choke as needed based on air temperature and how long it has been since the engine was last run; very cold weather may require full choke, while a warm engine may not require any choke.
Let the engine warm up before operating the vehicle.
Apply the brake and shift into the desired gear; look at the digital meter to confirm you are in the right gear.
Look for people or obstacles before operating in reverse.
Do not attempt to turn around or make abrupt maneuvers on slopes.
Set the parking brake before exiting the vehicle whenever you park.
Keep your arms and legs inside the enclosure if the vehicle starts to tip over; do not attempt to prevent the tip over with your arms or legs.
Understand that operating the Rhino without the required training, or operating it improperly and carelessly, may result in disciplinary action.

sections.heading: Features
steps: Use the 12V accessory jack on the dashboard to plug in accessories such as cell phone chargers or portable spotlights; never use the jack for a cigarette lighter.
Remove either seat by pulling up on the lever in the center of the seat base to access the V Belt Case drain plug and storage area.
Observe the load limits: the tilt cargo bed has a 400 lb. load capacity, and the receiver bracket for a standard 2-inch automotive-type trailer hitch has a 150 lb. tongue weight limit.
Do not exceed the total weight capacity load of the vehicle and trailer combined.
Operate in low range when carrying cargo or pulling a trailer.

sections.heading: Pre-Op Checks (T-CLOC)
steps: Inspect the vehicle before each ride; the off-highway environment can be harsh on vehicles, and an inspection minimizes the chance of being injured or stranded.
Allow five to ten minutes for the inspection, depending on the machine.

Use the owner's manual to identify the equipment to check on the particular machine, along with the ASI T-CLOC checklist: Tires and Wheels, Controls and Cables, Lights and Electrics, Oil and Fuel, Chain/Driveshaft and Chassis (Appendix A).

sections.heading: Proper Cleaning and Storage
steps: Block off the exhaust and intake openings before washing to prevent water intrusion.
Check the air filter depending on use, as it can become saturated with debris.
Fill the fuel tank to prevent condensation accumulation and add fuel stabilizer and conditioner to prevent fuel from becoming stale if the vehicle is stored for 60 days or longer.

appendices: Appendix A: T-CLOC Pre-Ride Inspection Checklist
Appendix B: Rhino Operator Quiz
