documents.title: Records and Information Management
documents.purpose: To ensure that records in the department's custody, regardless of media or format, are maintained and managed as required by California State Law and the County of Riverside Board Policy Manual, and to provide Department of Animal Services employees with the information and procedures necessary to understand and carry out their responsibilities under Board of Supervisors' Policy A-43, County Records Management and Archives Policy, including the requirements for managing County records and the manner in which records shall be preserved, transferred, or destroyed.
documents.authority: Adopted pursuant to County of Riverside Board of Supervisors' Policy A-43, Section B.5, which requires each department to adopt policies and procedures in accordance with established guidelines for responsible recordkeeping. Approved by the Director of Animal Services.
documents.scope: This policy applies to all Department of Animal Services employees, as well as publicly created advisory boards and committees within the department's purview that have been delegated the authority to perform a governmental function. It applies to all records regardless of their media (paper, electronic, or other). All managers, supervisors, and designated coordinators are responsible for communicating the purpose and intent of this policy to all employees and should develop procedures as necessary to implement it.
documents.supersedes: All previous records management and record retention policies issued by the Department (per original policy); original effective April 5, 2012
references: Board of Supervisors' Policy A-43, County Records Management and Archives Policy
Government Code Section 6250
Government Code Section 12168 and 12168.7
Board of Supervisors Policy A-68
AIIM Recommended Practice ARP1-2009
Departmental Records Retention Schedule DRRS_CHA-AS_2011_Rev01 (approved by the Board of Supervisors June 29, 2011, Agenda Item 3.10)
County General Records Retention Schedule (GRRS)

definitions.def: Individual retention schedule for records that are unique to the Department.
The countywide retention schedule for common housekeeping or administrative records, available on the County intranet at http://rmap.asrclkrec.com/ApprovedRRS.asp.
Records which have lost some of their value or have been superseded by new records, but have not reached their specified retention.

All papers, maps, plans, photographic films and prints, microfilm or other microformats, electronic data, audio and visual materials, and other documents, regardless of physical form or characteristics, which are produced, received, owned, used, or retained by a department in the regular course of transacting official County business.
The record specifically designated by the custodian as the official record.
The individual responsible for maintaining the record copy or copy of record for the duration of the retention period.
The individual(s) responsible for carrying out the purpose and intent of this policy in accordance with the guidelines provided by Board of Supervisors' Policy A-43 and related departmental policies.
A table or chart that identifies the length of time a record must be retained before its final disposition.

A group of related documents arranged under a single filing arrangement or kept together as a unit because they consist of the same form, relate to the same subject, result from the same activity, or have certain common characteristics.

A combination of techniques, policies, and procedures for which there is no plausible scenario in which a document retrieved from or reproduced by the system could differ substantially from the document that is originally stored. (GC 12168.7)

sections.heading: Responsibilities
steps: The Department Head is responsible for the complete, accurate, and timely implementation of this policy and will rely upon the following staff to implement it.
The Departmental Records Coordinator (see BOS A-43 Section B.5) shall be responsible for providing guidance related to this policy and for promoting compliance with this policy.

Managers shall be responsible for ensuring that staff complies with this policy and related procedures; should any division or section specific procedures be necessary to fully comply, managers shall draft these procedures in consultation with the Departmental Records Coordinator.
Employees shall be responsible for ensuring that records for which they are responsible are accurate and are maintained and disposed of in accordance with this policy.

sections.heading: Records Not Addressed by the GRRS or DRRS
steps: Retain records and other documents or materials not expressly addressed by the GRRS or a DRRS until the administrative purpose and function is assessed.
Link the records to an existing records series on the GRRS or DRRS, or add a new records series to cover them.

sections.heading: Storage of Records
steps: Store records in the Department's office space or equipment, including servers, if the records are in regular use or are maintained in the office for convenience or ready reference. Examples of active files appropriately maintained in the office include active chronological files, research and reference files, legislative drafting files, pending complaint files, administrative files, and personnel files.

Send inactive records, for which use or reference has diminished sufficiently to permit removal from the Department's office space or equipment, to the County Records Center off-site storage facility.

sections.heading: Historical Records
steps: Offer historical records, which are no longer of use to the Department but which may be of historical interest because of their enduring research value, to the County of Riverside Robert J. Fitch Archives for preservation.
Do not destroy historical records except in accordance with the procedures set forth in Board of Supervisors' Policy A-43 Section B.3.

sections.heading: Pending Claims and Litigation
steps: Do not apply the retention periods in the GRRS or the departmental records retention schedule to materials that are otherwise eligible for destruction but which may be relevant to a pending claim or litigation against the Department or County.
Retain all documents and other materials related to a claim, once the Department becomes aware of a claim against it, until the claim or subsequent litigation has been resolved.
Notify other departments of the need to retain records where the Department has reason to believe those departments also have records relating to the claim or litigation.

sections.heading: Records Management: Organization and Maintenance

steps.step: Organize, arrange, and maintain records using a filing or recordkeeping system that:
sub: Is appropriate to the nature, purpose, and use of the records,
Can be easily understood by all users, and
Facilitates the location of and access to those records by all users, when and where needed.

steps: Store all records on an appropriate media format to ensure their preservation for the entire length of the required retention. (Reference Board of Supervisors Policy A-68, Government Code 12168.7, and AIIM Recommended Practice ARP1-2009 posted by the Secretary of State in partial fulfillment of GC 12168.7.)
Box inactive paper records until the applicable retention period has been met; box records according to the appropriate records series to facilitate timely disposal.

sections.heading: Records Management: Inventory
steps: Maintain a current inventory of all records in the custody or control of each division or office in the Department.

Use any format at the discretion of each division or office, including documenting each record type the office maintains in a centralized list that is updated annually or as needed, or highlighting each applicable record series on a copy of the GRRS or the DRRS.

sections.heading: Records Management: Records Retention Schedules
steps: Ensure all records created and maintained by the Department are covered by a records retention schedule approved by the Board of Supervisors (BOS Policy A-43 Section D.9).

Note that many of the Department's records are covered by the County of Riverside General Records Retention Schedule (GRRS); any records not covered by the GRRS must be covered by a departmental records retention schedule.
Contact the Records Coordinator for assistance to establish an individual records retention schedule.

sections.heading: Records Management: Disposition
steps: Systematically dispose of records that have met their retention requirements according to the approved GRRS or DRRS and are no longer needed.

Regularly review records against the GRRS and/or DRRS to identify their eligibility for destruction. This review must involve staff who can identify any possible legal or operational reason to retain the records in excess of their scheduled retention periods; these same staff should also sign off on the records disposition certificate or other appropriate permanent document.

Document the disposition of these records properly. A records disposition certificate is available from RMAP's website. The Records Coordinator must retain the completed disposition certificates permanently.

steps.step: Include in the records disposition certificate, at minimum, the following information:
sub: The name of the division responsible for the records;
The name of the employee responsible for confirming that the records are eligible for destruction;
Citation, including the date of approval/adoption, of the authority by which legal destruction is allowed (e.g., the GRRS or DRRS Schedule Number and Records Series Code);
Documentation of the types (tied to an approved records retention schedule), quantities, and dates of the records to be destroyed;
Signature of the Department Head or designee giving approval to destroy County records;
Date and method of destruction.

steps: Recycle non-confidential paper records.
Shred or otherwise render unreadable confidential paper records.
Erase or destroy electronically stored data; contact the Department's Information Technology division for assistance in effectively disposing of this data.
Offer records determined to have historical value to the County of Riverside Robert J. Fitch Archives in accordance with Board Policy A-43 Section B.3.

sections.heading: Record Retention and Destruction Schedule
steps: See the Records Management and Archives Program (RMAP) website for the most recent version of the General Records Retention Schedule.
See also the Departmental Records Retention Schedule approved by the Board of Supervisors on June 29, 2011 as Agenda Item 3.10 for department and program specific records.
For records held for audit purposes, the Auditor-Controller's Office, or appropriate auditing authority, will notify the department once the audit is complete.
For records held for litigation, maintain the requested records until the close of litigation plus an additional ten (10) years.

Ascertain the correct date of closure in order to establish this ten (10) year retention period, and establish appropriate procedures to ensure that records are retained for the period of time mandated and remain accessible as required.
Once litigation has concluded, the Division of Risk Management will provide approval to proceed with the approved disposition.

sections.heading: Transfer of Records to the Records Center
steps: Store all records listed in the schedule at the RMAP Records Center.
Each unit of the department is responsible for packing records applicable to their areas of responsibility listed on the retention schedule.

steps.step: Pack records as follows:
sub: Use one box for every 14 inches of letter-size files.
Use one box for 11 inches of legal-size files.
Pack only one record series per box. (Note: the original policy's example reads 'kennel permit applications should be in the same box as service dog license records'; see edit log.)
Keep file folders upright, facing the same direction, with file labels visible, and in logical order. For example, bite reports must be filed according to year, month, and pet owner's last name.
Place files in order from front to back (County seal is at the front of the box).
Use only approved records storage boxes, measuring approximately 15 x 12 x 10 inches with hand inserts on each end.
Do not use hanging file folders; place contents in manila file folders instead.
Do not use 3-ring binders; use Strap Ease to keep contents together.
Leave 1 to 2 inches of space in the box.
Ensure the box does not bulge at the sides and the lid fits securely.
Include only paper records.
Notify the Records Coordinator if packing mixed media materials.
Keep each box at 30 lbs. or less.

sections.heading: Completing the Records Transfer List (Form 677)
steps: Enter in Cell 1 a temporary number (for example, #1) neatly written on the outside of the box.
Leave Cell 2 blank.
Enter in Cell 3 the value from the Code column of the records schedule.
Enter in Cell 4 the RMAP Category Title for the records being transferred; it must correspond with the code in Cell 5.
Enter in Cell 5 the RMAP Category Code for the records being transferred; it must correspond with the title in Cell 4.

Enter in Cell 6 the alphabetic or numeric range of files within the box. If files are arranged using another filing system, an index is required; indicate in the REMARKS section that an index will be included in the box.
Enter in Cell 7 the applicable year for the files within the box. Include only one calendar year (e.g., 1/1/2007 through 12/31/2007) or fiscal year (e.g., 7/1/2006 through 6/30/2007) per box.

Enter in Cell 8 the destruction date for the records within the box, per the DAS records retention schedule. Generally, calendar year records will have a destruction date of January 1st of the year following the close of the retention period.
Submit the form to the Records Coordinator.

related.name: Board of Supervisors' Policy A-43, County Records Management and Archives Policy
DAS Records Retention Schedule Acknowledgement Form (retained in employee personnel file)
Records Management and Archives Program (RMAP), 951-486-7151, PO Box 472, Riverside, CA 92502

appendices: Appendix A: Board Policy A-43 submittal and Departmental Records Retention Schedule DRRS_CHA-AS_2011_Rev01 (attached separately)
Appendix B: Records Coordinator Proposed Description and Responsibilities (attached separately)
Appendix C: DAS Records Retention Schedule Acknowledgement Form (attached separately)

indicate in the remarks section that an index will be included in the box. enter in cell 7 the applicable year for the files within the box. include only one calendar year (e.g., 1/1/2007 through 12/31/2007) or fiscal year (e.g., 7/1/2006 through 6/30/2007) per box. enter in cell 8 the destruction date for the records within the box, per the das records retention schedule. generally, calendar year records will have a destruction date of january 1st of the year following the close of the retention period. submit the form to the records coordinator. board of supervisors' policy a-43, county records management and archives policy government code section 6250 government code section 12168 and 12168.7 board of supervisors policy a-68 aiim recommended practice arp1-2009 departmental records retention schedule drrs_cha-as_2011_rev01 (approved by the board of supervisors june 29, 2011, agenda item 3.10) county general records retention schedule (grrs)

documents.title: Requisition Authority for Purchasing Accountability
documents.purpose: The Department of Animal Services (DAS) is required to adhere to the purchasing policies established by the County Purchasing Department, the County Board of Supervisors (BOS), and various regulatory agencies such as the State, OSHPD, and the Controller's Office. The BOS has established that the County Purchasing Department has overall responsibility for the control of all County purchasing activity and has established levels of requisition dollar approver authority for County Department Heads. The DAS Director (Department Head) may establish levels of requisition dollar approver authority within DAS.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services. The authority delegated by the Director is to requisition materials, supplies, and services; it is not an authority to purchase or to commit to any contract or memorandum of understanding with or without financial impact.
documents.scope: For Animal Services employees who are obtaining signatures on requisitions for purchasing supplies, equipment, and services needed for the continued operation of the Department of Animal Services.
documents.supersedes: None identified; original policy effective August 2012
references: County of Riverside Purchasing Manual
CHA Policies A-3, A-26, A-27, and F-3

definitions.def: An individual delegated authority by the DAS Director to approve requisitions up to a specified dollar amount.

sections.heading: Requisition Dollar Approval Authority

steps.step: Apply the following levels of requisition dollar approval authority for purchasing supplies, equipment, and services needed for the continued operations of DAS:
sub: DAS Managers: $0 to $10,000
Chief Veterinarian: $0 to $25,000
Deputy Director Operations: $0 to $25,000
Deputy Director Administration: $0 to $50,000
Deputy Director Administration (in absence of Department Head): $0 to $100,000
DAS Director (Department Head): $0 to unlimited

steps: The DAS Director may transfer requisition authority to the Deputy Director of Administration during an absence.
Do not process purchase requests without the appropriate signatures.

sections.heading: Procedures
steps: DAS Purchasing, in coordination with DAS Finance, is responsible for the adherence of DAS personnel to this policy.
Approve DAS contract purchases annually through the appropriate management staff, based on expected annual expenditures.
In the absence of an approver, the interim authorized requisition dollar approver may sign for purchases within the approved annual amount as designated in this policy.
Include in cost the purchase price and all applicable fees, i.e., sales tax, freight, installation fees, etc.

Obtain additional supervisor or management approvals for certain commodities, such as association fees or dues, computer software, equipment and services, and seminar requests, to comply with County Department procedures.

sections.heading: Electronic Authorization for Above Requisition Dollar Authority
steps: E-mail the individual with the appropriate requisition authority seeking their approval to electronically process a purchase which exceeds your requisition authority.
Treat the e-mail response from that individual authorizing the purchase as their approval.
Proceed with the approved purchase and retain the e-mail which approves the purchase as supporting documentation.

sections.heading: Purchasing Services Requisition Dollar Approver Signature Log
steps: Use the Purchasing Services Requisition Dollar Approver Signature Log form (attached to this policy) to record each approver's typed name, designation, authorized dollar level ($0 to $10,000, $25,000, $50,000, $100,000, or unlimited), signature, and date, with Department Head name and signature.

related.name: Purchasing Services Requisition Dollar Approver Signature Log

appendices: Appendix A: Purchasing Services Requisition Dollar Approver Signature Log (attached separately)

revisions.desc: Migrated into standard RCDAS SOP template. Content unchanged from 2012 version; substantive edits pending (see edit log).

documents.title: Attendance and Punctuality
documents.purpose: Regular staff attendance and punctuality are important expectations in the Department's efforts to provide high levels of customer service and satisfaction. Departmental goals are achieved only when all employees on the team contribute positively. When employees meet their obligation to report for work punctually, the burden of work is not passed on to co-workers or delayed; reworking assignments or schedules to accommodate absences or tardiness represents waste and inefficiency. Employees who are late or absent from work are expected to follow the attendance policies covered in this section. Failure to maintain an acceptable attendance record may result in an unsatisfactory performance evaluation and/or disciplinary action.
documents.authority: The Director, Deputy Director, DAS managers, and supervisors shall be responsible for scheduling hours of work and break times, assigning overtime, reviewing time off requests, and training employees about the supervisor's responsibilities and employees' responsibilities related to time and attendance. Approved by the Director of Animal Services.
documents.scope: For all Department of Animal Services (DAS) employees.
documents.supersedes: Prior version effective July 1, 2012
references: CHA P-30
County Ordinance 440
BOS Policy C-13
Bargaining Unit's Memorandum of Understanding
DAS Policy 000-51 Employee Time-Off Work

definitions.def: Absent Without Pay.

sections.heading: Reporting and Returning for Work, Lunch, and Breaks
steps: Be at your designated workstation and ready to begin work at the specified start time designated by your supervisor and at the end of your scheduled lunch and breaks; otherwise you will be considered late.
Notify your supervisor if you will be late.

Call your supervisor or designee within thirty (30) minutes before or after your scheduled starting time if reporting off work at the beginning of a shift for any reason. Having someone else call on behalf of the employee is not acceptable, except in extreme situations.

Contact the supervisor prior to shift start time as the recommended method to report unscheduled tardiness. If you do not receive a response from the supervisor, try to make contact following the chain of command.
Follow the specific call off procedure outlined by your supervisor.

Employees who are less than 15 minutes late may, at the discretion of their supervisor, make up the time at the end of their shift or use vacation time to cover the tardiness in exceptional cases of unforeseen delays. Each division has the right to be more restrictive, up to and including 5 minutes late.

Employees who are late more than twice in a pay period may not make up the time for the third and subsequent days they are late and will be considered AWOP for the amount of time they are late on such days.
Employees eligible to make up late time may not do so by reducing their normal lunch period to less than 30 minutes or eliminating their breaks.
Understand that repetitive tardiness will not be tolerated and may result in an unsatisfactory performance evaluation and/or disciplinary action.

sections.heading: Calling in Sick
steps: Notify your supervisor before your work shift begins, or within the first 30 minutes of the start of your shift, if you are sick and cannot come to work, so that the work can be redistributed.

Use accrued sick leave for the purpose of preventative medical care, dental care, and care of the family. Family, for this purpose, is defined to mean the employee's spouse, child, parent, brother, or sister (including step-relatives of the same categories), domestic partner (registered with the Secretary of State and providing a Declaration of Domestic Partnership), and child of a domestic partner. Family shall also include grandparents and/or grandchildren if the employee is the primary caregiver for such. (See BOS Policy C-13 for more information.)

Call your supervisor each day you are sick if your illness lasts more than one day, unless specifically instructed otherwise. An instance not requiring the employee to call in every day would be a pre-approved absence in response to a doctor's off-work notice.

Understand that unauthorized, repetitive absences will not be tolerated and may result in an unsatisfactory performance evaluation, placement on a medical certification program, and/or disciplinary action.
Indicate that you are utilizing sick time or FMLA time when calling out; this is the responsibility of the employee.

sections.heading: Leaving Work Before Scheduled Time
steps: Remain at your designated workstation through the completion of your assigned shift.
Leave early only with the express permission of your supervisor.
Cover the shift time not worked with appropriate leave balances or AWOP.
Understand that leaving early without supervisory permission will not be tolerated and may result in an unsatisfactory performance evaluation and/or disciplinary action.

documents.title: Department Employees Interaction with the Work Release Program
documents.purpose: To maintain a safe work environment for all department personnel while utilizing the Sheriff's Department Work Release Participants and establishing boundaries between the interactions of department staff and work release participants.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: For all Department of Animal Services employees.
documents.supersedes: 000-46 Department Employees Interaction with Work Release Program Policy (unsigned draft version, same effective date March 18, 2015; identical content, absorbed into this document)
references: Riverside County Sheriff's Department Work Release Program rules, policies, and procedures

definitions.def: A person serving a sentence by providing community service hours at DAS shelters through the Sheriff's Department Work Release Program. Participants are not county employees, volunteers, or constituents; they are wards of the Sheriff's Department and have rules, policies, and procedures they must follow to remain in the program and at the shelters.

sections.heading: Philosophy
steps: The Department of Animal Services recognizes the assistance that the Sheriff's Department provides by allowing participants to serve their sentence providing community service hours at our shelters.
These participants are serving community hours as if they were incarcerated and are here to perform work and complete their hours.

sections.heading: Policy
steps: Recognize work release participants by their work vests and inform the department's Work Release Supervisor immediately if there is any suspicious activity, safety violations, or indiscretions.
Notify the lead staff person if work release participant(s) are not engaged in work or are in areas that are off-limits to work release participants.

Staff specifically assigned to interact with work release participants will maintain a respectful work environment and have the authority to lead the work release participants in fulfilling the daily duties assigned.

No staff person or volunteer shall engage in establishing a friendship, personal relationship, or romantic relationship, or participate in any exchanges that would be identified as sexual in nature, with work release participants during the work release participants' assignment, which includes before, during, and after work.
No staff person or volunteer shall take breaks or lunches with work release participants in the employee break room or anywhere on or off campus.
No staff person or volunteer will smoke with work release participants.
No employee or volunteer will make a purchase from an outside vendor (e.g., In-N-Out Burger, etc.) for work release participants.
No employee or volunteer will receive or accept any gift or gratuity from a work release participant (e.g., food, monetary, miscellaneous gifts).

related.name: Acknowledgment of Policy 000-46 (retained in employee personnel file)
Riverside County Sheriff's Department Work Release Program

appendices: Appendix A: Acknowledgment of County of Riverside Department of Animal Services Policy for Department Employees Interaction with the Work Release Program Policy (attached separately)

documents.title: Overtime
documents.purpose: The County of Riverside Ordinance No. 440 and the Bargaining Units' Memoranda of Understanding (MOUs) discourage overtime work and limit it to emergencies and the performance of urgent and necessary functions. This policy, in accordance with County Ordinance No. 440 and applicable MOUs, provides the guidelines and procedures for Department of Animal Services (DAS) employees in the use and limitations of overtime work.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services. All overtime must be pre-authorized by the requesting employee's immediate Supervisor, Manager, Deputy Director, or Department Head.
documents.scope: For all Department of Animal Services employees.
documents.supersedes: CHA Policy P-13; original effective July 1, 2012, revised October 30, 2013
references: County Ordinance No. 440
Bargaining Units' Memoranda of Understanding
Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA)

definitions.def: Authorized work for non-exempt employees in excess of 40 hours in a Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) work week. Only time actually worked counts towards the overtime calculation; vacation, extra vacation, holiday, sick, and compensatory leave is not included. It does not include regularly scheduled work on a paid holiday for which the employee is entitled to equal compensatory time off.

sections.heading: Overtime Reimbursement
steps: Compensate overtime generally at the rate of 1-1/2 times the employee's regular rate of pay in compensatory time off.
Note that provisions of various Bargaining Units specify rates that may differ by the position represented.
Contact the Human Resources Office for assistance in determining the appropriate overtime reimbursement rate according to the specific Bargaining Unit. (Refer to Bargaining Units' MOUs.)

sections.heading: Limitations
steps: Ensure, whenever possible, that compensatory time balances do not exceed a 120-hour accumulation level for all FLSA nonexempt employees, unless prior Department Head or Deputy Director approval was obtained.

County Ordinance 440 permits employees who accumulate overtime in excess of 40 hours to be paid or bank the hours in excess of the 40 hours; payment of hours worked overtime in excess of 40 hours is at the discretion of the Deputy Director based upon budget needs for the Department.
Exempt employees of the Department are not permitted to receive overtime payment per FLSA rules.
Obtain pre-authorization for all overtime from the requesting employee's immediate Supervisor, Manager, Deputy Director, or Department Head.

sections.heading: Scheduling Compensatory Time Off
steps: Use compensatory time as soon as possible after it is earned; all employees are encouraged to do so.

Department Head, Deputy Director, Animal Services Chiefs, Managers, and Supervisors shall periodically review time balances and encourage their staff to reduce their compensatory time accruals before reaching or exceeding the 120-hour limit. (FLSA exempt employees do not earn overtime.)
Supervisors are encouraged to ask employees who request vacation time off to use accrued compensatory time rather than accrued vacation hours.
Schedule compensatory time off normally through mutual agreement between the immediate supervisor and the employee.

Any FLSA nonexempt employee with accumulated overtime credit of 120 hours or less may, and if requested by the Deputy Director or Department Head shall, no later than the next working day, specify the time desired to take as compensatory time off; the Deputy Director or Department Head may schedule compensatory time off for the employee.
The maximum bank for compensatory time is 120 hours; all compensatory time over 120 hours will automatically be paid out under the current FLSA overtime rules.

documents.title: Attendance Records
documents.purpose: The Department of Animal Services (DAS) Attendance Record is an electronic preprinted form used for data entry and legal documentation of labor. The Attendance Record is also referred to as the Time Sheet. The Time and Mileage Report is also part of the attendance report as an official record. The Time Card or Trip Sheets (for those in the field), referenced in DAS Policy 000-12 Time Clock Usage, are the methods for clocking in or logging in during the employee's shift.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: For all Department of Animal Services employees.
documents.supersedes: CHA Policy P-20; effective July 1, 2012
references: County Ordinance No. 440, Section 6b
DAS Policy 000-12 Time Clock Usage

definitions.def: An electronic preprinted form used for data entry and legal documentation of labor.
The methods for clocking in or logging in during the employee's shift, referenced in DAS Policy 000-12 Time Clock Usage; Trip Sheets are used by field staff.

sections.heading: Policy
steps: Maintain complete records of attendance and absences for each employee during each pay period; this is the responsibility of DAS supervisors and managers.
Certify attendance records properly by both the employee's and the supervisor's signature at the bottom of the time sheet.
Persons certifying attendance records cannot certify their own time sheet.
Ensure the original time sheet is signed only by those authorized, entered by the authorized and designated department timekeepers, and forwarded to Human Resources Personnel.

sections.heading: Procedures
steps: Enter all entries, including signatures on time sheets, electronically or write them legibly in ink.
Fill out the Attendance Record daily; the employee should complete it each day to provide an accurate record of employee time.
Ensure the hours worked and hours taken off each day add up to the correct total hours for the day according to the employee's regular work schedule.
Store the Time Cards and Trip Sheets, as referenced in DAS Policy 000-12, where the supervisor has access to review them at any time during the pay period.
Give the completed and employee-signed Attendance Record to the immediate supervisor or manager for verification and signature.
Verify that the signed Attendance Record is correctly completed and matches the employee's bi-weekly time card or trip sheet; this is the supervisor's or manager's responsibility.
Sign and submit time sheets to the authorized department timekeeper according to established deadlines.
The authorized department timekeeper will submit time sheets to Human Resources Personnel, who will process and keep the original time sheets on file for 7 years.
Access all procedures and Attendance Record (time sheet) templates on the DAS Intranet site under Forms: Time Sheets SEIU, Time Sheets LIUNA, and Time Sheets Instructions.

related.name: Attendance Record (Time Sheet) templates on DAS Intranet: Time Sheets SEIU, Time Sheets LIUNA, Time Sheets Instructions

documents.title: Personnel Compliance with Policies and Procedures
documents.purpose: To establish the expectation that all employees of the Riverside County Department of Animal Services (DAS) will be familiar with and adhere to all applicable written policies and procedures established and maintained within DAS or any of its shelters, and abide by all applicable federal, state, and local laws, regulations, ordinances, and rules.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: For all Department of Animal Services employees.
documents.supersedes: CHA Policy P-1; effective July 1, 2012
references: All relevant Federal, State and Local Laws, Acts and Codes such as Title 21 of the Codified Federal Register (CFR), Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA), Family and Medical Leave Act (FMLA), etc.
CDC Guidelines on Infection Control
OSHA Regulations and Cal-OSHA Standards
Health and Safety Code (H&SC), California Code of Regulations (CCR), etc.; Titles 8, 10, 17, and 22, etc.
Standards and California Association of Hospital and Healthcare Systems (CAHHS) Consent Manual as appropriate
County Ordinance 440 and all other relative Ordinances
BOS Policy Manual; BOS Resolutions
County Safety Policy Manual
County Auditor-Controller Standard Practice

sections.heading: Policy
steps: Be familiar with and adhere to all applicable written policies and procedures established and maintained within DAS or any of its shelters. See DAS Policy 000-05, Implementation of New Policies and Procedures.
Abide by all applicable federal, state, and local laws, regulations, ordinances, and rules, referenced or otherwise; see the reference section for citations.

An employee who fails to follow policies and procedures pertaining to his or her position and employment with the County, or any other federal, state, or local laws or regulations, may be the subject of progressive discipline, up to and including termination.

sections.heading: Questions About Policies and Procedures
steps: Direct questions about policies and procedures to the immediate supervisor.

sections.heading: Updating Policies and Procedures
steps: Inform the immediate supervisor if you believe an existing policy or procedure is not up to date or is no longer effective; this is required of every employee.
The immediate supervisor must take the necessary action to result in an updated, modified, or newly issued policy and procedure.
See DAS Policy 000-05 and departmental policies for additional direction on updating policies and procedures.

sections.heading: Supervisor Responsibilities
steps: Provide employees with access to applicable policies and procedures (hard copy and/or access to the DAS Intranet website) and ensure that the employee understands them.
Locate policies and procedures where they are accessible to employees; this is the supervisor's responsibility.

sections.heading: Violation of Law
steps: Employees cited by law enforcement for violation of law will be liable for any fines or court-ordered penalties which may be applied.

documents.title: Employee Time-Off Work
documents.purpose: It is vitally important to the Department of Animal Services (DAS) that staff attendance at appointed work sites is assured through regular scheduling on a consistent basis to provide complete, efficient service to clients and patients. Excessive absence results in hardship to co-workers, supervisors, and the public.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services. The Director, Deputy Director, Chief Veterinarian, or their designees may require a medical certification when in their judgment good cause exists for believing the employee may be abusing his or her sick leave privilege.
documents.scope: For all Department of Animal Services employees.
documents.supersedes: HSA Policy P-15 Employee Time-Off Work; 000-51 Employee Time-Off Work effective July 1, 2012, revised November 12, 2013 (routing draft 11/14/2013, absorbed into this document)
references: County Ordinance 440
BOS Policy C-13
Bargaining Units' Memoranda of Understanding
HSA P-15
DAS 000-53 (formerly HSA P-17), Continuing Education and Training Leave Time
DAS 000-44 Attendance and Punctuality

definitions.def: Absent Without Pay.

The California Family Rights Act and the federal Family and Medical Leave Act, which entitle qualified employees the right to take unpaid leave, or paid leave if it has been earned, for a period up to 12 weeks in any 12-month period for specified purposes.

sections.heading: Policy
steps: Adhere to the provisions of County Ordinance 440, County Board of Supervisors (BOS) policy, current Memoranda of Understanding of the applicable Bargaining Unit, DAS 000-44 Attendance and Punctuality, and this policy in regard to time-off work.
Request all time-off work in advance for supervisor or designee approval, or report it immediately to the supervisor in the case of unexpected sickness or emergency.

Use accrued sick leave for the purpose of sickness, medical treatment for illness or injury, preventive medical care, dental care, and care of the family. Family care is defined to mean a spouse, child, parent, brother, or sister of the employee, living in the same household as the employee, who is disabled by illness or by injury. (See BOS Policy C-13 for more information.) Prudent use of sick leave privileges will provide a substantial reserve in case of serious accident or lengthy illness. Accrued sick leave may also be used for bereavement purposes.

Contact your supervisor or designee when absent because of illness or other unplanned leave necessity. Unreported absences may result in the forfeiture of sick leave or other benefits and place the employee in an Absent Without Pay (AWOP) status; any unreported absence is subject to review and possible formal disciplinary action.

Every regular employee shall be able to use accrued vacation, compensatory time, or holiday time when sick leave has been exhausted due to extended illness or injury as verified by a doctor's statement, unless that employee is on a medical certification program.
Supervisors are encouraged to ask employees who request vacation leave to use accrued compensatory hours rather than accrued vacation hours.

Employees may have time off work granted for training and education purposes, which will be reviewed based on their current job description and need for continuing education (refer to HSA P-17, Continuing Education and Training Leave Time, for direction).

sections.heading: Unplanned Time Off
steps: Call your immediate supervisor within 15 minutes after your scheduled shift starts when reporting off work for unplanned leave, unless the immediate supervisor specifies otherwise.

sections.heading: Planned Time Off

steps.step: Submit a written request for planned time-off to your immediate supervisor, whenever possible, a minimum of two (2) weeks prior to the start date of the leave.
sub: This lead time allows the supervisor to verify projected staffing levels and make any necessary scheduling changes.

If there will be insufficient staff to provide services during the requested time-off, the supervisor, after discussion with the Manager, Director, Deputy Director, or Chief Veterinarian, may deny the request.
The supervisor may require more than a two (2) week minimum lead time to make the necessary scheduling changes; refer to local policies for additional requirements.
Individual or partial day planned time off requests, including time-off for doctor appointments, require the same lead time and determination that work coverage is available prior to approval.
Any pre-approved time-off requests may be canceled by management in cases of unexpected, critical staff shortage or with other determination of good cause.

steps: Refer disputes between the employee and the supervisor regarding a time-off request to the respective Executive Team member (Deputy Director, Chief Veterinarian, Director, or their designees) for final approval or disapproval. Supervisors are responsible for ensuring that adequate staffing levels are maintained.

steps.step: Advise clerical staff that receive public phone calls of the leave status of all personnel assigned to their respective work areas on a daily basis. This may be accomplished with a sign out/in log or board, or other appropriate means.
sub: If a sign out/in log or board is used, each employee is responsible for ensuring his or her status is listed correctly by notifying clerical staff of the departure date/time and anticipated return date/time prior to departing on approved planned leave, and updating the log or board upon return.
If the employee has voice mail or e-mail, appropriate notification of absence should be made to state where inquiries may be directed in the interim if the employee is out for more than 1 week.

sections.heading: Sick Leave
steps: Supervisors shall not approve and employees shall not use sick leave benefits for other than sickness, injury, pregnancy, medical or dental care, bereavement purposes, or care of an authorized family member, and as identified in Ordinance 440 and the Memoranda of Understanding. Any other use of sick leave is not authorized.

sections.heading: Medical Certification
steps: Place the employee on medical certification in writing when, in the judgment of the supervisor, good reason exists for believing the employee may be abusing sick leave. Supervisors should consult with Human Resources for making an appropriate determination of good cause.

Place the employee on a Medical Certification program (per BOS Policy C-13 and Memoranda of Understanding); the employee shall be allowed paid sick leave by producing a certificate of a physician, dentist, or other legally authorized person to provide health care services on the same level as a physician.

Ensure the certification includes a written statement signed by a physician, dentist, or other legally authorized person to provide health care services on the same level as a physician, stating the day(s) of the illness or injury and that the illness or injury prevents the employee from being able to work. With the supervisor's approval, the same information may be provided on the health care provider's stationery. (Refer to Ordinance No. 440 for additional information.)

Review the sick leave usage of employees on a Medical Certification program at least annually; if the review shows substantial improvement, remove them from the category of having to provide the certificate for each absence by formal letter.

Every regular employee shall be able to use accrued vacation, compensatory time, or holiday time when sick leave has been exhausted due to illness or injury, unless they are on a Medical Certification program.

sections.heading: Extended Off Work Authorization
steps: Provide a comprehensive health statement from your health care provider if off work, or contemplating being off work, due to illness or injury for an extended period of two (2) weeks or more. The Off-Work Order statement shall include the anticipated length of illness, the release for duty date, and, upon return, any duties the employee cannot perform and any restrictions. It will be held on file in the Human Resources Office.

Sick slips signed by Resident Physicians for any DAS employee are unacceptable unless the physician has seen the employee in clinic or as a private physician and has documented this information in the employee's medical record.

Return to Duty: All employees with an Off-Work Order must be cleared through Human Resources when returning to work. The employee shall submit the physician's release to return to duty to his or her supervisor, who shall forward it to the Human Resources Office.

Duty Restrictions: If the employee is released to work with restrictions, light duty, or modified duty, the department shall make reasonable effort to accommodate the light duty in the employee's assigned unit or other units within the employee's department. If it is not possible to accommodate the light duty, the employee may be provided with a temporary leave of absence as an alternative accommodation. (Note: the source document also retains the prior provision that the employee must remain off work until the physician releases the employee to full duty; see edit log.) Any weight restrictions dealing with lifting, pushing, or other light-duty restrictions should be indicated on the physician's note along with the date for medical re-examination. A duty restriction will be accommodated by DAS for no longer than 90 days; an employee who cannot be medically released for return to full duty after 90 days is expected to apply for disability.

Extension Notice: If the employee is unable to return to work at the expiration of the original off-work order, obtain an extension from the physician and send it to the Human Resources Office. The Extension Notice will be marked 'Submitted to Human Resources' and a copy will be forwarded to the Manager and/or the Director, Deputy Director, Chief Veterinarian, or appropriate supervisor of the employee.

Worker's Compensation: Where applicable, the Human Resources Office will report the extended off-work information to Workers' Compensation. For more information on Workers' Compensation, refer to Ordinance No. 440.

sections.heading: Family Care
steps: An employee who requests sick leave to care for a family member disabled by illness or injury may be requested to submit documentation from a medical doctor or dentist supporting the need for leave (see Attachment 1).

sections.heading: Bereavement Leave
steps: Accrued sick leave, not exceeding five (5) working days, may be used by regular employees, officers, or seasonal employees in an active payroll status compelled to be absent from duty by reason of the death, or critical illness where death appears imminent, of the employee's father, father-in-law, mother, mother-in-law, brother, sister, spouse, child, grandparent, grandchild, or step-relationships of the same categories.

sections.heading: Complications of Pregnancy
steps: Sick leave may be used for absence reasonably required by complications of pregnancy, continuing through delivery and a reasonable period of recovery, to be determined in accordance with a written report of the employee's personal physician specifying the expected date of delivery and the date the employee should cease work.
In no event shall an employee return to work after pregnancy prior to a date to be fixed by her physician in a signed statement that she is physically able to perform the duties of her position.

sections.heading: Regular Pregnancy Leave
steps: Furnish your supervisor, not later than the sixth month of the term of pregnancy, with a signed physician's certificate specifying the anticipated date of delivery.

Furnish your supervisor with a signed physician's certificate stating you are physically able to continue working through a specified date prior to delivery if you wish to work past the end of the seventh month of pregnancy.

sections.heading: Fitness for Duty
steps: The DAS Director, or delegate, when in his or her judgment good cause exists, may request from the Human Resources Director that an employee be ordered off work until the employee presents a certificate from a physician approved by the County stating the employee is able to return to work without impairing the health of the public, the employee's health, or the health of other employees in the Department.

The cost of the physician's visit and services will be at County expense, and the employee shall continue to be on paid Administrative Leave until the physician's report is received and the employee is officially notified of the County's determination of his or her status.

sections.heading: CFRA/FMLA Leave
steps: To qualify for this leave, an employee must have been employed for at least 12 months with the County of Riverside and have worked at least 1,250 hours during the 12-month period preceding the need for leave.

CFRA/FMLA leave may be taken for the birth of a child, the placement of a child due to adoption or foster care, the employee's need to care for a family member with a serious health condition, or the employee's own serious health condition.
Employees out on leave due to their own serious health condition will have accrued sick and vacation paid time-off charged against FMLA hours before an unpaid status can be authorized.
Advance Notice: Provide at least 30 days advance notice to your supervisor whenever the need for a CFRA/FMLA leave is foreseeable.
Unpaid Status: Employees on CFRA/FMLA leave shall exhaust their accrued sick and vacation hours before being placed on unpaid leave.

Benefits: Employees on CFRA/FMLA unpaid leave are entitled to have their health benefits maintained while on leave; however, employees are required to pay the employee's share of the premiums for such benefit coverage. While on CFRA/FMLA unpaid leave, employees may maintain their life and disability insurance by paying the premium cost for that coverage.

sections.heading: Leave of Absence (AWOP)
steps: Leave may be granted for personal reasons acceptable to the authority whose approval is required.

160 hours or less: A leave of absence up to 160 hours in any one calendar year period may be granted to any employee by the DAS Director or delegate. The DAS Director or delegate may require the leave of absence to be for a specified period of time, and appropriate conditions may be imposed, such as providing sufficient medical documentation or other evidence substantiating the leave. (Refer to Ordinance 440 for more information.)

2080 hours or less: A regular employee may request an official leave of absence exceeding 160 hours but not exceeding one year (2,080 hours). Official leaves of absence may be granted upon the written recommendation of the DAS Director and with the written approval of the County Human Resources Director. (Refer to Ordinance 440 for more information.)

An employee on leave of absence for illness or disability reasons shall be required to present a return-to-work statement from the attending physician releasing the employee to full duty prior to being allowed to return to work at full duty. Any release to less than full duty will be allowed only as accommodation as required under the Americans with Disabilities Act/Fair Employment and Housing Act.

sections.heading: Jury Duty and Court Appearances
steps: An employee summoned for attendance to any court for jury duty, or as a witness arising out of and in the course of County employment, during the employee's normal working hours shall be deemed to be on duty, and there shall be no loss of salary; any jury or witness fees received shall be paid into the County Treasury.
No loss of salary shall occur to employees who normally work a night or evening shift and who must report for jury duty during the day shift, necessitating their being off work that night or evening.
Employees working normal day hours shall report to work if released from jury duty during regular work hours or if jury duty begins after the normal work day begins.
With approval from Human Resources, supervisors have the authority to temporarily place a nightshift employee on days during jury duty.

related.name: DAS 000-53 (formerly HSA P-17) Continuing Education and Training Leave Time

appendices: Appendix A: Attachment 1, Family Care documentation (attached separately)

documents.title: Calling 911 for Emergencies When Needed
documents.purpose: To notify all DAS employees that there may be a need to contact emergency services (911) for medical or non-medical situations. When 911 is called, the department will support the reporting employee for any legal responsibility if the call was made in the best interest and for the safety of the public, a coworker, or a manager/director.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: All Department of Animal Services (DAS) employees
documents.supersedes: Policy 000-53, effective December 12, 2017 (non-worker's compensation related claims).
references: County Safety Manual Document Number 7002, General Emergency Plan Guidelines

sections.heading: Background
steps: In the past, employees have expressed discomfort in making the decision to call 911 for emergency services, mainly because the sick person objects or because of uncertainty about the appropriateness of calling for assistance.
Contacting 911 should never be an issue based on level of position, especially when the reporting employee is outranked by a higher seniority or authority level position.
Employees should feel empowered to contact 911 when they are doing so in the best interest or safety of the person needing care. The employee should not fear backlash or intimidation for doing so.

sections.heading: Policy
steps: This policy is based on protecting the public and employees for medical and safety reasons, with no discrimination based on race, color, national origin, ancestry, or religious creed.

All employees, from entry level through the Director of Animal Services, have the support and backing of the Department of Animal Services to contact 911 for emergency situations when the employee thinks emergency services are needed or required. A wide range of circumstances could dictate this necessity.
No individual who contacts 911 for what they believe is an emergency situation shall be held liable for the incident.

Once it has been determined that emergency services are required, the County and the Department of Animal Services have an ethical responsibility to help ensure the safety of the workforce and the public.

If the injured or sick person declines assistance, it is still the department's responsibility to ensure emergency services (911) is contacted. A medical professional on the ambulance can make the decision to transport the injured or sick person or not.

Employees are not medical professionals. Although they may have CPR or comparable training, this should only be used until emergency services arrive. Upon the arrival of emergency services, the ambulance and fire department emergency medical team will take over the care of the injured person. Based on their more advanced medical training, they may or may not recommend the injured person be transported for further examination or treatment.

steps.step: If the emergency response team determines that an employee's injury is non-life threatening, the employee can be driven safely to a hospital at the discretion of the injured employee and upon approval of the immediate supervisor.
sub: The employee's family or emergency contact person should be called at this time.
The public will never be driven by staff, but will be taken by emergency services.

steps: Every situation will be different (a fall, a broken bone, cuts, bruises, heart symptoms, seizures, etc.) and each will need to be evaluated based on the person injured. We cannot expect each person to react to or handle the injury the same way.

sections.heading: When to Call 911
steps: If the employee feels the situation requires 911, call 911.
If staff are unclear about the seriousness of the situation, call 911.
When in doubt, call 911.
Follow the Emergency 911 Operator's instructions until help arrives.
Every employee has the authority to contact 911 without a supervisor's authorization when they feel it is deemed necessary.
A supervisor or manager should always be contacted immediately, or immediately thereafter, in the event of the emergency.

documents.title: Issuance of Petty Cash
documents.purpose: To provide guidelines for the proper use of Petty Cash funds and the processes used to request petty cash funds.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: All Department of Animal Services (DAS) employees
documents.supersedes: Policy 000-54, effective July 1, 2014.
references: Petty Cash Request form (DAS 001)

sections.heading: General Provisions
steps: Both the Riverside County Animal Shelter (Jurupa Valley) and the Coachella Valley Animal Campus (Thousand Palms) maintain petty cash funds.
Use petty cash funds only for minor emergency purchases of less than $100. These should not be convenience purchases.
Under no circumstance shall a single transaction exceed $100 for the entirety of the purchase, including the product, tax, and freight.

The petty cash fund shall be administered by financial staff at both locations. A petty cash custodian or an alternate petty cash custodian shall be the only staff to issue or reconcile petty cash funds at each facility.
No petty cash funds will be signed out without proper documentation of the financial transaction.

sections.heading: Sign-Out Option 1: Purchase First, Then Reimburse
steps: Seek prior approval from a supervisor or manager before any purchase is made.
Purchase the item(s) needed and bring the receipt back to the petty cash custodian.
Complete the Petty Cash Voucher for Emergency Purchases with the assistance of the petty cash custodian.
Obtain all required signatures on the form. The petty cash custodian will then issue petty cash in the exact amount of the purchase.
Remember that $100.00 is the total amount that can be issued from the petty cash fund for any single transaction.

sections.heading: Sign-Out Option 2: Quote First, Then Purchase
steps: Obtain a quote for the item(s) needed.
Complete the Petty Cash Voucher for Emergency Purchases form.
Bring the completed form and quote to the petty cash custodian to request the funds.
The petty cash custodian will issue the amount requested. At this time the petty cash custodian shall confer with the requester to ensure that all additional expenses were included.

sections.heading: Closing Petty Cash Transactions
steps: The issuance of petty cash does not complete the transaction. Before any transaction is completed, reconcile the cash issued to the actual receipt.
When Option 2 is used to request petty cash, return the receipt from the purchase to the petty cash custodian on the same day the petty cash was issued.

appendices: Appendix A: Sample Petty Cash Request
Appendix B: Petty Cash Request (Example)
Appendix C: Instruction Sheet
Appendix: Petty Cash Request Form.xls (attached separately)

documents.title: Jury or County Witness Summons
documents.purpose: To facilitate compliance with a jury duty summons or County witness summons while maintaining quality customer service and animal care as a Department of Animal Services (DAS) employee.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: All Animal Services employees
documents.supersedes: Policy 000-59, effective July 1, 2012. Replaces HSA P-15.1.
references: HSA Policy P-15.1 (replaced by this policy)

sections.heading: Policy
steps: DAS employees who are summoned for jury duty or called as a witness arising out of or in the course of their County employment will be deemed to be on duty, and there will be no loss of salary during this period of time.
Jury duty and witness fees will be paid to the County Treasurer.
Reimbursement for mileage is to be retained by the employee unless a County vehicle is used for transportation.

sections.heading: Procedures
steps: Present a copy of the summons to your supervisor.
Receive and read the Jury Duty Instructions (see Appendix) regarding jury duty and witness fees, and ask for clarification if required.
Keep your supervisor informed of the jury duty or witness schedule.

appendices: Appendix: Jury Duty Instructions (attached separately)

documents.title: Diversity Policy for the Department of Animal Services
documents.purpose: To provide DAS employees a safe and healthy work environment where their performance and success is gauged solely on the job that they do and their actions in that job capacity. DAS has an obligation to protect the rights of its employees to be free from unlawful discrimination, harassment, or retaliation, and to set forth a procedure for promptly investigating and taking appropriate remedial action in dealing with internal complaints of unlawful discrimination, harassment, and retaliation.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: All DAS employees including, but not limited to, contract workers, volunteers, interns, externs, and officials
documents.supersedes: Policy 000-60, effective July 1, 2015.
references: Board of Supervisors Policy C-25, Non-Discrimination and Anti-Harassment Policy and Complaint Procedure
Attachment A: Acknowledgement Form of the DAS Diversity Policy
C-25 Attachment B: Discrimination/Harassment/Retaliation Complaint Form
Peace Officer Bill of Rights (POBR), California Government Code Sections 3300-3313

definitions.def: The disparate or adverse treatment of a person or applicant based on the group, class, or category to which that person or applicant belongs rather than on individual merit. Group, class, or category includes, but is not limited to, race, color, national origin, ancestry, religious creed, denial of family and medical care leave, sex (including pregnancy and medical conditions related to pregnancy), age, disability (physical and mental), medical condition, genetic information, marital status, military and veteran status, sexual orientation, gender (including gender identity and gender expression), status as a victim of domestic violence, sexual assault, or stalking, and/or retaliation for protesting illegal discrimination and/or harassment related to one of these categories, or any other legally protected classes under state or federal law.

Can be a form of discrimination if it is unwelcome and is sufficiently severe or pervasive and objectively offensive so as to substantially interfere with terms, conditions, or privileges of employment. Harassment may be verbal, physical, visual, or sexual. It may be made in general or directed to an individual or a group of people. Even if actions are not directed at specific persons, a hostile environment may be created when the conduct is sufficiently severe or pervasive and objectively offensive. Harassment may occur regardless of whether the behavior was intended to harass. Harassers may be supervisors, managers, co-workers, contractors, elected officials, or members of the public.

Speech such as lewd propositioning, epithets, stereotypical or derogatory comments, slurs, threats, offensive or degrading remarks, verbal abuse, or other behavior such as insulting, teasing, degrading, or ridiculing another person or group. This might include inappropriate sex-oriented comments on appearance, including dress or physical features, stories or jokes, or gender characteristics that do not conform to traditional or social expectations.

Unwelcome or inappropriate physical contact such as assault, impeding or blocking movement, or offensive touching, or any physical interference with normal work or movement when directed at an individual. This includes pinching, grabbing, patting, propositioning, leering, or making explicit or implied job threats or promises in return for submission to physical acts.

Derogatory, prejudicial, stereotypical, or otherwise offensive email messages, web pages, screen savers and other computer images; posters, photographs, cartoons, notes, notices, bulletins, or drawings; and staring or leering.

Unsolicited or unwanted sexual advances, requests for sexual favors, and/or other acts of a sexual nature, whether or not the sexually harassing conduct was motivated by sexual desire, where submission is made a term or condition of employment; where submission or rejection of the conduct is used as the basis for employment decisions; or where the conduct is intended to or actually does unnecessarily interfere with an individual's work performance or creates an intimidating, hostile, or offensive working environment.

Taking adverse employment action against an employee because of the employee's protected activities, including but not limited to opposing unlawful discrimination and/or harassment, filing a discrimination or harassment complaint, reporting discrimination or harassment, or participating in a discrimination or harassment investigation or lawsuit. Adverse employment actions may include, but are not necessarily limited to, denial of a promotion, refusal to hire, and/or imposition of discipline.

sections.heading: Background
steps: DAS is a Riverside County department full of a range of cultures, religions, and ethnicities. DAS is extremely proud of this and celebrates the fact.

Each individual brings a background that is unique and possibly different, but it is those differences that make the department more rounded and stronger as a group. Independent strengths, life impressions, and diversity allow staff to balance one another and support each other through daily challenges.

sections.heading: Policy
steps: DAS prohibits discrimination or harassment of an applicant or an employee by a supervisor, management employee, County Officer, or co-worker on the basis of actual or perceived race, color, national origin, ancestry, religious creed, denial of family and medical care leave, sex (including pregnancy and medical conditions related to pregnancy), age, disability (physical and mental), medical condition, genetic information, marital status, military and veteran status, sexual orientation, gender identity and gender expression, status as a victim of domestic violence, sexual assault, or stalking, and/or retaliation for protesting illegal discrimination and/or harassment related to one of these categories, or any other legally protected classes under state or federal law.

It is also the policy of the Board of Supervisors to provide a work environment free from sexual harassment, including unwelcome sexual overtures, advances, or coercion, although sexually harassing conduct need not be motivated by sexual desire.

Any retaliation against a person for opposing unlawful discrimination or harassment, filing a discrimination or harassment complaint, reporting discrimination or harassment, or participating in a discrimination or harassment investigation or lawsuit is prohibited. Employees found to be retaliating against another employee shall be subject to disciplinary action, up to and including termination.

This policy applies to all workplace behaviors and terms and conditions of employment, including but not limited to hiring, placement, promotion, disciplinary action, layoff, recall, transfer, leaves of absence, compensation, and training. It applies to a County employee acting on behalf of the County on or off the job site.
Disciplinary action, up to and including termination, will be instituted for unlawful discrimination, harassment, and/or retaliation as defined in this policy.

sections.heading: Training
steps: All non-management or non-supervisory employees shall attend Employee Harassment Prevention Training (offered by Human Resources) within the first six (6) months of hire and every four (4) years thereafter.

All supervisory and management employees shall attend Management Harassment Prevention Training (offered by Human Resources) within the first six (6) months of the hire or promotion date and every two (2) years thereafter.
The training provided shall include the prevention of abusive conduct.

sections.heading: Complaint Procedure

steps.step: An employee or job applicant who believes they have been discriminated against and/or harassed has a responsibility to immediately make a complaint, either orally or in writing, with any of the following:
sub: Immediate supervisor;
Any supervisor or management employee/officer within the County of Riverside;
The Human Resources Department, Employee Relations Division.

steps: The supervisor, management employee, or County officer who receives a discrimination or harassment complaint shall: (1) provide the employee and/or job applicant with a copy of the C-25 policy and refer them to Attachment B, and (2) immediately notify the Human Resources Department.

The employee or job applicant also has the right to file a complaint with the state Department of Fair Employment and Housing (DFEH), the federal Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC), or the Department of Industrial Relations (DIR).

An employee or job applicant may file a complaint with the DFEH by calling (800) 884-1684 or visiting the DFEH website at www.dfeh.ca.gov. The employee or job applicant may file a complaint with the EEOC by visiting the EEOC website for directions on how to file a charge of employment discrimination at www.eeoc.gov. The employee or job applicant may file a complaint for retaliation with the DIR by visiting the DIR website at www.dir.ca.gov.

Although the County recommends that an employee who believes they may be the victim of discrimination, harassment, and/or retaliation report such conduct, the County will not tolerate intentional false accusations of discrimination, harassment, and/or retaliation. The filing of any intentional false claims is considered unlawful and may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination.

sections.heading: Complaint Procedure for Public Safety Agencies
steps: Public safety agencies (Riverside County Sheriff's Department, District Attorney's Office, and Probation Department) are charged with conducting investigations of allegations of violation of this policy. These departments shall comply with the Peace Officer Bill of Rights (POBR), California Government Code Sections 3300-3313, for peace officers employed in their departments.

Upon receipt of verbal notification of a C-25 complaint, the public safety agency shall cross-report in writing the name of the complainant(s), name of the accused, allegation(s), and a brief summary on which the complaint is based to the Human Resources Employee Relations Division. The public safety agency will provide a copy of any written complaint received to the Human Resources Employee Relations Division.
The public safety agency will conduct the investigation pursuant to the requirements set forth in POBR Section 3303, for peace officers employed in their departments.

Acknowledgement of DAS Diversity Policy (Attachment A)

appendices: Appendix A: Acknowledgement of DAS Diversity Policy
Appendix: C-25 Non-Discrimination and Anti-Harassment Policy and Complaint Form (attached separately)
