documents.title: Procurement Process of Goods and Services
documents.purpose: To enhance the purchasing and payment workflow process within the Department of Animal Services. All purchases are to be made in compliance with department and County policies and procedures.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services. Authority to purchase rests exclusively with the Board of Supervisors and the Purchasing Agent, including the Purchasing Agent's delegation of authority.
documents.scope: Departmental guidelines for the procurement process of goods and services; applies to all DAS staff who request or approve purchases.
documents.supersedes: Policy 000-64, effective October 27, 2016. Absorbs the source document version (000-64 Procurement Process of Goods Services Policy.docm) and the standalone attachment document 000-64 DAS Procurement Flowchart Revised, which is incorporated here as Appendix A.
references: Government Code 31000
County of Riverside Purchasing Policy Manual, August 1, 2015
Board of Supervisors Ordinances A-62 and 459.5
Board of Supervisors Policy D-1
DAS Policy 000-19
All other applicable department and County policies

definitions.def: A purchase driven by a sudden and/or unexpected occurrence that poses a clear and imminent danger, requiring immediate action to prevent or mitigate the loss or impairment of life, health, property, or public services.

sections.heading: Procurement Card (P-Card) Requests
steps: Submit a signature-approved Procurement Card Request Form with a written quote or documentation directly to a P-Cardholder via email or inter-office mail. The purpose of the procurement card is to streamline the procurement process for small-dollar purchases.

Make all purchases with a County credit card in compliance with all department and County policies and procedures. The card is not intended to, and may not, be used to circumvent any department or County policies and procedures.
Procurement cardholders must follow the same guidelines on their P-Card purchases as outlined in this policy, including the rules on splitting of orders and annual limitations.

steps.step: Submit Procurement Card Requests via email or inter-office mail:
sub: Email: forward the electronic version of the signature-approved Procurement Card Request Form with written quote or documentation directly to the P-Cardholder. Contact the Fiscal Division Manager for a list of the current P-Cardholders' names. Requesters may also ask the authorizing official to approve the form electronically via email; the authorizing official may then forward the approval and form to the P-Cardholder.

Inter-office mail: send the signature-approved Procurement Card Request Form with written quotes or documentation to mail stop #3002, Attn: DAS Purchasing Division, or directly to the P-Cardholder's division.

sections.heading: Goods/Services $0 to $399.99 (includes tax, shipping, or any other fees)
steps: Submit an informal purchase request. The authorizing official may submit an informal request with written quote or documentation via email or inter-office mail to the DAS Purchasing Division.
DAS Purchasing staff will review, approve, and respond via email granting approval for super-low value purchase requests without issuing a formal hard copy Purchase Order.

sections.heading: Service Invoices up to $5,000.00 (includes labor, tax, shipping, or any other fees)
steps: Services on monthly billing cycles do not require a Purchase Order Request Form. Purchasing will issue a direct Purchase Order for the vouchering process. These types of services include waste removal, shredding, copier maintenance, etc.

This process does not include the original purchase order request that allows the department to begin the service on a monthly basis once an authorized Purchase Order has been issued. It also does not include one-time service repairs of forklifts, buses, surgical equipment, washers, etc.

sections.heading: Goods/Services $400.00 to $5,000.00 (includes tax, shipping, and any other fees)
steps: Submit a signature-approved Purchase Order Request Form with a written quote or documentation via email or inter-office mail to the DAS Purchasing Division.

sections.heading: Goods/Services $5,000.00 to $50,000.00 (includes tax, shipping, and any other fees)
steps: Follow the informal written bid process: competitive prices are required for all purchases via written quotes or documentation.
Submit a signature-approved Purchase Order Request Form with three (3) written quotes or documentation attached via email or inter-office mail to the DAS Purchasing Division.

steps.step: Submit Purchase Order Requests via email or inter-office mail:
sub: Email: forward the electronic version of the signature-approved Purchase Order Request Form with written quote or documentation to DASPORequest@rivco.org. Requesters may also ask the authorizing official to approve the form electronically via email; the authorizing official may then forward the approval and form to DASPORequest@rivco.org.
Inter-office mail: send the signature-approved Purchase Order Request Form with written quotes or documentation to mail stop #3002, Attn: DAS Purchasing Division.

steps: The DAS Purchasing group will evaluate the purchase request for completeness and accuracy in compliance with internal and County Purchasing policies and procedures. Purchase Order Request Forms submitted with incomplete information will be returned without issuance of a Purchase Order; the requester must re-submit once all proper documentation is obtained.
DAS Purchasing will then issue a Purchase Order and notify the requester. A hard copy will be provided upon request.

sections.heading: Goods/Services $50,000 and Over (includes tax, shipping, and any other fees)
steps: Follow the formal bid process: competitive bids are required in a written and sealed offer for commodities or services, signed by an authorized representative and in response to a formal proposal or quotation (RFQ or RFP).
Formal bids must be publicly posted on Publicpurchase.com, giving the opportunity for any vendor to participate in the bid process.
Submit your Scope of Services (RFP) or list of products/commodities (RFQ) directly to the Department Buyer, (951) 358-7318.

sections.heading: Exemptions to Seeking Competition
steps: Annual limitations: department expenses have not exceeded the County Purchasing limitation of $5,000 cumulative annual amount per vendor, per department.
The vendor has an awarded contract with the County from a formal bid process.
Single/Sole Source has been approved by County Purchasing (less than $50,000 per year).
Single/Sole Source has been approved by the Board of Supervisors through a Form 11 process (over $50,000 per year).
The exemption to seeking competitive bids does not exclude the requirement of submitting a written quote or documentation with the Purchase Order Request Form.

sections.heading: California Sales Tax, Freight, Shipping, and Handling
steps: Include taxes on all purchase requests and bid comparisons. Sales tax is a purchase tax imposed by state and local governments that is paid by the purchaser for goods and services. The County pays the State of California directly for goods or services purchased out of state, also known as a use tax.
Freight, shipping, and transportation are non-taxable and should not be noted as taxable on the purchase request.
Handling alone, or if shown in combination as Shipping/Handling or Freight/Handling, is taxable and should be noted as taxable on the purchase request.

sections.heading: Exceptions to the Procurement Process
steps: The Board of Supervisors authorizes the exceptions to the procurement process as listed under BOS Ordinance 459.5. The exceptions are advertising; legal briefing, transcripts, and similar documents; subscriptions to publications; insurance; public utility services; common carrier transportation and ordinary travel expense items; government publications and law books; and postage, including postage supplies and services.

sections.heading: Purchasing Authority
steps: Purchasing authority is limited by law and regulations. Departments cannot commit County funds without proper approval and adherence to the procurement procedures.
Authority to purchase is the exclusive right of the Board of Supervisors and the Purchasing Agent, including the Purchasing Agent's delegation of authority.

sections.heading: Inappropriate and Unauthorized Purchase Activities
steps: Splitting of orders: deliberate attempts to split orders, where the purpose is keeping the total cost of each order below bid limits, and failure to combine orders when practical for the best interest of the County in order to circumvent the limitations, is prohibited and may result in disciplinary actions.

Unauthorized purchases: purchase transactions that are entered into by an individual not authorized to contract on behalf of the County, or by an individual normally authorized to contract who uses an unauthorized procurement method or exceeds their delegated authority, may result in disciplinary actions. Repeated and/or flagrant violation of the authorization delegated to individuals by the Director of Purchasing may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination of employment.

sections.heading: Emergency Purchases
steps: Make emergency purchases for emergency needs only. An emergency purchase is defined as a sudden and/or unexpected occurrence that poses a clear and imminent danger, requiring immediate action to prevent or mitigate the loss or impairment of life, health, property, or public services.

Provide a signature-approved Purchase Order Request Form and Emergency Justification Form immediately after the purchase and no later than the next scheduled work period to the Department Buyer. Not immediately reporting the emergency purchase may result in an audit finding of after-the-fact expenses.
Do not use emergency purchase procedures to satisfy preferences or conveniences, or for any reason that would circumvent the regular procurement process.

related.name: Form 11 (Board of Supervisors sole source approval)
County of Riverside Purchasing Policy Manual

appendices: Appendix A: DAS Procurement Flowchart (summary below; original flowchart attached separately)
Appendix: DAS PO Request Form.xlsm (attached separately)
Appendix: P-Card Request Form.xlsm (attached separately)

revisions.desc: Original policy, approved by the Director of Animal Services. Procurement Flowchart issued as attachment and later revised.

documents.title: Counterfeit Bills
documents.purpose: To provide guidelines for how to detect a counterfeit bill, as well as what to do with suspect counterfeit bills, for all DAS employees who handle money.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: All personnel who are authorized to handle cash, check, and credit card payment processing
documents.supersedes: Policy 000-65, effective November 2, 2016; last updated September 30, 2019.
references: DAS Policy 000-22 Front Desk Cash Handling
U.S. Department of the Treasury website: https://www.treasury.gov/about/organizational-structure/offices/Treasurer-US/Pages/if-you-suspect.aspx

sections.heading: Identification of Counterfeit Bills
steps: Be aware that the growth of advanced color copiers, scanners, and printing technology has more than doubled the growth of counterfeit money.

Become familiar with currency to avoid accepting a counterfeit bill. Look for differences, not similarities. Compare a suspect note with a genuine note of the same denomination, paying attention to the quality of printing and to the paper's characteristics.

steps.step: Use the Counterfeit Money Detector Pen on denominations of bills that are $10.00 or greater.
sub: Obtain these pens from the DAS Fiscal Department or designee at each location.
The pen will leave a yellow mark if the bill is genuine.
A brown or black mark means the bill is suspect.

steps: Because the pen method is not 100% accurate, hold the suspect bill up to the light facing you. On newer bills there is a narrow 1/16 inch wide band across the width of the bill, about 1 inch from the edge on the left side, that prints US (or USA) and the dollar amount. To the right side of the green seal will appear a hidden portrait of the same face as in the center. The band and hidden face do not appear on one dollar bills.

sections.heading: If You Suspect a Counterfeit Bill

steps.step: If you suspect a counterfeit note or have information about counterfeiting activity, report it immediately to the U.S. Secret Service or to your local police:
sub: Secret Service Office: 3801 University Avenue, #550, Riverside, CA 92501; Phone: 951-276-6781
Jurupa Valley Sheriff Station: 7477 Mission Boulevard, Jurupa Valley, CA 92509; Phone: 951-955-2600
Palm Desert Sheriff Station: 73705 Gerald Ford Drive, Palm Desert, CA 92260; Phone: 760-836-1600
Hemet Sheriff Station: 43950 Acacia Avenue, Suite B, Hemet, CA 92544; Phone: 951-791-3400
Blythe Police Department: 240 N Spring Street, Blythe, CA 92225; Phone: 760-922-6111

sections.heading: Personal Safety and Handling of the Suspect Note
steps: Do not put yourself in danger.
Do not return the bill to the passer.
Delay the passer with some excuse, if possible.
Observe the passer's description, and their companions' descriptions, and write down their vehicle license plate numbers if you can.
Write your initials and the date in the white border area of the suspected counterfeit note.
Do not handle the counterfeit note. Place it inside a protective cover, a plastic bag, or an envelope to protect it until you place it in the hands of an identified Secret Service Agent.

steps.step: Surrender the note or coin only to a properly identified police officer or a Secret Service Special Agent, or mail it to your nearest U.S. Secret Service field office:
sub: 3801 University Avenue, #550, Riverside, CA 92501; Phone: 951-276-6781 (Resident Office)
200 W Santa Ana Blvd, #500, Santa Ana, CA 92701-4164; Phone: 714-246-8257 (Resident Office)
725 South Figueroa Street, #1300, Los Angeles, CA 90017; Phone: 213-894-4830 (Field Office)

sections.heading: Clerks
steps: Once you have ascertained there was a suspect counterfeit bill, ask for another form of payment.
At the time of the transaction, if another form of payment was not obtained, you can cancel the Chameleon transaction, but the counterfeit bill shall still be turned in to the Secret Service.

If you discover a counterfeit bill and are not able to determine whom it came from, you will be short in your deposit for that day. Fill out the shortage portion on your daily deposit envelope and have your supervisor sign the envelope (Attachment C).

sections.heading: Deposit Clerks
steps: Once the shortage portion of the envelope is filled out, verify the shortage and then have the clerk and their supervisor fill out form AR-3 (Attachment A).
Once the form is filled out, fill out the Daily Deposit Memo (DDM) (Attachment B) and use account code 523210.
Attach the local police report with the DDM to turn in with the form AR-3.

related.name: DAS Policy 000-22 Front Desk Cash Handling
Payment transaction (cancellation of a transaction involving a counterfeit bill)
U.S. Department of the Treasury, If You Suspect a Counterfeit

documents.title: Workplace Lactation Accommodation
documents.purpose: To ensure employees participating in the workplace lactation accommodation program are provided time and a private location to express milk while at work. This policy establishes guidelines for scheduling, facilities, notification, compliance, atmosphere of support, unusual circumstances, and employee responsibilities.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: All Department of Animal Services (DAS) employees.
documents.supersedes: Policy 000-68 Workplace Lactation Accommodation, effective May 4, 2017
references: California Labor Code Chapter 3.8, Sections 1030-1033 (Lactation Accommodation for all California employees)

sections.heading: Policy
steps: It is the policy of DAS to ensure employees participating in the workplace lactation accommodation program are provided time and a private location to express milk while at work.

sections.heading: Scheduling
steps: Permit employees to use their scheduled breaks and lunch period for lactation accommodation.
If additional time is required beyond the normal break or lunch period, the employee will discuss scheduling options with her supervisor.
With supervisory approval, scheduling options may include making up the time with annual/vacation leave or a flexed work schedule.

sections.heading: Facilities
steps: Provide employees the use of a clean, comfortable space for lactation accommodation in their worksite.
If a designated lactation room is not available, vacant offices or conference rooms are possible options. A toilet stall or bathroom is not acceptable.

Ensure the space is secure, equipped with an electrical outlet, in close proximity to the employee's work area, and contains comfortable seating with a table or other flat surface, with windows covered.
Riverside Animal Shelter has a lactation room available for use on the 2nd floor in room A209.

sections.heading: Notification
steps: The Employee Resources Team will inform staff during the Employee Resources Orientations of the department's current lactation policy and its means to support those employees who have an interest in breastfeeding.

Direct employees who seek out Family Medical Leave Act (FMLA) maternity leave to the County's Workforce Exchange to access information regarding lactation accommodation, breastfeeding support, and the WIC program prior to their leave. This information will also be provided upon request to spouses of expectant women prior to taking FMLA.
Supervisors of employees requesting lactation accommodation will work with their employees to ensure a clean, comfortable space is made available at their worksite if one is not already available.

sections.heading: Compliance
steps: All managers and supervisors shall respond to any request for lactation accommodation in a manner consistent with the procedures outlined in this policy.

sections.heading: Atmosphere of Support
steps: Lactation accommodation shall not be a source of discrimination in employment or in access to employment.
It is prohibited under this policy to harass a breastfeeding employee or exercise any conduct that creates an intimidating, hostile, or offensive working environment.
Address any incident of harassment related to breastfeeding in accordance with policies and procedures for discrimination and harassment.

sections.heading: Unusual Circumstances
steps: DAS acknowledges that some work locations may not have adequate facilities to accommodate a breastfeeding employee. In these situations, the employee and the employee's supervisor will work with Employee Resources staff to create a mutually acceptable solution.

sections.heading: Employee Responsibilities
steps: Inform your supervisor of the request for lactation accommodation upon your return to work.
Keep your supervisor informed if lactation accommodation needs change or are no longer needed.
Leave the lactation accommodation area clean after each use.

related.name: County of Riverside Workforce Exchange (lactation accommodation, breastfeeding support, and WIC program information)
County policies and procedures for discrimination and harassment

documents.title: Use and Protection of County Assets
documents.purpose: To protect the assets of the Riverside County Department of Animal Services (DAS), to ensure appropriate use of its resources, and to ensure adequate support of its operations. Additionally, this policy serves to prevent exposing DAS and the County of Riverside to serious liability or security risks.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: All Animal Services employees.
documents.supersedes: Policy 000-70 Use and Protection of County Assets, effective July 1, 2017
references: BOS Policy D-2 (Use and Purchase of County Vehicles)
BOS Policy A-50 (Electronic Media and Use Policy)
BOS Policy D-10 (Overnight Retention of County Vehicles)
DAS Policy 000-55 (Use of Telephones for Personal Calls)
Riverside County Ordinance No. 440, Section 3K(4) and Section 9
GSA/Purchasing Division Procedure 086 (To Process Surplus County Property)

definitions.def: Any correspondence, documents, or other printed materials that contribute to the conduct of official County business.
Hardware, software, communications networks, and information in various forms.

sections.heading: Policy
steps: Take all necessary and prudent steps to protect and ensure the proper use of County assets. All DAS employees are charged with the protection and proper utilization of County-owned equipment, property, facilities, and vehicles.
Protect DAS's investment in computing resources, use the resources for business purposes only, and ensure DAS's right to use the systems and information upon which it depends.
No DAS employee may claim or take any County property for personal use or disposal. This includes expendable items such as postage stamps, pencils, paperclips, and paper.

The use of telephones, cellular phones, two-way radios, copying and FAX machines, tools and equipment, computers, and computer software for personal use, as well as the use of non-expendable equipment for private personal gain, is prohibited.

Use equipment, computing resources, and vehicles only for their intended purpose; they shall not be abused. Employees may be held personally liable for the misuse or abuse of any County equipment. Such actions may result in disciplinary actions in accordance with Section 3K(4) of Ordinance No. 440 for inefficiency or negligence in performance of duties and/or neglect of duty.
All employees of this Department will acknowledge their responsibilities in writing when first employed with DAS.

sections.heading: Vehicles
steps: Abide by the provisions of Section 9 of Ordinance No. 440 as amended by Board of Supervisors (BOS) Policy D-10 when operating County-owned vehicles.
Use County vehicles only for the purpose of County business and not for personal business or pleasure of any person whatsoever.

Do not take a County vehicle outside the County or keep any vehicle at your residence without authorization from the County Executive Officer or the DAS Department Head. NOTE: DAS authorizes the use of County vehicles to travel to and through San Bernardino County, for official business, without the employee having to gain separate authorization for each trip.

Exercise due caution and prudence in the operation of any County vehicle in your custody and minimize the risk of damage by using available off-street parking wherever reasonably possible, particularly during the night.
Employees shall be liable to the County for damages and other expenses resulting from and caused by their willful misconduct in the use or operation of a County vehicle.

sections.heading: Telephones
steps: Use County-owned or leased telephones and cellular phones for the conduct of necessary official County business only. Refer to DAS Policy 000-55 regarding use of DAS telephones.

sections.heading: Mail and FAX
steps: Use mail, including FAX machines, for official DAS correspondence only. Employees are not authorized to use the departmental or office mailing address or any DAS mail room for the receipt or dispatch of personal mail.
Use DAS mail meters and DAS-purchased postage stamps for official County business only.

steps.step: FAX equipment procurement:
sub: When submitting a request to procure FAX equipment, the supervisor should justify the costs to obtain the equipment, any additional phone lines required, and installation, as well as the operational costs including supplies and maintenance.

Review the costs in terms of the need of the office to have its own FAX equipment. Many DAS copiers now have fax capability; if phone lines are available to accommodate faxes, a copier with FAX function will be considered.
Submit requests for procurement of FAX equipment to the Department Head or designated representative for approval unless within the signature authority of the requestor or requestor's supervisor.
Forward the approved request for procurement to the DAS Fiscal Division to coordinate the purchase or lease and installation of the FAX.

Pre-approval of IT related items will be initiated by IT personnel. Purchases of IT related items will only be authorized by the DAS Buyer II position. Equipment costs will be charged to the appropriate department office, division, branch, or unit.
All electrical checks are to be performed by Facilities before equipment is installed. Facilities will be responsible for the initial in-service of staff for all new installations of FAX equipment.

steps.step: FAX use:
sub: Limit FAX transmittals to documents requiring urgent delivery; do not use FAX for routine correspondence. Due to the higher cost of transmitting documents by this method, FAX use should be limited to documents that must reach their destination address in less than 48 hours.
Consider other mailing alternatives for a document that exceeds five pages. NOTE: If the FAX machine has the ability to FAX a document during the night, this can save on the cost of the transmission.
Department, office, division, branch, or unit policies may be developed to restrict usage of FAX equipment to staff assigned to the location that pays the cost of the FAX.
Do not move or relocate equipment without proper department approval and coordination with IT and the DAS Fiscal Division Asset Coordinator.
No modification or removal of components is permitted without prior approval of IT and the DAS Fiscal Division Asset Coordinator.
Prepare a FAX Cover Sheet for any FAX transmission. The form is to be transmitted with the document to assist the receiving office in identifying the intended recipient.

steps: Copier/FAX machine repair: FAX machines that are built into the copier will be serviced by the contracted copier service unless the issue is phone line related. Phone line related issues will be handled by IT.

sections.heading: Computers and Computing Resources
steps: Use DAS computing resources for DAS business only. Non-business use of these resources may be authorized by managers in the case of projects related to DAS sponsored employee education or training. Unauthorized non-business use of DAS computing resources is prohibited.

All information contained in DAS computer systems, including electronic mail (e-mail) and Internet activity, is DAS property. DAS reserves the right to monitor or review the use of computing resources as deemed necessary. This monitoring activity may limit the level of privacy that employees can reasonably expect for information stored on computers or transmitted over e-mail, Internet, or other communications media.
Department Heads will ensure that all e-mail users acknowledge in writing that they have received and read BOS Policy A-50, Electronic Media and Use Policy.
County-owned or leased computers are provided for the conduct of official County business purposes only. DAS computers will not be used for personal business, gain, or entertainment.

Contact the IT Help Desk to report a problem or request services, either through the RCIT Intranet at https://intranet.rivcoca.org/ or by phone at micro number 59900. It is the computer user's responsibility to make this contact.
Do not move computers from one DAS location to another without prior supervisor approval. Supervisors will submit a request to move or install computers through RCIT and the Fiscal Team.

sections.heading: Facilities
steps: Employee responsibility: Ensure that DAS offices and the equipment therein are protected from abuse or theft to the maximum degree possible. Any employee witnessing destruction or theft of County/public property is obliged to report it to their immediate supervisor. After normal work hours when a supervisor is not available, report such incidents to the local police or County Sheriff's office.

Supervisor responsibility: Ensure that the facility or office used by your staff is properly secured at the end of the work day. If necessary, designate one or more individuals to be responsible for turning off lights, radios, coffee pots, etc., and for locking entrances and exits to floors and/or offices, including building entrances where custodial personnel have not been assigned this responsibility.

sections.heading: Control of Facility Keys
steps: Issue office keys to employees upon written request from the supervisor.

Issue DAS keys for exterior doors to employees only upon written request to the Key Custodian or from the Department Director, Deputy Director, or Operations Chief. A signature receipt of the employee is required for door keys.

Request exterior door keys for other County-owned buildings through management via the locally designated agent of the County Department of Building Services. Exterior door keys for leased facilities will be issued by the appropriate supervisors or management staff.
Access to County buildings after regular work hours is restricted to employees in possession of the keys. Employees working in DAS facilities must be knowledgeable of the security system.

Individuals approved to work overtime on weekends may gain access from a co-worker authorized for this purpose. In an emergency, assistance may be requested from custodial personnel; proof of identity and purpose will be necessary in these cases.
Employees entering locked facilities assume full personal responsibility to secure the facility upon departure.
Control (master) keys may be authorized by the DAS Director for issuance to a Program Chief, Manager, or other staff as approved.
Supervisors are responsible for ensuring that all signed-for keys in the possession of terminating employees are recovered and returned to DAS or Facility Management as appropriate.

sections.heading: Safe and Cash Registry Security
steps: Keep safes and cash registers locked at all times except when they are under the direct observation and control of an employee who is trusted with the combination/key or is assigned access to the cash or other materials stored in the safe or cash register.
The Fiscal Manager will determine who should know the combination of safes or have cash register keys in their functional units.
Hold the number of staff with knowledge of a safe combination to an absolute minimum.
Change combinations whenever a person with knowledge of the combination is terminated, resigns, retires, transfers, or otherwise is no longer a member of staff of the particular functional unit.
Do not commingle personal articles, personal funds, or office coffee funds with official County funds or materials in a County-owned safe or cash register.
Designate two DAS employees for each safe: one responsible to lock the safe at the end of each day, while the other checks the safe at the end of the day to ensure it is locked.
Never count money in an area which is accessible to or in view of the public.

sections.heading: Surplus Equipment and Furniture
steps: Obtain authorization from the Director, Program Chief, Manager, or Division Head before removing excess equipment and furniture.
Decisions regarding computer equipment use or salvage are made by DAS IT if the equipment is no longer needed.
When disposing of excess equipment and furniture, change DAS inventory records to reflect the loss.

Coordinate with the Fiscal Division for transfer or final disposition of materials. The Fiscal Division will follow GSA/Purchasing Division Procedure 086, To Process Surplus County Property, for transfer or disposal of materials.

documents.title: Dogs Playing for Life
documents.purpose: To establish the procedure for safely conducting a dog play group. Through dog play groups, the Department offers a more natural environment and comprehensive approach to the care of shelter animals. This helps staff better assess behavior, maintain healthy behavior, and support better adoption matches. Play groups provide opportunities for enrichment, assessment, training, and behavior management.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: All staff and volunteers participating in canine play groups at RCDAS shelters.
documents.supersedes: Policy 000-76 Dogs Playing for Life, effective December 27, 2020; absorbs SOP Dogs Playing for Life, effective December 27, 2020
references: Dogs Playing for Life website: www.dogsplayingforlife.com
Dogs Playing for Life Manual: https://epd.bfb.myftpupload.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/08/Dogs-Playing-for-Life-Manual.pdf
Five Freedoms: www.animalhumanesociety.org

definitions.term: Freedom from thirst, hunger, and malnutrition
definitions.def: By providing ready access to fresh water and a diet to maintain full health and vigor.
By providing an appropriate environment including shelter and a comfortable resting area.

definitions.term: Freedom from pain, injury, and disease
definitions.def: By prevention or rapid diagnosis and treatment.
By ensuring conditions and treatment which avoid mental suffering.
By providing sufficient space, proper facilities, and company of the animal's own kind.
Appropriate and playful with other dogs.
Usually more tolerant than playful, or shows no interest at all. Will likely act appropriately in social settings.
Plays well with some dogs but can become intolerant of other dogs. Can play with others when set up correctly by the human.
Playful with some dogs, but reactive to dogs that challenge them.
Not able to play with or tolerate other dogs. Rarer than commonly assumed, but can be very dangerous for other dogs.
Allowing the socially appropriate dog involved in a conflict to keep playing so it ends on a positive experience, attending to its behavioral and emotional needs right away.

sections.heading: Policy
steps: The Riverside County Department of Animal Services (RCDAS) is committed to the policy that no adoptable animals will be euthanized, and to ensuring the five freedoms are being met for every animal that enters DAS.

The five freedoms are: (1) freedom from hunger and thirst; (2) freedom from discomfort; (3) freedom from pain, injury, or disease; (4) freedom to express normal behavior; (5) freedom from fear and distress.

Dogs Playing for Life (DPFL) directly ensures the freedom to express normal behavior. "Every Dog, Every Day!" is an out-of-kennel enrichment program that offers daily playgroups to canine populations. Playgroups enhance the quality of life for most dogs, resulting in maximum lifesaving while reducing length of stay.

With playgroups, natural behavioral expression and canine communication can occur, helping to identify dogs that can fast track into homes. Dogs with potential behavioral challenges can be better identified and benefit from a targeted action plan for successful placement.

Philosophy: RCDAS wants to ensure that every dog entering its shelters has the best possible chance of a live outcome. The intention is to provide this out-of-kennel enrichment program to every available dog every day.

sections.heading: Play Yard Setup
steps: Include all healthy animals impounded at DAS that have been released from their stray hold in a play group.
Conduct play groups in a secure yard. While play groups are possible in a variety of yard types, the ideal set-up includes the following.
Provide a catch pen at every entrance: a secure, gated area outside of each entrance point, with gates that swing in both directions.
Use kiddie pools in place of bowls and buckets of water to minimize resource guarding.
Attach clips, such as carabiners, to the fencing, spaced far apart from one another, to be used to secure dogs in an emergency situation.
Keep toys, treats, and any other valued resources out of the yard since they can become a potential distraction or issue.

sections.heading: Human Participants
steps: Staff each play group with a team of at least three handlers, including a group leader, an assistant, and a runner/assistant.
If more people are available, they may participate as an assistant or observer. It is highly recommended that someone be tasked with taking notes.

sections.heading: Equipment and Tools
steps: Assemble the following equipment: flat collars (fitted properly), leashes, nylon muzzles, walkie talkies (if available), spray water bottles, shake cans, Spray Shield, Pet Corrector air blast, and airhorn.

sections.heading: Medical Protocols
steps: Consult with the medical team prior to placing some dogs into play groups.
The behavioral and medical teams should work together at times to determine medical protocols.

sections.heading: Identifying Participants for Play Groups
steps: The majority of dogs can participate in play groups. Play groups can be beneficial for dogs of all ages, sizes, breeds, and temperaments.
Base inclusion in play groups on the individual dog; do not exclude dogs due to blanket restrictions, such as breed.

Dogs that display on-leash reactivity or barrier reactivity can still be candidates for play groups. Those behaviors are not accurate predictors of aggression or social ability in an off-leash scenario such as play groups.
Do not disqualify a dog from play group participation based on reproductive status. Being intact is not an accurate predictor or cause of aggression.

sections.heading: Leadership Roles
steps: The handler's role in the play group is to be a calm, neutral presence that monitors the group. The handler's comfort level and ability to stay calm around the dogs is critical in determining the success of the play group.
The group leader makes the decisions about which dogs enter and exit the play yards, decides when to interrupt or redirect the dogs, and determines how to handle the dogs in general.
A runner brings dogs from the kennels to the yards. An assistant may also be present as a second set of hands.
Whenever more than one person is present, they must take direction from the group leader. Only one person is in charge and making decisions at any time.

steps.step: Your voice:
sub: Refrain from unnecessary talking.
Keep your voice gentle and calm, but confident.
Avoid sounding panicked, reactive, or excited.
Limit talking to the dogs. Play groups are designed for dog-to-dog interactions.

steps.step: Using negative verbal markers:
sub: Choose and use a consistent negative marker such as "eh-eh" or "enough" to clearly communicate with the dogs when they are behaving inappropriately.
Teach the dogs that this verbal marker will precede an interruption or correction so that they can learn to settle down with just a verbal marker.

steps.step: Your body:
sub: Keep hands away from the dogs. Limit interactions, such as petting, so that the dogs can remain focused on their time with one another.
Be aware that you might become a highly valued resource to the dogs by giving attention or affection.

steps.step: Using body blocks:
sub: Use your body to block the dogs when necessary.
Teach the dogs to yield to your legs by using your lower body, rather than your hands, to calmly communicate with them.
Use your body to calmly step in between dogs to reduce tension if necessary.

steps.step: Moving the dogs:
sub: When handling the dogs in the play yards by their collars or on leash, always guide the dogs smoothly; do not drag them.
Use calm, consistent movements and a soft, loose leash or guiding hand. Refrain from using pressure on the collar as long as the dog is following you.
Soft and gentle guidance communicates to the dogs that they are correct, while a tight leash or firm grip conveys that they are in trouble.

steps.step: Working the gate:
sub: Runners should bring dogs to the entrance gate with a loose leash to convey that approaching the play yard is a positive experience.
Runners help the group leader by allowing the dogs to enter only when they are calm. Group leaders will want to watch the other dogs when a new dog approaches the gate.
Use your legs to take the space when opening the gate. Show the entering dog where the opening is and where to go, while blocking the dogs already in the yard from seeing an opening to get out.
If necessary, use interrupters at the gate to help dogs respect the space that you are occupying and to enter more calmly.

sections.heading: Understanding Play Styles
steps: Since the majority of dogs are able to participate in play groups, there will be a variety of play styles occurring under supervision. Understanding play styles helps staff and volunteers choose better play mates for the dogs and support healthy social behaviors.

steps.step: Gentle and Dainty:
sub: Very mutual play.
Relatively quiet.
Frequently starts and stops play.
Easily becomes nervous.

steps.step: Rough and Rowdy:
sub: Mostly mutual play.
Typically very noisy and vocal.
Very physical, wrestling play style; grabbing and holding other dogs; chasing and tumbling with other dogs.
Can ramp up and escalate in play.
This style of play can trigger conflict with other dogs or tip over from play to arguments and/or conflicts.

steps.step: Push and Pull:
sub: Less mutual play.
Chasing, driving, and nipping the other dogs.
Not interested in directly engaging; prefers to move other dogs around.
Can get noisy; may use demand barking during play.
Can escalate; often ends in conflict because it can be annoying or antagonistic to other dogs.
May need handler intervention in order to keep things polite.

steps.step: Seek and Destroy:
sub: Usually not mutual play; always monitor the other dogs to determine if they are mutually re-engaging in play.
Rooted in prey drive, but it is still play.
Often noisy: growling and squealing.
Can escalate into potentially unsafe interactions.
Tends to need handler intervention more than other styles of play.
Provocative to the other dogs in the yard and may influence them to behave aggressively towards the more vulnerable dog.

steps: Once you have determined the dogs' individual play styles, aim to set them up with dogs they will enjoy interacting with and/or that will help them learn new social skills.

Rotate dogs so they meet and play with different dogs. Do not create mini packs of three or four dogs who play together every day. Change up playmates regularly to avoid creating packs within the common and potentially competitive territory of the yards and kennels.

sections.heading: Introduction and Play Group Building Process
steps: Create an organized list and review every dog before starting a play group. Check previous notes, especially previous notes that pertain to play groups.
Make sure when reviewing the list that the RCAC list is being reviewed as well. If a dog has recently had surgery, it cannot participate in a play group.
Assemble the team of ACTs that will be assisting with play group.
Decide who will be the leaders, assistants, and runners for the day.
Retrieve the cart that has all essential items attached (collars, leashes, spray bottles, shake cans, etc.).
Once the team has decided who the leaders and runners are, it is solely up to the group leader which dog will be coming out first.
Ideally, there are dogs classified as greeters; bring those dogs out to play group first. Greeters are especially useful for dogs being assessed for the first time.

Once the group leader has called out a dog, the runner will retrieve the dog. The runner is responsible for bringing the dog out on a slip lead, then transferring the dog to a collar and leash, and bringing the dog to the catch pen area.
The group leader or assistant within the yard controls the gate. Only the players in the yard shall control the gate.
Once the group leader is comfortable with the dog in the catch pen, the group leader or assistant will open the catch pen gate and allow the new dog to join the play group.

sections.heading: When to Use Interruptions and Corrections
steps: By and large, the dogs will move through their communications and work out their own issues without human assistance. Whenever possible, allow them to teach each other these skills rather than becoming dependent on the human to tell them how to interact in a healthy way as a dog.

steps.step: Intervene to keep things running smoothly and safely in these situations:
sub: When their play is not mutual.
When one dog is having fun at the expense of the other.
When the response is disproportionate to the feedback from the other dog.
When they are struggling to complete the greeting ritual due to arousal, immaturity, fear, etc.
When an actual fight breaks out.

steps: The group leader will make the call when and how to intervene, via interruptions or correction tools.

steps.step: Interruptions:
sub: The purpose of an interruption is to momentarily influence the interactions of the dogs with a distraction. It is not a punishment or intended to communicate to the dogs that they are in trouble.
Choose an interruption when the behavior is inappropriate, rather than concerning.
An interruption is helpful for dogs that need to develop different, more appropriate play habits.
A typical interruption may be a negative verbal marker, with or without a shake can or a spray bottle.
An interruption is only necessary if a dog is not responding to another. The goal is for the dogs to learn how to communicate with each other first.
Interruptions are often used at the gate in order to help excited dogs enter the play yard more appropriately.

steps.step: Corrections:
sub: The goal of a correction is to extinguish behavior.
Choose a correction when the behavior is concerning and must not escalate for safety reasons.
A correction is helpful for dogs that need to show increased responsiveness to the handler (or the other dogs), while decreasing drive in play.
A typical correction may be a negative verbal marker with a shake can, spray bottle, an air horn, or a spray deterrent.
Rather than distracting the dog away from the issue, use a correction to teach the dog to make a different choice next time.
Corrections are intended to quickly, clearly, and calmly provide valuable information that will teach dogs to behave more appropriately so they can remain in play groups.

steps.step: Spray bottle (water only):
sub: Uses and benefits: an interruption or correction used for a single dog; particularly helpful at the gate in order to help dogs enter the yard more calmly and to enforce respectful space from the dogs already in the yard.
Possible disadvantages: dogs may become fearful of the handler; some dogs may not consider this an aversive (some may even consider it a reward) and will not respond to water as intended.

steps.step: Shake can (4-5 pennies in a small can, or a tin can wrapped in duct tape):
sub: Uses and benefits: an audible and/or physical interruption or correction used for multiple dogs; can be rattled lightly or firmly, at or near the offending dog, or tossed towards the offending dog as a startling tool.
Possible disadvantages: many dogs will hear it simultaneously, so non-offending dogs may be affected in addition to the offending dog; dogs may become fearful of the handler.

steps.step: Spray Shield or Pet Corrector:
sub: Uses and benefits: an interruption or correction used for multiple dogs; Spray Shield can be sprayed at or near a dog's face; for some dogs the sound of the spray is enough of a correction, so contact with the spray or air is not needed, while for other dogs the pairing of sound and touch is more effective in settling them down.
Possible disadvantages: non-offending dogs will be affected in addition to the offending dog; dogs may become fearful of or reactive to the handler.

steps.step: Air horn:
sub: Uses and benefits: an interruption or correction used for multiple dogs; can be helpful in startling apart dogs that are engaged in a fight.

Possible disadvantages: air horns can be intense and scary to many dogs; this correction typically affects all dogs in the yard; possible physical reaction to extreme volume too close to ears; dogs may develop a generalized fear of the handler and/or play yard.

sections.heading: Understanding, Controlling, and Stopping Dog Fights
steps: In the case of a fight breaking out, always keep your cool and focus on the safety of the dogs and the people involved.

The goal of play groups is for dogs to learn how to communicate with one another appropriately, which may sometimes include brief arguments in order to establish themselves with one another. Handlers do not dictate dogs' relationships with one another (the dogs decide who they like or do not like), but do monitor their behavior, stepping in only when necessary.
The group leader should make the decision about how to move forward during a dog fight.

Always use startling tools first to divert the attention of the dogs. Most dogs are steerable and can be corrected by quick startling devices (shake can, spray bottle, air horn). Be sure to keep them separated after an argument.
If the startling tools fail, remain calm. Yelling or becoming very vocal may escalate the situation.
Do not hit the dogs. This may cause the dog's behavior to escalate and may even cause the dog to redirect to the handler.
Use any object available to get between the two dogs: trash can, chair, clipboard, pig board, or any agility equipment.
If the dogs remain engaged, grab the dogs by the hind legs or their leashes. Grab the collar as a last resort.
If the dogs are tightly engaged, DO NOT PULL THE DOGS APART. Pulling can cause far more serious injuries and can cause a dog's grip to become tighter.
Immobilize the dog who is holding on to the other dog and feed the bite. This is done by controlling the back of the head and pushing into the other dog. Push in, do not pull.
Only pull the dogs apart once the dogs have become separated from holding on to each other.
Do not let go of the dogs yet. Assess the situation and remove the offender.

Perform CPR (Continue-Play-Recover) on the dog who was attacked: allow the socially appropriate dog that was involved to keep playing so it has another positive experience instead of ending on a bad one. This attends to its behavioral and emotional needs right away.

Unless there are serious injuries, allow 30 minutes of recovery time in kennels or yard prior to invasive physical examination. This time allows the dogs to safely decompress and decreases potential defensive behaviors. Dogs with punctures or abrasions are typically better off for being given time to CPR. Discuss this approach with the veterinary team.

sections.heading: Utilizing Play Group Behavior Information
steps: Play groups make new information available to staff and to potential adopters or rescues. This information assists in adoption consultations. After observing the dogs play, it is easier to match adopters with dogs whose social skills match their lifestyles.
Classify observed behavior using the dog social categories defined in this policy (Dog Social, Dog Tolerant, Dog Selective, Dog Defensive, Dog Aggressive).

When speaking to anyone with regard to dog behavior, note that no predictions are guaranteed. This is observable information accessible in a shelter environment, and behavior may differ outside of the shelter or in a home.

revisions.author: Outcomes for Pets (draft)
revisions.desc: Migrated into standard RCDAS SOP template; policy 000-76 and companion SOP Dogs Playing for Life merged into one document. Content unchanged from 2020 versions; substantive edits pending (see edit log).

documents.searchText: to establish the procedure for safely conducting a dog play group. through dog play groups, the department offers a more natural environment and comprehensive approach to the care of shelter animals. this helps staff better assess behavior, maintain healthy behavior, and support better adoption matches. play groups provide opportunities for enrichment, assessment, training, and behavior management. all staff and volunteers participating in canine play groups at rcdas shelters. director of animal services freedom from thirst, hunger, and malnutrition by providing ready access to fresh water and a diet to maintain full health and vigor. freedom from discomfort and exposure by providing an appropriate environment including shelter and a comfortable resting area. freedom from pain, injury, and disease by prevention or rapid diagnosis and treatment. freedom from fear and distress by ensuring conditions and treatment which avoid mental suffering. freedom to express normal behavior by providing sufficient space, proper facilities, and company of the animal's own kind. dog social appropriate and playful with other dogs. dog tolerant usually more tolerant than playful, or shows no interest at all. will likely act appropriately in social settings. dog selective plays well with some dogs but can become intolerant of other dogs. can play with others when set up correctly by the human. dog defensive playful with some dogs, but reactive to dogs that challenge them. dog aggressive not able to play with or tolerate other dogs. rarer than commonly assumed, but can be very dangerous for other dogs. cpr (continue-play-recover) allowing the socially appropriate dog involved in a conflict to keep playing so it ends on a positive experience, attending to its behavioral and emotional needs right away. policy the riverside county department of animal services (rcdas) is committed to the policy that no adoptable animals will be euthanized, and to ensuring the five freedoms are being met for every animal that enters das. the five freedoms are: (1) freedom from hunger and thirst; (2) freedom from discomfort; (3) freedom from pain, injury, or disease; (4) freedom to express normal behavior; (5) freedom from fear and distress. dogs playing for life (dpfl) directly ensures the freedom to express normal behavior. "every dog, every day!" is an out-of-kennel enrichment program that offers daily playgroups to canine populations. playgroups enhance the quality of life for most dogs, resulting in maximum lifesaving while reducing length of stay. with playgroups, natural behavioral expression and canine communication can occur, helping to identify dogs that can fast track into homes. dogs with potential behavioral challenges can be better identified and benefit from a targeted action plan for successful placement. philosophy: rcdas wants to ensure that every dog entering its shelters has the best possible chance of a live outcome. the intention is to provide this out-of-kennel enrichment program to every available dog every day. play yard setup include all healthy animals impounded at das that have been released from their stray hold in a play group. conduct play groups in a secure yard. while play groups are possible in a variety of yard types, the ideal set-up includes the following. provide a catch pen at every entrance: a secure, gated area outside of each entrance point, with gates that swing in both directions. use kiddie pools in place of bowls and buckets of water to minimize resource guarding. attach clips, such as carabiners, to the fencing, spaced far apart from one another, to be used to secure dogs in an emergency situation. keep toys, treats, and any other valued resources out of the yard since they can become a potential distraction or issue. human participants staff each play group with a team of at least three handlers, including a group leader, an assistant, and a runner/assistant. if more people are available, they may participate as an assistant or observer. it is highly recommended that someone be tasked with taking notes. equipment and tools assemble the following equipment: flat collars (fitted properly), leashes, nylon muzzles, walkie talkies (if available), spray water bottles, shake cans, spray shield, pet corrector air blast, and airhorn. medical protocols consult with the medical team prior to placing some dogs into play groups. the behavioral and medical teams should work together at times to determine medical protocols. identifying participants for play groups the majority of dogs can participate in play groups. play groups can be beneficial for dogs of all ages, sizes, breeds, and temperaments. base inclusion in play groups on the individual dog; do not exclude dogs due to blanket restrictions, such as breed. dogs that display on-leash reactivity or barrier reactivity can still be candidates for play groups. those behaviors are not accurate predictors of aggression or social ability in an off-leash scenario such as play groups. do not disqualify a dog from play group participation based on reproductive status. being intact is not an accurate predictor or cause of aggression. leadership roles the handler's role in the play group is to be a calm, neutral presence that monitors the group. the handler's comfort level and ability to stay calm around the dogs is critical in determining the success of the play group. the group leader makes the decisions about which dogs enter and exit the play yards, decides when to interrupt or redirect the dogs, and determines how to handle the dogs in general. a runner brings dogs from the kennels to the yards. an assistant may also be present as a second set of hands. whenever more than one person is present, they must take direction from the group leader. only one person is in charge and making decisions at any time. handling techniques {"step": "your voice:", "sub": ["refrain from unnecessary talking.", "keep your voice gentle and calm, but confident.", "avoid sounding panicked, reactive, or excited.", "limit talking to the dogs. play groups are designed for dog-to-dog interactions."]} {"step": "using negative verbal markers:", "sub": ["choose and use a consistent negative marker such as \"eh-eh\" or \"enough\" to clearly communicate with the dogs when they are behaving inappropriately.", "teach the dogs that this verbal marker will precede an interruption or correction so that they can learn to settle down with just a verbal marker."]} {"step": "your body:", "sub": ["keep hands away from the dogs. limit interactions, such as petting, so that the dogs can remain focused on their time with one another.", "be aware that you might become a highly valued resource to the dogs by giving attention or affection."]} {"step": "using body blocks:", "sub": ["use your body to block the dogs when necessary.", "teach the dogs to yield to your legs by using your lower body, rather than your hands, to calmly communicate with them.", "use your body to calmly step in between dogs to reduce tension if necessary."]} {"step": "moving the dogs:", "sub": ["when handling the dogs in the play yards by their collars or on leash, always guide the dogs smoothly; do not drag them.", "use calm, consistent movements and a soft, loose leash or guiding hand. refrain from using pressure on the collar as long as the dog is following you.", "soft and gentle guidance communicates to the dogs that they are correct, while a tight leash or firm grip conveys that they are in trouble."]} {"step": "working the gate:", "sub": ["runners should bring dogs to the entrance gate with a loose leash to convey that approaching the play yard is a positive experience.", "runners help the group leader by allowing the dogs to enter only when they are calm. group leaders will want to watch the other dogs when a new dog approaches the gate.", "use your legs to take the space when opening the gate. show the entering dog where the opening is and where to go, while blocking the dogs already in the yard from seeing an opening to get out.", "if necessary, use interrupters at the gate to help dogs respect the space that you are occupying and to enter more calmly."]} understanding play styles since the majority of dogs are able to participate in play groups, there will be a variety of play styles occurring under supervision. understanding play styles helps staff and volunteers choose better play mates for the dogs and support healthy social behaviors. {"step": "gentle and dainty:", "sub": ["very mutual play.", "relatively quiet.", "frequently starts and stops play.", "easily becomes nervous."]} {"step": "rough and rowdy:", "sub": ["mostly mutual play.", "typically very noisy and vocal.", "very physical, wrestling play style; grabbing and holding other dogs; chasing and tumbling with other dogs.", "can ramp up and escalate in play.", "this style of play can trigger conflict with other dogs or tip over from play to arguments and/or conflicts."]} {"step": "push and pull:", "sub": ["less mutual play.", "chasing, driving, and nipping the other dogs.", "not interested in directly engaging; prefers to move other dogs around.", "can get noisy; may use demand barking during play.", "can escalate; often ends in conflict because it can be annoying or antagonistic to other dogs.", "may need handler intervention in order to keep things polite."]} {"step": "seek and destroy:", "sub": ["usually not mutual play; always monitor the other dogs to determine if they are mutually re-engaging in play.", "rooted in prey drive, but it is still play.", "often noisy: growling and squealing.", "can escalate into potentially unsafe interactions.", "tends to need handler intervention more than other styles of play.", "provocative to the other dogs in the yard and may influence them to behave aggressively towards the more vulnerable dog."]} once you have determined the dogs' individual play styles, aim to set them up with dogs they will enjoy interacting with and/or that will help them learn new social skills. rotate dogs so they meet and play with different dogs. do not create mini packs of three or four dogs who play together every day. change up playmates regularly to avoid creating packs within the common and potentially competitive territory of the yards and kennels. introduction and play group building process create an organized list and review every dog before starting a play group. check previous notes, especially previous notes that pertain to play groups. make sure when reviewing the list that the rcac list is being reviewed as well. if a dog has recently had surgery, it cannot participate in a play group. assemble the team of acts that will be assisting with play group. decide who will be the leaders, assistants, and runners for the day. retrieve the cart that has all essential items attached (collars, leashes, spray bottles, shake cans, etc.). once the team has decided who the leaders and runners are, it is solely up to the group leader which dog will be coming out first. ideally, there are dogs classified as greeters; bring those dogs out to play group first. greeters are especially useful for dogs being assessed for the first time. once the group leader has called out a dog, the runner will retrieve the dog. the runner is responsible for bringing the dog out on a slip lead, then transferring the dog to a collar and leash, and bringing the dog to the catch pen area. the group leader or assistant within the yard controls the gate. only the players in the yard shall control the gate. once the group leader is comfortable with the dog in the catch pen, the group leader or assistant will open the catch pen gate and allow the new dog to join the play group. when to use interruptions and corrections by and large, the dogs will move through their communications and work out their own issues without human assistance. whenever possible, allow them to teach each other these skills rather than becoming dependent on the human to tell them how to interact in a healthy way as a dog. {"step": "intervene to keep things running smoothly and safely in these situations:", "sub": ["when their play is not mutual.", "when one dog is having fun at the expense of the other.", "when the response is disproportionate to the feedback from the other dog.", "when they are struggling to complete the greeting ritual due to arousal, immaturity, fear, etc.", "when an actual fight breaks out."]} the group leader will make the call when and how to intervene, via interruptions or correction tools. {"step": "interruptions:", "sub": ["the purpose of an interruption is to momentarily influence the interactions of the dogs with a distraction. it is not a punishment or intended to communicate to the dogs that they are in trouble.", "choose an interruption when the behavior is inappropriate, rather than concerning.", "an interruption is helpful for dogs that need to develop different, more appropriate play habits.", "a typical interruption may be a negative verbal marker, with or without a shake can or a spray bottle.", "an interruption is only necessary if a dog is not responding to another. the goal is for the dogs to learn how to communicate with each other first.", "interruptions are often used at the gate in order to help excited dogs enter the play yard more appropriately."]} {"step": "corrections:", "sub": ["the goal of a correction is to extinguish behavior.", "choose a correction when the behavior is concerning and must not escalate for safety reasons.", "a correction is helpful for dogs that need to show increased responsiveness to the handler (or the other dogs), while decreasing drive in play.", "a typical correction may be a negative verbal marker with a shake can, spray bottle, an air horn, or a spray deterrent.", "rather than distracting the dog away from the issue, use a correction to teach the dog to make a different choice next time.", "corrections are intended to quickly, clearly, and calmly provide valuable information that will teach dogs to behave more appropriately so they can remain in play groups."]} using communication tools {"step": "spray bottle (water only):", "sub": ["uses and benefits: an interruption or correction used for a single dog; particularly helpful at the gate in order to help dogs enter the yard more calmly and to enforce respectful space from the dogs already in the yard.", "possible disadvantages: dogs may become fearful of the handler; some dogs may not consider this an aversive (some may even consider it a reward) and will not respond to water as intended."]} {"step": "shake can (4-5 pennies in a small can, or a tin can wrapped in duct tape):", "sub": ["uses and benefits: an audible and/or physical interruption or correction used for multiple dogs; can be rattled lightly or firmly, at or near the offending dog, or tossed towards the offending dog as a startling tool.", "possible disadvantages: many dogs will hear it simultaneously, so non-offending dogs may be affected in addition to the offending dog; dogs may become fearful of the handler."]} {"step": "spray shield or pet corrector:", "sub": ["uses and benefits: an interruption or correction used for multiple dogs; spray shield can be sprayed at or near a dog's face; for some dogs the sound of the spray is enough of a correction, so contact with the spray or air is not needed, while for other dogs the pairing of sound and touch is more effective in settling them down.", "possible disadvantages: non-offending dogs will be affected in addition to the offending dog; dogs may become fearful of or reactive to the handler."]} {"step": "air horn:", "sub": ["uses and benefits: an interruption or correction used for multiple dogs; can be helpful in startling apart dogs that are engaged in a fight.", "possible disadvantages: air horns can be intense and scary to many dogs; this correction typically affects all dogs in the yard; possible physical reaction to extreme volume too close to ears; dogs may develop a generalized fear of the handler and/or play yard."]} understanding, controlling, and stopping dog fights in the case of a fight breaking out, always keep your cool and focus on the safety of the dogs and the people involved. the goal of play groups is for dogs to learn how to communicate with one another appropriately, which may sometimes include brief arguments in order to establish themselves with one another. handlers do not dictate dogs' relationships with one another (the dogs decide who they like or do not like), but do monitor their behavior, stepping in only when necessary. the group leader should make the decision about how to move forward during a dog fight. always use startling tools first to divert the attention of the dogs. most dogs are steerable and can be corrected by quick startling devices (shake can, spray bottle, air horn). be sure to keep them separated after an argument. if the startling tools fail, remain calm. yelling or becoming very vocal may escalate the situation. do not hit the dogs. this may cause the dog's behavior to escalate and may even cause the dog to redirect to the handler. use any object available to get between the two dogs: trash can, chair, clipboard, pig board, or any agility equipment. if the dogs remain engaged, grab the dogs by the hind legs or their leashes. grab the collar as a last resort. if the dogs are tightly engaged, do not pull the dogs apart. pulling can cause far more serious injuries and can cause a dog's grip to become tighter. immobilize the dog who is holding on to the other dog and feed the bite. this is done by controlling the back of the head and pushing into the other dog. push in, do not pull. only pull the dogs apart once the dogs have become separated from holding on to each other. do not let go of the dogs yet. assess the situation and remove the offender. perform cpr (continue-play-recover) on the dog who was attacked: allow the socially appropriate dog that was involved to keep playing so it has another positive experience instead of ending on a bad one. this attends to its behavioral and emotional needs right away. unless there are serious injuries, allow 30 minutes of recovery time in kennels or yard prior to invasive physical examination. this time allows the dogs to safely decompress and decreases potential defensive behaviors. dogs with punctures or abrasions are typically better off for being given time to cpr. discuss this approach with the veterinary team. utilizing play group behavior information play groups make new information available to staff and to potential adopters or rescues. this information assists in adoption consultations. after observing the dogs play, it is easier to match adopters with dogs whose social skills match their lifestyles. classify observed behavior using the dog social categories defined in this policy (dog social, dog tolerant, dog selective, dog defensive, dog aggressive). when speaking to anyone with regard to dog behavior, note that no predictions are guaranteed. this is observable information accessible in a shelter environment, and behavior may differ outside of the shelter or in a home. dogs playing for life website: www.dogsplayingforlife.com dogs playing for life manual: https://epd.bfb.myftpupload.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/08/dogs-playing-for-life-manual.pdf five freedoms: www.animalhumanesociety.org

documents.title: Use of Monetary Donations
documents.purpose: To define the Department's donation funds and establish the procedure for using monetary donations, both through the Chameleon database and outside of it, including documentation and Board of Supervisors reporting requirements.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services; Departmental Executive Team (majority vote required for use of donations outside of Chameleon)
documents.scope: All Department of Animal Services staff requesting or administering the use of donated funds.
documents.supersedes: Policy 000-81 Use of Monetary Donations, effective July 1, 2022

definitions.term: Care Fund (Chameleon code DCF, project code AN0050012)
definitions.def: [Definition appears on page 1 of the source document, which did not extract. Restore from original in pass 2.]

definitions.term: Disaster Relief Fund (Chameleon code DDR, project code AN0050013)
definitions.def: [Definition appears on page 1 of the source document, which did not extract. Restore from original in pass 2.]

definitions.term: Animal Health Fund (Chameleon code DMF, project code AN0050014)
definitions.def: Covers treatment by veterinarians in our shelters and for emergency treatment and/or hospitalization in outside institutions. It also covers behavior programs, materials, and support. The Fund also may cover special care for animals suffering from cruelty or neglect and for those animals in our Foster Care Program. [Opening of this definition may be incomplete; first page of source did not extract.]

definitions.term: Pet Zone Fund (Chameleon code DPZF, project code AN0050015)
definitions.def: Allows the Department to provide pet owners with programs that help them retain their pets and become responsible pet owners. This helps keep animals in their loving homes and prevents animals from coming into the shelters. Programs include but are not limited to pet spay and neuter, education and outreach, community cats, diversion and safety net programs, and other programs dedicated to the human animal bond and supporting pet owners in crisis.

sections.heading: Use of Donations Within Chameleon
steps: Each donation fund definition has a corresponding code in the Chameleon database. The Chameleon codes are tied to a project code on the fiscal side: Care Fund (DCF / AN0050012), Disaster Relief Fund (DDR / AN0050013), Animal Health Fund (DMF / AN0050014), and Pet Zone Fund (DPZF / AN0050015).
Use the funds according to their definitions, with a detailed list of each animal helped being produced from the Chameleon database.

The database administrator pulls the report from the Chameleon database; this report is used as proof of the funds being expended. Common expenditures reported include whether the animal was spayed or neutered, microchipped, vaccinated, or received a rabies shot. Each report lists the animal identification number and every line of the work completed on the animal.

steps.step: Once the report has been secured by the Finance Team, the Finance Team will:
sub: Journal funds from the Donations Sub-fund (11109) and corresponding project code into the general fund (10000) with corresponding project code.

Report an accounting of the used funds to the Board of Supervisors (BOS) at the next BOS Quarterly report meeting. This report will include the additional estimated revenue, additional appropriations, and a brief explanation of what the donations funded.

sections.heading: Use of Donations Outside of Chameleon
steps: From time to time donors have more specific wishes for the funds they donate (for example, a donor might give $5,000 to be used at a specific location or on a specific project). In those instances, create an additional project code in the financial system that identifies the funds.
Place these donation funds into the donation sub-fund until the use of the donation is formally requested by a staff member.

To use funds outside of the Chameleon system, send a request to the Department's Executive Team describing the purchase, with any supporting documentation, to help the Executive Team decide on the use of the donation funds.
Once the Executive Team reviews the request, each member of the Executive Team will cast a vote on the use of the funds. A simple majority will authorize the use of the funds.

The Executive Team consists of the following six departmental positions: (1) Director of Animal Services, (2) Assistant Director of Animal Services, (3) Chief Veterinarian, (4) Fiscal Manager, (5) Commander of Field Services, (6) Public Information Officer.
Use the Donation Use Purchase Request sheet to document the request and the vote of the Executive Team.
Keep all documentation in files for a period of 7 years.

related.name: Donation fund codes DCF, DDR, DMF, DPZF

appendices: Appendix: 000-81 Use of Monetary Donations (Request Sheet).xlsx (attached separately)

documents.title: Administration and Reconciliation of Buprenix
documents.purpose: To provide consistent guidelines for use, documentation, and reconciliation of Buprenix.
documents.authority: The Supervising Registered Veterinary Technician (SRVT) or RVT designee controls issuance; the balanced final log is submitted to the Chief or SRVT designee.
documents.scope: This procedure is applicable for the Department of Animal Services (DAS) veterinarians and registered veterinary technicians (RVT).
documents.supersedes: SOP Administration and Reconciliation of Buprenix, effective September 5, 2017 (duplicate file copy absorbed)
references: DAS Policy 100-08, Controlled Substances Chain of Custody

definitions.def: Supervising Registered Veterinary Technician
Registered Veterinary Technician

sections.heading: Chain of Custody
steps: Upon receipt of the Buprenix order, the Supervising Registered Veterinary Technician (SRVT) or Registered Veterinary Technician (RVT) designee will log each bottle in the safe.

steps.step: Number each bottle in the following format:
sub: 17-01: year and bottle number for the Riverside shelter only.
SJ17-01: shelter location, year, and bottle number for the San Jacinto shelter only.
TP17-01: shelter location, year, and bottle number for the CVAC shelter.

steps: The SRVT or RVT designee shall issue one box or five (5) bottles at a time to the designated premise license area (Receiving, Clinic, etc.). Refer to Policy 100-08.

sections.heading: Administration - Desktop Chameleon
steps: Enter each dose administered by the RVT in Chameleon accurately.
If a treatment window does not exist that matches the medication date, create a new treatment window (medical screen).
Enter Buprenix in the medication field, including dosage and the correct format of the bottle number.
If the round of the medication is not completed and the dose is changed, cancel the remaining todos.
Create a new medication protocol from the date changed forward. Note: the treatment window must match the medication date.

sections.heading: Administration - WebChameleon
steps: Tap on the check icon to complete the task.
Enter the bottle number in the correct format.
Tap on Enter.

If the dose being administered is different than what is shown on the todo list, the RVT shall create a new task by tapping on the animal record and choosing Create New Task. It is recommended to use desktop Chameleon if possible.
Enter the new task (medication) and dose (description) with the applicable time the medication was supposed to be administered.
Save.
Repeat the new-task steps for the second dose of the same medication.
Tap on the Home menu icon at the top left corner.
Tap on Kennel, then Daily Tasks.

steps.step: When prompted, enter the following:
sub: Kennel number: enter the beginning kennel letter and first number. For example, if performing follow-up treatments in POD 200, enter R 2.
Tap on OK. This will allow the user to view a refreshed todo list that shall include the new medication task.

steps: Repeat the task completion steps (check icon, bottle number, Enter).

sections.heading: Reconciliation
steps: On a daily basis, at the end of the day, the closing RVT will balance use based on the bottle number.
Print the balanced Buprenix drug log.
Place the printed log in the designated drug folder.
Once a Buprenix bottle is empty, repeat the daily balancing, printing, and filing steps above.
Submit the balanced final Buprenix log.
Sign.
Submit to the Chief or SRVT designee.

campusVariations.variation: Bottle numbering format 17-01 (year and bottle number).
Bottle numbering format SJ17-01 (location, year, and bottle number).
Bottle numbering format TP17-01 (location, year, and bottle number).

related.name: DAS Policy 100-08, Controlled Substances Chain of Custody
