documents.title: Post Seizure Hearing or Court Restitution Billing in Chameleon
documents.purpose: To establish consistent procedures when initiating a post-seizure hearing or court restitution bill in Chameleon.
documents.authority: Not stated in source document; to be confirmed in review.
documents.scope: The supervisor or designee from whom field services or contracted cities request an itemized bill.
documents.supersedes: None identified
references: DAS Policy 000-01, Chameleon Kennel Window
DAS SOP, Payment Plan Procedure
Riverside County Ordinance Title 6, 630, Section 11

sections.heading: Rules
steps: Initiate a post-seizure or court restitution bill only when requested by Field Services or contract city animal control.

steps.step: Confirm that all Chameleon kennel records are complete and accurate:
sub: The owner ID is the owner in the post-seizure case for all animals involved.
A Chameleon tag/link window is stored for each applicable kennel record.
All treatment records are entered.
The number of animals provided by field services matches the number of impounded animals in the Chameleon kennel window.

steps: Charge all services provided to each animal involved as of the current date, including but not limited to: IMP 1 collection, STATE FINE collection, board collection (all fees due), QT board collection if applicable, rabies vaccination collection if applicable, DA2PPV collection, Bordetella collection, microchip collection, any and all medications provided to each animal, and personnel charges.

sections.heading: Procedure: Impound Fees
steps: Start from the Chameleon kennel window.
Enter the PID in the Person To field.
Perform a search by pressing the F12 function key or the magnifying glass icon.
Perform a thorough check on the animals by matching the animal IDs on the grid with the list provided by animal control.

steps.step: Once all animal IDs are accounted for and the count matches the count provided by animal control, double-click on each animal ID's original impound record, then:
sub: Place the cursor on the kennel tag field.
Bring up the tag/link window by pressing the F4 key. This ensures that a link or microchip record is stored for the owner and animal.
Once the tag/link record is verified, press the Enter key to go back to the kennel window.
Click on the treatment icon (caduceus) to bring up the treatment window.
Verify that all treatments and medications are entered.
Close the treatment window.
Place the cursor on the outcome information Type field and enter RTO.
Press the tab key to move the cursor to the outcome information Subtype field and enter ABANDONMNT.
Press the tab key to move the cursor to the outcome information Date field and press F5 to enter the current date.
Press the tab key to move the cursor to the outcome information By field and enter the first name initial and full last name.
Press the F8 key to update the record.
Repeat these substeps until all impound records have a disposition.

steps.step: On the last animal ID impound, complete the bill:
sub: Press the tab key to move the cursor to the Receipt No field.
Press the F4 key to bring up the receipt window. Chameleon will automatically populate the receipt with item codes of debts owed.
Enter the activity number in the receipt ReferenceNo field.
Leave the receipt Received From field blank.
If field services or animal control indicates that the bill should not go to collection until the court case is resolved, enter FTB HOLD in the receipt Extra2 field.
In the receipt Comment field, enter REQUESTED FOR COURT RESTITUTION/LIEN LETTER.
Change each applicable item code to show collection for each animal ID.

steps.step: Pay attention to the following:
sub: If the owner has paid a portion of the fees, such as owner surrender fees or board fees, animal control or the district attorney may still require a full cost recovery bill minus what the owner has already paid. Make sure to deduct the number of board days already paid for.
Check the receipt window for court restitution transactions that can be applied to the entire bill.
The balance total cannot be more than $99,999.

steps: Prior to storing the bill, verify that all animal IDs are accounted for and all debts are accounted for each animal.
Store the bill by pressing the F9 key.
Store the memo using the Billing template. Refer to the SOP Payment Plans.
Print two receipts. One goes to field services or the requesting staff; the second receipt is submitted with the daily deposit slip at the close of business.
Copy the receipt number.
Go back to the kennel window.
For each animal involved, update the kennel window by removing the outcome information Type, Subtype, and Date.
Paste the receipt number in the Receipt No field.
Press the F8 key to update the kennel window. Repeat for each animal involved.

sections.heading: Procedure: Personnel Charges
steps: The district attorney will sometimes also require personnel charges, for which field services will request a bill. Obtain the following information from field services: number of regular hours, number of overtime hours, names of staff involved in the case, position of staff involved in the case, and, when applicable, the activity performed.

steps.step: Initiate a separate bill:
sub: In the receipt window, enter the PID of the confiscated animals.
Make sure that the receipt jurisdiction matches the person jurisdiction.
Enter the activity number in the receipt ReferenceNo field.
Leave the receipt Received From field blank.
Enter FTB HOLD in the receipt Extra2 field.
In the receipt Comment field, enter REQUESTED FOR COURT RESTITUTION/LIEN LETTER.

steps.step: Enter the charges:
sub: Qty: enter the number of hours for the first officer or staff member.

Item Code: each officer may apply hours to regular hours, additional hours for other activities, and overtime hours. Refer to Title 6, Section 11, for other staff charges. Item codes used are REG HOURS FIELD SVCS CHG at $53.00 and AFTER HOURS PERSONNEL CHG at $104.00.
Extra3: enter the full name of the officer who worked the number of hours.
Extra4: enter the position of the staff member who worked the number of hours, or the activity performed if a second line of hours exists for the same officer.

steps: Prior to storing the receipt, make sure that all hours, charges, activities, positions, and staff information are complete and accurate.
Store the receipt by pressing the F9 key.
Store the memo using the Billing template. Refer to the SOP Payment Plans.
Print two receipts. One goes to field services or the requesting staff; the second receipt is submitted with the daily deposit slip at the close of business.

appendices: Appendix A: Employee acknowledgment form (signature page; retained in employee's personnel file)

documents.title: Processing a Contract Veterinarian Invoice
documents.purpose: To establish consistent procedures for processing contract veterinarian invoices.
documents.authority: Not stated in source document; to be confirmed in review.
documents.scope: Veterinary staff supervisors and managers.
documents.supersedes: None identified

sections.heading: Procedure
steps: Once the invoice has been received, open Chameleon. The No Print Preview option must be unchecked. Click on Reports on the top left side of the menu bar.
Click on Run a Report.
Under VET SERVICES MGMNT REPORTS, click on Spay or Neuter Log.

Enter the begin date and end date covering all days worked on the invoice. For example, if the days worked on the invoice were 8-8-14, 8-22-14, and 8-29-14, the begin date would be 8-8-14 and the end date would be 8-29-14. If only one day of work is on the invoice, that date is both the begin date and the end date.
Click on the name of the veterinarian who treated the animals.
Click on the OK button. This pulls up the entire report.
Print only the last pages that are in chart form.
Initial and date the invoice. An OK-to-process stamp can be used if available.
Either make copies of the invoice and surgery log or scan them into the computer for your records.
Attach the report and invoice with a staple or paperclip and turn them in to the DAS accounts payable section of the fiscal department.

Send an email to the veterinarian stating that the invoice has been turned in for processing. Include the date it was turned in to accounts payable and close the email with this reminder: Please remember that your invoice will be processed in net 30 days from the date of your invoice. Please allow 35 days to receive your check. Thank you for your services.

revisions.author: Outcomes for Pets (draft)
revisions.desc: Migrated into standard RCDAS SOP template. Content unchanged from 2014 version; substantive edits pending (see edit log).

documents.title: Professional and Courteous Conduct
documents.purpose: Place all staff members on notice of the Department's expectations regarding interactions with others. It is a fundamental expectation of all employees that they work cooperatively with one another and that all individuals are treated with dignity and respect.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services; issued by supervisors and managers as a directive memorandum
documents.scope: All Department of Animal Services staff
documents.supersedes: Directive Memorandum, General Directive on Professional and Courteous Conduct (undated, issued under the Director and Chief Veterinarian; individually addressed notice version); Directive Memorandum Kind and Courteous, April 2022
references: Policy 000-60 Diversity Policy for Department of Animal Services

sections.heading: Expected Conduct
steps: Work cooperatively with fellow employees and treat all individuals with dignity and respect.
Conduct yourself in a professional, courteous, responsive, respectful, and civil manner with co-workers, clients, and others in the workplace at all times.
Understand that rude and discourteous behavior is unacceptable and will not be tolerated.

sections.heading: Examples of Inappropriate Behavior
steps: Recognize that inappropriate behavior includes, but is not limited to: glaring at others, rolling eyes in a derisive manner, giving dirty looks, ignoring or snubbing others, failing to respond to civil greetings, making snide comments about or belittling others, berating others, having angry exchanges, gossiping, using profanity, and name calling.

sections.heading: Reporting
steps: Immediately notify your supervisor if you are subjected to or witness this kind of treatment, in order to ensure a harmonious work environment.

sections.heading: Acknowledgment and Retention
steps: Sign the receipt acknowledgment and print your name when the directive is issued.
Keep this memorandum in a convenient place and refer to it whenever necessary to refresh your recollection about expected behavior.
Route copies to the supervisor's file and the DAS Employee Resources Division.

related.name: Receipt acknowledgment (employee signature and printed name)

documents.title: Refund Request City and County
documents.purpose: To establish procedures for processing refund requests for animal license, kennel permit, adoption, and veterinary payments for city and county customers. (Purpose statement reconstructed; original page 1 not legible in source scan.)
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: DAS staff who initiate refund requests and the Refund Clerk who processes them.
documents.supersedes: Policy 000-39, Refund Request City and County (effective date not legible in source scan)
references: County of Riverside Refund Request Form
Form ISS-013
Chart of Accounts
PeopleSoft payment review guidelines

sections.heading: Animal License and Kennel Permit Refunds

steps.step: Determine the customer's jurisdiction. (Note: the opening of this section, including step 1 and substep a, was not captured in the source scan.)
sub: If the customer resides in a contracted city, advise the constituent they will have to contact their city for the refund. If the request was received via mail, the Refund Clerk will forward it to the contracted city.
If the customer resides in unincorporated Riverside County or out of jurisdiction, fill out the County of Riverside Refund Request Form.

steps: Initiate and store a Chameleon Memo prior to printing and submitting the refund request to the Refund Clerk.
From the Tag/Link window, pop up (F4) the Person ID.
Click on the two-documents icon to bring up the Memo window.
Verify the Person ID matches the Memo ID.
In the Memo Type field, enter Accounting.

steps.step: In the Memo Description field, enter the following information:
sub: Date the text was initiated.
The reason for the refund.
The receipt number. If there is more than one pet involved, include the license number.
Amount of refund being requested.
Initials of the employee.
Store (F9) the memo.
Press the Enter key to go back to the Tag window.
Go to the Receipt Number field and pop up (F4) the receipt window.
Print the receipt.
Submit the receipt to the Refund Clerk with the reason for the refund and employee initials written on the original copy.

sections.heading: Animal Adoption Refunds
steps: Process all animal adoption refunds on County of Riverside Refund Request Forms, with the exception of the City of Riverside and City of La Quinta, which receive a daily deposit for revenue collected from their residents.

steps.step: Initiate and store a Chameleon Memo prior to printing and submitting the refund request to the Refund Clerk:
sub: From the Kennel Screen window, pop up (F4) the Person ID in the Outcome Information window.
Type in the customer PID from the receipt and press F12 to search.
Tab to Receipt Number and pop up (F4).
Click on the Memo icon next to the Question Mark icon.
Verify the receipt number and person match the receipt you are processing the refund for, and type ACCOUNTING in the note type.
Follow the Memo Description steps from the Animal License and Kennel Permit Refunds section.

steps.step: Complete the County of Riverside Refund Request Form as follows:
sub: From Division: Department of Animal Services.
RE: Request for refund. Amount $: amount refunding.
Name, Address, City, State, and Zip: complete name, address, city, and zip.
Date payment received: date on receipt.
Permit type and number or type of services: leave blank for DAS Fiscal.
Receipt number: receipt refunding from.
Reason for refund: general reason for refund, for example Return Adoption or License overpayment. Use notes for additional comments.
Requested by: full signature name of employee. Make sure it is legible.
Division: DAS division name issuing the refund.
Send the County Refund Request Form and receipt to the Refund Clerk.

steps.step: Initiate veterinary refunds by completing form ISS-013 as follows:
sub: Deliver to: Department of Animal Services.
Date: current date.
Fiscal Year: current fiscal year.
Address: division budgeted location.
Room number.
Department telephone number.
Department ID: 4200600100 for Vet Services.
Program: as applicable.
Class/Distribution Code: refer to the Chart of Accounts.
Project Grant Number: if you have one assigned.
Requester's name (your name).
Requester's telephone (your direct line).
Approval signature.
Suggested vendor: name of the customer receiving the refund.
Vendor address: customer address and phone number.
Description: Please remit (dollar amount refunding) for attached invoice number (invoice number) dated (date of invoice) for (reason for refunding).
Unit price: the amount you are refunding.
Total price: the total amount refunding.
Account number: 525520, Outside Veterinary Services.
Make a copy of the invoice; it must accompany the copy of the original receipt to show the customer paid for services and to identify the form of payment (cash, check, or credit).
Detach the goldenrod copy of ISS-013, attach a copy of the invoice, and file it for your records to follow up with CHA Procurement and CHA Fiscal.
Attach the original receipt to ISS-013 (white, yellow, and pink copies) and forward to CHA Procurement.

sections.heading: Refund Clerk Duties: Processing Refunds
steps: Verify all information on the Refund Request Form.
If the form is incomplete, return it to the Supervisor to correct.
On the Permit Type and Number or Type of Services line, fill in the correct revenue account codes set by DAS Fiscal to refund monies.
On the Requested By line, sign next to the employee who initiated the request.
Make a copy of the receipt and attach it to the pink copy of the Refund Request Form to retain in DAS files.
Enter the refund in the Chameleon Person ID window by following the Memo steps from the Animal License and Kennel Permit Refunds section.
Attach the white and yellow copies of the County Refund Request Form and the original receipt together.
Send the packet to CHA Fiscal for processing. Allow 8 to 10 weeks.
Review payments by following PeopleSoft guidelines to identify whether payment has been processed.
When payment has been issued by the Auditor-Controller, write the warrant number on the department copy of the Refund Request Form.

related.name: Tag/Link window; Memo window; Kennel Screen; Receipt window
County of Riverside Refund Request Form

appendices: Appendix: Refund form samples, source pages 4 through 8 (attached separately)

documents.title: Replacing a Dog License
documents.purpose: To establish a consistent process for replacing dog licenses as required by the Riverside County Codified Ordinance.
documents.authority: Health Officer or designee (approves the written replacement request).
documents.scope: Staff who process license tag replacement requests.
documents.supersedes: None identified
references: Riverside County Codified Ordinance
P&P General Chameleon Information

sections.heading: Requirements
steps: Obtain a written request from the owner, signed and submitted to the Health Officer or designee, providing the justification for replacement.
Attach the request to Chameleon through the ChamCam twain device.
Verify that the license record being replaced is current.
Collect the replacement cost of six dollars ($6).
Store the new license record (F9).
Update the old license record with a status of REPLACED (F8).
Document the LIC DUP transaction in the receipt window.
Ensure the new license record has the same tag type, tag expiration date, and vaccination expiration date as the old license record.

sections.heading: Replacing the License Tag

steps.step: Go to the Chameleon tag/link window (refer to P&P General Chameleon Information):
sub: Type the entire license number in the tag number field, in the format LYY-######.
Press the F12 key or click on the magnifying glass icon to find the record.
Verify that the correct license record is displayed, the license is not expired, and the vaccination is current.

steps.step: Issue the new tag number:
sub: Type in the new tag number, keeping the LYY prefix the same.
Do not change the tag type, tag price, or tag expiration date.
Press the F5 key or type in the date of the transaction.
Delete the receipt number currently displayed.
Store the record by pressing the F9 function key.
Click on the receipt number field.

Press the F4 key to pop up the receipt window and apply the transaction: double-click on the defaulted license item code; change the item code to LIC DUP; verify the correct fee is charged by clicking on the $ icon; add the item code and fee to the line item of the receipt window by pressing the + icon or Alt+A; click once on the defaulted license item code to highlight the line item; remove the line item from the grid by pressing the - icon; enter the amount paid based on the type of payment; and press the F9 key to store the receipt.
Print the receipt.
Trifold the receipt so the name and address of the owner display through a window envelope.
Staple the new tag to the folded receipt.
Enclose in an envelope and mail to the pet owner.
Press the Enter key to bring the receipt information back to the tag/link window.

steps.step: Process the old tag record:
sub: Using the same records displayed after processing the new tag, double-click on the old tag in the list box.
Change the old tag record status from RENEWED to REPLACED.
Update the record by pressing the F8 function key.

steps: Verify that the list box of the tag/link record shows two license numbers with the same license expiration date: one with a REPLACED status and one with a CURRENT status.

related.name: Letter of Request from Pet Owner (signed)

documents.title: Review and Resolution of Customer Complaints Regarding Fees
documents.purpose: To establish a uniform procedure to ensure that customer complaints regarding fees charged or fees paid are adequately reviewed and documented in the Chameleon animal management system.
documents.authority: Supervisors who handle late fee and penalty disputes; adjustments follow DAS Policy 000-58.
documents.scope: Supervisors who handle late fee and penalty disputes.
documents.supersedes: None identified
references: DAS Policy 000-58, Adjustment of Penalties, Late Fees, and Voiding Administrative Citations
California Code of Regulations, Title 17, Section 2606.4
California Health and Safety Code, Section 121690
Riverside County Codified Ordinance 630.12, Title 6, Section 2(a-i), Mandatory Dog Licensing and Vaccination

sections.heading: Processing Late Fee Adjustments
steps: Upon receipt of a customer complaint via mail, find the license number in the Chameleon tag/link window for which a late fee adjustment is being requested.

steps.step: Assess the situation. Circumstances that may support an adjustment include:
sub: Death in the family (for example, going out of state to attend a funeral).
Problems with the postal service (for example, mail not delivered or mail stolen).
Financial restraints (for example, seniors on fixed income).
Medical reasons (for example, pet owner hospitalized).
Note: licenses that have been late before or adjusted previously are not eligible for an adjustment unless there is a reasonable explanation for being late again.

steps.step: Decide whether the late fee for the late payment of a license will be adjusted:
sub: If the fee is adjusted, advise the owner that the adjustment is a one-time accommodation only.

Create a memo in the Animal ID using the LATE FEE ADJUSTMENT memo template, containing: the date of the conversation, the time of the conversation, the exact name of the person spoken to, the reason for the adjustment of the fee, and your initials.
If the fee is not adjusted, notify the owner verbally or in writing of the reasons why the late fee was not adjusted.
If the owner has more than one pet, the owner can pay one late fee to cover all the pets, one time only.

sections.heading: Processing Letters for Late Fee Disputes
steps: Retrieve the owner or custodian's information from the Chameleon database to become familiar with the facts on record, such as past payment history, number of dogs licensed, and notes on past transactions.

steps.step: If the owner or custodian is requesting an adjustment of a late fee due to lack of notification:
sub: Review the License Notification reports in the Q:\Vet_Services\License_Notification folder for the date range in question to verify whether renewals were mailed.
Bring up the backup file and search by pet license number, owner or custodian's name, or address to find the owner in question using Adobe Reader.

steps.step: If verification of the billing invoice is found:
sub: Notify the owner or custodian, via telephone or in writing, that the late fee is due.
Notify the owner or custodian what the follow-up action may be if the fee is not paid in a timely manner.
Scan a copy of all correspondence received and letter responses sent to the corresponding tag number, and store the appropriate memo documenting each telephone conversation.

steps.step: If verification of the billing invoice is not found and the payment history does not show a prior late payment, the late fee may be adjusted:
sub: Create a memo in the Chameleon Person ID window stating what decision was made regarding the late payment and why.
Enter the date and your name.
Scan a copy of all correspondence received and letter responses sent to the corresponding tag number, and store the appropriate memo documenting each telephone conversation.

related.name: DAS Policy 000-58, Adjustment of Penalties, Late Fees, and Voiding Administrative Citations

appendices: Appendix A: Sample response letters, authority to license and late fee adjustment (attached separately; the source includes two sample letters from the Licensing Support Unit citing licensing and rabies vaccination mandates)

documents.title: Revolving Fund Custodian
documents.purpose: To establish a method of obtaining and/or changing a departmental revolving fund. Revolving funds are issued to key personnel so the department can make change for cash transactions conducted at the front counters and special events.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services; CHA-Fiscal Manager approves fund issuance
documents.scope: This procedure is applicable to all Fiscal - Cash Reporting Unit staff that handle revolving funds for the Department of Animal Services.
documents.supersedes: Policy 000-29, Revolving Fund Custodian, effective October 6, 2009 (revised from April 19, 2007)
references: CHA Cash Handling Manual, Section VII - Change Fund (dated June 17, 2004)

sections.heading: Procedure
steps: Choose key personnel to act as the custodian for individual change funds.

Send an email request to the CHA-Fiscal Manager from the Department of Animal Services Administrative Services Division Director, including the requested custodian's name, department ID, location, the requested amount, and the reason for the request.
Once approved by the CHA-Fiscal Manager, an email will be routed containing a Revolving Fund Custodian Form (Appendix A) for signatures.
Once signatures are obtained from the requested custodian and the Administrative Services Division Director, deliver the original form to the CHA-Fiscal Manager.
Once the CHA-Fiscal Manager receives the authorization, they will issue the funds to the custodian.
The custodian will follow the guidelines outlined in Section VII - Change Fund (dated June 17, 2004) of the CHA Cash Handling Manual.

appendices: Appendix A: Revolving Fund Custodian Form (attached separately)

revisions.author: Outcomes for Pets (draft)
revisions.desc: Migrated into standard RCDAS SOP template. Content unchanged from 2009 version; substantive edits pending (see edit log).

documents.title: Scan It - Automated Exam and Treatment Entry in Chameleon
documents.purpose: To provide an automated data entry process that creates standardization and expeditiously enters information into the Chameleon software.
documents.authority: Veterinary Services
documents.scope: Veterinary services staff using the automated exam types within Chameleon. For the CVAC shot clinic, use scripts that start with T only. Initial exam types can be used at all locations.
documents.supersedes: Vet Services Scan It SOP, effective May 23, 2022

definitions.def: The desktop scripting tool used to run automated exam and treatment entries in Chameleon.

sections.heading: Launching an Automated Treatment Record
steps: Double click on the Scan It desktop icon.
Launch Chameleon.
Click on the caduceus to open the treatment window.
Click on the arrow in the treatment window.
Right mouse click on the Scan It icon.
Choose Scripts.
Click on the applicable script.
Enter the Animal ID in the treatment window.
Confirm the selected script is running.
Press F2.

steps.step: The Initial Exam script will enter the following automatically:
sub: Initial exam in the Visit Type field.
Condition will be BAR.
Treated By will pop up; enter your user ID and press Enter.
Patient weight.
Description: Scan Negative, appears healthy at this time. You are able to go back and edit the description once stored.
For dogs, enters DA2PPV, BORD, and Vectra for you.

documents.title: School and Community Presentations
documents.purpose: To establish a universal protocol for conducting a school room or community presentation.
documents.authority: Riverside County Department of Animal Services
documents.scope: All staff and volunteers who conduct school or community presentations on behalf of the Department.
documents.supersedes: School/Community Presentation SOP, effective October 20, 2009

steps.step: Gather the following items and information in advance of the presentation:
sub: Get the address, date, time, and a contact number to confirm the presentation.
Know the age group of your target audience so you can prepare an age appropriate message.
Find out how many people will be attending, and pack extra materials in case more show up (5 to 10 extras).
Choose a lesson plan and rehearse what you are going to present so you will feel confident in your delivery and know how long your presentation will take.
Pack educational materials and visual aids appropriate to the presentation.
Pack incentive items.
Check equipment to make sure it is working properly, such as the laptop computer and projector if doing a PowerPoint presentation.
Have a backup plan if equipment fails: print out copies of the presentation and an outline for yourself.
If using PowerPoint, save it on the computer desktop and bring it on a disk or USB drive for backup.
Check videos on the computer to ensure they play properly and that speakers work and are loud enough.
Bring a camera to document outreach if possible.

sections.heading: Set Up
steps: Arrive early to find the presentation site, check in on time, and set up equipment.

sections.heading: If Using Live Animals in a Presentation
steps: Be sure all animals are in top physical condition and in the best of health. Make sure they are accustomed to being handled, are not fearful, and pose no risk to the public if the public may potentially come into direct contact with them.
Pay attention to the animals' behavior to ensure they are not being stressed, scared, or agitated.

If the public can come into physical contact with the animals, be sure a hand sanitizer is available for them to use. This is especially important when using reptiles and when around small children prone to putting their hands in their mouths. Salmonella is a fecal/oral transmission; the bacteria must be ingested from contaminated feces, not from simply touching or being close to a reptile.
If presenting to a large group, where it may be difficult to control the situation, it is best to not allow the public to handle or touch the animals.

For young students, stress upon them the importance of handling all animals in a quiet, gentle manner. Do not take animals out until students are in their proper places and quiet. Demonstrate how to properly touch and stroke an animal using only two fingers.
Bring necessary supplies for animal care and cleanup (water, food, shade, paper towels, etc.).
Make sure animals are transported in safe, secure containers or carriers.

sections.heading: Presentation Techniques
steps: Rehearse your presentation so that you are confident and knowledgeable about the subject being presented.
Stay on time and leave time for questions.
Make eye contact.
Be animated.
Encourage participation from students.
Use a confident, friendly voice.
Ask questions to engage the students.
Use props and visual aids.
Conclude by summarizing and emphasizing the main points.
Leave behind information and materials teachers and students can use if they want to know more.
Do not distribute materials until they are ready to be used so that they will not distract the students from your presentation.
If you are not sure about the answer to a question, refer them to someone who does know, get back to them, or say you do not know.

sections.heading: Clean Up
steps: Clean up any messes made.
Collect any extra handouts.
Double check to make sure you take all of your visual aids, equipment, and other items back with you.
Thank everyone involved.

appendices: Appendix A: Presentation Checklist

revisions.author: Outcomes for Pets (draft)
revisions.desc: Migrated into standard RCDAS SOP template. Content unchanged from 2009 version; substantive edits pending (see edit log).

documents.title: Special Surgeries and Additional Medical Treatment
documents.purpose: To establish the approval and scheduling process used when determining if an animal will receive special surgery or additional medical treatment past mandated holds.
documents.authority: SRVT (Senior Registered Veterinary Technician), Operations Chief, Chief Veterinarian, or designee.
documents.scope: All animals receiving additional care beyond mandated holds.
documents.supersedes: Special Surgeries SOP (flow chart), effective October 12, 2022

sections.heading: Approval Requirements
steps: Obtain written approval for any special surgery or additional treatment past mandated holds from the SRVT, Operations Chief, Chief Veterinarian, or designee.
Document the approval in the Chameleon database, including the medical note and the approving party.
Follow this process for all animals receiving additional care beyond mandated holds.

steps.step: The SRVT, Operations Chief, Chief Veterinarian, or designee will look at the acuity schedule to determine when surgery will be performed, considering:
sub: A willing surgeon on staff that day.
The animal's behavior and ease of rehab.
An exit plan for the animal.
Time and resources (being able to fit the surgery in without bumping too many spays and neuters that day).

sections.heading: Exit Plan
steps: Confirm the animal has an identifiable exit from the shelter system, immediately to a rescue or foster, since the rehab can be very time consuming for the medical department to do in house.

revisions.author: Outcomes for Pets (draft)
revisions.desc: Migrated into standard RCDAS SOP template. Content unchanged from 2022 version; substantive edits pending (see edit log).

documents.title: System Access Requests Through RIVCOHelp ServiceNow
documents.purpose: To provide steps for submitting system access requests electronically through the RIVCOHelp ServiceNow system. Animal Services is no longer required to complete a paper SAR (System Access Request) form for new employees needing systems access, changes to a user's access, or deletion of an employee's access.
documents.authority: RCIT Help Desk processes submitted requests; department supervisors and managers submit requests.
documents.scope: Staff who submit system access requests for new employees, changes to a user's access, or termination of an employee's access.
documents.supersedes: RIVCOHelp ServiceNow Systems Access Request Tip Sheet (undated); paper SAR (System Access Request) form process

definitions.def: System Access Request.
The County of Riverside's electronic help desk and request system, accessible from the County of Riverside intranet page.

sections.heading: Submitting a Systems Access Request
steps: Open the RIVCOHelp ServiceNow application to open a new request. You can access RIVCOHelp from the County of Riverside intranet page.
If you have signed into ServiceNow previously, select your account from the menu. If you have not, select Use another account and enter your County of Riverside email address and your password.
From your Self-Service Portal homepage, select Make a Request to submit a new Systems Access Request to the RCIT Help Desk.
Click on the graphic labeled Computer Access, Accounts and Password to start a new Systems Access Request.
Select the Employee Request option to Add/Move/Update/Terminate a systems access request.

steps.step: Answer the series of questions to determine which type of request you are submitting; the answers direct you to the appropriate form:
sub: Select whether you would like to Add, Move/Update, or Terminate an employee's access.
Select if your request is for 2 or more employees.
Answer the Additional Items needed question. This ensures that the appropriate tasks are assigned properly within RCIT or within your department.

steps: Complete the information regarding the employee, filling in any required fields indicated by a red asterisk. The form will reflect the type of request you initially selected (Add, Move/Update, or Delete).

In the Please describe your request field, enter the specifics of your system access request with as much detail as possible. This ensures the RCIT Help Desk creates the appropriate tasks to get the request completed.
Once you have completed all required fields for your request, scroll back to the top of the screen and click the Submit button on the right-hand side of the screen.

sections.heading: Reporting Issues and Requesting Assistance
steps: If you experience any difficulties with entering a new request, contact the RCIT Help Desk for assistance at (951) 955-9900.

revisions.author: Outcomes for Pets (draft)
revisions.desc: Migrated into standard RCDAS SOP template. Content unchanged from source tip sheet; substantive edits pending (see edit log).

documents.title: Use of Panic Buttons
documents.purpose: To provide instructions for the use of panic buttons installed at shelter locations.
documents.authority: Approved by the Director of Animal Services. Any staff member has authorization to use the panic buttons if there is an imminent threat and need to alert others.
documents.scope: All staff at shelter locations equipped with panic buttons.
documents.supersedes: Use of "Panic Buttons" SOP, effective July 16, 2021
references: 000-53 Calling 911 for Emergencies When Needed (Non-Worker's Compensation Related Claims)

sections.heading: General Use
steps: Use the panic buttons to covertly alert other staff of an issue that requires immediate assistance and response from other staff.
Activate the panic buttons if staff, customers, or animals are threatened, to bring attention and get a response from others to the area in need of assistance.
If the panic button is activated accidentally, return it to its original position to de-activate the silent alarm.

sections.heading: Response Responsibilities
steps: Supervisors and staff working in areas where the panic buttons are located are responsible for being familiar with their exact locations.
All working staff have a responsibility to respond to the silent alarms when activated.
If there is an active threat needing immediate attention, call 9-1-1 to report it. All staff members have the authority to call 9-1-1 to report serious threats.

campusVariations.campus: Western Riverside County/City Animal Shelter (Jurupa Valley)
campusVariations.variation: Panic buttons are installed in three reception areas: underneath the counters of the kiosk (Reception) inside the main entrance, the animal turn in (Admission) counter, and the Clinic front counter. The buttons activate a silent alarm consisting of a strobe light with three indicator lights. There are three strobe alarms, located in Receiving, the ACO dispatch office, and the executive suite. When activated, all three locations will be in alarm mode with the strobe flashing and the LED light lit, indicating where the call is being made from. The color code is: Green for Reception, Red for Admission, Blue for Clinic.

Two panic buttons are located in the Main Lobby reception counter and in the Adoption Partner office. The panic buttons activate a blue strobe located in the receiving area. When activated, staff are required to respond to the front lobby counter or the adoption partner office.

revisions.author: Outcomes for Pets (draft)
revisions.desc: Migrated into standard RCDAS SOP template. Content unchanged from 2021 version; substantive edits pending (see edit log).

documents.searchText: to provide instructions for the use of panic buttons installed at shelter locations. all staff at shelter locations equipped with panic buttons. approved by the director of animal services. any staff member has authorization to use the panic buttons if there is an imminent threat and need to alert others. general use use the panic buttons to covertly alert other staff of an issue that requires immediate assistance and response from other staff. activate the panic buttons if staff, customers, or animals are threatened, to bring attention and get a response from others to the area in need of assistance. if the panic button is activated accidentally, return it to its original position to de-activate the silent alarm. response responsibilities supervisors and staff working in areas where the panic buttons are located are responsible for being familiar with their exact locations. all working staff have a responsibility to respond to the silent alarms when activated. if there is an active threat needing immediate attention, call 9-1-1 to report it. all staff members have the authority to call 9-1-1 to report serious threats. 000-53 calling 911 for emergencies when needed (non-worker's compensation related claims)

documents.title: Volunteer Guidelines - Coachella Valley and San Jacinto Valley Animal Campuses
documents.purpose: To establish department wide uniformity regarding the procedures to effectively assist as volunteers following the processes as established for each shelter location.
documents.authority: Volunteer Services Manager and Volunteer Services Coordinator
documents.scope: All volunteers supporting Animal Services at the Coachella Valley Animal Campus (Thousand Palms) and the San Jacinto Valley Animal Campus.
documents.supersedes: Volunteer Guidelines for Supporting Animal Services at the Coachella Valley Animal Campus (CVAC), effective July 11, 2019; Volunteer Guidelines for Supporting Animal Services at the San Jacinto Valley Animal Campus, effective July 11, 2019 (absorbed)

definitions.def: Animal Care Technician.

Purple: animal is already spayed/neutered and may go home the same day. White: animal will need to stay for spay/neuter surgery. Yellow: kittens that may be socialized; for dogs, puppies that may not be socialized or removed from their kennels by volunteers (staff cares for puppies as their immune system is not fully developed).
Handwritten kennel card notes indicating the dog may not be pulled; immediately see an Animal Care Technician.
Dead on Arrival book.

sections.heading: Sign In
steps: Before starting any volunteer task, sign in using the iPad, using the unique PIN given to you by the Volunteer Services Coordinator. At CVAC the iPad is located behind front desk staff, attached to the wall; once signed in, go to the volunteer office and put on a volunteer apron and name badge.
If you cannot make it to your scheduled day, contact the Volunteer Coordinator so he or she is aware and can find coverage.

sections.heading: Kiosk Greeter

steps.step: Prepare the greeting station:
sub: The station should be clean and free of clutter. Inspect the informational turnstile to be neat and organized, stocked with current informational pages, and discard expired or old informational sheets.
Stock 5 to 6 leashes, 2 to 3 clean blue cat boxes, and 5 to 6 large towels.
Check for any specific instructions left for the day, such as interview schedules or clinic supplies to be assembled.

steps: Greet the public: smile and welcome every visitor as they enter the facility. Ask and offer if you can help them. Always remain polite and courteous, even to the most disgruntled visitor. Should you need assistance with anything or anyone, ask an employee.

Citations/tickets, animal licensing, or bills: instruct the public to take a number from the red numbering dispenser located to the right of the front counter and then to take a seat. Explain that one of the staff will call them up by number.

Dropping off stray animals: instruct the public to take a number from the red numbering dispenser and take a seat. If the animal is not properly secured with a leash (dogs) or a cage (cats), provide a leash or blue cat box as required. No animals should be allowed to run free while on property.
Dead animals: if the animal is exposed (not covered or concealed), use a towel to cover it up.

steps.step: Looking for a lost animal: ask the person if they have already been contacted by the shelter stating their animal was in our custody.
sub: Yes: instruct them to take a number from the red numbering dispenser and take a seat; staff will call them up by number.

No: direct them into adoptions to look for their animal. Explain the location of the Adoption Gallery, Kennel A, Kennel B, Puppy Room, Cat Room, and the bulletin boards in the hallway between kennels.

steps: Adoptions: for dog or cat adoptions, direct the public to go through the double doors into the Adoption Gallery.
DOA (Dead on Arrival) book: the book is located to the far left of the front receptionist station.
Restrooms: located to the far left of the front receptionist station next to the water fountains.
Volunteer interest: direct the customer to the website at rcdas.org to fill out an application.
Rescue: if a rescue organization is there to pick up an animal, contact the Rescue Coordinator. If the Rescue Coordinator is unavailable, ask an Animal Care Technician to assist them.

sections.heading: Animal Socialization - Cats
steps: Wash hands in between handling cats and kittens.
Do an initial check of all the cat kennels.

steps.step: If any of the cat food or water dishes are empty, refill them:
sub: To refill the water bowl, place the bowl under the blue water container located on the counter and refill.
If the bowls are messy or dirty, get a new bowl out of the cabinet.
Do not put the cat food bowl into the storage bin. Always use the food scooper located in the bucket under the storage bin. Only fill the food bowl halfway.

steps: Remove and replace any dirty blankets or toys. Place dirty items in designated areas identified by Animal Care Technicians.

Check the litter pans and clean litter if needed. If the litter pan is very dirty, retrieve a new pan, fill it with litter, and place it back into the proper kennel. Perform tasks one cage at a time so you do not mix litter pans belonging to a different cat, and wash hands between each cage.

When socializing with cats, only approach the friendly ones. Always socialize the kittens first before you socialize any adult cats. Kittens under the age of 5 months may not be handled or socialized (subject to change due to illnesses; check with Animal Care Technicians).
If you notice a cat has any health or behavior issues, notify an Animal Care Technician (ACT).
Help keep the counters, floors, and other surfaces clean and safe for yourself and everyone else.
Return tools to their proper locations before you leave your area.
Log your name into the cat socialization binder, identifying which cat you socialized with by animal ID number and kennel number.

sections.heading: Visitation and Adoption - Cats
steps: Smile and welcome every visitor as they enter each animal pod. Ask if you can help or assist them. Always be polite and courteous, even to the most disgruntled visitor. Should you need assistance with anything or anyone, ask an employee.

If a customer expresses interest in visiting with a cat, ask if they are interested in adopting. If not, explain that we only pull animals for prospective adopters, to ensure that animals are always available for potential adopters.

steps.step: Prepare to pull the cat for possible adoption:
sub: Verify the cat's age. The cat must be 5 months of age or older; if younger than 5 months, call an ACT to assist with the adoption.
Availability: the review date on the kennel card must be the current date or in the past.

Behavior status: the status at the top of the kennel card must not indicate OBSERVED. If OBSERVED is identified, call for an Animal Care Technician. Animals with an Under Vet Care sign may not be handled or pulled.
Place an Out for Visitation sign on the outside of the kennel.
Put the cat in one of the sanitized blue cat crates to transfer to the Visitation Room. Never transfer a cat without using a blue crate, and never use cardboard boxes to transport cats.

steps: Wait until everyone in the customer's group is in the Visitation Room and the door is completely shut before you let the cat out of the blue crate.
Never leave the customers unattended with the cat.
Never give medical or behavioral advice to the customer.
Use your best judgment if the customers who want to see the cat have a large group with them. Only allow 2 to 4 people in with the cat at a time.
At the end of each visitation, place the cat back into the blue crate before opening the door.

steps.step: Ask if they would like to adopt:
sub: No: thank the public for coming in, place the cat back into its kennel, and log the visitation in the cat binder.

Yes: have the adopter fill out adoption paperwork. While the adopter is filling out paperwork, return the cat to its kennel and retrieve the kennel card. After the adopter completes the paperwork, ask the ACT to sign off. Take the adopter and completed paperwork to the lobby, instruct the adopter to have a seat, and explain they will be called up by name. Congratulate the adopter.

steps.step: Spay and neuter check:
sub: Cat is already spayed/neutered: set paperwork in the adoption rack; take the cat to receiving for a final check, microchip, and/or vaccinations. The cat is taken to the new owners in the lobby in a carton box after the adoption is paid for.

Cat is not spayed/neutered: assign the cat to the next opening in the spay/neuter log and indicate the date on the adoption paperwork. Take the cat to receiving for a final check, microchip, and/or vaccinations. Return the cat to its original kennel with an updated kennel card indicating adopted. Log the adoption in the cat binder.

steps: Place the dirty blue crate in the dish room. Never use a dirty crate.

sections.heading: Cat and Dog Photography
steps: Wash hands in between handling animals. Have a pen and notepad on hand. Always return borrowed supplies to the Volunteer Office, and notify the Volunteer Coordinator staffing if supplies are running low.
Select the animals you would like to photograph. Ensure the kennel card says adoptable and not treatable; treatable cats and dogs may only be photographed within their kennel.

Litters of kittens may be transported within the same blue crate and may be photographed individually only in the socialization/visitation room. Unlock the kennel door, make a note of which kennel the cat came from, transport the cat using a sanitized blue crate, and be aware of any sudden changes in the cat's body language or behavior.

Dress-up: you may use the items found in the photography room to dress up the animal, and you can brush the pet using a brush found in the grooming area. Use of the photo signs found in the photography room is optional.
Photograph the animal and log the animal into the photography log.
Return the animal to its original kennel, referring to your note of which kennel the animal came from.
Once complete, notify Volunteer Coordinator staff to edit, upload, and print new kennel cards with the new photos. Allow time for completion of this task.

sections.heading: Animal Socialization - Dogs
steps: Check out the dog kennel key from the front reception kiosk desk.

steps.step: Before removing any dog, have the following supplies:
sub: Dog leash (inspect it for damage).
Animal waste bag.
Out for Walk sign to be placed on kennels notifying staff and public of the animal's absence.
A pen and notepad are highly recommended. Always return borrowed supplies to the Volunteer Office, and inform the Volunteer Coordinator if supplies are running low.

steps.step: Before pulling any dog, review the kennel card:
sub: Only animals with walk cards are available for pulling.
Age: the dog must be 6 months of age or older.
Availability: the review date on the kennel card must be the current date or in the past.
Behavior status: the status at the top of the kennel card must not indicate OBSERVED, even if the kennel card is purple.

If multiple dogs are within a single kennel, none of the dogs may be pulled. If you have any questions regarding the ability to pull an animal, see an Animal Care Technician or the Volunteer Coordinator.
Dogs may only be pulled from the inside kennel door, never the outside.
Animals with an Under Vet Care sign may not be handled or pulled.

steps.step: Return the dog to its original kennel:
sub: Double check the kennel card to make sure the dog is going into the right kennel, referring back to your note.
If there are any new dogs in the kennel not present when the dog was originally pulled, stop and ask an Animal Care Technician before re-kenneling the dog.
If the dog has other dogs in its kennel, use the guillotine door to separate them before you put the dog in. After you put the dog in and secure the door, reopen the guillotine door.
Remove the Out for a Walk sign.

Log the socialization on the walk card, including any observations regarding the dog's behavior while being socialized (for example: dog pulls on leash, dog does not pull on leash, dog will not walk on leash).

steps: When describing an animal's behavior, keep descriptions simple but clear. Avoid words or descriptions that are vague or could be interpreted differently by different people. Do not use descriptions like "Dog is sweet." Explain the behavior observed, for example: "Dog has a very relaxed body and immediately approached me with a low wagging tail." If unsure about how to describe a dog's behavior, ask an Animal Care Technician.

sections.heading: Visitation and Adoption - Dogs
steps: Smile and welcome every visitor as they enter each animal pod. Ask if you can help or assist them. Always be polite and courteous, even to the most disgruntled visitor. Should you need assistance with anything or anyone, ask an employee.

If a customer expresses interest in visiting with a dog, ask if they are interested in adopting. If not, explain that we only pull animals for prospective adopters, to ensure that animals are always available for potential adopters.

steps.step: Before pulling any dog, review the kennel card:
sub: Age: the dog must be 6 months of age or older.
Availability: the review date on the kennel card must be the current date or in the past.
Behavior status: if the status at the top of the kennel card indicates OBSERVED, do not pull the dog. Immediately see an Animal Care Technician.
Any handwritten notes such as A.A., F.A., or N.O.D.: do not pull the dog. Immediately see an Animal Care Technician.
If multiple dogs are within a single kennel, none of the dogs may be pulled.
Animals with an Under Vet Care sign may not be handled or pulled.

steps: Place an Out for Visitation sign on the outside of the kennel.

Get the leash ready before you unlock the door. If there is more than one dog, separate the other dogs from the one you want using the guillotine door. If you need help, ask a staff member or another volunteer.
Unlock the kennel door, leash up the dog, and always make sure to close and lock the kennel door.
Only walk dogs in designated walking areas, and always pick up your dog's waste.
Never do a Meet and Greet with any of the shelter dogs or the public's dog. If a potential adopter is requesting a Meet and Greet, notify an Animal Care Technician.
Keep at least a 6 to 10 foot distance between all dogs, and be aware of any sudden changes in the dog's body language or behavior.
Escort the dog and potential adopters to the Get Acquainted Room or outside play yards.
Never give out medical or behavioral advice. If you do not know the answer to a question, refer them to an Animal Care Technician.
Do not hand over the leash to a potential adopter. Dogs may only be walked by the potential adopter within the confines of the play yards, never outside of them.

steps.step: At the conclusion of the visitation, ask if they would like to adopt:
sub: No: thank the public for coming in. Place the dog back into its kennel, double checking the kennel card to make sure the dog is going into the right kennel. If there are any new dogs in the kennel, stop and ask staff before re-kenneling. If the dog has kennel mates, use the guillotine door to separate them before putting the dog in, then reopen the guillotine door. Log the visitation on the walk card.

Yes: have the adopter fill out adoption paperwork. While the adopter is filling out paperwork, return the dog to its original kennel and retrieve the kennel card. After the adopter completes the paperwork, ask the Animal Care Technician to sign off. Take the adopter and completed paperwork to the lobby, instruct the adopter to have a seat, and explain they will be called up by name. Congratulate the adopter.

steps.step: Spay and neuter:
sub: Dog is spayed/neutered: set the paperwork in the adoption rack and take the dog to receiving for a final check, microchip, and/or vaccinations. The dog is taken to the new owners in the lobby after the adoption is paid for.

Dog is not spayed/neutered: assign the dog to the next opening in the spay/neuter log and indicate the date on the adoption paperwork. Bathe the dog per the Brushing and Bathing instructions. Take the dog to receiving for a final check, microchip, and/or vaccinations. Return the dog to its original kennel and log the adoption on the walk card.

sections.heading: Brushing and Bathing - Dogs
steps: Check the grooming room for required supplies needed to bathe or groom a dog: towels, shampoo, and brushes/combs.
Make sure the wash tub and kennels in the grooming room have been sanitized and wiped clean prior to use.

steps.step: Select the dog to be washed and review the kennel card:
sub: Only dogs that are adoptable can be washed.

No badly matted dogs are to be selected unless you are a professional groomer recognized by our department. If you are a professional groomer, talk to the Volunteer Services Manager or Volunteer Coordinator before you begin.
Check with staff to see if there are any dogs that need to be bathed before they are scheduled for surgery.
If a dog has been recently spayed or neutered, always ask an Animal Care Technician to check the surgery date to make sure the dog is ready to bathe.
Make sure you can handle the dog you are going to bathe or groom. Get help if you need it and always work with another volunteer or staff person.

steps: Place an Out for a Bath sign on the kennel and write down the animal ID and kennel number before taking the dog out of the kennel.

Get the leash ready before you unlock the door. If there is more than one dog, separate the other dogs from the one you want using the guillotine door, asking for help if needed. Unlock the kennel door, leash up the dog, and always close and lock the kennel door.
Walk or carry the dog to the grooming room. Do not carry a dog that is too heavy for you; always request help. Only walk dogs in designated walking areas and always pick up your dog's waste.

Never do a meet and greet with any of the shelter dogs or the public's dogs. Keep at least a 6 to 10 foot distance between all dogs and be aware of any sudden changes in the dog's body language or behavior.
Walk the dog up the ramp into the tub or pick it up and place it inside the tub, then place the tub's harness around the dog's neck to keep it inside the tub.

steps.step: Adjust the water temperature for the dog and bathe:
sub: Test the water temperature prior to using it on the dog to ensure it is not too hot or cold.
Begin washing from the top of the dog's head down to the tail.
Make sure not to get water or shampoo in the dog's eyes, mouth, nose, or ears.
Rinse thoroughly from top to bottom.
Wrap the dog in a towel and transfer it to the drying table, securing it with the harness. If the dog is too big for the table, place the dog on the floor and secure it to the table with a leash.
Turn on the blow dryer and watch the dog's reaction. If the reaction is not positive, proceed with towel drying; if the reaction is positive, proceed with blow drying.

steps: Return the dog to its kennel, double checking the kennel card and following the standard re-kenneling steps (check for new dogs, use the guillotine door, then reopen it).
Remove the Out for Bath sign and log the bath on the walk card, including any observations regarding the dog's behavior while being bathed, using simple but clear descriptions.
Go back to the grooming room and clean and disinfect everything that was used (tub, brushes, floor, etc.). All used towels must be placed inside the dirty bin in the laundry room.

sections.heading: Events
steps: Onsite shelter events: check in at the designated check-in area at your scheduled time. Ask at check-in what your task will be. If you do not feel comfortable doing a certain task, tell staff immediately. Do the task you have been given, and report any problems to a staff member.

Offsite shelter events: follow the same check-in and task steps as onsite events. It is especially important that you always tell a lead staff member where you are going before you leave offsite events.
Remember to be respectful while volunteering, as you are representing the Riverside County Department of Animal Services.

steps.step: Washer:
sub: Put on gloves. It is extremely important that gloves are worn, as you will be working with various germs and diseases.
Throw out bloody items or items that are heavily soiled.
Never wash collars, leashes, or metal tags.
Load the washer until three-quarters full. Do not overload, and do not run when the load is less than two-thirds full.
Large beds, heavy blankets, and pillows: only half full. The washer will stop if the load is too heavy.
Close and secure the door, ensuring nothing is pinched in the door seal.
The washer should be set to Heavy Soil - Hot.
Press the red Start button.

steps.step: Dryer:
sub: First, check the lint trap on the bottom of the dryer and clean it out.
Put washed items into the dryer, untangling items when possible. No plastic toys, no cat beds, and no large dog beds.
The dryer should be set for 60 minutes, 15 minutes cool, high heat.
Push the green button.
Pillows and some thick items must be dried twice.
Fold items and put them on shelves. Only clean items should be placed on the table, shelves, and in cubbies.

sections.heading: Clerical Duties
steps: General office duties include but are not limited to filing, typing/data entry, and answering telephones, as needed and directed by the Volunteer Services Manager.

sections.heading: Dishes
steps: Wear gloves.
Wipe litter out of litter pans with paper towels.
Using a small clean bucket, mix a 50/50 solution of Triple Two and water.
Using the green Triple Two solution in the bucket, use a sponge to wipe out food bowls, litter pans, and similar items.

Once they have been soaked and cleaned out, insert dishes into the commercial dishwasher, which will steam off any germs that may not be visible. Press the only button on the right-hand side to start the steaming process, which takes about 30 seconds. Allow the dishes to get up to a minimum temperature of 140 degrees.

Carefully remove the sanitized dishes from the commercial washer; they may be hot. Place a towel on the counter to allow them to dry, or hand dry them using a clean towel. Once the dishes are dry, place them on the shelf and neatly organize them.
Ensure that the dish room is clean and that any excess water on the floor or counter has been wiped down before leaving.

sections.heading: Petco Cattery Care (La Quinta and Indio)
steps: Enter through the door next to grooming, then make a left into the cattery door. Quietly close the doors when coming in and out, because the noise could scare the dogs being groomed.
Sign in and grab the key hanging near the sign-in log.
If there are soiled towels in the kennels, remove them and place them in a bag near the laundry area, or bring the soiled laundry back to the shelter to be washed.
Cleaning supplies are in a plastic container on the bottom left.
Remove the cat from the kennel you wish to clean by kenneling him or her or using the divider to section the cat off.
Spray all of the kennels with disinfectant and wipe them thoroughly using paper towels, including the top of the kennel and the glass on the inside and outside.
Place clean towels and cat beds into the kennels.
After emptying all litter, wipe the pans out with disinfectant. Ensure the pans have been fully sterilized and are dry before refilling them with litter.
Sanitize food and water bowls near the sink section, which is near the fish and has filtered water for the cats.
Fill the cat water bowls with filtered water and fill the food bowls with food found in the bottom left cupboard.
Ensure that cats are divided and closed off from different shelter cats unless they are kennel mates.
Place trash in the red trash can located in the back of the store through the grey double doors, and place a clean bag into the trash in the cattery.

Ensure that the water, food, and litter containers are filled prior to signing out. Fill water near the fish section. For litter, use the litter station in the back of the store. For food and all other supplies, ask a manager.
You may socialize the cats within the room to help make them more adoptable.
Answer any customer questions and show cats available for adoption.
Sign out on the log.

sections.heading: Clinic Cage Cleaning (San Jacinto Valley Animal Campus)
steps: Be prepared with the following supplies: gloves, disinfecting spray, paper towels, scrubbing pad, trashcan, and newspaper and shredded paper (when available).
Place a pair of gloves on each hand.
Empty the cage of any solid waste and newspapers.
If there is a towel or blanket in the cage, remove any solid waste and then place it into the dirty laundry basket.
Use newspaper and/or paper towels to soak up any urine or water in the kennel.

Spray all five sides of the cage liberally with disinfectant, and spray the cage door liberally with disinfectant. If there is more than one cage to be cleaned, you can set up the other cages by following the same steps.

After soaking the cages, wipe the cages down with gloved hands using the scrubbing pad, paper towels, and more spray if needed. Wipe down the cage doors, paying particular attention to all of the crevices where the metal meets, where mucus, urine, and feces can build up. Change gloves as often as needed. If needed, the cage door can be removed for cleaning; see a Veterinary Assistant or Registered Veterinary Technician for assistance.
When the cage is dry, place a layer of newspaper at least 5 sheets thick on the entire bottom of the cage. In double cages you can use more newspaper, as bigger animals make bigger messes.
Bring all of the dirty laundry to the outside laundry room, located across from the Volunteer Office; the door is labeled Grooming.
Bring back the same equivalent of items you dropped off. For example, if you dropped off 10 dirty towels, bring back 10 clean towels.

sections.heading: Livestock and Barn (San Jacinto Valley Animal Campus, Experienced Volunteers Only)
steps: Schedule dates and times with the Volunteer Coordinator.
Check in at the Volunteer Office when you arrive, and check in with staff working in the barn during the time you are volunteering. Staff will instruct you on what needs to be accomplished.

Review the livestock white board on the tack room door to see what animals are present and which animals can be interacted with. Some animals are restricted to staff only; staff will inform you of these animals when you arrive.

Feeding barn animals: information is posted on the white board. Staff will organize feed and supplements prior to feeding and will instruct you on what to feed and the quantities required for the individual animals.
Prior to entering stalls or cages, observe the animals' behavior. If the animals appear to be agitated or acting aggressively, do not enter the stall; ask staff for assistance.

Entering stalls: be aware of the location of the animal, open the cage or gate slowly, open the gate just enough so that you can fit through the door but not so much that the animal can fit through, and close the door behind you.

Assisting with cleaning stalls: enter the stall following the steps above, attain proper tools for mucking stalls (manure fork, manure bucket), slide the manure bucket under the stall rails away from the stall door, use a manure fork to pick up manure and place it in the large manure buckets, slide the bucket under the stall rail after removing manure, and exit the stall safely to dump the manure. If the bucket is heavy, ask for help to dump it.

steps.step: Grooming barn animals (only after cleaning is finished), using the tools kept in the tack room:
sub: Use a rubber curry comb to remove dirt and debris from the coat, in a circular motion on fleshy areas of the body. Do not use this on the face or below the knee.
Use a hard brush to remove the dirt and debris that was loosened from the coat. The hard brush can be used on fleshy areas and down the legs, but not on the face.
Use a soft brush to remove remaining dirt and debris on the top of the coat. This brush can be used on the face and sensitive areas of the body.
Use a moist rag to wipe around the eyes and nose if the horse allows it.
Place all tools in their designated areas when you are finished for the day.

sections.heading: Petco Cattery Care (Hemet and Beaumont)
steps: Petco Hemet: enter through the door next to grooming, then make a left into the cattery door, closing doors quietly. Sign in and grab the key hanging near the sign-in log. Follow the same cattery cleaning, feeding, watering, trash, and socialization steps as the La Quinta and Indio locations. Cleaning supplies are in the cupboard on the bottom left.

Petco Beaumont: as you enter Petco, the cattery is located to your right. Cleaning supplies are located at the back of the store on a black cart; everything you need will be on the cart. The sign-in sheet is placed inside the drawer; sign in and ask for the kennel key at the register. If towels are soiled, remove them and place them by the washer located in the grooming room; if nothing is inside the washer, put soiled towels and blankets inside to wash. Follow the same cattery cleaning, feeding, watering, trash, and socialization steps. Sanitize food and water bowls in the grooming tub. If you run out of litter, use the litter station in the back of the store; for food and all other supplies, ask a manager.

campusVariations.variation: Sign-in iPad is located in the volunteer office, attached to the wall. Kiosk greeters redirect citations/tickets, licensing, bills, stray animal drop-offs, and dead animals out the front door and immediately right into the Admissions Department; the DOA book is available to customers upon request. For lost pets, hand the person a Kennel Visitation Form, verify photo ID, and an Animal Care Technician will take the customer to search for their lost pet. Cat adoptions are in the building on the left displaying Cat Adoptions; dogs are in the buildings on the right displaying Dog Adoptions.

Dogs must be 7 months of age or older for volunteers to pull (versus 6 months at CVAC); only animals available for adoption with purple and white kennel cards may be pulled. Dog kennel keys are checked out from the key box in the Volunteer Office. Volunteers may let dogs off leash in the play yards. Use techniques taught at Welcome Training to retrieve dogs from kennels. Adopters are taken to Admissions and called up by number rather than by name. Cats are taken to receiving for a spay/neuter vet check while the adopter completes paperwork; if vet staff share any information about the animal, tell only the Animal Care Technician, never the customer. Change gloves between cat cages when cleaning litter pans, and use a sanitized blue crate to transport cats to the visitation/socialization room, placing used crates in the dish room.
Laundry soap and bleach are self-dispensed. Guillotine doors should be reopened slowly to reintroduce dogs when re-kenneling.
