documents.searchText: to establish department wide uniformity regarding the procedures to effectively assist as volunteers following the processes as established for each shelter location. all volunteers supporting animal services at the coachella valley animal campus (thousand palms) and the san jacinto valley animal campus. volunteer services manager and volunteer services coordinator act animal care technician. kennel card colors purple: animal is already spayed/neutered and may go home the same day. white: animal will need to stay for spay/neuter surgery. yellow: kittens that may be socialized; for dogs, puppies that may not be socialized or removed from their kennels by volunteers (staff cares for puppies as their immune system is not fully developed). a.a., f.a., n.o.d. handwritten kennel card notes indicating the dog may not be pulled; immediately see an animal care technician. doa book dead on arrival book. sign in before starting any volunteer task, sign in using the ipad, using the unique pin given to you by the volunteer services coordinator. at cvac the ipad is located behind front desk staff, attached to the wall; once signed in, go to the volunteer office and put on a volunteer apron and name badge. if you cannot make it to your scheduled day, contact the volunteer coordinator so he or she is aware and can find coverage. kiosk greeter {"step": "prepare the greeting station:", "sub": ["the station should be clean and free of clutter. inspect the informational turnstile to be neat and organized, stocked with current informational pages, and discard expired or old informational sheets.", "stock 5 to 6 leashes, 2 to 3 clean blue cat boxes, and 5 to 6 large towels.", "check for any specific instructions left for the day, such as interview schedules or clinic supplies to be assembled."]} greet the public: smile and welcome every visitor as they enter the facility. ask and offer if you can help them. always remain polite and courteous, even to the most disgruntled visitor. should you need assistance with anything or anyone, ask an employee. citations/tickets, animal licensing, or bills: instruct the public to take a number from the red numbering dispenser located to the right of the front counter and then to take a seat. explain that one of the staff will call them up by number. dropping off stray animals: instruct the public to take a number from the red numbering dispenser and take a seat. if the animal is not properly secured with a leash (dogs) or a cage (cats), provide a leash or blue cat box as required. no animals should be allowed to run free while on property. dead animals: if the animal is exposed (not covered or concealed), use a towel to cover it up. {"step": "looking for a lost animal: ask the person if they have already been contacted by the shelter stating their animal was in our custody.", "sub": ["yes: instruct them to take a number from the red numbering dispenser and take a seat; staff will call them up by number.", "no: direct them into adoptions to look for their animal. explain the location of the adoption gallery, kennel a, kennel b, puppy room, cat room, and the bulletin boards in the hallway between kennels."]} adoptions: for dog or cat adoptions, direct the public to go through the double doors into the adoption gallery. doa (dead on arrival) book: the book is located to the far left of the front receptionist station. restrooms: located to the far left of the front receptionist station next to the water fountains. volunteer interest: direct the customer to the website at rcdas.org to fill out an application. rescue: if a rescue organization is there to pick up an animal, contact the rescue coordinator. if the rescue coordinator is unavailable, ask an animal care technician to assist them. animal socialization - cats wash hands in between handling cats and kittens. do an initial check of all the cat kennels. {"step": "if any of the cat food or water dishes are empty, refill them:", "sub": ["to refill the water bowl, place the bowl under the blue water container located on the counter and refill.", "if the bowls are messy or dirty, get a new bowl out of the cabinet.", "do not put the cat food bowl into the storage bin. always use the food scooper located in the bucket under the storage bin. only fill the food bowl halfway."]} remove and replace any dirty blankets or toys. place dirty items in designated areas identified by animal care technicians. check the litter pans and clean litter if needed. if the litter pan is very dirty, retrieve a new pan, fill it with litter, and place it back into the proper kennel. perform tasks one cage at a time so you do not mix litter pans belonging to a different cat, and wash hands between each cage. when socializing with cats, only approach the friendly ones. always socialize the kittens first before you socialize any adult cats. kittens under the age of 5 months may not be handled or socialized (subject to change due to illnesses; check with animal care technicians). if you notice a cat has any health or behavior issues, notify an animal care technician (act). help keep the counters, floors, and other surfaces clean and safe for yourself and everyone else. return tools to their proper locations before you leave your area. log your name into the cat socialization binder, identifying which cat you socialized with by animal id number and kennel number. visitation and adoption - cats smile and welcome every visitor as they enter each animal pod. ask if you can help or assist them. always be polite and courteous, even to the most disgruntled visitor. should you need assistance with anything or anyone, ask an employee. if a customer expresses interest in visiting with a cat, ask if they are interested in adopting. if not, explain that we only pull animals for prospective adopters, to ensure that animals are always available for potential adopters. {"step": "prepare to pull the cat for possible adoption:", "sub": ["verify the cat's age. the cat must be 5 months of age or older; if younger than 5 months, call an act to assist with the adoption.", "availability: the review date on the kennel card must be the current date or in the past.", "behavior status: the status at the top of the kennel card must not indicate observed. if observed is identified, call for an animal care technician. animals with an under vet care sign may not be handled or pulled.", "place an out for visitation sign on the outside of the kennel.", "put the cat in one of the sanitized blue cat crates to transfer to the visitation room. never transfer a cat without using a blue crate, and never use cardboard boxes to transport cats."]} wait until everyone in the customer's group is in the visitation room and the door is completely shut before you let the cat out of the blue crate. never leave the customers unattended with the cat. never give medical or behavioral advice to the customer. use your best judgment if the customers who want to see the cat have a large group with them. only allow 2 to 4 people in with the cat at a time. at the end of each visitation, place the cat back into the blue crate before opening the door. {"step": "ask if they would like to adopt:", "sub": ["no: thank the public for coming in, place the cat back into its kennel, and log the visitation in the cat binder.", "yes: have the adopter fill out adoption paperwork. while the adopter is filling out paperwork, return the cat to its kennel and retrieve the kennel card. after the adopter completes the paperwork, ask the act to sign off. take the adopter and completed paperwork to the lobby, instruct the adopter to have a seat, and explain they will be called up by name. congratulate the adopter."]} {"step": "spay and neuter check:", "sub": ["cat is already spayed/neutered: set paperwork in the adoption rack; take the cat to receiving for a final check, microchip, and/or vaccinations. the cat is taken to the new owners in the lobby in a carton box after the adoption is paid for.", "cat is not spayed/neutered: assign the cat to the next opening in the spay/neuter log and indicate the date on the adoption paperwork. take the cat to receiving for a final check, microchip, and/or vaccinations. return the cat to its original kennel with an updated kennel card indicating adopted. log the adoption in the cat binder."]} place the dirty blue crate in the dish room. never use a dirty crate. cat and dog photography wash hands in between handling animals. have a pen and notepad on hand. always return borrowed supplies to the volunteer office, and notify the volunteer coordinator staffing if supplies are running low. select the animals you would like to photograph. ensure the kennel card says adoptable and not treatable; treatable cats and dogs may only be photographed within their kennel. litters of kittens may be transported within the same blue crate and may be photographed individually only in the socialization/visitation room. unlock the kennel door, make a note of which kennel the cat came from, transport the cat using a sanitized blue crate, and be aware of any sudden changes in the cat's body language or behavior. dress-up: you may use the items found in the photography room to dress up the animal, and you can brush the pet using a brush found in the grooming area. use of the photo signs found in the photography room is optional. photograph the animal and log the animal into the photography log. return the animal to its original kennel, referring to your note of which kennel the animal came from. once complete, notify volunteer coordinator staff to edit, upload, and print new kennel cards with the new photos. allow time for completion of this task. animal socialization - dogs check out the dog kennel key from the front reception kiosk desk. {"step": "before removing any dog, have the following supplies:", "sub": ["dog leash (inspect it for damage).", "animal waste bag.", "out for walk sign to be placed on kennels notifying staff and public of the animal's absence.", "a pen and notepad are highly recommended. always return borrowed supplies to the volunteer office, and inform the volunteer coordinator if supplies are running low."]} {"step": "before pulling any dog, review the kennel card:", "sub": ["only animals with walk cards are available for pulling.", "age: the dog must be 6 months of age or older.", "availability: the review date on the kennel card must be the current date or in the past.", "behavior status: the status at the top of the kennel card must not indicate observed, even if the kennel card is purple.", "if multiple dogs are within a single kennel, none of the dogs may be pulled. if you have any questions regarding the ability to pull an animal, see an animal care technician or the volunteer coordinator.", "dogs may only be pulled from the inside kennel door, never the outside.", "animals with an under vet care sign may not be handled or pulled."]} {"step": "return the dog to its original kennel:", "sub": ["double check the kennel card to make sure the dog is going into the right kennel, referring back to your note.", "if there are any new dogs in the kennel not present when the dog was originally pulled, stop and ask an animal care technician before re-kenneling the dog.", "if the dog has other dogs in its kennel, use the guillotine door to separate them before you put the dog in. after you put the dog in and secure the door, reopen the guillotine door.", "remove the out for a walk sign.", "log the socialization on the walk card, including any observations regarding the dog's behavior while being socialized (for example: dog pulls on leash, dog does not pull on leash, dog will not walk on leash)."]} when describing an animal's behavior, keep descriptions simple but clear. avoid words or descriptions that are vague or could be interpreted differently by different people. do not use descriptions like "dog is sweet." explain the behavior observed, for example: "dog has a very relaxed body and immediately approached me with a low wagging tail." if unsure about how to describe a dog's behavior, ask an animal care technician. visitation and adoption - dogs smile and welcome every visitor as they enter each animal pod. ask if you can help or assist them. always be polite and courteous, even to the most disgruntled visitor. should you need assistance with anything or anyone, ask an employee. if a customer expresses interest in visiting with a dog, ask if they are interested in adopting. if not, explain that we only pull animals for prospective adopters, to ensure that animals are always available for potential adopters. {"step": "before pulling any dog, review the kennel card:", "sub": ["age: the dog must be 6 months of age or older.", "availability: the review date on the kennel card must be the current date or in the past.", "behavior status: if the status at the top of the kennel card indicates observed, do not pull the dog. immediately see an animal care technician.", "any handwritten notes such as a.a., f.a., or n.o.d.: do not pull the dog. immediately see an animal care technician.", "if multiple dogs are within a single kennel, none of the dogs may be pulled.", "animals with an under vet care sign may not be handled or pulled."]} place an out for visitation sign on the outside of the kennel. get the leash ready before you unlock the door. if there is more than one dog, separate the other dogs from the one you want using the guillotine door. if you need help, ask a staff member or another volunteer. unlock the kennel door, leash up the dog, and always make sure to close and lock the kennel door. only walk dogs in designated walking areas, and always pick up your dog's waste. never do a meet and greet with any of the shelter dogs or the public's dog. if a potential adopter is requesting a meet and greet, notify an animal care technician. keep at least a 6 to 10 foot distance between all dogs, and be aware of any sudden changes in the dog's body language or behavior. escort the dog and potential adopters to the get acquainted room or outside play yards. never give out medical or behavioral advice. if you do not know the answer to a question, refer them to an animal care technician. do not hand over the leash to a potential adopter. dogs may only be walked by the potential adopter within the confines of the play yards, never outside of them. {"step": "at the conclusion of the visitation, ask if they would like to adopt:", "sub": ["no: thank the public for coming in. place the dog back into its kennel, double checking the kennel card to make sure the dog is going into the right kennel. if there are any new dogs in the kennel, stop and ask staff before re-kenneling. if the dog has kennel mates, use the guillotine door to separate them before putting the dog in, then reopen the guillotine door. log the visitation on the walk card.", "yes: have the adopter fill out adoption paperwork. while the adopter is filling out paperwork, return the dog to its original kennel and retrieve the kennel card. after the adopter completes the paperwork, ask the animal care technician to sign off. take the adopter and completed paperwork to the lobby, instruct the adopter to have a seat, and explain they will be called up by name. congratulate the adopter."]} {"step": "spay and neuter:", "sub": ["dog is spayed/neutered: set the paperwork in the adoption rack and take the dog to receiving for a final check, microchip, and/or vaccinations. the dog is taken to the new owners in the lobby after the adoption is paid for.", "dog is not spayed/neutered: assign the dog to the next opening in the spay/neuter log and indicate the date on the adoption paperwork. bathe the dog per the brushing and bathing instructions. take the dog to receiving for a final check, microchip, and/or vaccinations. return the dog to its original kennel and log the adoption on the walk card."]} brushing and bathing - dogs check the grooming room for required supplies needed to bathe or groom a dog: towels, shampoo, and brushes/combs. make sure the wash tub and kennels in the grooming room have been sanitized and wiped clean prior to use. {"step": "select the dog to be washed and review the kennel card:", "sub": ["only dogs that are adoptable can be washed.", "no badly matted dogs are to be selected unless you are a professional groomer recognized by our department. if you are a professional groomer, talk to the volunteer services manager or volunteer coordinator before you begin.", "check with staff to see if there are any dogs that need to be bathed before they are scheduled for surgery.", "if a dog has been recently spayed or neutered, always ask an animal care technician to check the surgery date to make sure the dog is ready to bathe.", "make sure you can handle the dog you are going to bathe or groom. get help if you need it and always work with another volunteer or staff person."]} place an out for a bath sign on the kennel and write down the animal id and kennel number before taking the dog out of the kennel. get the leash ready before you unlock the door. if there is more than one dog, separate the other dogs from the one you want using the guillotine door, asking for help if needed. unlock the kennel door, leash up the dog, and always close and lock the kennel door. walk or carry the dog to the grooming room. do not carry a dog that is too heavy for you; always request help. only walk dogs in designated walking areas and always pick up your dog's waste. never do a meet and greet with any of the shelter dogs or the public's dogs. keep at least a 6 to 10 foot distance between all dogs and be aware of any sudden changes in the dog's body language or behavior. walk the dog up the ramp into the tub or pick it up and place it inside the tub, then place the tub's harness around the dog's neck to keep it inside the tub. {"step": "adjust the water temperature for the dog and bathe:", "sub": ["test the water temperature prior to using it on the dog to ensure it is not too hot or cold.", "begin washing from the top of the dog's head down to the tail.", "make sure not to get water or shampoo in the dog's eyes, mouth, nose, or ears.", "rinse thoroughly from top to bottom.", "wrap the dog in a towel and transfer it to the drying table, securing it with the harness. if the dog is too big for the table, place the dog on the floor and secure it to the table with a leash.", "turn on the blow dryer and watch the dog's reaction. if the reaction is not positive, proceed with towel drying; if the reaction is positive, proceed with blow drying."]} return the dog to its kennel, double checking the kennel card and following the standard re-kenneling steps (check for new dogs, use the guillotine door, then reopen it). remove the out for bath sign and log the bath on the walk card, including any observations regarding the dog's behavior while being bathed, using simple but clear descriptions. go back to the grooming room and clean and disinfect everything that was used (tub, brushes, floor, etc.). all used towels must be placed inside the dirty bin in the laundry room. events onsite shelter events: check in at the designated check-in area at your scheduled time. ask at check-in what your task will be. if you do not feel comfortable doing a certain task, tell staff immediately. do the task you have been given, and report any problems to a staff member. offsite shelter events: follow the same check-in and task steps as onsite events. it is especially important that you always tell a lead staff member where you are going before you leave offsite events. remember to be respectful while volunteering, as you are representing the riverside county department of animal services. laundry {"step": "washer:", "sub": ["put on gloves. it is extremely important that gloves are worn, as you will be working with various germs and diseases.", "throw out bloody items or items that are heavily soiled.", "never wash collars, leashes, or metal tags.", "load the washer until three-quarters full. do not overload, and do not run when the load is less than two-thirds full.", "large beds, heavy blankets, and pillows: only half full. the washer will stop if the load is too heavy.", "close and secure the door, ensuring nothing is pinched in the door seal.", "the washer should be set to heavy soil - hot.", "press the red start button."]} {"step": "dryer:", "sub": ["first, check the lint trap on the bottom of the dryer and clean it out.", "put washed items into the dryer, untangling items when possible. no plastic toys, no cat beds, and no large dog beds.", "the dryer should be set for 60 minutes, 15 minutes cool, high heat.", "push the green button.", "pillows and some thick items must be dried twice.", "fold items and put them on shelves. only clean items should be placed on the table, shelves, and in cubbies."]} clerical duties general office duties include but are not limited to filing, typing/data entry, and answering telephones, as needed and directed by the volunteer services manager. dishes wear gloves. wipe litter out of litter pans with paper towels. using a small clean bucket, mix a 50/50 solution of triple two and water. using the green triple two solution in the bucket, use a sponge to wipe out food bowls, litter pans, and similar items. once they have been soaked and cleaned out, insert dishes into the commercial dishwasher, which will steam off any germs that may not be visible. press the only button on the right-hand side to start the steaming process, which takes about 30 seconds. allow the dishes to get up to a minimum temperature of 140 degrees. carefully remove the sanitized dishes from the commercial washer; they may be hot. place a towel on the counter to allow them to dry, or hand dry them using a clean towel. once the dishes are dry, place them on the shelf and neatly organize them. ensure that the dish room is clean and that any excess water on the floor or counter has been wiped down before leaving. petco cattery care (la quinta and indio) enter through the door next to grooming, then make a left into the cattery door. quietly close the doors when coming in and out, because the noise could scare the dogs being groomed. sign in and grab the key hanging near the sign-in log. if there are soiled towels in the kennels, remove them and place them in a bag near the laundry area, or bring the soiled laundry back to the shelter to be washed. cleaning supplies are in a plastic container on the bottom left. remove the cat from the kennel you wish to clean by kenneling him or her or using the divider to section the cat off. spray all of the kennels with disinfectant and wipe them thoroughly using paper towels, including the top of the kennel and the glass on the inside and outside. place clean towels and cat beds into the kennels. after emptying all litter, wipe the pans out with disinfectant. ensure the pans have been fully sterilized and are dry before refilling them with litter. sanitize food and water bowls near the sink section, which is near the fish and has filtered water for the cats. fill the cat water bowls with filtered water and fill the food bowls with food found in the bottom left cupboard. ensure that cats are divided and closed off from different shelter cats unless they are kennel mates. place trash in the red trash can located in the back of the store through the grey double doors, and place a clean bag into the trash in the cattery. ensure that the water, food, and litter containers are filled prior to signing out. fill water near the fish section. for litter, use the litter station in the back of the store. for food and all other supplies, ask a manager. you may socialize the cats within the room to help make them more adoptable. answer any customer questions and show cats available for adoption. sign out on the log. clinic cage cleaning (san jacinto valley animal campus) be prepared with the following supplies: gloves, disinfecting spray, paper towels, scrubbing pad, trashcan, and newspaper and shredded paper (when available). place a pair of gloves on each hand. empty the cage of any solid waste and newspapers. if there is a towel or blanket in the cage, remove any solid waste and then place it into the dirty laundry basket. use newspaper and/or paper towels to soak up any urine or water in the kennel. spray all five sides of the cage liberally with disinfectant, and spray the cage door liberally with disinfectant. if there is more than one cage to be cleaned, you can set up the other cages by following the same steps. after soaking the cages, wipe the cages down with gloved hands using the scrubbing pad, paper towels, and more spray if needed. wipe down the cage doors, paying particular attention to all of the crevices where the metal meets, where mucus, urine, and feces can build up. change gloves as often as needed. if needed, the cage door can be removed for cleaning; see a veterinary assistant or registered veterinary technician for assistance. when the cage is dry, place a layer of newspaper at least 5 sheets thick on the entire bottom of the cage. in double cages you can use more newspaper, as bigger animals make bigger messes. bring all of the dirty laundry to the outside laundry room, located across from the volunteer office; the door is labeled grooming. bring back the same equivalent of items you dropped off. for example, if you dropped off 10 dirty towels, bring back 10 clean towels. livestock and barn (san jacinto valley animal campus, experienced volunteers only) schedule dates and times with the volunteer coordinator. check in at the volunteer office when you arrive, and check in with staff working in the barn during the time you are volunteering. staff will instruct you on what needs to be accomplished. review the livestock white board on the tack room door to see what animals are present and which animals can be interacted with. some animals are restricted to staff only; staff will inform you of these animals when you arrive. feeding barn animals: information is posted on the white board. staff will organize feed and supplements prior to feeding and will instruct you on what to feed and the quantities required for the individual animals. prior to entering stalls or cages, observe the animals' behavior. if the animals appear to be agitated or acting aggressively, do not enter the stall; ask staff for assistance. entering stalls: be aware of the location of the animal, open the cage or gate slowly, open the gate just enough so that you can fit through the door but not so much that the animal can fit through, and close the door behind you. assisting with cleaning stalls: enter the stall following the steps above, attain proper tools for mucking stalls (manure fork, manure bucket), slide the manure bucket under the stall rails away from the stall door, use a manure fork to pick up manure and place it in the large manure buckets, slide the bucket under the stall rail after removing manure, and exit the stall safely to dump the manure. if the bucket is heavy, ask for help to dump it. {"step": "grooming barn animals (only after cleaning is finished), using the tools kept in the tack room:", "sub": ["use a rubber curry comb to remove dirt and debris from the coat, in a circular motion on fleshy areas of the body. do not use this on the face or below the knee.", "use a hard brush to remove the dirt and debris that was loosened from the coat. the hard brush can be used on fleshy areas and down the legs, but not on the face.", "use a soft brush to remove remaining dirt and debris on the top of the coat. this brush can be used on the face and sensitive areas of the body.", "use a moist rag to wipe around the eyes and nose if the horse allows it.", "place all tools in their designated areas when you are finished for the day."]} petco cattery care (hemet and beaumont) petco hemet: enter through the door next to grooming, then make a left into the cattery door, closing doors quietly. sign in and grab the key hanging near the sign-in log. follow the same cattery cleaning, feeding, watering, trash, and socialization steps as the la quinta and indio locations. cleaning supplies are in the cupboard on the bottom left. petco beaumont: as you enter petco, the cattery is located to your right. cleaning supplies are located at the back of the store on a black cart; everything you need will be on the cart. the sign-in sheet is placed inside the drawer; sign in and ask for the kennel key at the register. if towels are soiled, remove them and place them by the washer located in the grooming room; if nothing is inside the washer, put soiled towels and blankets inside to wash. follow the same cattery cleaning, feeding, watering, trash, and socialization steps. sanitize food and water bowls in the grooming tub. if you run out of litter, use the litter station in the back of the store; for food and all other supplies, ask a manager.

documents.title: Volunteer Guidelines - Riverside Animal Shelter
documents.purpose: To establish department wide uniformity regarding the procedures to effectively assist as volunteers following the processes as established for each shelter location.
documents.authority: Volunteer Services Manager and Volunteer Services Coordinator
documents.scope: All volunteers supporting Animal Services at the Riverside Animal Shelter.
documents.supersedes: Volunteer Guidelines for Supporting Animal Services in Riverside, effective September 3, 2019

definitions.def: Animal Care Technician.

Purple: animal is already spayed/neutered and may go home the same day. White: animal will need to stay for spay/neuter surgery. Yellow: kittens that may be socialized; for dogs, puppies that may not be socialized or removed from their kennels by volunteers. Red (dogs): dog is already adopted and awaiting surgery.
Handwritten kennel card notes: A.A. for animal aggressive, F.A. for food aggressive, N.O.D. for no other dog. Do not pull the dog; immediately see an Animal Care Technician.
Dead on Arrival book.

sections.heading: Sign In
steps: Before starting any volunteer task, sign in using the iPad located in the volunteer office, using the unique PIN given to you by your Volunteer Services Coordinator. Once signed in, put on a volunteer apron and name badge.

sections.heading: Kiosk Greeter

steps.step: Prepare the greeting station:
sub: The station should be clean and free of clutter. Inspect the informational turnstile to be neat and organized, stocked with current informational pages, and discard expired or old informational sheets.
Stock 5 to 6 leashes, 2 to 3 clean blue cat boxes, and 5 to 6 large towels.
Check for any specific instructions left for the day, such as interview schedules or clinic supplies to be assembled.

steps: Greet the public: smile and welcome every visitor as they enter the facility. Ask and offer help. Always remain polite and courteous, even to the most disgruntled visitor. If you need to step away to find a staff person, inform the customer that you will be right back.
Picking up dogs from spay/neuter surgery: refer the customer to the clinic. If the clinic is closed, refer the customer to the licensing window staff near the elevators.

Donations: for newspapers, towels, or food, direct the customer to leave the donations in the lobby in the corner by Critter Corner and thank them; the customer may go to the licensing window if they want a receipt. Money or checks can be paid at the licensing window or inserted in the ASK Foundation box located at the kiosk.

Deliveries: deliveries are accepted by licensing staff or assigned staff only. Volunteers do not sign for any packages. A phone list is kept at the kiosk in case you need to contact assigned staff to sign.
Citations/tickets, animal licensing, or bills: refer them to the licensing window counters, the first hallway on the left, at the windows across from the elevator.

Dropping off stray animals: redirect the public out the front door and immediately right into the Admissions Department (a sign above the door indicates dogs on the left and cats on the right). Instruct the public to take a number from the red numbering dispenser and take a seat. If the animal is not properly secured, provide a leash or blue cat box. No animals should be allowed to run free while on property. Volunteers do not hold any stray animals; if assistance is needed, ask an Animal Care Technician.

Lost and Found book: the book is available to the right of the computer at the kiosk desk. The customer can leave a copy of the animal's information with a date, whether the pet is lost or found, and a contact phone number or email. Animal Care Technicians update the list daily.

Dead animals: redirect the public out the front door and immediately right into the Admissions Department, where they take a number and wait to be called. If the animal is exposed (not covered or concealed), use a towel to cover it up. The customer is to keep the dead animal in their vehicle while finishing the transaction, then drive toward the back of the shelter to meet an Animal Care Technician who will take care of the disposal.
DOA book: a customer can review the book for information; it is available to the right of the computer on the kiosk desk.

Lost pets: ask the person if they have already been contacted by the shelter by phone or letter stating their animal was in our custody, then redirect them to the Admissions Department. An Animal Care Technician will take the customer to search for their lost pet.
Restrooms: located in the first hallway on the left next to the elevators, with additional restrooms outside in the quad area.

Barn animals: ask customers requesting information on barn animals to go out through the double doors to the livestock board on the wall in the main quad area next to the vending machine. This board gives detailed information on which animals are available for adoption or on stray hold. Anyone interested in adopting a barn animal should be referred to a staff member.

Rescues: direct rescue organizations to the Rescue Office located next to the kiosk. If Rescue Coordinator staff are occupied when rescue groups arrive for their appointment, get the rescue name and the Animal ID number when calling for rescue staff.

Adoptions: direct the public to go out through the double doors and make a right toward the kennel buildings. Cats are housed in the first building, marked Wond-Purr-Land. Dogs are housed in the next two buildings, marked Barking Glen and Wagging Acres. If the customer has an Animal ID ready for possible adoption or viewing, an Animal Care Technician can assist them with the adoption.

sections.heading: Animal Socialization - Cats
steps: Wash hands in between handling cats and kittens. Hand sanitizer stations are located on the wall.

Do an initial check of all the cat kennels and pick which cat you would like to socialize with. It may be best to ask staff if a particular cat needs to be worked with. Step stools are located by the food bins and can be used to reach top kennels.
Socialize the cat in the Community Cat Room first. If the visitation rooms are available, you may use them.
To use a visitation room, put the cat in one of the sanitized blue cat crates to transfer it. Never transfer a cat without using a blue crate, and never use cardboard boxes to transport cats.
When picking up a cat, slip one hand under the chest while holding the front legs gently but firmly with your fingers, and cup your free hand under the cat's hindquarters.

Use enrichment items such as feather toys, balls, and jingle bells to engage with the cat. For infection control purposes, once a toy goes into a cat's kennel, it is that cat's toy to keep until it is properly sanitized and can be used again with another cat.
Place a Gone for a Visit sign in front of the cat's kennel card.

If you notice a cat has any health issues (sneezing, watery eyes, discharge, diarrhea) or behavior issues, write the Animal ID number on the boards located on both inner sides of the cat building and notify an Animal Care Technician or staff member.
Do not re-use the same blue crate after socializing. Take the used blue crate and place it into the dish room to be sanitized.
Return all items you may have used to their correct locations (step stool, laser toy, etc.).

If any of the cat food or water dishes are empty, refill them. Use watering cans to refill water bowls. If bowls are messy or dirty, get a new bowl out of the cabinet. Do not put the cat food bowl into the storage bin; always use the food scooper located in the bucket under the storage bin, and only fill the food bowl halfway.
Remove and replace any dirty blankets or toys. Place dirty items in designated areas identified by Animal Care Technicians.
When assisting with cleaning, perform tasks one cage at a time so you do not mix litter pans belonging to a different cat, to prevent disease.

Check the litter pans and clean litter if needed. Clean pans are located under the counter labeled Cat Litter Pans/Bowls. If the litter pan is dirty, retrieve a new pan, fill it with litter, and place it back into the proper kennel.

When socializing with cats, only approach the friendly ones. Always socialize the kittens first before any adult cats. Kittens under the age of 5 months may not be handled or socialized (subject to change due to illnesses; check with Animal Care Technicians).
Help keep the counters, floors, and other surfaces clean and safe, and return tools to their proper locations before you leave your area.

sections.heading: Visitation and Adoption - Cats
steps: Smile and welcome every visitor as they enter each animal pod. Ask and offer help. Always be polite and courteous, even to the most disgruntled visitor. Should you need assistance with anything or anyone, ask an employee.

If a customer expresses interest in visiting with a cat, ask if they are interested in adopting. If not, explain that we only pull animals for prospective adopters, to ensure that animals are always available for potential adopters.

steps.step: Prepare to pull the cat for possible adoption:
sub: Verify the cat's age. The cat must be 5 months of age or older; if younger than 5 months, call an Animal Care Technician for assistance.
Availability: the review date on the kennel card must be the current date or in the past.

Behavior status: the status at the top of the kennel card must not indicate OBSERVED. If OBSERVED is identified, call for an Animal Care Technician. Animals with an Under Vet Care sign may not be handled or pulled.
Place an Out for Visitation sign on the outside of the kennel door where the kennel card is located, making sure not to cover the card.
Put the cat in one of the sanitized blue cat crates to transfer to the visitation room. Never transfer a cat without using a blue crate; never use cardboard boxes.

steps: Wait until everyone in the customer's group is in the visitation room and the door is completely shut before you let the cat out of the blue crate. Use your best judgment with large groups; only allow 2 to 4 people in with the cat at a time.
Never leave the customers unattended with the cat, and never give medical or behavioral advice to the customer.
At the end of each visitation, place the cat back into the blue crate before opening the door.

steps.step: Ask if they would like to adopt:
sub: No: thank the public for coming in, place the cat back into its kennel using a blue crate, remove the Gone Visiting sign, and spray the blue crate with disinfectant to leave it clean for the next cat.

Yes: mark ADOPTED and the current date on the kennel card. Have the adopter fill out adoption paperwork. Ask an Animal Care Technician to explain the paperwork to the public, including the FELV/FIV test, and print out an extra kennel card. While the adopter is filling out paperwork, take the cat to receiving for a spay/neuter check (vet check). After the adopter completes the paperwork, ask the ACT to sign off. Take the adopter and completed paperwork to the lobby, instruct them to have a seat, and explain they will be called up by name. Congratulate the adopter.

steps.step: Cat is already spayed/neutered:
sub: Set the purple kennel card paperwork in the adoption rack.

If the new adopter chooses to accept the FELV/FIV test and the cat is already fixed, the blood test will be done at the time of the adoption exam (test time is 10 to 15 minutes). Have the customer fill out the FELV/FIV test slip.

Once the customer fills out the FELV/FIV test slip, transport the cat in a blue crate to the receiving area by the clinic for the veterinary exam (final check, microchip, and/or vaccinations), with the virus check request slip and the kennel card.
Stand behind the tape line and wait to be called. Inform vet staff that you are there for an adoption exam.
If the cat receives a microchip while in receiving, place the AVID microchip packet along with the kennel card and FELV/FIV slip into the second black inbox in the hallway by the adoption windows.
If the vet staff tell you any information about the cat, only tell the Animal Care Technician. Never tell the customer what vet staff have told you.
If the cat is going home, advise the adopter to pay at the licensing windows located in the hallway in the lobby and come back to the cat building with the receipt for pickup.

Get a cardboard cat carrier ready if the cat is going home (located in the second office in the cat room). Do not put the cat into the carrier until the customer comes back with the adoption receipt; the cat can be placed back into its original kennel until the adopter returns. The cat is given to the adopter in the cat building, Wond-Purr-Land, after the adoption receipt is reviewed by an Animal Care Technician.
Log the adoption in the cat binder and place the dirty blue crate in the dish room.

steps.step: Cat is not spayed/neutered:
sub: If the cat is determined to be too young or too sick for surgery, the cat will go home On Contract and come back later to have the surgery done. If the cat is 3 months old or less and going home On Contract, the blood test will be done during the time of surgery.
If veterinary staff determine the cat is healthy enough for surgery, it will stay for surgery, a note will be entered in Chameleon, and a new kennel card will be printed.

Ask the customer to pay for the adoption at the licensing window. The customer will be instructed at that time to wait for the phone call from the clinic when their cat is available for pickup after the spay/neuter surgery.
Put the cat back in its original kennel with its kennel card clearly marked ADOPTED.
Place a red collar on the cat with the Animal ID number and date. Red collars are located in the Animal Care Technician office inside the first drawer of the filing cabinet.

sections.heading: Cat Photography
steps: Wash or sanitize hands in between handling cats and kittens.

Check out a badge to access available cats through the Animal Services Supervisor or Volunteer Coordinator staff. Have a pen and notepad on hand. Always return borrowed supplies to the Volunteer Office and notify the Volunteer Coordinator staff if supplies are running low.
Select the cat you would like to photograph. Ensure the kennel card says adoptable and not treatable; treatable cats may only be photographed within their kennel.

Litters of kittens may be transported within the same blue crate and photographed individually only in the socialization/visitation room. Unlock the kennel door, note which kennel the cat came from, transport the cat using a sanitized blue crate, and be aware of any sudden changes in the cat's body language or behavior.
Dress-up: you may use the items found in the photography room to dress up the animal, and brush the pet using a brush found in the grooming area. Use of the photo signs is optional.
Photograph the cat and log the cat into the photography log.
Return the cat to its original kennel, referring to your note.
Once complete, notify Volunteer Coordinator staff to edit, upload, and print new kennel cards with the new photos. Allow time for completion of this task.

sections.heading: Animal Socialization - Dogs
steps: Wash your hands in between handling dogs.
Check out the dog kennel key from the front kiosk desk. Keys will be hanging inside the key box. If keys are not available, ask a Supervisor or Animal Care Technician to assist.

steps.step: Before removing any dog, have the following supplies:
sub: Dog leash (inspect it for damage).
Animal waste bag.
Out for Walk sign to be placed on kennels notifying staff and public of the animal's absence.
A pen and notepad to note which kennel the animal was pulled from.
If supplies are running low, notify your Volunteer Coordinator staff. Always return borrowed supplies to the Volunteer Office.

steps.step: Before pulling any dog, review the kennel card:
sub: Only animals with purple and white kennel cards are available for pulling.
Age: the dog must be 7 months of age or older.
Availability: the review date on the kennel card must be the current date or in the past.
Behavior status: the status at the top of the kennel card must not indicate OBSERVED, even if the kennel card is purple.
Animals with an Under Vet Care sign may not be handled or pulled.

steps.step: Once you have selected a social and friendly dog:
sub: Write down the dog's Animal ID and kennel number on your notepad.
Place an Out for a Walk sign on the kennel card.
Use techniques taught at Welcome Training to retrieve the dog from the kennel.
Dogs may only be pulled from the inside kennel door, never the outside.

Get the leash ready before you unlock the door. If there is more than one dog, separate the other dogs from the one you want using the guillotine door; ask an Animal Care Technician or another volunteer for help if needed.
Unlock the kennel door, leash up the dog, and always close and lock the kennel door.
Only walk dogs in designated walking areas. When in the play yards, volunteers may let large dogs off leash; dogs must weigh more than 25 pounds.
Always pick up your dog's waste.
Never do a meet and greet with any of the shelter dogs or the public's dogs; request an Animal Care Technician to help.
Keep at least a 6 to 10 foot distance between all dogs and be aware of any sudden changes in the dog's body language or behavior.

steps.step: Return the dog to its original kennel:
sub: Double check the kennel card to make sure the dog is going into the right kennel, referring back to your note.
If there are any new dogs in the kennel not present when the dog was originally pulled, stop and ask an Animal Care Technician before re-kenneling the dog.

If the dog has other dogs in its kennel, use the guillotine door to separate them before you put the dog in. After you put the dog in and secure the door, reopen the guillotine door slowly to reintroduce the dogs.
Remove the Out for a Walk sign.
Log the socialization in the dog binder and mark the back of the kennel card with today's date to let others know when the dog was walked.
Include any observations regarding the dog's behavior while being socialized.

steps: When describing an animal's behavior, keep descriptions simple but clear. Avoid words or descriptions that are vague or could be interpreted differently by different people. Do not use descriptions like "Dog is sweet." Explain the behavior observed, for example: "Dog has a very relaxed body and immediately approached me with a low wagging tail." If unsure, ask an Animal Care Technician.

sections.heading: Visitation and Adoption - Dogs
steps: Wash your hands in between handling dogs.
Smile and welcome every visitor as they enter each animal pod. Ask and offer help. Always be polite and courteous, even to the most disgruntled visitor.

If a customer expresses interest in visiting with a dog, ask if they are interested in adopting. If not, explain that we only pull animals for prospective adopters. If yes, ask if they have the Animal ID number available.

steps.step: Before pulling any dog, review the kennel card:
sub: Age: the dog must be 6 months of age or older. If the public wishes to view a puppy 6 months or younger, have an Animal Care Technician assist.
Availability: the review date on the kennel card must be the current date or in the past.
Behavior status: if the status at the top of the kennel card indicates OBSERVED, do not pull the dog. Immediately see an Animal Care Technician.
Any handwritten notes (A.A. for animal aggressive, F.A. for food aggressive, N.O.D. for no other dog): do not pull the dog. Immediately see an Animal Care Technician.
If multiple dogs are within a single kennel, none of the dogs may be pulled.
Animals with an Under Vet Care sign may not be handled or pulled.

steps: Place an Out for Visitation sign on the outside of the kennel.
Take down key facts from the kennel card such as the dog's age, sex, spay/neuter status, weight, and the kennel number the dog was taken from.
Ask the customer to step outside of the kennel while you pull the dog from the kennel and pod.
Get the leash ready before you unlock the door, separating kennel mates with the guillotine door if needed. Unlock the kennel door, leash up the dog, and always close and lock the kennel door.
Escort the dog and potential adopters to the Get Acquainted Room or outside play yards. Only walk and show dogs in designated areas.
If a potential adopter is requesting a Meet and Greet, notify an Animal Care Technician. Never do a Meet and Greet with any of the shelter dogs or the public's dog.
Keep at least a 6 to 10 foot distance between all dogs, be aware of any sudden changes in the dog's body language or behavior, and never give out medical or behavioral advice.

Do not hand over the leash to a potential adopter. The leash must stay in the volunteer's hands at all times, no exceptions. Dogs may only be walked by the potential adopter within the confines of the play yards, never outside of them. A second leash can be used so that the potential adopter can walk the dog, but the volunteer must not let go of their leash at any time.
If you do not know the answer to a question, refer them to an Animal Care Technician. Always pick up your dog's waste.

steps.step: At the conclusion of the visitation, ask if they would like to adopt:
sub: No: thank the public for coming in. Return the dog to its original kennel, double checking the kennel card. If there are new dogs in the kennel, stop and ask staff before re-kenneling. If the dog has kennel mates, use the guillotine door, then reopen it slowly to reintroduce the dogs. Log the visitation in the dog binder.

Yes: have the adopter fill out the adoption paperwork on the clipboard you hand them. While the adopter is filling out paperwork, take the dog for a vet exam located in receiving, making sure to pull the kennel card for the dog you are taking. After the adopter completes the paperwork, ask the Animal Care Technician to sign off. Take the adopter and completed paperwork to the lobby, instruct the adopter to have a seat, and explain that the Animal Care Technician will meet with them in the counseling room. Congratulate the adopter.

steps.step: Spay and neuter:
sub: Dog is spayed/neutered: set the paperwork in the adoption rack and take the dog to receiving for a final check, microchip, and/or vaccinations. Stand behind the taped line and wait to be helped; receiving can be very busy. The dog is taken to the new owners in the lobby after the adoption is paid for.

Dog is not spayed/neutered: take the dog to receiving for a final check, microchip, and/or vaccinations. Return the dog to its original kennel, log the adoption in the dog binder, and remove the Out Visiting sign.

sections.heading: Dog Photography
steps: Wash hands in between handling dogs and puppies.
Make sure to have your photo identification card to check out a kennel key and leash from the designated area. Ask staff for assistance pulling dogs from the Life Room kennels.

Grab a dog leash and animal waste bags. A pen and notepad are highly recommended. Always return borrowed supplies to the Volunteer Office or kiosk desk, and notify the Volunteer Coordinator staff if supplies are running low.
Puppies under 6 months of age should not be taken out of their kennel for photographing.

Select the dogs you would like to photograph. Ensure the kennel card says adoptable and not treatable. Never pull a dog from the outside of the kennel. If there is more than one dog, separate the other dogs using the guillotine door, asking for help if needed.
Get the leash ready before you unlock the door, unlock the kennel door, leash up the dog per your training, and always close and lock the kennel door.

Take the dog on a potty break before photographing, walking only in designated walking areas. Always pick up your dog's waste. Never do a meet and greet with any shelter dogs or the public's dogs. Keep a 6 to 10 foot distance between all dogs and be aware of any sudden changes in the dog's body language or behavior.
Dress-up: you may use the items found in the photography room to dress up the animal. Use of the photo signs is optional.
Photograph the dog and log the dog into the photography log.

Return the dog to its original kennel, referring to your note. If the dog has kennel mates, use the guillotine door to separate them before you put the dog in, then slowly move the guillotine door up to reintroduce the dog. If there are any new dogs in the kennel, ask staff before adding the dog back.
Wash your hands in between handling dogs.
Once complete, notify Volunteer Coordinator staff to edit, upload, and print new kennel cards with the new photos. Allow time for completion of this task.

sections.heading: Brushing and Bathing - Dogs
steps: Wash your hands in between handling dogs.
Check the grooming room for required supplies: towels/rags, shampoo, and brushes/combs.
Make sure the wash tub and kennels in the grooming room have been sanitized and wiped clean prior to use. If they have not been, clean them now.
Make sure to have your photo identification card to check out a kennel key and leash from the kiosk.

steps.step: Check in with staff to receive a list of dogs that need to be washed, and review each kennel card:
sub: Only dogs that are one year or older and adoptable are allowed to be bathed and groomed.

Extremely matted dogs should not be pulled for grooming unless you are a professional groomer recognized by our department or have been properly trained to handle animals needing extensive grooming. If you are a professional groomer, talk to the Volunteer Services Manager or Volunteer Coordinator before you begin. All volunteers who want to work with dogs requiring significant grooming will be trained on proper techniques prior to working with these animals.
Check with staff to see if there are any dogs that need to be bathed before they are scheduled for surgery.
If a dog has been recently spayed or neutered, always ask an Animal Care Technician to check the surgery date and incisions to ensure the dog is suitable for bathing and grooming.
Make sure you can handle the dog you are going to bathe or groom. Ask for help if necessary and always work with another volunteer or staff person.

steps: Place an Out for a Bath sign on the kennel to indicate the dog is out of the kennel and will be returning, and write down the animal ID and kennel number before taking the dog out.

Always follow the procedures for pulling dogs: get the leash ready before you unlock the door, use the guillotine door to separate kennel mates, carefully enter the kennel and leash the dog, exit taking care to avoid letting other dogs out, and always close and lock the kennel door and open the guillotine door.

Walk or carry the dog to the grass and allow the animal an opportunity to go to the restroom, then proceed to the grooming room. Only walk dogs in designated walking areas and always pick up your dog's waste. Do not carry a dog that is too heavy for you; always request help.
Be aware of any sudden changes in the dog's body language or behavior.
Fill out the grooming log with the correct information. If sheets are needed for the log book, ask a staff member for copies.

Place the dog on the grooming table, fit the noose/harness to the animal, and assess the coat for necessary grooming procedures. Comb tangles and remove matts if necessary; only remove matts if you are trained.

Either walk the dog up the ramp or stairs into the tub or pick up the dog and place it gently inside the tub. Place the grooming noose or tub harness gently around the dog's neck and clip it to the tub loops to safely restrain the animal. Do not leave the animal unattended while in the tub.

Adjust the water temperature. If using warm water, preheat and test the water before using it on the dog. Test the water temperature to ensure it is not too hot or cold; temperature may depend on weather.
Begin rinsing the dog from top to bottom, being careful to apply water gently around the face and other sensitive areas.

Choose a shampoo suitable for the coat type and purpose, read the application instructions, and apply shampoo washing from the top of the dog's head down to the tail. Make sure not to get water or shampoo in the dog's eyes, mouth, nose, or ears. Use small amounts of shampoo and add water to create a lather to work through the coat. If conditioner is desired, apply it to the coat along with the shampoo.

Rinse thoroughly from top to bottom, head to tail, until shampoo and conditioner residue cannot be felt. If the dog is not rinsed thoroughly, the coat will have a greasy residue which will irritate the skin and attract dirt.

Pick a suitable towel and begin to towel dry the dog in the tub. Wrap the dog in a towel and transfer it to the grooming table, securing with the noose/harness. If the dog is too big for the table, place the dog on the floor and secure it to the table with a leash.

Apply any desired conditioning sprays to the coat after towel drying. Turn on the blow dryer and watch the dog's reaction: if the reaction is anxious, proceed with towel drying; if positive, proceed with blow drying. When the animal is dry, use a comb to brush the hair, then use a slicker brush to smooth out the coat.
Sign out of the grooming book.

Return the dog to its proper kennel by double checking the kennel card and following the standard re-kenneling steps. Remove the Out for Bath sign from the kennel door, log the bath in the dog binder, and include any observations regarding the dog's behavior while being bathed, using simple but clear descriptions.

Return to the grooming room and clean and disinfect everything that was used (tub, brushes, floor, etc.). Place all used towels inside a bag and take them to the laundry room. Sweep up debris from the floor and place it in the garbage can. If the garbage can is full, remove the bag, replace the garbage bag, turn off the light, shut the door, and take the dirty garbage bag to the dumpster.

sections.heading: Events
steps: Onsite shelter events: check in at the designated check-in area at your scheduled time. Ask at check-in what your task will be. If you do not feel comfortable doing a certain task, tell staff immediately. Do the task you have been given, and report any problems to a staff member.

Offsite shelter events: follow the same check-in and task steps as onsite events. It is especially important that you always tell a lead staff member where you are going before you leave offsite events.
Remember to be respectful while volunteering, as you are representing the Riverside County Department of Animal Services.

steps.step: Washer:
sub: Put on gloves. It is extremely important that gloves are worn, as you will be working with various germs and diseases.
Throw out bloody items or items that are heavily soiled with a lot of feces on them.
Separate the towels, sheets, and blankets into separate piles. Never wash collars, leashes, or metal tags.

Load the washer until three-quarters full; the washer will hold up to 100 pounds. Do not overload, and do not run when the load is less than two-thirds full. Large beds, heavy blankets, and pillows: only half full. The washer will stop if the load is too heavy.
Push the red sticker on the door then pull the handle to open the washer.
Once the items are in the washer, close and secure the door, ensuring nothing is pinched in the door seal.
Soap or detergent is automatically dispensed with the correct amount.
The washer should be preset to Heavy Soil - Hot. Press 01, then start.

steps.step: Dryer:
sub: First, check the lint trap on the bottom of the dryer and clean it out.
Put washed items into the dryer, untangling items when possible. No plastic toys, no cat beds, and no large dog beds.
The dryer is preset; just press start. Set the cool time for 10 minutes and push the green button to start.
Pillows and some thick items must be dried twice.
Fold items and put them on shelves. Only clean items should be placed on the table, shelves, and in cubbies.

sections.heading: Clerical Duties
steps: General office duties include but are not limited to filing, typing/data entry, and answering telephones, as needed and directed by the Volunteer Services Manager.

sections.heading: Clinic Cage Cleaning
steps: Be prepared with the following supplies: gloves, disinfecting spray, paper towels, scrubbing pad, trashcan, and newspaper and shredded paper (when available).
Place a pair of gloves on each hand.
Empty the cage of any solid waste and newspapers.
If there is a towel or blanket in the cage, remove any solid waste and then place it into the dirty laundry basket.
Use newspaper and/or paper towels to soak up any urine or water in the kennel.

Spray all five sides of the cage liberally with disinfectant, and spray the cage door liberally with disinfectant for at least 10 minutes. If there is more than one cage to be cleaned, set up the other cages by following the same steps.

After soaking the cages, wipe them down with gloved hands using the scrubbing pad, paper towels, and more spray if needed. Wipe down the cage doors, paying particular attention to the crevices where the metal meets, where mucus, urine, and feces can build up. Change gloves as often as needed. If needed, the cage door can be removed for cleaning; see a Veterinary Assistant or Registered Veterinary Technician for assistance.
When the cage is dry, place a layer of newspaper at least 5 sheets thick on the entire bottom of the cage. In double cages you can use more newspaper, as bigger animals make bigger messes.
Bring all of the dirty laundry to the outside laundry room.
Bring back the same equivalent of items you dropped off. For example, if you dropped off 10 dirty towels, bring back 10 clean towels.

sections.heading: Dishwashing
steps: Wear gloves.
Wipe litter out of litter pans with paper towels.
Using a small clean bucket, mix a 50/50 solution of Triple Two and water.
Using the green Triple Two solution in the bucket, use a sponge to wipe out food bowls, litter pans, and similar items.

Once they have been soaked and cleaned out, insert dishes into the commercial dishwasher, which will steam off any germs that may not be visible. Press the only button on the right-hand side to start the steaming process, which takes about 30 seconds. Allow the dishes to get up to a minimum temperature of 140 degrees.

Carefully remove the sanitized dishes from the commercial washer; they may be hot. Place a towel on the counter to allow them to dry, or hand dry them using a clean towel. Once dry, place the dishes on the shelf and neatly organize them.
Ensure that the dish room is clean and that any excess water on the floor or counter has been wiped down before leaving.

sections.heading: Livestock and Barn
steps: Schedule dates and times with the Volunteer Coordinator. Dates for volunteering include Monday through Friday, 8:30 a.m. to 10:30 a.m.
Check in at the Volunteer Office when you arrive, and check in with staff working in the barn during the time you are volunteering. Staff will instruct you on what needs to be accomplished.

Review the livestock white board on the tack room door to see what animals are present and which animals can be interacted with. Some animals are restricted to staff only; staff will inform you of these animals when you arrive.

Feeding barn animals: information is posted on the white board. Staff will organize feed and supplements prior to feeding and will instruct you on what to feed and the quantities required for the individual animals.
Prior to entering stalls or cages, observe the animals' behavior. If the animals appear agitated or aggressive, do not enter the stall; ask staff for assistance.
Entering stalls: be aware of the location of the animal, open the cage or gate slowly, open the gate just enough so that you can fit through but the animal cannot, and close the door behind you.

Assisting with cleaning stalls: enter the stall following the steps above, attain proper tools (manure fork, manure bucket), slide the manure bucket under the stall rails away from the stall door, use the manure fork to pick up manure and place it in the large manure buckets, slide the bucket under the stall rail when done, and exit safely to dump the manure. If the bucket is heavy, ask for help.

steps.step: Grooming barn animals (only after cleaning is finished), using tools kept in the tack room:
sub: Use a rubber curry comb to remove dirt and debris from the coat, in a circular motion on fleshy areas. Do not use it on the face or below the knee.
Use a hard brush to remove the loosened dirt and debris. The hard brush can be used on fleshy areas and down the legs, but not on the face.
Use a soft brush to remove remaining dirt and debris on the top of the coat. This brush can be used on the face and sensitive areas.
Use a moist rag to wipe around the eyes and nose if the horse allows it.
Place all tools in their designated areas when you are finished for the day.

sections.heading: Agility Yard and Visitation Yards
steps: The big arena with bleachers is used for watching demonstrations such as agility, police K-9, and Frisbee dogs. This arena is also used for evening and special events.
Fenced grass areas located behind each dog building are dedicated as dog visitation yards. The large grass quad located at the center of the shelter can also be used for dog visitations.

The dog agility yard is available for dogs over 25 pounds to exercise off leash while being supervised by a staff member or volunteer. Only one person with one dog is allowed in the agility yard at any given time.

related.name: Volunteer Guidelines for Supporting Animal Services at the Coachella Valley and San Jacinto Valley Animal Campuses
FELV/FIV test slip (Virus Check Request slip)

revisions.author: Outcomes for Pets (draft)
revisions.desc: Migrated into standard RCDAS SOP template. Content unchanged from 2019 version; substantive edits pending (see edit log).

documents.searchText: to establish department wide uniformity regarding the procedures to effectively assist as volunteers following the processes as established for each shelter location. all volunteers supporting animal services at the riverside animal shelter. volunteer services manager and volunteer services coordinator act animal care technician. kennel card colors purple: animal is already spayed/neutered and may go home the same day. white: animal will need to stay for spay/neuter surgery. yellow: kittens that may be socialized; for dogs, puppies that may not be socialized or removed from their kennels by volunteers. red (dogs): dog is already adopted and awaiting surgery. a.a., f.a., n.o.d. handwritten kennel card notes: a.a. for animal aggressive, f.a. for food aggressive, n.o.d. for no other dog. do not pull the dog; immediately see an animal care technician. doa book dead on arrival book. sign in before starting any volunteer task, sign in using the ipad located in the volunteer office, using the unique pin given to you by your volunteer services coordinator. once signed in, put on a volunteer apron and name badge. kiosk greeter {"step": "prepare the greeting station:", "sub": ["the station should be clean and free of clutter. inspect the informational turnstile to be neat and organized, stocked with current informational pages, and discard expired or old informational sheets.", "stock 5 to 6 leashes, 2 to 3 clean blue cat boxes, and 5 to 6 large towels.", "check for any specific instructions left for the day, such as interview schedules or clinic supplies to be assembled."]} greet the public: smile and welcome every visitor as they enter the facility. ask and offer help. always remain polite and courteous, even to the most disgruntled visitor. if you need to step away to find a staff person, inform the customer that you will be right back. picking up dogs from spay/neuter surgery: refer the customer to the clinic. if the clinic is closed, refer the customer to the licensing window staff near the elevators. donations: for newspapers, towels, or food, direct the customer to leave the donations in the lobby in the corner by critter corner and thank them; the customer may go to the licensing window if they want a receipt. money or checks can be paid at the licensing window or inserted in the ask foundation box located at the kiosk. deliveries: deliveries are accepted by licensing staff or assigned staff only. volunteers do not sign for any packages. a phone list is kept at the kiosk in case you need to contact assigned staff to sign. citations/tickets, animal licensing, or bills: refer them to the licensing window counters, the first hallway on the left, at the windows across from the elevator. dropping off stray animals: redirect the public out the front door and immediately right into the admissions department (a sign above the door indicates dogs on the left and cats on the right). instruct the public to take a number from the red numbering dispenser and take a seat. if the animal is not properly secured, provide a leash or blue cat box. no animals should be allowed to run free while on property. volunteers do not hold any stray animals; if assistance is needed, ask an animal care technician. lost and found book: the book is available to the right of the computer at the kiosk desk. the customer can leave a copy of the animal's information with a date, whether the pet is lost or found, and a contact phone number or email. animal care technicians update the list daily. dead animals: redirect the public out the front door and immediately right into the admissions department, where they take a number and wait to be called. if the animal is exposed (not covered or concealed), use a towel to cover it up. the customer is to keep the dead animal in their vehicle while finishing the transaction, then drive toward the back of the shelter to meet an animal care technician who will take care of the disposal. doa book: a customer can review the book for information; it is available to the right of the computer on the kiosk desk. lost pets: ask the person if they have already been contacted by the shelter by phone or letter stating their animal was in our custody, then redirect them to the admissions department. an animal care technician will take the customer to search for their lost pet. restrooms: located in the first hallway on the left next to the elevators, with additional restrooms outside in the quad area. barn animals: ask customers requesting information on barn animals to go out through the double doors to the livestock board on the wall in the main quad area next to the vending machine. this board gives detailed information on which animals are available for adoption or on stray hold. anyone interested in adopting a barn animal should be referred to a staff member. rescues: direct rescue organizations to the rescue office located next to the kiosk. if rescue coordinator staff are occupied when rescue groups arrive for their appointment, get the rescue name and the animal id number when calling for rescue staff. adoptions: direct the public to go out through the double doors and make a right toward the kennel buildings. cats are housed in the first building, marked wond-purr-land. dogs are housed in the next two buildings, marked barking glen and wagging acres. if the customer has an animal id ready for possible adoption or viewing, an animal care technician can assist them with the adoption. animal socialization - cats wash hands in between handling cats and kittens. hand sanitizer stations are located on the wall. do an initial check of all the cat kennels and pick which cat you would like to socialize with. it may be best to ask staff if a particular cat needs to be worked with. step stools are located by the food bins and can be used to reach top kennels. socialize the cat in the community cat room first. if the visitation rooms are available, you may use them. to use a visitation room, put the cat in one of the sanitized blue cat crates to transfer it. never transfer a cat without using a blue crate, and never use cardboard boxes to transport cats. when picking up a cat, slip one hand under the chest while holding the front legs gently but firmly with your fingers, and cup your free hand under the cat's hindquarters. use enrichment items such as feather toys, balls, and jingle bells to engage with the cat. for infection control purposes, once a toy goes into a cat's kennel, it is that cat's toy to keep until it is properly sanitized and can be used again with another cat. place a gone for a visit sign in front of the cat's kennel card. if you notice a cat has any health issues (sneezing, watery eyes, discharge, diarrhea) or behavior issues, write the animal id number on the boards located on both inner sides of the cat building and notify an animal care technician or staff member. do not re-use the same blue crate after socializing. take the used blue crate and place it into the dish room to be sanitized. return all items you may have used to their correct locations (step stool, laser toy, etc.). if any of the cat food or water dishes are empty, refill them. use watering cans to refill water bowls. if bowls are messy or dirty, get a new bowl out of the cabinet. do not put the cat food bowl into the storage bin; always use the food scooper located in the bucket under the storage bin, and only fill the food bowl halfway. remove and replace any dirty blankets or toys. place dirty items in designated areas identified by animal care technicians. when assisting with cleaning, perform tasks one cage at a time so you do not mix litter pans belonging to a different cat, to prevent disease. check the litter pans and clean litter if needed. clean pans are located under the counter labeled cat litter pans/bowls. if the litter pan is dirty, retrieve a new pan, fill it with litter, and place it back into the proper kennel. when socializing with cats, only approach the friendly ones. always socialize the kittens first before any adult cats. kittens under the age of 5 months may not be handled or socialized (subject to change due to illnesses; check with animal care technicians). help keep the counters, floors, and other surfaces clean and safe, and return tools to their proper locations before you leave your area. visitation and adoption - cats smile and welcome every visitor as they enter each animal pod. ask and offer help. always be polite and courteous, even to the most disgruntled visitor. should you need assistance with anything or anyone, ask an employee. if a customer expresses interest in visiting with a cat, ask if they are interested in adopting. if not, explain that we only pull animals for prospective adopters, to ensure that animals are always available for potential adopters. {"step": "prepare to pull the cat for possible adoption:", "sub": ["verify the cat's age. the cat must be 5 months of age or older; if younger than 5 months, call an animal care technician for assistance.", "availability: the review date on the kennel card must be the current date or in the past.", "behavior status: the status at the top of the kennel card must not indicate observed. if observed is identified, call for an animal care technician. animals with an under vet care sign may not be handled or pulled.", "place an out for visitation sign on the outside of the kennel door where the kennel card is located, making sure not to cover the card.", "put the cat in one of the sanitized blue cat crates to transfer to the visitation room. never transfer a cat without using a blue crate; never use cardboard boxes."]} wait until everyone in the customer's group is in the visitation room and the door is completely shut before you let the cat out of the blue crate. use your best judgment with large groups; only allow 2 to 4 people in with the cat at a time. never leave the customers unattended with the cat, and never give medical or behavioral advice to the customer. at the end of each visitation, place the cat back into the blue crate before opening the door. {"step": "ask if they would like to adopt:", "sub": ["no: thank the public for coming in, place the cat back into its kennel using a blue crate, remove the gone visiting sign, and spray the blue crate with disinfectant to leave it clean for the next cat.", "yes: mark adopted and the current date on the kennel card. have the adopter fill out adoption paperwork. ask an animal care technician to explain the paperwork to the public, including the felv/fiv test, and print out an extra kennel card. while the adopter is filling out paperwork, take the cat to receiving for a spay/neuter check (vet check). after the adopter completes the paperwork, ask the act to sign off. take the adopter and completed paperwork to the lobby, instruct them to have a seat, and explain they will be called up by name. congratulate the adopter."]} {"step": "cat is already spayed/neutered:", "sub": ["set the purple kennel card paperwork in the adoption check, microchip, and/or vaccinations), with the virus check request slip and the kennel card.", "stand behind the tape line and wait to be called. inform vet staff that you are there for an adoption
