adopter to pay at the licensing windows located in the hallway in the lobby and come back to the cat building with the receipt for pickup.", "get a cardboard cat carrier ready if the cat is going home (located in the second office in the cat room). do not put the cat into the carrier until the customer comes back with the adoption receipt; the cat can be placed back into its original kennel until the adopter returns. the cat is given to the adopter in the cat building, wond-purr-land, after the adoption receipt is reviewed by an animal care technician.", "log the adoption in the cat binder and place the dirty blue crate in the dish room."]} {"step": "cat is not spayed/neutered:", "sub": ["if the cat is determined to be too young or too sick for surgery, the cat will go home on contract and come back later to have the surgery done. if the cat is 3 months old or less and going home on contract, the blood test will be done during the time of surgery.", "if veterinary staff determine the cat is healthy enough for surgery, it will stay for surgery, a note will be entered in chameleon, and a new kennel card will be printed.", "ask the customer to pay for the adoption at the licensing window. the customer will be instructed at that time to wait for the phone call from the clinic when their cat is available for pickup after the spay/neuter surgery.", "put the cat back in its original kennel with its kennel card clearly marked adopted.", "place a red collar on the cat with the animal id number and date. red collars are located in the animal care technician office inside the first drawer of the filing cabinet."]} cat photography wash or sanitize hands in between handling cats and kittens. check out a badge to access available cats through the animal services supervisor or volunteer coordinator staff. have a pen and notepad on hand. always return borrowed supplies to the volunteer office and notify the volunteer coordinator staff if supplies are running low. select the cat you would like to photograph. ensure the kennel card says adoptable and not treatable; treatable cats may only be photographed within their kennel. litters of kittens may be transported within the same blue crate and photographed individually only in the socialization/visitation room. unlock the kennel door, note which kennel the cat came from, transport the cat using a sanitized blue crate, and be aware of any sudden changes in the cat's body language or behavior. dress-up: you may use the items found in the photography room to dress up the animal, and brush the pet using a brush found in the grooming area. use of the photo signs is optional. photograph the cat and log the cat into the photography log. return the cat to its original kennel, referring to your note. once complete, notify volunteer coordinator staff to edit, upload, and print new kennel cards with the new photos. allow time for completion of this task. animal socialization - dogs wash your hands in between handling dogs. check out the dog kennel key from the front kiosk desk. keys will be hanging inside the key box. if keys are not available, ask a supervisor or animal care technician to assist. {"step": "before removing any dog, have the following supplies:", "sub": ["dog leash (inspect it for damage).", "animal waste bag.", "out for walk sign to be placed on kennels notifying staff and public of the animal's absence.", "a pen and notepad to note which kennel the animal was pulled from.", "if supplies are running low, notify your volunteer coordinator staff. always return borrowed supplies to the volunteer office."]} {"step": "before pulling any dog, review the kennel card:", "sub": ["only animals with purple and white kennel cards are available for pulling.", "age: the dog must be 7 months of age or older.", "availability: the review date on the kennel card must be the current date or in the past.", "behavior status: the status at the top of the kennel card must not indicate observed, even if the kennel card is purple.", "animals with an under vet care sign may not be handled or pulled."]} {"step": "once you have selected a social and friendly dog:", "sub": dog has other dogs in its kennel, use the guillotine door to separate them before you put the dog in. after you put the dog in and secure the door, reopen the guillotine door slowly to reintroduce the dogs.", "remove the out for a walk sign.", "log the socialization in the dog binder and mark the back of the kennel card with today's date to let others know when the dog was walked.", "include any observations regarding the dog's behavior while being socialized."]} when describing an animal's behavior, keep descriptions simple but clear. avoid words or descriptions that are vague or could be interpreted differently by different people. do not use descriptions like "dog is sweet." explain the behavior observed, for example: "dog has a very relaxed body and immediately approached me with a low wagging tail." if unsure, ask an animal care technician. visitation and adoption - dogs wash your hands in between handling dogs. smile and welcome every visitor as they enter each animal pod. ask and offer help. always be polite and courteous, even to the most disgruntled visitor. if a customer expresses interest in visiting with a dog, ask if they are interested in adopting. if not, explain that we only pull animals for prospective adopters. if yes, ask if they have the animal id number available. {"step": "before pulling any dog, review the kennel card:", "sub": ["age: the dog must be 6 months of age or older. if the public wishes to view a puppy 6 months or younger, have an animal care technician assist.", "availability: the review date on the kennel card must be the current date or in the past.", "behavior status: if the status at the top of the kennel card indicates observed, do not pull the dog. immediately see an animal care technician.", "any handwritten notes (a.a. for animal aggressive, f.a. for food aggressive, n.o.d. for no other dog): do not pull the dog. immediately see an animal care technician.", "if multiple dogs are within a single kennel, none of the dogs may be pulled.", "animals with an under vet care sign may not be handled or pulled."]} place an out for visitation sign on the outside of the kennel. take down key facts from the kennel card such as the dog's age, sex, spay/neuter status, weight, and the kennel number the dog was taken from. ask the customer to step outside of the kennel while you pull the dog from the kennel and pod. get the leash ready before you unlock the door, separating kennel mates with the guillotine door if needed. unlock the kennel door, leash up the dog, and always close and lock the kennel door. escort the dog and potential adopters to the get acquainted room or outside play yards. only walk and show dogs in designated areas. if a potential adopter is requesting a meet and greet, notify an animal care technician. never do a meet and greet with any of the shelter dogs or the public's dog. keep at least a 6 to 10 foot distance between all dogs, be aware of any sudden changes in the dog's body language or behavior, and never give out medical or behavioral advice. do not hand over the leash to a potential adopter. the leash must stay in the volunteer's hands at all times, no exceptions. dogs may only be walked by the potential adopter within the confines of the play yards, never outside of them. a second leash can be used so that the potential adopter can walk the dog, but the volunteer must not let go of their leash at any time. if you do not know the answer to a question, refer them to an animal care technician. always pick up your dog's waste. {"step": "at the conclusion of the visitation, ask if they would like to adopt:", "sub": ["no: thank the public for coming in. return the dog to its original kennel, double checking the kennel card. if there are new dogs in the kennel, stop and ask staff before re-kenneling. if the dog has kennel mates, use the guillotine door, then reopen it slowly to reintroduce the dogs. log the visitation in the dog binder.", "yes: have the adopter fill out the adoption paperwork on the clipboard you hand them. while the adopter is filling out paperwork, take the dog for a vet exam located in receiving, making sure to pull the kennel card for the dog you are taking. after the adopter completes the paperwork, ask the animal care technician to sign off. take the adopter and completed paperwork to the lobby, instruct the adopter to have a seat, and explain that the animal care technician will meet with them in the counseling room. congratulate the adopter."]} {"step": "spay and neuter:", "sub": ["dog is spayed/neutered: set the paperwork in the adoption rack and take the dog to receiving for a final check, microchip, and/or vaccinations. stand behind the taped line and wait to be helped; receiving can be very busy. the dog is taken to the new owners in the lobby after the adoption is paid for.", "dog is not spayed/neutered: take the dog to receiving for a final check, microchip, and/or vaccinations. return the dog to its original kennel, log the adoption in the dog binder, and remove the out visiting sign."]} dog photography wash hands in between handling dogs and puppies. make sure to have your photo identification card to check out a kennel key and leash from the designated area. ask staff for assistance pulling dogs from the life room kennels. grab a dog leash and animal waste bags. a pen and notepad are highly recommended. always return borrowed supplies to the volunteer office or kiosk desk, and notify the volunteer coordinator staff if supplies are running low. puppies under 6 months of age should not be taken out of their kennel for photographing. select the dogs you would like to photograph. ensure the kennel card says adoptable and not treatable. never pull a dog from the outside of the kennel. if there is more than one dog, separate the other dogs using the guillotine door, asking for help if needed. get the leash ready before you unlock the door, unlock the kennel door, leash up the dog per your training, and always close and lock the kennel door. take the dog on a potty break before photographing, walking only in designated walking areas. always pick up your dog's waste. never do a meet and greet with any shelter dogs or the public's dogs. keep a 6 to 10 foot distance between all dogs and be aware of any sudden changes in the dog's body language or behavior. dress-up: you may use the items found in the photography room to dress up the animal. use of the photo signs is optional. photograph the dog and log the dog into the photography log. return the dog to its original kennel, referring to your note. if the dog has kennel mates, use the guillotine door to separate them before you put the dog in, then slowly move the guillotine door up to reintroduce the dog. if there are any new dogs in the kennel, ask staff before adding the dog back. wash your hands in between handling dogs. once complete, notify volunteer coordinator staff to edit, upload, and print new kennel cards with the new photos. allow time for completion of this task. brushing and bathing - dogs wash your hands in between handling dogs. check the grooming room for required supplies: towels/rags, shampoo, and brushes/combs. make sure the wash tub and kennels in the grooming room have been sanitized and wiped clean prior to use. if they have not been, clean them now. make sure to have your photo identification card to check out a kennel key and leash from the kiosk. {"step": "check in with staff to receive a list of dogs that need to be washed, and review each kennel card:", "sub": ["only dogs that are one year or older and adoptable are allowed to be bathed and groomed.", "extremely matted dogs should not be pulled for grooming unless you are a professional groomer recognized by our department or have been properly trained to handle animals needing extensive grooming. if you are a professional groomer, talk to the volunteer services manager or volunteer coordinator before you begin. all volunteers who want to work with dogs requiring significant grooming will be trained on proper techniques prior to working with these animals.", "check with staff to see if there are any dogs that need to be bathed before they are scheduled for surgery.", "if a dog has been recently spayed or neutered, always ask an animal care technician to check the surgery date and incisions to ensure the dog is suitable for bathing and grooming.", "make sure you can handle the dog you are going to bathe or groom. ask for help if necessary and always work with another volunteer or staff person."]} place an out for a bath sign on the kennel to indicate the dog is out of the kennel and will be returning, and write down the animal id and kennel number before taking the dog out. always follow the procedures for pulling dogs: get the leash ready before you unlock the door, use the guillotine door to separate kennel mates, carefully enter the kennel and leash the dog, exit taking care to avoid letting other dogs out, and always close and lock the kennel door and open the guillotine door. walk or carry the dog to the grass and allow the animal an opportunity to go to the restroom, then proceed to the grooming room. only walk dogs in designated walking areas and always pick up your dog's waste. do not carry a dog that is too heavy for you; always request help. be aware of any sudden changes in the dog's body language or behavior. fill out the grooming log with the correct information. if sheets are needed for the log book, ask a staff member for copies. place the dog on the grooming table, fit the noose/harness to the animal, and assess the coat for necessary grooming procedures. comb tangles and remove matts if necessary; only remove matts if you are trained. either walk the dog up the ramp or stairs into the tub or pick up the dog and place it gently inside the tub. place the grooming noose or tub harness gently around the dog's neck and clip it to the tub loops to safely restrain the animal. do not leave the animal unattended while in the tub. adjust the water temperature. if using warm water, preheat and test the water before using it on the dog. test the water temperature to ensure it is not too hot or cold; temperature may depend on weather. begin rinsing the dog from top to bottom, being careful to apply water gently around the face and other sensitive areas. choose a shampoo suitable for the coat type and purpose, read the application instructions, and apply shampoo washing from the top of the dog's head down to the tail. make sure not to get water or shampoo in the dog's eyes, mouth, nose, or ears. use small amounts of shampoo and add water to create a lather to work through the coat. if conditioner is desired, apply it to the coat along with the shampoo. rinse thoroughly from top to bottom, head to tail, until shampoo and conditioner residue cannot be felt. if the dog is not rinsed thoroughly, the coat will have a greasy residue which will irritate the skin and attract dirt. pick a suitable towel and begin to towel dry the dog in the tub. wrap the dog in a towel and transfer it to the grooming table, securing with the noose/harness. if the dog is too big for the table, place the dog on the floor and secure it to the table with a leash. apply any desired conditioning sprays to the coat after towel drying. turn on the blow dryer and watch the dog's reaction: if the reaction is anxious, proceed with towel drying; if positive, proceed with blow drying. when the animal is dry, use a comb to brush the hair, then use a slicker brush to smooth out the coat. sign out of the grooming book. return the dog to its proper kennel by double checking the kennel card and following the standard re-kenneling steps. remove the out for bath sign from the kennel door, log the bath in the dog binder, and include any observations regarding the dog's behavior while being bathed, using simple but clear descriptions. return to the grooming room and clean and disinfect everything that was used (tub, brushes, floor, etc.). place all used towels inside a bag and take them to the laundry room. sweep up debris from the floor and place it in the garbage can. if the garbage can is full, remove the bag, replace the garbage bag, turn off the light, shut the door, and take the dirty garbage bag to the dumpster. events onsite shelter events: check in at the designated check-in area at your scheduled time. ask at check-in what your task will be. if you do not feel comfortable doing a certain task, tell staff immediately. do the task you have been given, and report any problems to a staff member. offsite shelter events: follow the same check-in and task steps as onsite events. it is especially important that you always tell a lead staff member where you are going before you leave offsite events. remember to be respectful while volunteering, as you are representing the riverside county department of animal services. laundry {"step": "washer:", "sub": ["put on gloves. it is extremely important that gloves are worn, as you will be working with various germs and diseases.", "throw out bloody items or items that are heavily soiled with a lot of feces on them.", "separate the towels, sheets, and blankets into separate piles. never wash collars, leashes, or metal tags.", "load the washer until three-quarters full; the washer will hold up to 100 pounds. do not overload, and do not run when the load is less than two-thirds full. large beds, heavy blankets, and pillows: only half full. the washer will stop if the load is too heavy.", "push the red sticker on the door then pull the handle to open the washer.", "once the items are in the washer, close and secure the door, ensuring nothing is pinched in the door seal.", "soap or detergent is automatically dispensed with the correct amount.", "the washer should be preset to heavy soil - hot. press 01, then start."]} {"step": "dryer:", "sub": ["first, check the lint trap on the bottom of the dryer and clean it out.", "put washed items into the dryer, untangling items when possible. no plastic toys, no cat beds, and no large dog beds.", "the dryer is preset; just press start. set the cool time for 10 minutes and push the green button to start.", "pillows and some thick items must be dried twice.", "fold items and put them on shelves. only clean items should be placed on the table, shelves, and in cubbies."]} clerical duties general office duties include but are not limited to filing, typing/data entry, and answering telephones, as needed and directed by the volunteer services manager. clinic cage cleaning be prepared with the following supplies: gloves, disinfecting spray, paper towels, scrubbing pad, trashcan, and newspaper and shredded paper (when available). place a pair of gloves on each hand. empty the cage of any solid waste and newspapers. if there is a towel or blanket in the cage, remove any solid waste and then place it into the dirty laundry basket. use newspaper and/or paper towels to soak up any urine or water in the kennel. spray all five sides of the cage liberally with disinfectant, and spray the cage door liberally with disinfectant for at least 10 minutes. if there is more than one cage to be cleaned, set up the other cages by following the same steps. after soaking the cages, wipe them down with gloved hands using the scrubbing pad, paper towels, and more spray if needed. wipe down the cage doors, paying particular attention to the crevices where the metal meets, where mucus, urine, and feces can build up. change gloves as often as needed. if needed, the cage door can be removed for cleaning; see a veterinary assistant or registered veterinary technician for assistance. when the cage is dry, place a layer of newspaper at least 5 sheets thick on the entire bottom of the cage. in double cages you can use more newspaper, as bigger animals make bigger messes. bring all of the dirty laundry to the outside laundry room. bring back the same equivalent of items you dropped off. for example, if you dropped off 10 dirty towels, bring back 10 clean towels. dishwashing wear gloves. wipe litter out of litter pans with paper towels. using a small clean bucket, mix a 50/50 solution of triple two and water. using the green triple two solution in the bucket, use a sponge to wipe out food bowls, litter pans, and similar items. once they have been soaked and cleaned out, insert dishes into the commercial dishwasher, which will steam off any germs that may not be visible. press the only button on the right-hand side to start the steaming process, which takes about 30 seconds. allow the dishes to get up to a minimum temperature of 140 degrees. carefully remove the sanitized dishes from the commercial washer; they may be hot. place a towel on the counter to allow them to dry, or hand dry them using a clean towel. once dry, place the dishes on the shelf and neatly organize them. ensure that the dish room is clean and that any excess water on the floor or counter has been wiped down before leaving. livestock and barn schedule dates and times with the volunteer coordinator. dates for volunteering include monday through friday, 8:30 a.m. to 10:30 a.m. check in at the volunteer office when you arrive, and check in with staff working in the barn during the time you are volunteering. staff will instruct you on what needs to be accomplished. review the livestock white board on the tack room door to see what animals are present and which animals can be interacted with. some animals are restricted to staff only; staff will inform you of these animals when you arrive. feeding barn animals: information is posted on the white board. staff will organize feed and supplements prior to feeding and will instruct you on what to feed and the quantities required for the individual animals. prior to entering stalls or cages, observe the animals' behavior. if the animals appear agitated or aggressive, do not enter the stall; ask staff for assistance. entering stalls: be aware of the location of the animal, open the cage or gate slowly, open the gate just enough so that you can fit through but the animal cannot, and close the door behind you. assisting with cleaning stalls: enter the stall following the steps above, attain proper tools (manure fork, manure bucket), slide the manure bucket under the stall rails away from the stall door, use the manure fork to pick up manure and place it in the large manure buckets, slide the bucket under the stall rail when done, and exit safely to dump the manure. if the bucket is heavy, ask for help. {"step": "grooming barn animals (only after cleaning is finished), using tools kept in the tack room:", "sub": ["use a rubber curry comb to remove dirt and debris from the coat, in a circular motion on fleshy areas. do not use it on the face or below the knee.", "use a hard brush to remove the loosened dirt and debris. the hard brush can be used on fleshy areas and down the legs, but not on the face.", "use a soft brush to remove remaining dirt and debris on the top of the coat. this brush can be used on the face and sensitive areas.", "use a moist rag to wipe around the eyes and nose if the horse allows it.", "place all tools in their designated areas when you are finished for the day."]} agility yard and visitation yards the big arena with bleachers is used for watching demonstrations such as agility, police k-9, and frisbee dogs. this arena is also used for evening and special events. fenced grass areas located behind each dog building are dedicated as dog visitation yards. the large grass quad located at the center of the shelter can also be used for dog visitations. the dog agility yard is available for dogs over 25 pounds to exercise off leash while being supervised by a staff member or volunteer. only one person with one dog is allowed in the agility yard at any given time.

documents.title: The Chameleon Bite Window
documents.flag: Numbered (=Policy per rule) but reads as a step-by-step protocol
documents.purpose: To establish a uniform procedure for entering animal bite or scratch reports received using the Chameleon software system. The information entered is the basis for the compilation of statistics required by the State annually.
documents.authority: Approved by the Director of Animal Services. Bite cases are closed by the Chief Veterinarian or his designee.
documents.scope: Applies to the Veterinary Staff or Office Assistant III or back-up staff assigned to the Rabies Control Desk.
documents.supersedes: Policy 000-02, effective December 21, 2016 (revised from November 21, 2011; revised from May 28, 2007)
references: Rabies Control Investigation Report (Bite/Scratch Report) form

definitions.def: The Chameleon screen used to store animal bite or scratch records based on the Rabies Control Investigation Report.
Fluorescent rabies antibody test (head test) performed by the Public Health Lab; status code used while waiting for test results.
Unable to quarantine; disposition entered for unable to quarantine reports.

sections.heading: 1. When a Bite Window Record Must Be Stored

steps.step: Store a bite window record using the Chameleon Bite Window screen based on the Rabies Control Investigation Report with any of the following results:
sub: Quarantine has been imposed on the biting or scratching animal.
The animal involved in a bite or scratch has been released from quarantine.
The case was closed by the Chief Veterinarian or his designee.
The results of rabies testing for a suspected rabid animal were received (i.e., Survey Only, Wild Animal vs. Human).

sections.heading: 2. Bite Information (ALT+1)
steps: BITE NO. The computer will generate a number when the screen is stored.
TYPE. Refer to Line 34 of the Rabies Control Investigation Report and enter the applicable bite type code (see Appendix A: Bite Type Codes).
BITE DATE. Refer to Line 12a of the Rabies Control Investigation Report. Enter the numerical date and time of the exposure or skin break.
REPORT DATE. Refer to Line 10 of the Rabies Control Investigation Report. Enter the date the report was received.

steps.step: ACTIVITY NO. Used for ACOs only.
sub: Refer to Line 1c of the Rabies Control Investigation Report.
Input the activity number from the top right-hand corner of the Rabies Control Investigation Report.
Press F4. This takes you to the activity screen (animal and, if applicable, owner PID are to be completed by the ACO).
Press the Enter key and the owner and animal ID will transfer to the bite screen.

steps.step: TAG NO. Refer to Line 36 of the Rabies Control Investigation Report.
sub: If the CONTROLLED BY field is equal to LIC, this field must have a complete license number (LYY-######).
If the biting animal is currently licensed and rabies vaccinated, this field must be filled.

steps.step: STATUS. Enter the status of the investigation using one of the following codes:
sub: FRA: used when waiting for FRA test results.
PENDING APR: used to flag that the bite report is waiting for Chief Veterinarian or designee approval for UTQs.
CLOSED: used only when the biting animal has been released from quarantine.
RTN FIELD: used to flag that the bite report was returned to field services for corrections.
RTN VET: used to flag that the bite report was returned to veterinary services for corrections.
RTN OTHER: used to flag that the bite report was returned to units other than field services or veterinary services.
YELLOW: rabies desk only has the yellow copy of the bite report.

sections.heading: 3. Biting Animal Information (ALT+2)

steps.step: OWNER ID. Refer to Lines 32a-33 of the Rabies Control Investigation Report.
sub: Create an owner ID if not found in Chameleon.
Verify that the information on the bite report is the same as in Chameleon.

steps.step: ANIMAL ID. Create an animal ID, if not done by the ACO. Refer to Lines 34 and 35 of the Rabies Control Investigation Report.
sub: Mark the bite box with a Y. This will create a bite icon for the animal.
Write the animal ID number on Line 1a of the bite report.

steps: PRIOR BITES. Enter the number of prior bites pertaining to the specific biting animal.

steps.step: VALID VAC. Type Y if the rabies vaccination was current at the time of the QI.
sub: Refer to Line 39 of the Rabies Control Investigation Report.
Leave blank if the Controlled By field shows STRAY or NOVAC.

steps.step: CONTROLLED BY. Must be used for DOG10/CAT10 bites only. Use the abbreviation that applies:
sub: STRAY = no owner information.
LIC = current license and vaccination.
VAC = current rabies vaccine but no license.
NOVAC = owned but no rabies vaccine or license.

steps: INCIDENT LOCATION. Enter the exact address of where the bite occurred or the descriptive location of occurrence. Press F4 to enter the street number and correct spelling of the street name. This information is taken from Line 11, Place of Attack.
GEO. Take this information from the Activity Window GeoCode. It refers to the patrol area so the Animal Control Officer can respond. See MAP REF of the Rabies Control Investigation Report.

JURISDICTION. This information should reflect the pet owner's city jurisdiction, or the jurisdiction of the city where the attack took place, or if there is no owner, should reflect the victim's city jurisdiction.
PRICE. Used for RAC home quarantine reports only. Enter the home quarantine fee.

steps.step: RECEIPT NO. Press F4 then F12.
sub: Find the receipt number created by the ACO.
If the owner has been billed, leave the box empty.

sections.heading: 4. Victim Information (ALT+3)

steps.step: VICTIM ID. Create a victim ID if not found in Chameleon.
sub: This field refers to a person who was exposed or had a skin break.
If victim information is unavailable or incomplete and there is owner information, leave blank.

steps: AGE. Refer to Line 4. Enter the age of the victim in whole numbers.

steps.step: RELATION. Enter the applicable relation code:
sub: V/O: the victim is also the owner of the biting or scratching animal.
NEIGHBOR: the victim resides in the vicinity of the biting or scratching animal.
FRIEND: the victim does not reside in the area of the biting or scratching animal, nor is the person the owner of the biting or scratching animal.
USPS: the victim is a mail carrier.
METER: the victim is a meter reader for a gas company or electric company.

steps: LOCATION. Refer to Line 14. Enter the general location of injury on the victim (such as leg, arm, face, calf). 10 characters only.
SEVERITY. Describe the general injury from the skin break: Punc = puncture, Lac = laceration, or Tear. 10 characters only.
CIRCUMSTANCE. Describe the circumstance (i.e., petting, grooming, walking by, riding by, attacked, injured, scared). 10 characters only.
ANI VIC. If the victim is an animal (DOG30, DOG180, CAT30, CAT180), create an animal ID and skip to Begin Date.
GUARDIAN. If the victim is a minor, prior to creating a parent's person record, perform a search by street address, then by phone number, and finally by name.
WITNESS. If there is a witness to the incident reporting the incident other than the person involved, create the person's record following procedures.

sections.heading: 5. Treatment Information (ALT+4)
steps: TREATMENT BY. Refer to Line 17 (Dr., self, parent, fire dept.).
DATE. Refer to Line 16. Enter the date of treatment.
DESC. Refer to Line 16. Describe the treatment received: cleansed, dressed, meds, antibiotics, sutures, plastic surgery, etc.

sections.heading: 6. Synopsis (ALT+5)
steps: Refer to Line 31 of the Rabies Control Investigation Report.
Enter a concise or condensed summary of the pertinent information relating to the bite circumstances. This is not intended for documenting other investigations such as PDRs.

sections.heading: 7. Quarantine Information (ALT+6)
steps: QUARANTINE BEGIN DATE. Enter the date the quarantine is imposed. See the Line 40 date of the bite report.
BY. Enter the RAC Chameleon ID, last name, or agency abbreviation of the person imposing the quarantine (RAC Field Officer, Chief Veterinarian, or Shelter Registered Vet Tech).
RESPONSIBLE ID. Enter the Person ID of the initial staff member imposing the quarantine. Refer to Line 40.

steps.step: LOCATION. Refer to Line 40 of the Rabies Control Investigation Report.
sub: Enter SHELTER if the quarantined animal is impounded at an RAC shelter.
Enter HOME if the quarantine was imposed by RAC at home.
Enter VET if the quarantine was imposed by RAC at an approved veterinarian.

steps: END DATE. Refer to Line 43 of the Rabies Control Investigation Report. Enter the numerical date the quarantine was ended or the date the case was closed.

ABATED BY. Refer to Line 43 of the Rabies Control Investigation Report. Enter the initial and full last name of the person who released the animal from quarantine, the Field Officer's Person ID (999385), or the Chief Veterinarian who closed out the investigation.

steps.step: EUTH DATE. Refer to Line 44 of the Rabies Control Investigation Report.
sub: For use only if the animal involved in the bite or scratch was not quarantined.
Enter the date the animal being tested was euthanized. This must match the euthanasia date in the Kennel Window.

steps: EUTH BY. Refer to Line 44 of the Rabies Control Investigation Report. Enter the initial and full last name of the person who euthanized the animal. This must match the Outcome By field in the Kennel Window.

steps.step: NOTIFY DATE. Refer to Line 28 of the Rabies Control Investigation Report.
sub: To be used only by the Rabies Control desk or back-up.
Enter the date the victim or guardian was notified of the quarantine or head test results.

steps: NOTIFY BY. Refer to Line 28 of the Rabies Control Investigation Report. Enter the first initial and full last name of the Rabies Control clerk.

sections.heading: 8. Lab Information (ALT+7)

steps.step: TOLAB DATE. Refer to the Activity New Date of the Activity Window for transporting the specimen.
sub: This date must match the activity new date for transporting the specimen to the Public Health Lab.

steps: LAB SPECIMEN NO. Refer to Line 21 of the Rabies Control Investigation Report. Enter the numerical code given by the lab. It runs along the left upper side of the bite report.
EXAM DATE. Refer to Line 21 of the bite report. Enter the numerical date when the lab received the specimen.
DISPOSITION. Type Head Test for FRA tests. Enter UTQ for unable to quarantine reports.
HEAD DATE. Refer to Line 22 of the Rabies Control Investigation Report. Enter the date the specimen was tested by the lab.
HEAD RESULT. Refer to Line 23 of the bite report. To be entered as negative, positive, or unsat only.

sections.heading: 9. Storing the Record
steps: Store the record by clicking on the plus (+) icon or pressing the F9 key.

related.name: Kennel Window (euthanasia date and Outcome By must match)

appendices: Appendix A: Bite Type Codes (inlined below)
Appendix B: Rabies Control Investigation Report (Bite/Scratch Report) form (attached separately)
Appendix C: Chameleon Bite Investigation screen reference image (attached separately)

revisions.author: Outcomes for Pets (draft)
revisions.desc: Migrated into standard RCDAS SOP template. Content unchanged from 2016 version; substantive edits pending (see edit log).

documents.title: Cash Handling
documents.purpose: To establish procedures for money handling by employees within the Department of Animal Services working at all DAS shelters.
documents.authority: Approved by the Director of Animal Services. The Fiscal Division administers cash handling training, cash drawer issuance, and deposit processing. All new employees must attend the DAS Fiscal cash handling class prior to handling cash and on an annual basis thereafter.
documents.scope: This procedure is applicable to all personnel who are authorized to handle financial transactions. DAS employees who handle financial transactions must be knowledgeable in the cash handling procedure prior to working with cash, check, or credit card payment processing, and must know the process for checking a cash drawer in and out and submitting monies for deposit with the Fiscal Division within DAS.
documents.supersedes: Policy 000-22, effective July 1, 2017 (revised from September 29, 2009; revised from June 14, 2007)
references: DAS Policy 000-65, Counterfeit Bills
DAS Policy 000-24, Cash Box Issuance
Board of Supervisors Policy B-1, Acceptance of Negotiable Paper
DAS Policy 000-66, Completion of the Chameleon Receipt Window
SOP: How to Input Official County Receipts (OCR) into Chameleon

definitions.def: Official County Receipt.
The Fiscal Division staff member designated to issue cash drawers, verify counts, and instruct staff on overage/shortage reporting.

sections.heading: 1. Cash Drawer Cash Sign Out Sheet (Attachment A)
steps: Have the fiscal designee fill out the Cash Drawer Cash Sign Out Sheet for release. For more information, refer to DAS Policy 000-24, Cash Box Issuance.
The fiscal designee counts the cash in the presence of the employee.
The employee verifies the cash by counting the monies in front of the fiscal designee.
Both the employee and the verifier must sign the Cash Drawer Cash Sign Out Sheet (Attachment A).
Take the cash to the proper work location and keep it secured and out of the view of the public.
At the end of the business day, separate the cash drawer money from the cash funds and place it in the deposit envelope.

sections.heading: 2. Cash Drawers
steps: Keep cash drawers locked and inaccessible to the public.
Lock cash drawers and remove keys when left unattended.
Do not store cash in cash drawers overnight.

Employees will be held personally liable for the loss of any County monies entrusted to their care, whether due to negligence or failure to follow procedures. Any misuse or misappropriation of collected fees will be reported to local law enforcement as a theft in accordance with County policy.

sections.heading: 3. Safe and Cash Drawer Security
steps: Keep safes and cash drawers locked at all times except when they are under the direct observation and control of an employee who is trusted with the combination or key or is assigned access to the cash or other materials stored in the safe or cash drawer. The supervisor determines who should know the combination of safes or have cash drawer keys in their functional units.

Hold the number of staff with knowledge of a safe combination to an absolute minimum. Change combinations whenever a person with knowledge of the combination is terminated, resigns, retires, transfers, or otherwise is no longer a member of staff of the particular functional unit.
Designate two DAS employees for each safe: one is responsible for locking the safe at the end of the day, while the other checks the safe at the end of the day to ensure it is locked.

The two designated DAS employees are also responsible for maintaining the Safe Sign In/Out Sheet (Attachment B) of incoming and outgoing placements with signatures, dates, and times. Designated employees responsible for all safes will replace the Safe Sign In/Out Sheet when full and will turn in the completed log to the Fiscal Chief.
Never count money in an area which is accessible to or in view of the public.

sections.heading: 4. Cash Handling (General)
steps: All monies collected for DAS immediately become public funds. DAS personnel have the responsibility to ensure that public funds are handled in accordance with all relevant County policies and procedures. Public funds cannot be borrowed, commingled with other monies, or used for personal reasons.

Explain all overages and shortages in any receipts and have the appropriate supervisor sign for them on the Cash Overage/Shortage portion of the deposit envelope (Attachment E). The fiscal designee will instruct the cashier and supervisor on how to completely fill out the Cash Overage/Shortage Daily Report (Attachment D); this report must be filled out by the clerk and their supervisor.
Do not print Chameleon receipts unless the customer requests it; instead, email the receipt to the customer via a current email address.

Do not print receipts for deposits except for receipts requiring signatures, such as payment plan receipts, balance payment receipts, balance credit receipts, and receipts with a negative balance (credits being applied). Memos for all payment plans and balances must be printed on the receipts for deposits, no exceptions.

sections.heading: 5. Acceptable Payment
steps: Per BOS Policy B-1, Acceptance of Negotiable Paper, every officer in the County of Riverside and its judicial districts is authorized to accept negotiable paper, consisting of bank checks and drafts, and express and post office money orders, in payment of any assessment, license, permit, fee, fine, or other monies owing to DAS.
Accept credit cards in some cases as provided in Section 8.
Do not accept foreign monies (foreign coins included).

sections.heading: 6. Cash Acceptance Instructions
steps: Receive the money first before making change or issuing a receipt for service.

steps.step: Place the cash on the sill of the cash drawer in view of the public while change is made and presented to the customer.
sub: Counterfeit bills: refer to DAS Policy 000-65, Counterfeit Bills. Become familiar with currency to avoid accepting a counterfeit bill. Look for differences, not similarities.
Use the counterfeit money detection pen on denominations of $10.00 and above. These pens are available from the Fiscal supply clerk.

steps: Count the cash to the customer by placing it in the customer's hand and counting out all of the money in accurate total. This step assists in giving back accurate change and prevents a loss or shortage to the employee's cash drawer.

Complete the Chameleon receipt and email or hand the appropriate copy to the customer, if requested. Refer to DAS Policy 000-66, Completion of the Chameleon Receipt Window, for further instruction on storing a receipt.
Once collected, place all monies received in the cash drawer, always kept secured and out of the public's view.

sections.heading: 7. Check Acceptance Instructions
steps: Run all checks through the check conversion machine. If a check is rejected from the machine, refer to DAS Policy 000-66, Completion of the Chameleon Receipt Window, on how to process the check.
Verify the check has a pre-printed name and serial number (no temporary checks).
Record the driver's license number, with state abbreviation, on the front of the check (except checks processed via the mail).
Ensure the check is made out to County of Riverside or Department of Animal Services (no third party checks).
Record or verify the physical address on the check. No PO boxes.
Record the phone number with area code on the check.
For check guarantee cards, record the type of card and the expiration date.
Initial the front of the check.
Do not accept checks written in an amount in excess of the exact fee (except checks processed via the mail).
Out of state checks may be accepted, if necessary, upon verification of state issued identification.
Notify customers that the penalty for checks returned by the bank is $25.00 (except checks processed via the mail).
Run the check through the Elavon point of sale machine. Write the Chameleon receipt number legibly, or the OCR number, on all checks and check conversion slips.
Complete the Chameleon receipt and email or give the appropriate copy to the customer, if requested. Refer to DAS Policy 000-66 for further instruction on storing a receipt.
Once collected, place all checks received in the cash drawer, kept secured and out of the public's view.

sections.heading: 8. Credit Card Acceptance
steps: Verify that the name on the credit card matches the identity of the customer making the payment. Collect the credit card number, expiration date, and signature of the cardholder.
Accept Visa or MasterCard as the primary cards.
Notify the customer of the 3% charge for using a credit card.
Process the payment via the Elavon point of sale machine, according to its procedure.
Write the Chameleon receipt number legibly on each credit card receipt.
Complete the Chameleon receipt and email or hand the appropriate copy to the customer, if requested. Refer to DAS Policy 000-66 for further instruction on storing a receipt.
Once collected, place all credit card receipts in the cash drawer, always kept secured and out of the public's view.

sections.heading: 9. Reconciling the Chameleon Generated Daily Deposit Batch Control Slip (Attachment C)
steps: Fill out the Daily Deposit Batch Control Slip (Attachment C) with every deposit. Turn in all OCR receipts the same day they were entered into Chameleon; refer to the SOP on How to Input Official County Receipts (OCR) in Chameleon.

Have the final deposit verified by the employee and either their lead or supervisor on the Daily Deposit Batch Control Slip before it is hand carried to the DAS Fiscal Division for deposit. If the Fiscal Office is closed, place the deposit in the SAFE drop box outside of the Fiscal Office, located inside the second floor break room.

steps.step: The employee will:
sub: Balance all monies collected with the Daily Deposit Batch Slip (Attachment C).
Sign and date the Daily Deposit Batch Slip.

Reconcile the transactions: run a calculator tape or an Excel spreadsheet of total cash, total checks, and credit card totals. Checks run through the machine will be counted as a credit on the tape, on the Deposit Envelope (Attachment E), and in Chameleon; refer to DAS Policy 000-66 for more information.
Initial and date the tape or Excel spreadsheet.
Attach the tape or Excel spreadsheet to the Daily Deposit Batch Slip along with all the credit card and check conversion receipts.

Fill out the front of the deposit envelope: write the total cash, check, and credit card amounts on the lines indicated. The amounts under Total Collected (Actual) and Total County Receipts should match.

For any shortages or overages, enter the amount on the Diff: Over (Short) line and sign the envelope. The fiscal designee will contact you to complete a Cash Overage/Shortage Daily Report (Attachment D) if needed.
Sign and date the deposit envelope (Attachment E).
Place the Chameleon Generated Daily Deposit Batch Control Slip with tapes, money, and receipts in the deposit envelope.
Get the work verified by a supervisor or a lead.
Once verified, walk to the safe with a witness.

steps.step: The verifier will:
sub: Verify actual amounts against the totals indicated on the Daily Deposit Batch Control Slip (Attachment C).
Run a separate calculator tape or Excel spreadsheet.
Initial and date the tape or Excel spreadsheet.
Sign the Daily Deposit Batch Slip.
Sign and seal the deposit envelope (Attachment E).

documents.searchText: to establish procedures for money handling by employees within the department of animal services working at all das shelters. this procedure is applicable to all personnel who are authorized to handle financial transactions. das employees who handle financial transactions must be knowledgeable in the cash handling procedure prior to working with cash, check, or credit card payment processing, and must know the process for checking a cash drawer in and out and submitting monies for deposit with the fiscal division within das. approved by the director of animal services. the fiscal division administers cash handling training, cash drawer issuance, and deposit processing. all new employees must attend the das fiscal cash handling class prior to handling cash and on an annual basis thereafter. ocr official county receipt. fiscal designee the fiscal division staff member designated to issue cash drawers, verify counts, and instruct staff on overage/shortage reporting. 1. cash drawer cash sign out sheet (attachment a) have the fiscal designee fill out the cash drawer cash sign out sheet for release. for more information, refer to das policy 000-24, cash box issuance. the fiscal designee counts the cash in the presence of the employee. the employee verifies the cash by counting the monies in front of the fiscal designee. both the employee and the verifier must sign the cash drawer cash sign out sheet (attachment a). take the cash to the proper work location and keep it secured and out of the view of the public. at the end of the business day, separate the cash drawer money from the cash funds and place it in the deposit envelope. 2. cash drawers keep cash drawers locked and inaccessible to the public. lock cash drawers and remove keys when left unattended. do not store cash in cash drawers overnight. employees will be held personally liable for the loss of any county monies entrusted to their care, whether due to negligence or failure to follow procedures. any misuse or misappropriation of collected fees will be reported to local law enforcement as a theft in accordance with county policy. 3. safe and cash drawer security keep safes and cash drawers locked at all times except when they are under the direct observation and control of an employee who is trusted with the combination or key or is assigned access to the cash or other materials stored in the safe or cash drawer. the supervisor determines who should know the combination of safes or have cash drawer keys in their functional units. hold the number of staff with knowledge of a safe combination to an absolute minimum. change combinations whenever a person with knowledge of the combination is terminated, resigns, retires, transfers, or otherwise is no longer a member of staff of the particular functional unit. designate two das employees for each safe: one is responsible for locking the safe at the end of the day, while the other checks the safe at the end of the day to ensure it is locked. the two designated das employees are also responsible for maintaining the safe sign in/out sheet (attachment b) of incoming and outgoing placements with signatures, dates, and times. designated employees responsible for all safes will replace the safe sign in/out sheet when full and will turn in the completed log to the fiscal chief. never count money in an area which is accessible to or in view of the public. 4. cash handling (general) all monies collected for das immediately become public funds. das personnel have the responsibility to ensure that public funds are handled in accordance with all relevant county policies and procedures. public funds cannot be borrowed, commingled with other monies, or used for personal reasons. explain all overages and shortages in any receipts and have the appropriate supervisor sign for them on the cash overage/shortage portion of the deposit envelope (attachment e). the fiscal designee will instruct the cashier and supervisor on how to completely fill out the cash overage/shortage daily report (attachment d); this report must be filled out by the clerk and their supervisor. do not print chameleon receipts unless the customer requests it; instead, email the receipt to the customer via a current email address. do not print receipts for deposits except for receipts requiring signatures, such as payment plan receipts, balance payment receipts, balance credit receipts, and receipts with a negative balance (credits being applied). memos for all payment plans and balances must be printed on the receipts for deposits, no exceptions. 5. acceptable payment per bos policy b-1, acceptance of negotiable paper, every officer in the county of riverside and its judicial districts is authorized to accept negotiable paper, consisting of bank checks and drafts, and express and post office money orders, in payment of any assessment, license, permit, fee, fine, or other monies owing to das. accept credit cards in some cases as provided in section 8. do not accept foreign monies (foreign coins included). 6. cash acceptance instructions receive the money first before making change or issuing a receipt for service. {"step": "place the cash on the sill of the cash drawer in view of the public while change is made and presented to the customer.", "sub": ["counterfeit bills: refer to das policy 000-65, counterfeit bills. become familiar with currency to avoid accepting a counterfeit bill. look for differences, not similarities.", "use the counterfeit money detection pen on denominations of $10.00 and above. these pens are available from the fiscal supply clerk."]} count the cash to the customer by placing it in the customer's hand and counting out all of the money in accurate total. this step assists in giving back accurate change and prevents a loss or shortage to the employee's cash drawer. complete the chameleon receipt and email or hand the appropriate copy to the customer, if requested. refer to das policy 000-66, completion of the chameleon receipt window, for further instruction on storing a receipt. once collected, place all monies received in the cash drawer, always kept secured and out of the public's view. 7. check acceptance instructions run all checks through the check conversion machine. if a check is rejected from the machine, refer to das policy 000-66, completion of the chameleon receipt window, on how to process the check. verify the check has a pre-printed name and serial number (no temporary checks). record the driver's license number, with state abbreviation, on the front of the check (except checks processed via the mail). ensure the check is made out to county of riverside or department of animal services (no third party checks). record or verify the physical address on the check. no po boxes. record the phone number with area code on the check. for check guarantee cards, record the type of card and the expiration date. initial the front of the check. do not accept checks written in an amount in excess of the exact fee (except checks processed via the mail). out of state checks may be accepted, if necessary, upon verification of state issued identification. notify customers that the penalty for checks returned by the bank is $25.00 (except checks processed via the mail). run the check through the elavon point of sale machine. write the chameleon receipt number legibly, or the ocr number, on all checks and check conversion slips. complete the chameleon receipt and email or give the appropriate copy to the customer, if requested. refer to das policy 000-66 for further instruction on storing a receipt. once collected, place all checks received in the cash drawer, kept secured and out of the public's view. 8. credit card acceptance verify that the name on the credit card matches the identity of the customer making the payment. collect the credit card number, expiration date, and signature of the cardholder. accept visa or mastercard as the primary cards. notify the customer of the 3% charge for using a credit card. process the payment via the elavon point of sale machine, according to its procedure. write the chameleon receipt number legibly on each credit card receipt. complete the chameleon receipt and email or hand the appropriate copy to the customer, if requested. refer to das policy 000-66 for further instruction on storing a receipt. once collected, place all credit card receipts in the cash drawer, always kept secured and out of the public's view. 9. reconciling the chameleon generated daily deposit batch control slip (attachment c) fill out the daily deposit batch control slip (attachment c) with every deposit. turn in all ocr receipts the same day they were entered into chameleon; refer to the sop on how to input official county receipts (ocr) in chameleon. have the final deposit verified by the employee and either their lead or supervisor on the daily deposit batch control slip before it is hand carried to the das fiscal division for deposit. if the fiscal office is closed, place the deposit in the safe drop box outside of the fiscal office, located inside the second floor break room. {"step": "the employee will:", "sub": ["balance all monies collected with the daily deposit batch slip (attachment c).", "sign and date the daily deposit batch slip.", "reconcile the transactions: run a calculator tape or an excel spreadsheet of total cash, total checks, and credit card totals. checks run through the machine will be counted as a credit on the tape, on the deposit envelope (attachment e), and in chameleon; refer to das policy 000-66 for more information.", "initial and date the tape or excel spreadsheet.", "attach the tape or excel spreadsheet to the daily deposit batch slip along with all the credit card and check conversion receipts.", "fill out the front of the deposit envelope: write the total cash, check, and credit card amounts on the lines indicated. the amounts under total collected (actual) and total county receipts should match.", "for any shortages or overages, enter the amount on the diff: over (short) line and sign the envelope. the fiscal designee will contact you to complete a cash overage/shortage daily report (attachment d) if needed.", "sign and date the deposit envelope (attachment e).", "place the chameleon generated daily deposit batch control slip with tapes, money, and receipts in the deposit envelope.", "get the work verified by a supervisor or a lead.", "once verified, walk to the safe with a witness."]} {"step": "the verifier will:", "sub": ["verify actual amounts against the totals indicated on the daily deposit batch control slip (attachment c).", "run a separate calculator tape or excel spreadsheet.", "initial and date the tape or excel spreadsheet.", "sign the daily deposit batch slip.", "sign and seal the deposit envelope (attachment e)."]} das policy 000-65, counterfeit bills das policy 000-24, cash box issuance board of supervisors policy b-1, acceptance of negotiable paper das policy 000-66, completion of the chameleon receipt window sop: how to input official county receipts (ocr) into chameleon

documents.title: Behavioral Assessment and Pathway Planning for Shelter Animals
documents.purpose: This SOP establishes standardized procedures for gathering behavioral information, assessing behavior, and making pathway decisions for animals in RCDAS care. RCDAS utilizes a multi-source, observation-based approach that gathers information from intake history, daily staff and volunteer observations, structured interactions such as walks and playgroups, and, when indicated, individualized evaluations conducted by qualified behavior staff.
documents.authority: RCDAS management staff have authority over behavioral assessments, pathway assignments, and behavioral outcome decisions.
documents.scope: This procedure applies to all RCDAS staff, volunteers, and foster caregivers at all four campuses (Jurupa Valley, San Jacinto, Coachella Valley, and Blythe) who interact with, observe, handle, or make decisions about animals in RCDAS custody.
documents.supersedes: Policy 000-34, Behavior Assessment of Animals (revised from October 8, 2009; originally April 15, 2008)
references: ASV Guidelines for Standards of Care in Animal Shelters, 2nd Edition (2022), Section 7: Behavioral Health
ASPCA Position Statement on Shelter Dog Behavior Assessments
ASPCA Behavior Pathway Resources
California Food and Agriculture Code
Riverside County Ordinance No. 630
RCDAS Policy 000-34 (revised from October 8, 2009; originally April 15, 2008)

definitions.def: The ongoing collection of information about an animal's behavior through daily interactions, handling, walks, playgroups, socialization sessions, and staff and volunteer reports. This is the primary method of behavioral assessment under this SOP.
A supervised, off-leash group interaction among dogs in an enclosed area that serves as both enrichment and a source of information about social behavior in a more natural setting.
The shelter's current ability to provide behavior assessment, enrichment, modification, and treatment based on available staff expertise, space, time, and resources.

sections.heading: 1. Guiding Principles
steps: Recognize that the shelter environment is inherently stressful, and stress changes animal behavior. A single observation or interaction is insufficient to characterize an animal's behavioral profile.
Gather behavioral information from multiple sources over time whenever possible.
Identify each animal's needs and find the most appropriate pathway to a positive outcome.
Reserve outcome decisions based on behavior for animals whose behavior poses a genuine, documented safety risk that cannot be reasonably managed or modified.

sections.heading: 2. Intake Behavioral Screening

steps.step: Record all behavioral observations during intake in Chameleon, including:
sub: The animal's demeanor during transport and handling.
Any bite history or aggression history reported by the owner, finder, or Animal Control Officer.
Any known behavioral concerns reported by the surrendering owner.
The circumstances of impoundment and any relevant context about the animal's prior living situation.

steps: For owner surrenders, ask about the animal's behavior history using open-ended questions covering interactions with people, children, and other animals, as well as responses to handling, resource guarding, and novel environments.
Flag animals that display severe, unprovoked aggression during intake for immediate management review.

Allow fearful, stressed, or mildly reactive animals time to decompress before further behavioral assessment. Stress-related behavior at intake should be documented but is not a basis for pathway decisions.

sections.heading: 3. Ongoing Behavioral Observation
steps: Document behavior in Chameleon using objective, descriptive language. All staff and volunteers who interact with animals are responsible for this documentation. Describe what the animal did rather than using subjective labels.

steps.step: Gather behavioral information from the following sources throughout the animal's stay:
sub: Daily care interactions: feeding, cleaning, leashing, and general handling.
Walks: leash behavior, response to other animals and environmental stimuli, comfort in novel environments.
Playgroups (dogs): off-leash social behavior, play style, response to handler intervention.
Socialization sessions: one-on-one interactions with staff, volunteers, or potential adopters.
Foster home observations, when available.
Previous owner or finder reports.
Veterinary findings, particularly when medical conditions may influence behavior.

steps: Update behavioral notes in Chameleon promptly whenever an animal's behavior changes.

sections.heading: 4. Behavioral Pathway Assignment
steps: Individual shelter managers assign a behavioral pathway to each animal based on the totality of behavioral information gathered. Document pathway assignments in Chameleon and review them as new information becomes available.

steps.step: Assign one of the following pathways:
sub: Adoption Eligible: the animal's behavior is consistent with safe placement. Adoption counselors review behavioral notes to support appropriate matching.

Adoption Eligible with Disclosure: the animal has identified behavioral needs that are manageable with an informed adopter. Adoption counselors provide written disclosure and adopters sign an acknowledgment.
Transfer to Adoption Partner: the animal's behavioral needs exceed RCDAS's current capacity for care but may be manageable by a rescue or adoption partner with specialized resources.
Euthanasia (Behavioral): reserved for animals whose behavior poses a severe, documented safety risk that cannot be reasonably managed, modified, or safely transferred.

steps: Support timely outcomes through pathway assignments. Behavioral assessment should not delay adoption or transfer for animals that are eligible for placement.

sections.heading: 5. Staff and Volunteer Training
steps: Provide all staff and volunteers who interact with animals with training in body language recognition, objective behavioral documentation, and safe handling techniques as part of onboarding.
Report observations of concerning behavior in Chameleon and, if the behavior poses an immediate safety concern, notify a supervisor directly.

related.name: RCDAS Behavioral Disclosure Acknowledgment Form (Attachment A)
ASV Guidelines for Standards of Care in Animal Shelters, 2nd Edition (2022)
ASPCA Position Statement on Shelter Dog Behavior Assessments

documents.title: Restraint and Care of Personal Pets at All County Shelter Locations
documents.purpose: To provide consistent guidelines and safety practices for DAS employees and volunteers permitted to bring their personal pets to work.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: DAS employees and volunteers permitted to bring their personal pets to work at all County shelter locations.
documents.supersedes: Policy 000-40, effective May 11, 2016 (source is an undated, unsigned draft revision of the 2016 policy)

sections.heading: Policy
steps: It is the intention of the Department to permit personal pets to accompany employees and volunteers to work when their job functions allow such an opportunity.
To ensure the safety of employees, personal pets, and customers of the Department, personal pets must not interfere with day to day work activities or customer interactions.

Animal shelters contain disease and safety hazards. Employees and volunteers electing to bring their personal pets to work acknowledge the potential hazards and risks and understand that the department is not liable for any issues that may arise.
Mutual consent and consideration to fellow staff is required when bringing personal pets into a shared office space.
Obtain approval from your supervisor to ensure the pet's presence does not impact business needs.

Failure to comply with this policy could result in a denial to violators. Widespread failure could result in elimination of this policy and prevention of employees bringing their personal pets to the shelter.

sections.heading: Dogs
steps: Keep dogs on a leash (standard 6-foot leash, no retractable leads) and maintain control over the dog at all times when in public and non-public areas. Exceptions include having the dog contained in a closed play yard, office, or cubicle. Dogs may not run free in open office areas.

steps.step: Dogs should be well-behaved. Bad behavior examples include:
sub: Excessive barking.
Tendency to snarl, growl, or snap to protect food or other resources.
Fearfulness in new situations or around strangers.
Severe attacks on other animals such as cats.
Attempts to mount people's legs.
Snapping and snarling when petted, groomed, or lifted.
Frequent attempts to chase moving objects such as bicycles, skateboards, cars, and trucks.
Biting, regardless of size.

steps: Dogs reported to supervision to be in violation of the well-behaved requirement must complete a Behavioral Assessment completed by a department approved and assigned Animal Behaviorist. The Animal Behaviorist's assessment will be reviewed by the Director of Animal Services or their designee to determine if the dog may return.

Keep dogs a minimum of 25 feet from kennels, corrals, or other areas where shelter animals are being housed outdoors or with outdoor access. Designated play yards and shelter lobbies are exempt from this rule. No personal dogs are permitted inside a pod, critter corner, cat room, or puppy room.

Dogs must be parasite free and not currently sick or suffering from an auto-immune disease or other ailment which may be worsened by exposure at an animal shelter. It is up to the pet owner to decide the best type of flea or tick program for their dog, and treatment is required.

The Department allows only three bathroom accidents per dog. After that the pet must stay home. The dog may return once it has been successfully potty-trained. Puppies up to 4 months in age are exempt from this rule.

Pick up pet waste always and immediately, whether inside or outside. No exceptions. The Department provides garbage cans located outside for owners to easily throw away any feces. Failure to do so could result in lost privileges.

Introduce a personal dog to other dogs only in secured play yards and not in an office or shared area. The owner or custodian of all animals involved must agree to the interaction prior to the interaction taking place. Those introducing personal pets to unfamiliar pets must take great care to ensure all animals are safe and comfortable.
Dogs must be currently licensed, vaccinated, well-groomed, and clean.

sections.heading: Cats
steps: Confine cats in a carrier or in a closed office or cubicle.
Cats should be well-behaved (not aggressive towards people or animals).

Cats must be parasite free and not currently sick or suffering from an auto-immune disease or other ailment which may be worsened by exposure at an animal shelter. It is up to the pet owner to decide the best type of flea or tick program for their cat.

The Department allows only three bathroom accidents per cat. After that the pet must stay home. The owner must provide a litter pan for the cat during its visit and must keep it clean for the duration of the stay.
Cats must be currently vaccinated, well-groomed, and clean.

sections.heading: All Personal Pets
steps: Business needs of the department are first priority, followed by employee safety.

Too many animals in a work area can cause hazards to staff and stress to the animals. This may require staff to coordinate and plan ahead to ensure too many animals or incompatible animals are not in the same area. Supervisors will review and approve all requests.

Animals such as reptiles, arachnids, or others may frighten some staff or volunteers. Take special care to ensure co-workers are comfortable when handling any personal pet; consent is required during all interactions.
Pets must have water available at all times.
All pets must come with appropriate collars, leashes, and carriers for the size and type of pet.

Some work areas may require some animals to be kept in a crate, kennel, playpen, tank, or other appropriate enclosure (for example, public areas). Employees are responsible for providing proper confinement when needed.
Pets are not allowed in any break room or eating area.
Pets requiring a tank must have clean water and proper habitat. Cleaning of tanks must be done on the employee's personal time.
When animals are loose in an office or work area, post a sign stating there is a loose animal where it is visible before entering the office or work area.

steps.step: Pets, including foster pets, are not to be left overnight in an office or cubicle.
sub: Exemption: with a supervisor's permission, any pets that live in tanks or terrariums (reptiles, fish, turtles) may be left overnight.
Exemption: with a supervisor's permission, pets that live in specifically designed habitats (mice, guinea pigs, hamsters, rabbits) may be left overnight.

steps: In the event a pet is injured or gets sick, it is the owner's responsibility to seek veterinary care at their own expense.

related.name: Restraint and Care of Personal Animals at County Shelter Acknowledgement Form (retained in the employee's personnel file)

appendices: Appendix A: Acknowledgement Form

well-behaved (not aggressive towards people or animals). cats must be parasite free and not currently sick or suffering from an auto-immune disease or other ailment which may be worsened by exposure at an animal shelter. it is up to the pet owner to decide the best type of flea or tick program for their cat. the department allows only three bathroom accidents per cat. after that the pet must stay home. the owner must provide a litter pan for the cat during its visit and must keep it clean for the duration of the stay. cats must be currently vaccinated, well-groomed, and clean. all personal pets business needs of the department are first priority, followed by employee safety. too many animals in a work area can cause hazards to staff and stress to the animals. this may require staff to coordinate and plan ahead to ensure too many animals or incompatible animals are not in the same area. supervisors will review and approve all requests. animals such as reptiles, arachnids, or others may frighten some staff or volunteers. take special care to ensure co-workers are comfortable when handling any personal pet; consent is required during all interactions. pets must have water available at all times. all pets must come with appropriate collars, leashes, and carriers for the size and type of pet. some work areas may require some animals to be kept in a crate, kennel, playpen, tank, or other appropriate enclosure (for example, public areas). employees are responsible for providing proper confinement when needed. pets are not allowed in any break room or eating area. pets requiring a tank must have clean water and proper habitat. cleaning of tanks must be done on the employee's personal time. when animals are loose in an office or work area, post a sign stating there is a loose animal where it is visible before entering the office or work area. {"step": "pets, including foster pets, are not to be left overnight in an office or cubicle.", "sub": ["exemption: with a supervisor's permission, any pets that live in tanks or terrariums (reptiles, fish, turtles) may be left overnight.", "exemption: with a supervisor's permission, pets that live in specifically designed habitats (mice, guinea pigs, hamsters, rabbits) may be left overnight."]} in the event a pet is injured or gets sick, it is the owner's responsibility to seek veterinary care at their own expense.

documents.title: 412 Morning Dog Kennel Cleaning
documents.purpose: This SOP establishes the standard daily cleaning and disinfection protocol for dog kennels at all RCDAS campuses. Consistent sanitation practices reduce disease transmission, maintain a healthy shelter environment, and support animal welfare. This procedure follows the ASV Guidelines for Standards of Care in Animal Shelters (2022) and the manufacturer's labeled instructions for Rescue disinfectant.
documents.authority: Shelter supervisors are responsible for ensuring daily kennel cleaning is completed to standard at each campus. The RCDAS veterinary team establishes disinfection protocols and approves cleaning products.
documents.scope: This procedure applies to all RCDAS employees and volunteers responsible for daily dog kennel cleaning at all campuses.
documents.supersedes: SOP 300-08-412, Shelter & Kennel Sanitation Procedure (source document is a redraft numbered 300-XX)
references: ASV Guidelines for Standards of Care in Animal Shelters, 2nd Edition (2022), Section 5: Sanitation
Rescue (Accelerated Hydrogen Peroxide) Concentrate Product Label and Dilution Chart
ASPCA Shelter Sanitation Protocols

definitions.def: The length of time a disinfectant must remain visibly wet on a surface to effectively kill pathogens. Contact times vary by dilution ratio and target organism. Surfaces that dry before the required contact time has elapsed have not been properly disinfected.

A full cleaning and disinfection of all kennel surfaces, performed when a kennel changes occupants or at minimum every two weeks for long-stay animals. Uses a stronger disinfectant concentration than daily spot cleaning.

Removal of visible soils, waste, and soiled bedding from an occupied kennel while the same animal remains housed there. Spot cleaning reduces animal stress and handling while maintaining sanitary conditions between deep cleans.
The RCDAS radio code used to request immediate assistance for a dog fight or aggressive animal emergency. The call includes the pod and kennel location.

sections.heading: 1. Preparation
steps: Record the date, staff member name, kennel pod number, and start time on the Morning Dog Kennel Cleaning Checklist.

Weather permitting, move the entire row of dogs to the inside kennel areas and close the transfer doors. This separates the animals from all cleaning products and spray, as required by the ASV Guidelines. Animals are never hosed down or sprayed while inside their kennels.
For non-trough setups, remove food and water bowls. Empty and set aside bowls for sanitizing.

sections.heading: 2. Cleaning and Disinfection
steps: Scoop all solid waste into a trash receptacle. At the CVAC campus location, use the flushable drain system.

Apply Rescue disinfectant at the correct dilution for the cleaning type. For daily cleaning, use a 1:64 dilution (2 oz per gallon of water) and allow a 5-minute contact time for virucidal efficacy or 10 minutes for full bactericidal efficacy. For deep cleans, outbreak conditions, or high-risk kennels (parvo-exposed, quarantine), use a 1:32 dilution (4 oz per gallon) and allow a 10-minute contact time. The hose-end foamer automatically dilutes the concentrate when a metering tip is properly installed. Verify the correct metering tip is in place for the intended dilution ratio.
Scrub all surfaces including walls, floor, door, and fixtures. Allow the solution to remain visibly wet on surfaces for the full required contact time before rinsing.
Rinse thoroughly with a hose. Direct the spray away from animals in adjoining runs.
Squeegee excess water toward the drain and clear the drain area of any debris.

sections.heading: 3. Kennel Setup and Animal Return
steps: Place a clean blanket, Kuranda bed, or other approved bedding inside the kennel.
Install sanitized food and water bowls. Fill water bowls with fresh water and place food in the bowls.
Open the transfer doors and coax the dogs through to the inside areas.
Close the transfer doors and clean the outside kennel area following the same cleaning protocol (Section 2, steps 1 through 5).
Once both the inside and outside areas are clean, reopen and secure the transfer doors so the dogs can move freely between both sides of the kennel.

sections.heading: 4. Emergency Response During Cleaning
steps: If an animal is visibly in distress, appears severely ill, or a dog fight is occurring, stop cleaning immediately and address the emergency before resuming.

steps.step: Sick or injured dog:
sub: Note the kennel number and AID from the kennel card.
If possible, separate the animal from other dogs in the kennel using the divider.
Escalate symptoms and details to the clinic team immediately.

steps.step: Dog fight:
sub: Radio for assistance with the pod and kennel location using CODE FLUFFY.
Locate the safety equipment bag in the pod and use the appropriate supplies in an escalating manner until the dogs have been separated.
Escalate symptoms and details to the clinic team immediately.

sections.heading: 5. Completion Standard
steps: Confirm the kennel is clean and dry with no visible waste, dirt, or stains. Bedding and bowls are clean and in place, and fresh water is available.
Confirm all kennel and transfer doors are securely latched.
Record the completion time on the checklist and obtain supervisor initials.

campusVariations.variation: Use the flushable drain system for solid waste rather than scooping into a trash receptacle.

related.name: Rescue Concentrate Dilution Chart (posted in each pod)
Rescue product label and Safety Data Sheet
Safety equipment bag contents and location map (per pod)

revisions.desc: Original Shelter & Kennel Sanitation Procedure (300-08-412); redrafted as Morning Dog Kennel Cleaning with effective date left as placeholder in source
