documents.title: Animal Handling and Restraint
documents.purpose: This SOP establishes safe, humane, and low-stress handling and restraint standards for all animals impounded at RCDAS shelter facilities. Current best practices, supported by Fear Free and Low Stress Handling (LSH) principles, prioritize the use of the minimum effective restraint necessary to keep both handler and animal safe. Forceful restraint increases fear and stress, raises bite risk, and negatively affects behavioral outcomes. Staff apply the least restrictive technique that allows for safe handling and escalate only when necessary.
documents.authority: All RCDAS staff who handle animals must complete approved animal handling training before working independently with animals. Supervisors are responsible for monitoring handling techniques and intervening when unsafe or unnecessarily forceful methods are observed. Staff who are unsure how to safely handle a particular animal must request assistance from a supervisor or experienced colleague before proceeding.
documents.scope: This procedure applies to all RCDAS employees and volunteers at all shelter locations who handle, restrain, transport, or care for any animal in RCDAS custody, including dogs, cats, rabbits, birds, reptiles, and other species.
documents.supersedes: SOP 300-10, Animal Handling Policy (undated redraft; original was filed in a CVAC-specific policy folder)
references: Fear Free Shelter Program Guidelines
Low Stress Handling, Restraint and Behavior Modification of Dogs and Cats (Yin, 2009)
ASV Guidelines for Standards of Care in Animal Shelters, 2nd Edition (2022)
California Code of Regulations Title 16, Section 2039
RCDAS Dog Care and Safety Recommendations

definitions.def: The least amount of physical control needed to safely accomplish a handling task without causing unnecessary fear, pain, or distress.
A handling methodology that uses body language, environment, and technique to reduce fear and stress during animal interactions.
An illness transmissible from animals to humans through direct or indirect contact.

sections.heading: 1. Universal Handling Principles (apply to every animal interaction regardless of species)
steps: Assess before approaching. Read the animal's body language and environment before making contact. A calm assessment prevents the majority of handling incidents.
Move slowly and speak quietly. Sudden movements and loud sounds increase stress and defensive responses in all species.
Use the minimum effective restraint. Apply only as much physical control as the situation requires. Escalate technique only when a lesser approach has failed or is unsafe.
Report all bites and scratches to a supervisor immediately, regardless of severity. Complete an incident report and seek medical evaluation as needed.

sections.heading: 2. Personal Protective Equipment and Zoonosis Prevention
steps: Recognize that zoonotic diseases transmissible in shelter settings include rabies, ringworm, roundworm, salmonella, and toxoplasmosis, among others.
Wash hands thoroughly after handling any animal or animal waste and before eating, drinking, or touching the face.
Wear nitrile or latex gloves when cleaning kennels or handling animals with suspected illness or unknown health status.
Clean and disinfect any surface contaminated with animal waste before resuming work in that area.
Report any unexplained symptoms, bites, or scratches to a supervisor and seek medical attention promptly.

sections.heading: 3. Dogs
steps: Before approaching, assess body language. Signs of a relaxed dog include a loose body, soft eyes, a tail carried at mid-height, and a relaxed mouth. Signs of fear or stress include a tucked tail, whale eye (visible sclera), flattened ears, lip licking, yawning, or a stiff and still posture. Signs of potential aggression include a hard direct stare, raised hackles, stiff forward posture, or exposed teeth. Adjust your approach based on what you observe.

To approach a calm or uncertain dog, move slowly from the side rather than head-on, avoid direct eye contact, and offer the back of a loose hand for sniffing before attempting to touch. Slip a looped leash over the dog's head to secure before moving or lifting.

For dogs requiring additional restraint, use a catch pole as a tool of guidance and control, not force. Approach slowly with the loop held low. Slip the loop over the head and snug it gently. Walk beside the dog and guide the head in the direction of travel. Release the pole once the dog is secured in a kennel or vehicle. Catch poles are never used to lift, drag, or choke a dog.

sections.heading: 4. Cats
steps: Assess the cat before any contact by observing ear position, tail movement, pupil size, and body posture. A fearful cat will flatten its ears, dilate its pupils, crouch, and may hiss or growl. An aggressive cat may arch its back, puff its tail, or move sideways. Approach apprehensive cats by moving slowly and avoiding direct eye contact.
For cooperative cats, place one hand beneath the chest to support the front legs and use the other hand to support the hindquarters. Hold the cat close to your body to reduce struggling.

For anxious or fractious cats, use a towel wrap (the burrito technique): drape a towel over the cat, fold it snugly around the body, and lift the wrapped cat to transfer to a carrier. A carrier is the safest transport method for any cat.

Do not use scruffing (grasping the loose skin at the back of the neck) as a routine handling technique. Current veterinary and shelter medicine guidelines recognize that scruffing increases fear and stress and is not appropriate for most handling situations. Scruffing may be used only as a brief, last-resort technique when a cat presents an immediate safety risk and no other option is available, and must be paired with hindquarter support at all times.
Use nets for feral or highly aggressive cats when a towel approach is not feasible.

sections.heading: 5. Rabbits
steps: Never lift or restrain a rabbit by the ears.
To lift for short distances, grasp the loose skin at the nape of the neck and support the hindquarters with the other hand.
For longer transport, tuck the rabbit's head into the crook of your elbow with the body along your forearm and the hindquarters supported.
Use a carrier for all transport beyond a few steps. Rabbits can fracture their own spine by kicking during improper restraint.

sections.heading: 6. Birds, Reptiles, and Other Species
steps: Handle small birds with bare hands using a gentle wrap that does not compress the chest. Pressure on a bird's sternum causes suffocation.

For large exotic birds and raptors, contact a supervisor before handling; these species require specific training. For raptors, contact a wildlife rehabilitation organization promptly and house the bird in a quiet, covered cage until transfer.
For snakes, immobilize the head at the base of the skull and support the body. Use snake tongs when necessary. Transport in a secured pillowcase or canvas bag.
For lizards, block vision with a towel and grasp around the shoulder girdle, supporting the pelvic area.
For turtles, approach from the rear and grasp securely by the rear legs or shell. Towels aid control for difficult individuals.
For all exotic or unfamiliar species, contact a supervisor before attempting handling. Minimum intervention is the appropriate default until additional guidance is available.

related.name: Document all bite/scratch incidents in the animal's record

Low Stress Handling, Restraint and Behavior Modification of Dogs and Cats (Yin, 2009)

documents.title: Documenting Spay-Neuter Appointments in the Chameleon Animal Window
documents.purpose: To establish consistent documentation for confirmed spay/neuter appointments and for tracking purposes.
documents.authority: Not stated as a separate section in the original. The Chameleon administrator maintains the automated appointment reminder job described in Section C.
documents.scope: Applicable to all DAS employees who have access to Chameleon.
documents.supersedes: Prior versions of this SOP effective February 11, 2016 and May 13, 2015 (as listed in the April 1, 2020 header)
references: Animal Services Policy 000-01, General Chameleon Policy

sections.heading: Documenting a Spay/Neuter Appointment
steps: From the tag/link window, go to the animal ID field.
Press the F4 key to pop up the Chameleon animal window.
Create (store) a new animal record or update (F8) an existing animal record following Policy 000-01, General Chameleon Policy.
Move the cursor to the Spay/Neuter fields.
In the Due field, enter the spay or neuter appointment date (mm/dd/yy) or press F5 to enter today's date.
At the Western Riverside SN clinic, enter an additional appointment time in the Extra1 field of the animal window using the following format: H:MMAM or H:MMPM.

steps.step: Use the tab key to move the cursor to the Vet ID field and enter the applicable group ID where the surgery will be performed:
sub: G005718 - San Jacinto Valley Animal Campus - SN Clinic
G005719 - Western Riverside County/City Animal - SN Clinic
G005720 - Western Riverside County/City Animal - SN Bus
G003247 - Coachella Valley Animal Campus

steps: Press the F8 key to update or the F9 key to store the information.

sections.heading: Spay/Neuter Audits
steps: To run the report, click on Report (F3).
Choose SN APPT Audit.
Choose the location from the drop-down list.
Enter the beginning date and ending date of the appointment range, then press OK.
The report displays appointments by time.

sections.heading: Chameleon Postmaster SN Appointment Reminder
steps: A postmaster job schedule created by the Chameleon administrator runs at 4:00 PM prior to the appointment date.
The email contains pre-surgery instructions, the SN clinic location, and the date and/or time of the SN appointment.

steps.step: The automated notification is based on information entered in the following fields:
sub: Animal Window: Extra1 field, Due Date field, and Vet ID field.
Person Window: email address (must be a valid email).

sections.heading: Spay/Neuter Completion
steps: From the tag/link window, go to the animal ID field.
Enter the animal ID.
Press the F4 key to pop up the Chameleon animal window.
Change the animal sex field from M or F to N or S.
Move the cursor to the Spay/Neuter fields.
In the Completed field, enter the spay or neuter surgery date (mm/dd/yy) or press F5 to enter today's date.
Use the tab key to move the cursor to the Vet ID field and change the group ID to the appropriate veterinarian who performed the surgery (for example, G008330, Chief Veterinarian).

related.name: Animal Window (Spay/Neuter fields, Extra1, Vet ID)

documents.title: Dog Co-Housing Candidate Selection and Pairing
documents.purpose: This SOP establishes a consistent process for selecting and pairing dogs for co-housing at RCDAS shelter facilities.
documents.authority: A shelter supervisor or designee must approve all co-housing placements. Veterinary staff clear or restrict animals based on health status. Any staff member may separate an incompatible pair immediately without prior approval and must notify a supervisor as soon as practicable. Approved by: Director of Animal Services.
documents.scope: This procedure applies to all RCDAS staff and volunteers at all shelter locations involved in dog kennel management or population planning. It covers dogs four months of age and older. Same-litter puppies and confirmed bonded pairs follow an abbreviated process described in the Same-Litter Puppies and Bonded Pairs section.
documents.supersedes: None identified (new draft SOP; source shows placeholder number and effective date)
references: ASV Guidelines for Standards of Care in Animal Shelters, 2nd Edition (2022), pp. 14-18
Grigg et al. (2017)
RCDAS Capacity for Care Assessment (2025)
RCDAS Dog Care and Safety Recommendations

definitions.def: Intentional placement of two compatible dogs in a shared kennel run for welfare purposes.
Two dogs with a documented prior relationship who show affiliative behavior toward each other.
The interior kennel divider used to separate dogs for feeding, cleaning, and safe individual removal.

steps.step: Supervisors select partners based on the following criteria, consistent with ASV Guidelines (2022). Dogs should be matched for:
sub: Similar age and life stage
Compatible size, energy level, and play style
Sex: all mixed-sex co-housed dogs must be spayed or neutered or have a documented spay/neuter appointment
No documented history of dog-directed aggression in Chameleon records for either dog
Both dogs cleared by veterinary staff as healthy and current on intake vaccinations

sections.heading: Meet-and-Greet Protocol
steps: Conduct a supervised meet-and-greet in a neutral outdoor space before co-housing any pair except same-litter puppies and confirmed bonded pairs.
Remove all food and high-value items from the introduction space before beginning.
Walk both dogs in parallel on separate leashes, handled by separate staff, allowing them to orient to each other before any direct contact.

Allow the dogs to approach at their own pace. Green-light signals include loose body posture, play bows, and relaxed sniffing. Stop the introduction if either dog shows stiff posture, hard staring, sustained raised hackles, growling, or snapping.
If signals remain positive, drop leashes and allow a minimum five-minute off-leash interaction. Neither dog should be repeatedly pinned or prevented from disengaging.
If the off-leash introduction is successful, move the pair to the shared kennel for a supervised 30-minute trial before leaving them unsupervised.
Document the meet-and-greet outcome, including both dogs' A numbers, the date, the conducting staff member, and the result, in Chameleon.

sections.heading: Same-Litter Puppies and Bonded Pairs
steps: Same-litter puppies and dogs arriving as confirmed bonded pairs may be placed together without a formal meet-and-greet, provided both dogs receive standard health clearance at intake.
Observe the pair for at least 30 minutes in the shared kennel on the first day and document observations in Chameleon.

sections.heading: Monitoring and Separation
steps: Notify a supervisor and reassess the pair when either dog shows new or worsening stress despite co-housing, when resource guarding or sustained conflict is observed, or when one dog in the pair is removed for any reason.
If a fight occurs, separate the dogs immediately using the guillotine door, notify a supervisor, document the incident in Chameleon, and do not reunite the pair without supervisor review.

related.name: ASV Guidelines for Standards of Care in Animal Shelters, 2nd Edition (2022)

documents.searchText: this sop establishes a consistent process for selecting and pairing dogs for co-housing at rcdas shelter facilities. this procedure applies to all rcdas staff and volunteers at all shelter locations involved in dog kennel management or population planning. it covers dogs four months of age and older. same-litter puppies and confirmed bonded pairs follow an abbreviated documents.title: Mandatory Dog Collaring
documents.purpose: This policy establishes guidelines for providing all dogs leaving a RCDAS shelter facility with a collar. RCDAS employees are expected to fit all dogs with collars before handing them off to an adopter, foster, or confirmed owner. All dog collars should be equipped with a free tag including caretaker contact information, whenever possible.
documents.authority: Approval signature and date pending; source draft contains a blank policy approval block.
documents.scope: This policy applies to all shelter staff members approved to handle dogs.
documents.supersedes: None (new draft; not currently an implemented program)

sections.heading: 1. Dog Collaring Process (prior to the pet's exit from the shelter facility)
steps: Print a custom ID tag for the newly adopted pet.
Select an appropriate collar size and place the collar on the pet after attaching the custom ID tag.

If the dog exhibits behavior leading to handling concerns, follow established safe handling protocols or request assistance from ACT Supervision. If not available, staff may provide the adopter with a collar to take home with the pet.
Provide the adopter with a leash if available.
Store additional collars near greeter stations at each shelter. Notify RCDAS leadership if collar supplies run low so they can be reordered.

sections.heading: 2. Unanticipated Handling Concerns
steps: In the event of unanticipated handling concerns, clinic staff will follow standard safe handling protocols and ensure handling notes are passed on to the person taking the animal home if the pet is still allowed to leave the facility.

sections.heading: 3. Equipment and Supplies
steps: RCDAS will provide martingale collars for staff use.
Store collars in areas accessible to clinic staff.

revisions.author: RCDAS
revisions.desc: Original draft; program drafted for future consideration and not currently implemented

documents.title: Processing Dangerous and Potentially Dangerous Restraint Orders in Chameleon
documents.purpose: To provide a consistent business flow for dogs declared dangerous or potentially dangerous by the Department of Animal Services.
documents.authority: Not stated as a separate section in the originals. Office assistants store restraint order records and process payments; the Chameleon administrator generates annual registration notices and monthly non-compliance reports; the supervisor, office assistant, field sergeant, and field commander receive the automated non-compliance report.
documents.scope: This procedure is applicable to any Department of Animal Services (DAS) staff member who processes restraining orders.
documents.supersedes: SOP Processing Dangerous Restraint Orders in Chameleon and Follow-up, effective October 3, 2017 (revised from August 25, 2016); SOP Processing Potentially Dangerous Restraint Orders in Chameleon and Follow-up, effective December 13, 2016 (revised from August 25, 2016)
references: Riverside County Board Ordinance 6.16.010, 771 § 1, 1999
Riverside County Board Ordinance 6.16.020, 771 § 2, 1999
Riverside County Board Ordinance 6.16.030, 771 § 3, 1999
Riverside Municipal Code Section 8.04.070
Food & Agricultural Code 31641
DAS Policy 000-01, General Chameleon Information
SOP Administrative Restraining Orders
SOP Renewing Pet Licenses
SOP Issuing New Licenses

definitions.def: The order form documenting a dog declared dangerous by the Department of Animal Services.
The order form documenting a dog declared potentially dangerous by the Department of Animal Services. A PDR is good for 48 months for the City of Riverside and all other County areas.
The tag number Chameleon generates automatically when a tag/link record is stored with the tag number field left blank.

sections.heading: Business Flow: Dangerous Restraint Orders
steps: Upon receipt of the Dangerous Restraint Order (DRO), the office assistant stores a DANGEROUS REGISTRATION and an AA DANGEROUS record in the Chameleon tag/link window.
Scan the completed DRO form to the activity number that has a result of DR.
Store a License tag/link record (LIC) in addition to the DANGEROUS REGISTRATION and AA DANGEROUS records, if applicable, in accordance with state law.
The office assistant processes payment of the annual registration fee in the Chameleon receipt window.
The Chameleon administrator generates the annual dangerous registration notice based on the DANGEROUS REGISTRATION tag expiration date with a status of CURRENT.
Direct mailed-in payments to the field services office assistant charged with the responsibility of handling processed dangerous restraint orders.

sections.heading: Storing an AA DANGEROUS Record in Chameleon
steps: In a blank activity window, enter the activity number written on the DRO in the activity number field.
Press the F12 function key to perform a search of the activity.
Double-click on the activity number and sequence with a result of DR.

Scan the completed and signed DRO to the activity, making sure the mouse cursor is on the activity number field of the displayed record. Refer to SOP Attaching Images, Documents, or Photos to Chameleon.
Tab to the tag number field in the same activity window record.
Press the F4 function key to bring up the tag/link window.

steps.step: Person ID:
sub: Confirm that the person ID from the activity window is the same as the owner written on the DRO form.
If not the same, press the F4 key to bring up the person window.

Refer to Policy 000-01, General Chameleon Information, Person Window, for performing a search prior to storing a new person record or storing a new person record. Note: Failure to follow this procedure/policy will result in an error email from the Chameleon administrator for a duplicate record discrepancy.
Press the enter key to bring the new person record to the tag/link window.

steps.step: Animal ID:
sub: Confirm that the animal ID carried over from the activity window is the same animal written on the DRO form.
If not the same, based on the person search, determine if the animal has an existing record under the owner or store a new animal record. Refer to Policy 000-01, General Chameleon Information.
Press the enter key to bring the animal record to the tag/link window.

steps: Tag No: skip the tag number field. Chameleon will generate a "U" number once the record is stored.
Tag Type: enter AA DANGEROUS.
Subtype: enter FIELD.
Jurisdiction: the tag jurisdiction must match the person jurisdiction.
Status: enter CURRENT.
Problem code: leave blank.
Tag Date: press F5 to apply the system date or enter the date the animal was declared dangerous according to the DRO form.
Tag Term: enter 99 to allow Chameleon to calculate and populate the tag expiration date. Entering 99 indicates the declaration is for the life of the animal.
Tag Exp: do not touch.
Vac Date, Vac Term, and Vac Exp: leave blank. Refer to the license tag/link window for rabies vaccination information.
Tag Price: enter $0.00 if not populated by Chameleon automatically.
Store (F9) the record.

sections.heading: Storing a DANGEROUS REGISTRATION Record in Chameleon (New DRO Billing)

steps.step: With the AA DANGEROUS record displayed, complete the following fields:
sub: Person ID: confirm the person ID matches the owner written on the DRO form. If not, follow the person search and store procedure in Policy 000-01, General Chameleon Information.
Animal ID: confirm the animal ID matches the animal written on the DRO form. If not, determine if the animal has an existing record under the owner or store a new animal record per Policy 000-01.
Tag No: skip the tag number field. Chameleon will generate a "U" number once the record is stored.
Tag Type: enter DANGEROUS REGISTRATION.
Subtype: enter FIELD.
Jurisdiction: the tag jurisdiction must match the person jurisdiction.
Status: enter TMP for TEMPORARY.
Problem code: enter M for missing fee.
Tag Date: press F5 to apply the system date or enter the date the animal was declared dangerous according to the DRO form.
Tag Term: enter 0 to allow Chameleon to calculate and populate the tag expiration date for billing purposes.
Tag Exp: do not touch.
Vac Date, Vac Term, and Vac Exp: leave blank. Refer to the license tag/link window for rabies vaccination information.
Store (F9) the record.

steps.step: Generate the notice:
sub: The DANGEROUS REGISTRATION tag/link record must be displayed.
Press F3 to access reports and press D or scroll down to Dangerous Registration Bill.
Click OK to display the notice.
Review the notice and make sure the instructions match the missing requirement.
Create an Animal ID memo using the Temporary License memo template. The text and balance must match what is missing or needed.
The due date in the memo must match the due date on the temporary notice or bill.
Print the notice and mail it.

steps.step: Create a receipt:
sub: When payment is received with the notice, bring up the tag/link window.
Press F11 to clear the screen.
Enter the reference number indicated on the notice in the tag number field.
Press the F12 key to perform a search. The PID in the person ID field or AID in the animal ID field can also be used to perform a search.
A search based on the tag number will display the DANGEROUS REGISTRATION tag window record. A search based on PID or AID requires the user to double-click on the correct record to display it.
Change the status from TEMPORARY to CURRENT.
Remove or delete the M from the problem code field.
Change the tag term to 12. This allows Chameleon to calculate the next annual dangerous registration fee date for the tag expiration field.
Delete the date in the tag expiration field and leave it blank.
Press the ALT key + 5 to move the cursor to the batch field, then press the tab key until the cursor is on the tag price.
Enter the amount of $250 in the tag price field.
Press F8 to update the DANGEROUS REGISTRATION tag/link record. Review that the tag expiration field now shows the next fee date.
Press the tab key to go to the receipt number field.
Pop up (F4) the receipt window.
Make sure the person ID is the PID on the DANGEROUS REGISTRATION tag/link record.
The item code, license number, animal ID, department number, fee type, and price must be displayed.
Enter the total amount of monies received. The balance must be $0.
Store (F9) the receipt.
A print window will appear. Print one copy for the deposit and one to send to the customer. Refer to cash handling policies and procedures.

sections.heading: Annual DANGEROUS REGISTRATION Fee Process
steps: The Chameleon administrator generates the annual registration fee for DANGEROUS REGISTRATION records that are due the next month.

steps.step: Tag/Link Window:
sub: When payment is received with the notice, bring up the tag/link window.
Press F11 to clear the screen.
Enter the reference number indicated on the notice in the tag number field.
Press the F12 key to perform a search. The PID in the person ID field or AID in the animal ID field can also be used to perform a search.
A search based on the tag number will display the DANGEROUS REGISTRATION tag window record. A search based on PID or AID requires the user to double-click on the correct record to display it.
Click on Procedure > Renew or press ALT+P+W. This automatically changes the status of the previous DANGEROUS REGISTRATION record to RENEWED.
The tag number field must be blank to allow Chameleon to generate the next "U" number.
Make no change to the following tag/link fields: tag type, subtype, jurisdiction, status, problem code, tag term, vac date, and vac exp.
Tag Date: automatically shows the current system date.
Tag Exp: Chameleon will calculate the next tag expiration date (12 months from the prior year's annual dangerous registration fee tag expiration date). Do not touch.
Press the ALT key + 5 to move the cursor to the batch field, then press the tab key until the cursor is on the tag price.
Enter the amount of $250 in the tag price field.
Press F9 to store the DANGEROUS REGISTRATION tag/link record. Review that the tag expiration field now shows the next fee date.
Press the tab key to go to the receipt number field.

steps.step: Store a receipt:
sub: Pop up (F4) the receipt window.
Make sure the person ID is the PID on the DANGEROUS REGISTRATION tag/link record.
The item code, license number, animal ID, department number, fee type, and price must be displayed.
Enter the total amount of monies received. The balance must be $0.
Store (F9) the receipt.
A print window will appear. Print one copy for the deposit and one to send to the customer. Refer to cash handling policies and procedures.

sections.heading: Collection Process
steps: On the 31st day of each month or at the beginning of the new month, the supervisor, office assistant, field sergeant, and field commander receive an automated report of non-compliant (unpaid) DANGEROUS REGISTRATION records.

steps.step: Based on the emailed list, perform the following procedure to place the bill into collections:
sub: Bring up the TAG window.
Enter the PID and press F12 to perform a search based on the PID. All tags associated with the PID will appear.
Double-click on the DANGEROUS REGISTRATION line with a status of CURRENT or TEMPORARY.
Place the cursor on the Receipt line and press F4 to pop up the receipt window.

steps.step: Create the receipt:
sub: Remove the name from the Received From field.
Remove all item codes except DANGEROUS REGISTRATION. Change the item code to read DANGEROUS REGISTRATION - COLLECTION.
In the Extra 3 field, enter the specific term dates (by year or by term). Click the green + to add the line item and the red - to remove the old line item.
Double-click on the line item DANGEROUS REGISTRATION - COLLECTION, then change the item code to PYMNT PLAN FEE - COLLECTION. Press the LOOK UP ($) button to update the line item price to $19.00.
Press the green + button to add the line item.
Store the receipt (F9).

steps.step: Generate the memo:
sub: In the Type field, enter ACC for ACCOUNTING and use the BILLING template format to enter the receipt memo information.
Press F9 to store.
Click on the green check mark or press the Enter key to bring the receipt number back to the tag window.
Change the subtype to COLLECTION and update (F8).

sections.heading: Business Flow: Potentially Dangerous Restraint Orders
steps: Upon receipt of the Potentially Dangerous Restraint Order (PDR), the office assistant stores an AA POT DANGER record in the Chameleon tag/link window.

If the corresponding activity number is not listed on the PDR, return the restraint order to the area Sergeant until the restraint order is filled out completely. Scan the completed PDR form to the activity number that has a result of PDR.

Store a License tag/link record of LIC RV PDR_VICIOUS for the City of Riverside (renewable every year whether the dog is spayed or neutered) or LIC% for all other County areas, in addition to the AA POT DANGER record, if applicable, in accordance with licensing state laws.
A PDR is good for 48 months for the City of Riverside and all other County areas.

steps.step: In accordance with Municipal Code 8.02.070, in addition to any other licensing requirement provided by law, the owner of a dog declared potentially dangerous or vicious in the City of Riverside shall provide the following:
sub: The dog and owner are required to complete a Public Works (or designee) approved obedience course and shall provide proof of completion of such course within sixty (60) days of the dog being deemed potentially dangerous. The dog owner pays for the course.

The owner of the dog, possessor of the dog, or owner of the premises where the dog is kept shall obtain and maintain liability insurance in the amount of one hundred thousand dollars ($100,000) and shall furnish a certificate or proof of insurance by which the City shall be notified at least thirty (30) calendar days prior to cancellation or nonrenewal.

The tag/link window will have a tag status of TEMPORARY with the following problem codes: I for missing proof of insurance; O for missing proof of completed obedience training. These can be combined with other licensing problem codes. Refer to SOPs Renewing Pet Licenses and Issuing New Licenses.
Scan proof of completed obedience training and proof of insurance to the tag/link window under the license for tag type LIC RV PDR_VICIOUS.

sections.heading: Storing an AA POT DANGER Record in Chameleon
steps: In a blank activity window, enter the activity number written on the PDR in the activity number field.
Press the F12 function key to perform a search of the activity.
Double-click on the activity number and sequence with a result of PDR.

Scan the completed and signed PDR to the activity, making sure the mouse cursor is on the activity number field of the displayed record. Refer to SOP Attaching Images, Documents, or Photos to Chameleon.
Tab to the tag number field in the same activity window record.
Press the F4 function key to bring up the tag/link window.

steps.step: Person ID and Animal ID:
sub: Confirm that the person ID from the activity window is the same as the owner written on the PDR form. If not, follow the person search and store procedure in Policy 000-01, General Chameleon Information. Note: Failure to follow this procedure/policy will result in an error email from the Chameleon administrator for a duplicate record discrepancy.

Confirm that the animal ID carried over from the activity window is the same animal written on the PDR form. If not, determine if the animal has an existing record under the owner or store a new animal record per Policy 000-01.
Press the enter key to bring the person and animal records to the tag/link window.

steps: Tag No: skip the tag number field. Chameleon will generate a "U" number once the record is stored.
Tag Type: enter AA POT DANGER.
Subtype: enter FIELD.
Jurisdiction: the tag jurisdiction must match the person jurisdiction.
Status: enter CURRENT.
Problem code: leave blank.
Tag Date: press F5 to apply the system date or enter the date the animal was declared potentially dangerous according to the PDR form.
Tag Term: enter 48 to allow Chameleon to calculate and populate the tag expiration date.
Tag Exp: do not touch.
Vac Date, Vac Term, and Vac Exp: leave blank. Refer to the license tag/link window for rabies vaccination information.
Tag Price: enter $0.00 if not populated by Chameleon automatically.
Store (F9) the record.

documents.title: Puppy Housing Movement and Parvovirus Prevention
documents.purpose: This SOP establishes housing, movement, and sanitation procedures for puppies six months of age and younger at all RCDAS campuses in order to reduce the risk of canine parvovirus (CPV) exposure. Puppies are the population most vulnerable to CPV because they may lack protective immunity before completing their full vaccination series. These procedures balance disease prevention with the behavioral needs of young dogs during the critical socialization period.
documents.authority: The RCDAS Chief Veterinarian and veterinary staff establish puppy housing and disease prevention protocols. Shelter supervisors are responsible for ensuring compliance at each campus. This SOP supersedes the November 14, 2013 Director's Directive on the same subject.
documents.scope: This procedure applies to all field officers, animal care staff, veterinary staff, and volunteers at all RCDAS campuses. It covers all puppies six months of age and younger from the time of intake through disposition.
documents.supersedes: RCDAS Director's Directive: Handling of Puppies in the Shelter, November 14, 2013
references: ASV Guidelines for Standards of Care in Animal Shelters, 2nd Edition (2022), Sections 5, 6, and 9
University of Florida Shelter Medicine: Canine and Feline Parvovirus Infections in Shelters (2021)
Koret Shelter Medicine Program: Balancing Parvovirus Risk and Puppy Socialization
American Veterinary Society of Animal Behavior (AVSAB) Position Statement on Puppy Socialization
RCDAS Director's Directive: Handling of Puppies in the Shelter (November 14, 2013)

definitions.def: A highly contagious and potentially fatal viral infection that primarily affects puppies and unvaccinated dogs. CPV is extremely resistant in the environment and can survive on surfaces, soil, and equipment for months to years. Transmission occurs through direct contact with infected dogs or contact with contaminated feces, surfaces, or fomites.

The removal of visible soils, waste, and soiled bedding from an occupied kennel while the animal remains in place. Spot cleaning reduces stress and unnecessary handling while maintaining sanitary conditions.

A full cleaning and disinfection of all kennel surfaces using a disinfectant effective against non-enveloped viruses (including CPV) at the appropriate concentration and contact time. Required whenever a kennel changes occupants.
Any object or material that can carry and transmit infectious organisms. In a shelter setting, fomites include clothing, hands, leashes, bowls, bedding, mops, and shared equipment.

sections.heading: Intake and Vaccination
steps: Vaccinate all puppies with a DA2PP vaccine at the time of intake, regardless of reported vaccination history. Administer booster vaccinations every two weeks while the puppy remains in the shelter, following the schedule established by the veterinary team.
Deworm all puppies at intake per the veterinary protocol.
Record vaccination and deworming in Chameleon at the time of administration.

sections.heading: Housing
steps: House puppies individually unless they were impounded together as a litter. Littermates may be co-housed.
House puppies separately from adult dogs. Place puppies in designated puppy housing areas when space allows.

Limit kennel changes to the minimum necessary. Move puppies from the stray pod to their assigned kennel when they become available for disposition, and avoid additional moves unless directed by veterinary or supervisory staff.

sections.heading: Cleaning and Sanitation
steps: Spot clean puppy kennels with the puppy remaining in the kennel. Use the transfer door or a barrier to separate the puppy from the area being cleaned. Remove soiled bedding, waste, and food debris and replace with clean materials.

Perform a full deep clean and disinfection with Rescue at the parvo-effective concentration (1:16 dilution, 5-minute contact time, or 1:32 dilution, 10-minute contact time) each time a kennel changes occupants. Remove the puppy from the kennel during deep cleaning.
Sanitize food and water bowls daily. Use dedicated bowls for each puppy and avoid sharing bowls between animals.
Change gloves or wash hands between handling different puppies. Change any contaminated clothing or PPE before moving to the next kennel to prevent fomite transmission.

sections.heading: Movement and Socialization
steps: Limit the movement of puppies through the shelter to reduce environmental exposure. When movement is necessary, carry puppies or transport them in a sanitized carrier rather than walking them through common areas.

Puppies that have received at least one DA2PP vaccination and show no signs of illness may receive individual socialization and enrichment interactions with staff, provided that staff follow hand hygiene and PPE protocols between animals. The critical socialization period for puppies closes between 12 and 16 weeks of age, and lack of positive human contact during this window can result in lasting fear and behavioral problems.
Keep litters of puppies together for socialization, but do not co-mingle puppies from other litters in shared exercise or play areas unless the surfaces can be properly disinfected between groups.
During an active parvo outbreak as declared by the veterinary team, suspend all puppy socialization and movement outside the kennel until the veterinary team lifts the restriction.

sections.heading: Expedited Placement
steps: Prioritize moving puppies out of the shelter as quickly as possible through foster placement, adoption, or transfer to rescue partners. Every additional day a puppy spends in the shelter increases the risk of CPV exposure.
Coordinate with the foster program to place puppies as soon as they are eligible. Foster placement is the single most effective strategy for protecting puppies from shelter-acquired parvovirus.
Provide foster caregivers with written instructions on puppy care, vaccination schedules, and signs of illness to watch for.

sections.heading: Illness Monitoring and Response
steps: Monitor all puppies daily for signs of illness, including lethargy, vomiting, diarrhea (especially bloody diarrhea), loss of appetite, and fever. Report any signs immediately to the veterinary team.
Isolate any puppy showing signs consistent with CPV from the general puppy population and notify the veterinary team for testing and treatment decisions.

If a puppy in the shelter tests positive for CPV, the veterinary team determines whether an outbreak protocol is warranted, including enhanced sanitation, movement restrictions, and review of all puppies that may have had contact with the infected animal.

documents.title: Safety Bucket Contents Placement and Maintenance
documents.purpose: This SOP establishes the contents, placement, inspection, and maintenance requirements for safety buckets and related dog fight intervention equipment at all RCDAS campuses. Safety buckets contain the tools needed to safely break up a dog fight, and immediate access to properly stocked and functioning equipment reduces the risk of injury to staff, volunteers, the public, and animals. This SOP covers equipment readiness only; for intervention techniques and procedures during a dog fight, refer to SOP #34, Breaking Up Dog Fights.
documents.authority: The Shelter Supervisor at each campus is responsible for maintaining safety buckets and ensuring all tools are available, functional, and properly placed. Shelter supervisors may delegate weekly inspections to designated staff but retain overall accountability for equipment readiness.
documents.scope: This procedure applies to all RCDAS staff and volunteers who handle dogs or work in kennel and play yard areas at all campuses. Staff who do not directly handle dogs but work in areas where dogs are present are responsible for knowing the location of safety buckets and for providing assistance as described in SOP #34, Breaking Up Dog Fights.
documents.supersedes: None identified (new draft SOP; source numbered 300-XX with placeholder effective date)
references: ASV Guidelines for Standards of Care in Animal Shelters, 2nd Edition (2022), Section 3: Animal Handling
Cal/OSHA General Industry Safety Orders, Title 8, California Code of Regulations
OSHA General Duty Clause, Section 5(a)(1) of the Occupational Safety and Health Act
American Humane OSHA and Safety Guide for Animal Shelters
University of Florida Shelter Medicine Program: How to Break Up a Dog Fight (Dr. Brenda Griffin)

definitions.def: A clearly marked container stationed in each play yard and kennel pod area containing the core tools needed to intervene in a dog fight. The bucket serves as a single, predictable location so staff can locate intervention tools under stress without searching.

A wood or rigid plastic barrier board used to physically separate fighting dogs by inserting the board between them. Break boards have built-in handles for safe grip. Also referred to as block boards or sorting boards.

A wooden or hard plastic stick with one end tapered to a thin wedge, used specifically to release a bite-hold (latch-on) during a dog fight by inserting the wedge behind the front canine teeth and twisting to open the jaw.
A rigid pole with an adjustable loop at one end used to safely restrain a dog at a distance when direct handling poses a bite risk.
The RCDAS radio code used to request immediate assistance for a dog fight or aggressive animal emergency. The call includes the pod and kennel location.

steps.step: Stock each safety bucket with the following items:
sub: Air horn (compressed gas canister type, with adequate pressure remaining)
Bite stick (break stick)
Citronella spray (Spray Shield or equivalent, within expiration date)
Heavy-duty gloves (leather or bite-resistant)

sections.heading: Additional Equipment Stationed Near Safety Buckets
steps: Hoses: located at each row of kennels. Hoses serve as both a cleaning tool and a fight intervention tool; a directed spray of water can startle and separate fighting dogs.

Break boards: wood or rigid plastic barrier boards positioned throughout the facility. Break boards may not be available at all campus locations. Where break boards are not available, the Kennel Supervisor identifies alternative barrier materials and their locations.
Catch poles: mounted on walls in the main kennel area.

Blankets: available from the laundry room. Blankets can be thrown over a dog to block visual stimulation and interrupt a fight, though they are less effective than break boards or water for bite-hold situations.
Slip leads: kept near play yards and kennel entrances for securing separated dogs after an incident.

sections.heading: Personal Equipment
steps: Carry a whistle on your person at all times when working in kennel and play yard areas. The whistle serves as both a startle tool and a way to alert nearby staff to an emergency.
Carry a radio when working in dog handling areas to call CODE FLUFFY if needed.

sections.heading: Placement and Accessibility
steps: Place one safety bucket in each play yard and each kennel pod area.
Position safety buckets in a visible, consistent location within each area so that staff can locate them immediately under stress. Mark bucket locations with signage where possible.

Keep all safety buckets and nearby tools unobstructed at all times for immediate access. Never store equipment behind other supplies, inside locked cabinets, or in locations that require moving objects to reach.
When a safety bucket or any tool is used during an incident, return or replace the items as soon as the situation is resolved.

sections.heading: Inspection and Maintenance
steps: Inspect all safety buckets and nearby tools on a monthly basis. The Kennel Supervisor assigns and tracks inspections using the Safety Bucket Inspection Log.
Verify that each bucket contains all required items listed in the Safety Bucket Contents section and that additional nearby equipment is present and accessible.
Check air horns for adequate pressure by giving a brief test burst. Replace any air horn that produces a weak or absent sound.
Check citronella spray canisters for expiration date and adequate fill level. Replace any canister that is expired, empty, or near empty.
Inspect bite sticks and break boards for cracks, splinters, or structural damage. Replace any tool that is damaged.
Confirm that catch poles are mounted in their designated locations and that the cable loop mechanism operates smoothly.
Replace any missing, expired, damaged, or non-functional item immediately. If a replacement is not available on site, notify the Kennel Supervisor the same day.
Record the inspection results, any items replaced, and the inspector's name and date on the Safety Bucket Inspection Log.

sections.heading: Training Requirements
steps: Train all staff and volunteers who handle dogs or work in kennel and play yard areas on the location and contents of safety buckets during their initial orientation.

Train all staff and volunteers who handle dogs on when and how to use each tool in the safety bucket as part of their SOP #34 (Breaking Up Dog Fights) training. This includes the escalating order of intervention methods: verbal commands and whistle, water from a hose, air horn, break board separation, citronella spray, catch pole, and bite stick. Physical contact methods such as the wheelbarrow technique are a last resort and require prior hands-on training.
Conduct refresher training on safety bucket contents, locations, and intervention techniques at least annually or whenever equipment or procedures change.

documents.title: Bite Report Call Procedures
documents.purpose: This SOP establishes standardized call handling procedures for animal bite reports. Accurate and timely documentation of bite incidents is essential for public health protection through rabies surveillance, quarantine enforcement, and compliance with California reporting requirements.
documents.authority: Call center staff are authorized to receive bite report information and route calls to the Rabies Control Desk. Bite investigations, quarantine orders, and follow-up enforcement are managed by the Rabies Control program and Animal Control Officers under the direction of field supervisors.
documents.scope: This procedure applies to all call center staff handling animal bite report calls across all RCDAS service areas.
documents.supersedes: None identified
references: California Health and Safety Code (rabies reporting requirements)
California Food and Agriculture Code
Riverside County Ordinance No. 630
RCDAS Rabies Control program procedures

definitions.def: The RCDAS program responsible for monitoring and preventing rabies transmission, including bite investigations, quarantine procedures, and public health reporting.

A mandatory observation period for an animal that has bitten a person, during which the animal is monitored for signs of rabies. Quarantine requirements and duration are determined by the Rabies Control program.
A formal record of an animal bite incident that includes information about the bite victim, the biting animal, the animal's owner, and the circumstances of the incident.

sections.heading: Initial Call Handling
steps: Determine whether the bite is actively occurring. If the animal is currently attacking or loose and aggressive, treat the call as a Priority 1 emergency.
For bites that have already occurred where the animal is contained or the situation is stable, proceed as a Priority 5.

sections.heading: Routing to Rabies Control
steps: Forward the call to the Rabies Control Desk when staff are available.
If the Rabies Control Desk is unavailable, collect the required information (see Required Information Collection) and create an Activity.
Email the Rabies Control desk at rabiescontrol@rivco.org if the caller has questions on specimen results, confirmation of quarantine completion, or more complex rabies questions.

sections.heading: Required Information Collection
steps: Verify the jurisdiction of the owner of the animal responsible for the bite, unless the bite is actively occurring at the time of the call.
If owner information is not available, verify jurisdiction based on the location where the bite occurred.

steps.step: Enter the following information in the Activity comment section, in this order:
sub: Date of bite
Time of bite
Victim name if a minor or if the person calling is different (create PID: first/last name, phone number, address, date of birth, and email)
Location of the bite or scratch wound
How the bite or scratch happened (circumstances of the bite)
Where the report originated from if faxed from a doctor or hospital
See Memo, for additional information

steps: Example comment entry: 02/13/26 at 0815, Victim was bitten on the knee. Circumstances: Provoked. Pomona Economic Opp Center Emailed. SEE MEMO/ATTACHMENTS.

sections.heading: Documentation
steps: Create an activity in Chameleon with all collected information.

Assign the appropriate priority level based on the situation: Priority 1 if the animal is currently loose and aggressive, or Priority 5 for bite investigations where the bite did not occur within the last hour and the animal is contained or its location is known.
Include a memo in the activity window using the 'Bite Details' template.

steps.step: Example memo using the 'Bite Details' template:
sub: Reported by... Owner
Address where bitten (if different from RP/CA)... 0 Bandini Ave, Riverside, CA 92506
Date Treated... 02/28/26
Treated by... self-treatment and hospital
Phone of person who treated... not provided
Type of treatment... cleaned wound with soap, water, alcohol (at home), antibiotics (hospital)

Remarks... The owner was unsure how the bite happened, but a minor was bitten on the left calf. The owner thinks the minor may have stepped on the dog by accident or tripped over the dog. Minor has been around the dog various times.
Father of Victim: [first name]. Phone: XXX-XXX-XXXX
... [staff username]

related.name: Chameleon shelter management system (Activity creation; 'Bite Details' memo template)

documents.title: Citations, Violations, Collections, and Administrative Process Call Procedures
documents.purpose: This SOP establishes standardized call handling procedures for inquiries related to animal control citations, violation notices, citation extensions, collections, kennel license applications, rescue permit applications, and spay/neuter appointment scheduling. These procedures ensure that callers are routed to the correct program contacts and that citation-related information is documented accurately.
documents.authority: Call center staff are authorized to look up citations, process first-time extension requests for administrative citations, and route callers to the appropriate program contacts. Second or additional extensions for administrative citations require approval from a field services supervisor (Sergeant or above). Non-Traffic Notice to Appear citations are not eligible for extensions.
documents.scope: This procedure applies to all call center staff handling citation, violation, collections, kennel license, rescue permit, and spay/neuter scheduling inquiries.
documents.supersedes: None identified
references: Riverside County Ordinance No. 630
California Food and Agriculture Code
RCDAS citation and hearing procedures

definitions.def: A door hanger left by an Animal Control Officer after visiting a property and not making contact with a resident.
A citation issued for violations of animal-related ordinances, which may be eligible for extension requests.
A citation requiring a court appearance that is not eligible for extension under any circumstances.
A license issued by RCDAS that permits more than four dogs to be maintained on a property.

sections.heading: Green Card (Official Notice) Inquiries
steps: Direct the caller to contact the Animal Control Officer using the phone number listed on the green card.
If no number was left, gather the caller's information and the activity information, then email the officer, copying the appropriate Sergeant or Lieutenant.
Inform the caller that an email has been sent and the officer will reach out.

sections.heading: Citation Inquiries
steps: Transfer citation-related calls to the following extensions: RIV/SJ 8-7885; CVAC/Blythe 3-7075.
Note that Animal Control Officers leave a yellow copy of citations for the property owner.

sections.heading: Citation Extension Requests
steps: Look up the citation in Chameleon by incident number.
Add a timestamp note explaining the extension needed.
Update the system and email DAScitations@rivco.org.
Process a first extension request for an Administrative Citation without supervisor approval.
Obtain approval from a field services supervisor (Sergeant or above) for second or additional extension requests for Administrative Citations.
Do not grant extensions for Non-Traffic Notice to Appear citations under any circumstances.

sections.heading: Collections Issues
steps: Email dascollections@rivco.org with the Person ID (PID), citation information, owner name, phone number, and email address.
For City of Riverside collections matters, direct the caller to 951-826-5557.

sections.heading: Kennel License and Rescue Permit Applications or Renewals
steps: Email daskennels@rivco.org with the applicant's name, address, phone number, and license or permit number if known.

sections.heading: Spay/Neuter Appointment Scheduling
steps: Inform callers that RCDAS does not currently schedule owned pet spay/neuter appointments at its facilities. Owned pet services are available through the mobile spay/neuter vehicle or on event days.
For shelter animals needing spay/neuter scheduling, follow the campus-specific process listed under Campus Variations.

steps.step: For owned pets seeking spay/neuter:
sub: Inform the caller that most mobile bus days are free.
Direct the caller to the RCDAS website for the current schedule.
Standard fees are $108 for dogs and $63 for cats when a fee applies.

campusVariations.variation: For shelter animal spay/neuter scheduling, add the animal to the shared Excel file.
For shelter animal spay/neuter scheduling, email the CVAC Shelter Manager and Counselor Supervisor the caller's contact information.
For shelter animal spay/neuter scheduling, collect information and send via email to the front counter team, copying their Supervisor.

documents.title: Public Relations
documents.purpose: To establish a uniform procedure for employees while interacting with the public.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: All County of Riverside Department of Animal Services employees
documents.supersedes: Policy 000-07 Public Relations, effective August 29, 2016 (prior revisions October 6, 2009 and October 16, 2000)
references: DAS Policy 000-06 Dress Code
County of Riverside Ordinance 440
Workplace Threat Incident Report Against County of Riverside Employee form (SOP form 2010-1; Attachment I)

sections.heading: General Conduct
steps: Conduct all Departmental business in a helpful, courteous, and respectful manner.

Recognize that Field, Shelter, or clerical staff may be the only personal contact constituents have with Animal Services or the County, and that the impression Animal Services employees make is very important to the Department and the County.

sections.heading: Language
steps: Use appropriate and clear language when communicating with the general public, and avoid technical terms and slang.
Handle each situation individually and according to the circumstance; there is no specific set of rules or fixed approach that covers all possible scenarios.
Address individuals in terms that are comprehensive and easy to understand.

sections.heading: Appearance
steps: Wear uniforms or business attire, depending on the area of assignment, in accordance with DAS Policy 000-06 Dress Code. Appropriate dress and adequate grooming allow for a positive impression on our customers and are expected of all Department employees.
Obtain preapproval from your supervisor for any exception to the dress standard.

sections.heading: Public Contact
steps: Consider carefully all statements made to the public. Animal Services employees represent an official voice in any discussion conducted with the public, and statements may have to be defended later.
Do not use sarcastic or joking comments; they are often misunderstood by the public and the news media.
Clarify all statements made so that other parties involved in the conversation understand exactly what the speaker intends.

Do not criticize, endorse, or recommend commercial products. Give necessary advice in generic terms and recommend types of products rather than specific name brands (e.g., sanitizing materials, construction materials).

sections.heading: Public Trust
steps: Recognize that a great deal of trust and confidence is placed on Animal Services employees, and that constituents often provide staff with information, observations, and materials they consider confidential.
Handle information shared by constituents appropriately and do not violate that trust and confidence.

sections.heading: Gratuities, Gifts, and Favors
steps: Do not accept any form of gratuity, gift, or favor from any constituent.
Conduct yourself in a manner above the possibility of any suspicion that your influence can be exchanged for gratuities, gifts, or favors.

sections.heading: Violence: De-escalation (All Staff)
steps: Treat employee safety, and the safety of others, as the Department of Animal Services first priority.

Attempt to de-escalate encounters with disruptive constituents, or constituents who subject employees to harassing or abusive treatment, by allowing the constituent to vent, remaining calm, listening, understanding and acknowledging, empathizing, and resolving.

Stay alert to possible underlying reasons for a customer's disruptive behavior, such as intoxication (alcohol or drugs), disabilities, mental health disorders, or other medical conditions that may result in specific disruptions.

Continue to gain voluntary compliance from the constituent if the constituent is receptive. There may be accommodations that allow the constituent to complete the transaction while minimizing any negative impact on other customers or further harassment of the employee.

sections.heading: Violence: Field Services
steps: Leave the situation immediately if a constituent in the field becomes exceptionally difficult or violent.
Complete the business with the constituent at another time, once the situation has de-escalated and the constituent has calmed down, if you feel business can be conducted.
Discuss the matter with your supervisor if you feel business cannot be completed at any other time without the possibility of the constituent becoming violent.

The supervisor, upon hearing the complaint, will provide the employee with a Workplace Threat Incident Report Against County of Riverside Employee form (SOP form 2010-1; Attachment I) to assure that all workplace threats and violent behavior are documented and addressed promptly.

Depart and return later with both the police/sheriff present and your supervisor once the emotional atmosphere for violence becomes established, even though many arguments can be avoided by careful choice of language.

sections.heading: Violence: Office Personnel
steps: End the discussion or transaction and leave the situation immediately, notifying the appropriate supervisor or manager, if a constituent becomes exceptionally difficult or violent.
Work with your supervisor or manager to arrange to complete business at a later date and time if you feel business can be completed once the constituent has calmed down.
Consider having a second employee or neutral person present in order to complete the intended business, due to hostilities established from the initial employee.
Excuse yourself and discuss the matter with your supervisor if you feel business cannot be completed at any other time without the possibility of the constituent becoming violent.

The supervisor, upon hearing the complaint, will provide the employee with a Workplace Threat Incident Report Against County of Riverside Employee form (SOP form 2010-1; Attachment I) to assure that all workplace threats and violent behavior are documented and addressed promptly.
Depart and return with your supervisor and/or police assistance, if necessary, once the emotional atmosphere for violence becomes established.

sections.heading: Violence: Telephone Calls
steps: Warn the constituent that you will terminate the call if the caller refuses to calm down and speak in an appropriate tone without the use of foul language.
Terminate the call and notify your supervisor if the caller persists.

The supervisor, upon hearing the complaint, will provide the employee with a Workplace Threat Incident Report Against County of Riverside Employee form (SOP form 2010-1; Attachment I) to assure that all workplace threats and violent behavior are documented and addressed promptly.
Remove yourself from the situation and seek the advice of your supervisor for further action once the emotional atmosphere becomes established.

sections.heading: Drugs and Alcohol
steps: Do not be under the influence of alcohol or drugs, consume alcohol or illicit drugs, or possess controlled substances or prescription drugs without a prescription, while on duty (including stand-by) or on County property. This is the policy of the County of Riverside and the Department of Animal Services.

Notify your supervisor before beginning work when taking medications or drugs that could foreseeably interfere with the safe and effective performance of duties or operation of County equipment or vehicles. While use of medically prescribed medications and drugs is not a violation of County Ordinance 440, failure to notify can result in discipline, up to and including termination.

Obtain clearance from a qualified physician, if required, in the event there is a question regarding an employee's ability to safely and effectively perform assigned duties while using such medications or drugs.

Contact your supervisor or the Employee Assistance Services (EAS) counselor for additional information on the County's voluntary EAS program, which assists employees who voluntarily seek help for alcohol or drug related problems. The County is committed to providing reasonable accommodations to those employees whose drug or alcohol problems classify them as handicapped under Federal and/or State law.

related.name: Workplace Threat Incident Report Against County of Riverside Employee (SOP form 2010-1; Attachment I)
County of Riverside Employee Assistance Services (EAS) program

appendices: Appendix: Workplace Threat Incident Report Against County of Riverside Employee form (SOP form 2010-1, Attachment I) (attached separately)

documents.title: Emergency Disaster Plan
documents.purpose: To establish the Department of Animal Services response structure and procedures when the department is requested to respond to a natural disaster, including notification, command roles, field coordination, and animal evacuation support.
documents.authority: The Director of Animal Services approves this procedure. The Animal Services Operations Chief, or the on-duty Supervising Animal Control Officer, is authorized to activate the disaster response, designate command roles, adjust work schedules, and authorize overtime expenditures during an extended disaster response.
documents.scope: All Animal Services employees. Applies whenever the department is requested to respond to, or observes, a natural disaster requiring departmental support.
documents.supersedes: Policy 200-23 (effective October 7, 2009; revised from February 7, 2002)
references: California Food and Agriculture Code (section to be confirmed during review)
Riverside County Emergency Services Ordinance, Chapter 2.100
Related RCDAS Policy: 200-29 Standard and Emergency Radio Policy
Related RCDAS Documents: Facility Emergency Response Plans (per campus); DAS Fiscal Requirements for Emergency Response

definitions.def: A stationary or mobile position established at the scene of a disaster, which serves as the headquarters for the Incident Commander.
A location in which all manpower and equipment is held pending assignment to a specific task or area.
A location used for rest and feeding of personnel assigned to a specific disaster.
An Animal Control Officer assigned to the command post to coordinate information from the Incident Commander to Animal Control units at the scene and to the Department of Animal Services.
Outlines and supervises civilian volunteers in case of disaster, formulates plans for animal evacuation and housing, supervises selected volunteers, and plans their assignments.
A location on the scene of the disaster used for temporary confinement of animals evacuated or rescued from the disaster site.

sections.heading: Notification
steps: Receive initial notification of a disaster of magnitude from County Fire officials via telephone, or through direct observation by Department of Animal Services employees.

sections.heading: Activation and Response
steps: Contact the Sheriff's radio dispatcher by any means possible upon receipt of information or observation of a disaster to determine whether a Command Post has been established. If so, request the exact location of the Command Post.

Designate an Animal Control Officer to respond to the Command Post (Animal Services Operations Chief or on-duty Supervising Animal Control Officer). Upon arrival, the officer contacts the Incident Commander and obtains pertinent information about the disaster, resources requested from the department, and other facts needed to implement the plan of action.

steps.step: If the department will be committed to a support role in the disaster, take the following steps:
sub: The Operations Chief, or designee, designates personnel to act as Liaison Officer and Field Coordinating Officer. In a normal situation, the Operations Chief performs as the Liaison Officer and the Supervising Animal Control Officer performs as the Field Coordinating Officer.
The Volunteer Coordinator reports to the Operations Chief and supervises volunteers and paid personnel in the evacuation, care, and housing of domestic, equine, and companion animals.

The Liaison Officer reports to the Command Post and establishes contact with the Incident Commander. The initially responding Animal Control Officer is then relieved and reports to the Staging Area to contact the Field Coordinating Officer for further direction.

The Field Coordinating Officer proceeds directly to the Staging Area with appropriate emergency equipment and takes charge of all staff, units, and equipment at the disaster site as second in command under the Liaison Officer.

The Field Coordinating Officer designates an Animal Control Officer to maintain a list of Field Services personnel and assigned unit numbers at the disaster site, ensure all units are serviced for continuous use, minimize physical risks to personnel, and ensure prompt medical treatment if a crew member is injured.

steps.step: If animals must be evacuated, use the closest Department of Animal Services shelter, or contracted site, as a temporary holding facility under the following guidelines:
sub: If the disaster will be of extended duration (8 continuous work hours or longer), the Operations Chief is authorized to adjust work schedules, expend overtime funds, or modify the work force to sustain support, such as placing support forces on a 12-hour on/12-hour off schedule.
The Operations Chief, or designee, maintains lines of communication with the Director of Animal Services and recommends use of additional staff to support ongoing actions.

camp a location used for rest and feeding of personnel assigned to a specific disaster. aco liaison officer an animal control officer assigned to the command post to coordinate information from the incident commander to animal control units at the scene and to the department of animal services. volunteer coordinator outlines and supervises civilian volunteers in case of disaster, formulates plans for animal evacuation and housing, supervises selected volunteers, and plans their assignments. holding area a location on the scene of the disaster used for temporary confinement of animals evacuated or rescued from the disaster site. notification receive initial notification of a disaster of magnitude from county fire officials via telephone, or through direct observation by department of animal services employees. activation and response contact the sheriff's radio dispatcher by any means possible upon receipt of information or observation of a disaster to determine whether a command post has been established. if so, request the exact location of the command post. designate an animal control officer to respond to the command post (animal services operations chief or on-duty supervising animal control officer). upon arrival, the officer contacts the incident commander and obtains pertinent information about the disaster, resources requested from the department, and other facts needed to implement the plan of action. {"step": "if the department will be committed to a support role in the disaster, take the following steps:", "sub": ["the operations chief, or designee, designates personnel to act as liaison officer and field coordinating officer. in a normal situation, the operations chief performs as the liaison officer and the supervising animal control officer performs as the field coordinating officer.", "the volunteer coordinator reports to the operations chief and supervises volunteers and paid personnel in the evacuation, care, and housing of domestic, equine, and companion animals.", "the liaison officer reports to the command post and establishes contact with the incident commander. the initially responding animal control officer is then relieved and reports to the staging area to contact the field coordinating officer for further direction.", "the field coordinating officer proceeds directly to the staging area with appropriate emergency equipment and takes charge of all staff, units, and equipment at the disaster site as second in command under the liaison officer.", "the field coordinating officer designates an animal control officer to maintain a list of field services personnel and assigned unit numbers at the disaster site, ensure all units are serviced for continuous use, minimize physical risks to personnel, and ensure prompt medical treatment if a crew member is injured."]} {"step": "if animals must be evacuated, use the closest department of animal services shelter, or contracted site, as a temporary holding facility under the following guidelines:", "sub": ["if the disaster will be of extended duration (8 continuous work hours or longer), the operations chief is authorized to adjust work schedules, expend overtime funds, or modify the work force to sustain support, such as placing support forces on a 12-hour on/12-hour off schedule.", "the operations chief, or designee, maintains lines of communication with the director of animal services and recommends use of additional staff to support ongoing actions."]} california food and agriculture code (section to be confirmed during review) riverside county emergency services ordinance, chapter 2.100 related rcdas policy: 200-29 standard and emergency radio policy related rcdas documents: facility emergency response plans (per campus); das fiscal requirements for emergency response

documents.title: Standard and Emergency Radio Policy
documents.purpose: To establish a uniform radio procedure for all Animal Services personnel and radio dispatchers to communicate effectively with each other and outside agencies, and to establish uniform emergency radio guidelines that ensure officer safety and timely assistance to the public.
documents.authority: The Director of Animal Services approves this procedure. Dispatch supervisors and field supervisors are responsible for training staff in proper radio use and enforcing radio discipline.
documents.scope: All Animal Services employees who operate a County vehicle equipped with a unit radio, use hand-held radios of any kind, or use department-issued communication devices.
documents.supersedes: Policy 200-29 (effective October 7, 2009; revised from July 18, 2005)
references: Related RCDAS Policy: 200-23 Emergency Disaster Plan
Appendix A: 10-Codes, 11-Codes, Standard Codes, and Phonetic Alphabet

definitions.def: Emergency traffic. All radio traffic stops immediately except for the officer calling in.
Resume normal radio traffic.
An officer's periodic call-in to dispatch for pending calls.
The unit number identifying an officer or dispatcher on the radio.
Emergency traffic pending for a specific unit; dispatch announces it at intervals until acknowledged.

sections.heading: Standard Radio Procedure
steps: Use designated channels: Channel 1 is South West; Channel 2 is Riverside Dispatch; Channel 3 is Supervisor Car-to-Car. Channels 1 and 2 carry regular radio traffic; Channel 3 carries lengthier dialog, information gathering, and group or personal communications. The primary communication area may be referred to as Dispatch, Station 8.
State your designator and station name at the beginning of each transmission and after any prolonged break in transmission.

Upon arriving at a call, contact dispatch with your designator and either the primary and cross streets or the activity/call number. Know your whereabouts at all times (block numbers, primary and cross streets, direction of travel) and keep dispatch apprised for your safety.

Upon completing a call, call in only the dispositions Chameleon will accept, in the order they are entered into the computer: state whether the call is closed or left open, the activity number, and the action taken. Keep transmissions brief; explanations and lengthy dialog cannot be entered into the computer and interfere with radio operation.

Call in at the beginning of each shift, after breaks, Code-7s, 10-6s, or any activity lasting longer than 15 minutes, and be prepared to write down pending calls. Make periodic traffic checks whenever you have been out of your vehicle or on a call for a lengthy period, at a minimum of half-hour intervals.

Dispatchers do not randomly contact officers about waiting traffic. Exception: if emergency traffic is pending, dispatch contacts the officer periodically announcing Priority Traffic, repeating at 5-minute intervals with the call holding time if there is no response. When calls build up in an area, dispatch may periodically announce the officer's designator and the number of calls holding, without expecting a response.

Dispatch new calls in this order: activity number, type of call, primary location, cross street, whether the previous is the C.A. or the R.P., map page and grid number, then any additional information.
Before leaving the field, complete a final traffic check and respond to any priorities before going 10-19.
Upon arrival at the station at end of shift, notify dispatch that you are 10-7, or 10-10 if on emergency standby status.

sections.heading: Emergency Radio Procedure
steps: In an obvious emergency or a dangerous or potentially dangerous situation, immediately contact dispatch with your designator and state 10-33. All radio traffic stops immediately except for the officer calling in. The dispatcher announces the channel is 10-33 and for which officer, then obtains information about the emergency. If two channels are working, the second dispatcher announces to their officers which channel is 10-33, for which officer, and the general location, then continues regular traffic; a simultaneous second emergency is handled by that operator, switching frequency if necessary.

When the first responder deems the incident under control, safe, and needing no further assistance, they direct the operator to go 10-34. If multiple units were dispatched, the operator asks whether any unit still needs 10-33 before each operator announces 10-34 and the time. When the time of day coincides with a ten or eleven code, add the word 'hours' (e.g., 10:23 hours).

If an officer attempts to call in but cannot be identified, sounds in distress, or cannot respond, dispatch advises officers of a 10-33 Roll Call and attempts contact with every officer and inspector on duty in designator order, by all available means. Each officer contacted responds Code-4 if okay. If an officer does not respond after continued attempts, notify supervision immediately, research the officer's last location, and dispatch the closest area officers to search. Supervision may contact law enforcement for assistance if the officer cannot be located.
Conduct routine safety checks at the dispatcher's discretion, when an officer has not made contact for an extended period, or at an officer's request when they feel their safety may be compromised.

sections.heading: Effective Radio Usage
steps: Know what you are going to say ahead of time; when you need to pause, say 'break' and un-key the mike (two-second rule).
Practice phonetics on license plates, street names, and signs, and practice descriptions of vehicles, people, and animals.
Know your directions of travel (north, south, east, west) and mentally practice emergency scenarios.
Speak calmly, audibly, and as unemotionally as possible. Break phonetics and numbers into blocks of three. Be brief and concise.

related.name: Appendix A: Radio Codes and Phonetic Alphabet

appendices: Appendix A: 10-Codes, 11-Codes, Standard Codes, and Phonetic Alphabet

revisions.author: Outcomes for Pets (draft)
revisions.desc: Migrated into standard RCDAS SOP template. Content unchanged from 2009 version except removal of references to Nextel devices (service defunct); substantive edits pending (see edit log).
