documents.title: Emergency Purchasing Procedure
documents.purpose: To define how DAS staff obtain goods and services during an emergency in compliance with County Purchasing rules, including who may declare a purchasing emergency and how after-hours emergency purchases are handled and reported.
documents.authority: Per County Purchasing Manual 4.2.2, only the Board of Supervisors and the Director or Assistant Director of Purchasing can deem a situation a true emergency. Sole source purchases during a state of emergency require sign-off by the Director and/or Assistant Director of County Purchasing before goods or services are purchased.
documents.scope: All DAS staff involved in procuring commodities or services during an emergency.
documents.supersedes: Emergency Plan Flowchart Purchasing (updated October 11, 2012)
references: Riverside County Purchasing Manual, Section 4.1.1 (Emergency Purchases)
Riverside County Purchasing Manual, Section 4.1.2 (Operational Necessities)
Riverside County Purchasing Manual, Section 4.2.2 (Emergency)
Riverside County Purchasing Manual, Section 19.2.1 (Emergency PO Coding System)
Related RCDAS SOP: Fiscal Requirements for Emergency Response

definitions.def: The immediate procurement of commodities and/or services caused by a sudden, unexpected turn of events beyond the control of the County involving public health, welfare, safety, injury, or loss. Emergency situations exist when there is a clear and present danger to life and property or a breach in security.

sections.heading: Emergency Purchase Process
steps: For a true emergency, phone County Purchasing and request an emergency purchase order. Provide a general description of the required purchases, suggested vendors, approximate dollar value, and the reason the situation is an emergency.
For sole source purchases during a state of emergency, obtain sign-off from the Director and/or Assistant Director of County Purchasing before purchasing goods or services.

Report any emergency purchase made without Purchasing's approval to Purchasing immediately and enter a requisition into PeopleSoft. Use the Departmental Buyer or Purchasing Liaison when possible to help with the emergency purchase.

After normal working hours, when the Purchasing Agent or designees are unavailable, departments may make emergency purchases to meet statutory deadlines or immediate unforeseen emergencies. Such purchases are for statutory or emergency needs only and must be reported to the Purchasing Agent or designee during the next scheduled work period.
Phone quotes may be used for purchases of personal property under $25,000; in emergencies where delay may cause damage to life or property, the $25,000 limit is waived.
If system-generated PO numbers cannot be obtained, Buyers or LVPA processors may generate their own emergency purchase orders using the Emergency PO Coding System (Purchasing Manual 19.2.1).

revisions.author: Outcomes for Pets (draft)
revisions.desc: Migrated into standard RCDAS SOP template. Dollar limits and manual section numbers must be verified against the current County Purchasing Manual (see edit log).

documents.title: Facility Emergency Response Plan
documents.flag: Unnumbered (=Protocol per rule) but is a required written program/plan - consider policy number
documents.purpose: To establish a consistent Emergency Response Plan for Department of Animal Services County-owned or leased facilities. The plan provides all employees with a document outlining responses to emergencies and natural or man-made disasters to ensure their safety, and establishes trained Emergency Response Teams at each facility.
documents.authority: The facility Emergency Manager (a supervisor with SEMS operations overview training at minimum) is the focal point for emergency planning, communication, and decisions at each facility, including the decision to evacuate and reoccupy. The Department Director approves the plan (original plans approved by Robert Miller, Director, Animal Services). The plan is also activated whenever the Department Head or Emergency Manager believes it necessary. During declared disasters, all County employees become Disaster Service Workers.
documents.scope: All Department of Animal Services employees and workplaces at County-owned or leased animal services facilities: Blythe Animal Campus, Coachella Valley Animal Campus, Western Riverside County/City Animal Shelter, and San Jacinto Valley Animal Campus. Employees at each facility must be instructed in these procedures at least annually.
documents.supersedes: Facility Emergency Response Plans for Blythe, CVAC, Riverside (2013) and SJVAC Emergency Response Plan (March 22, 2016)
references: California Code of Regulations, Title 8 GISO 3220
County of Riverside Standard Safety Operations Manual Document Number 7002: General Emergency Plan and Guidelines
Riverside County Board of Supervisors proclamation requiring two evacuation drills annually at every County-owned or leased facility
State Emergency Management System (SEMS)
County Emergency Operations Center (EOC), basement of County Administration Center, downtown Riverside; alternate EOC in basement of CAC, Indio

definitions.def: The supervisory employee who serves as facility Point of Contact during a disaster, major emergency, or evacuation; makes the decision to evacuate and reoccupy the facility; and administers the plan, drills, and records. Appoints Alternate Manager(s) to act in their absence.

Employees selected for emergency duties (area sweep, information runner, roll call, first aid, public/crowd control). Members should be conscientious, communicate well, be physically capable, and hold positions that do not routinely take them off site. Light search and rescue and bomb threat searches may be performed by trained team members only.
A designated location as far from the building as possible, away from overhead power lines, large trees, utility mains, and vehicle traffic, where evacuees report and remain until released.
The location where the Emergency Manager operates and coordinates recovery efforts, designated on the facility site plan map.

Non-life-threatening situations handled by in-house resources: power failures, interior flooding from water leaks or pipe breaks, minor smoke accumulations from non-threatening fires, minor earthquake disruptions with no damage or injuries, entrapments such as stalled elevators.

Moderate to severe and/or life-threatening emergencies, including significant property damage, involving employees plus external fire, medical, or law enforcement resources. The Emergency Manager is usually in charge. Examples: life-threatening medical emergencies, minor fires confined to a small room, bomb threats, moderate earthquake disruptions, evacuations, minor hazardous material spills confined to the room of origin.

Situations that overwhelm facility resources and possibly emergency personnel, managed from an Incident Command Post or the County Emergency Operations Center. Examples: mass triage casualties, major fire, uncontrolled hazardous material spills, explosions or bomb/chemical/biological/radiological releases, major earthquake damage with injuries.

The status all Riverside County employees assume during disasters; employees may be assigned emergency duties and may not be released to go home until dismissed by their supervisor or the Disaster Corps Commander.

sections.heading: Plan Activation and Maintenance
steps: Keep this plan in a red 3-ring binder labeled Facility Emergency Response Plan, accessible to all employees; the plan remains with the Acting Emergency Manager throughout an emergency.

steps.step: Consider the plan officially activated when any of the following occurs:
sub: Serious or life-threatening injury occurs.
Fire or smoke is discovered in the facility.
Evacuation of the facility is required.
Natural events such as a storm or earthquake have damaged the facility or threatened the life or safety of its occupants.

External sources of toxic fumes or smoke from a nearby accident or fire may enter the facility (sometimes the best course is not to evacuate but to prevent entry of smoke and fumes through mechanical means).
Whenever the Department Head or Emergency Manager believes it is necessary.

steps: Review and update the plan annually; the Emergency Manager updates the site-specific section at least annually.
Post evacuation plan maps in strategic locations throughout the facility (lobby, conference rooms, reception areas, restrooms, etc.).
Instruct employees at the facility in these emergency procedures at least annually.
Maintain records of employee training, required evacuation drills, and actual emergency evacuations.
Maintain a current confidential list of employees with disabilities requiring special evacuation assistance, each with an assigned attendant.
Maintain a confidential employee roster (name, home phone, cell/pager) for disaster call-back and post-evacuation accountability.

steps.step: Emergency Manager and Alternate Manager(s):
sub: Function as the facility Point of Contact during a disaster, major emergency, or evacuation.
Appoint competent alternate manager(s) to serve in their absence.
Designate a Safe Assembly Area away from overhead power lines, large trees, utility mains, and vehicle traffic.
Be familiar with the plan, locations of fire alarms, fire extinguishers, emergency/disaster supplies, and utility shut-offs for the facility.
Ensure adequate personnel are designated and trained; maintain regular contact with employees assigned emergency duties.
Make the decision to evacuate and subsequently reoccupy the facility, normally in coordination with local fire and law officials.
Conduct a post-incident review of the plan after a drill or actual evacuation.
Coordinate with the Department Emergency Response Coordinator, local fire and police, and other agencies to facilitate training.

steps.step: Emergency Response Team Members:
sub: Be familiar with the plan and the locations of fire alarms, fire extinguishers, and emergency/disaster supplies.
Report to the Emergency Manager for instructions during emergencies.
Maintain preparedness through recertification training (CPR and First Aid), regular meetings, and drills.

steps.step: All Employees:
sub: Become familiar with the plan and know where a copy is kept.
Know how and to whom to report emergencies.
Become familiar with facility exits, fire extinguishers, and fire alarm pull stations.
Know the emergency assembly area location and report there upon evacuation.
Follow the instructions of the Emergency Manager and team members during any emergency or drill.

steps.step: Team member duty assignments during a full building evacuation or disaster:
sub: Emergency Manager and Alternate: coordinate with safety agencies; receive and send information to staff through information runners.

Area sweep assistants: sweep their floor areas to ensure all persons have evacuated, document any disabled person unable to evacuate or anyone who refuses to evacuate, and report to the Emergency Manager when their area is cleared.
Information Runner: delivers information to and from the Emergency Manager and their department.
Roll Call: conducts roll call of designated groups to account for all staff; relays results to the Emergency Manager through the information runner.
First Aid trained assistants: take a first aid kit and portable disaster supplies to the assembly area.
Public/crowd control assistant: directs the public to a safe place away from the building.
Light search and rescue and bomb threat search: trained team members only.

steps.step: Assisting employees needing special evacuation help (assigned team member; any team member in their absence):
sub: Visually impaired persons: tell the person the nature of the emergency and guide them to a safe area.
Hearing impaired persons: get their attention with a gesture or light tap on the arm or shoulder; write a note explaining the emergency.
Persons using crutches, canes, or walkers: if time permits, guide them to a stairwell; carrying options include a two-person arm lock or carrying the person seated in a sturdy chair.

Persons in wheelchairs: always consult the person about the number of assistants needed, how to be moved, whether to extend or bend extremities when lifting, whether to be carried forward or backward on stairs, and assistance needed after evacuation.

sections.heading: Reporting Emergencies
steps: For any police, fire, or medical emergency, dial 9-1-1 (dial the designated number for an outside line first when applicable, e.g., 9 then 9-1-1). If 9-1-1 is down, use the facility's alternate 7-digit fire and police numbers (see campus variations).

steps.step: Be prepared to give emergency dispatchers:
sub: The type of emergency (fire, accident, injury, etc.).
The scope of the emergency (number of people involved, size of problem).
The location: city, street address, building or business name, floor and room number.
Your name and the phone number you are calling from.
Any other details responders should be aware of.

steps: Stay on the line until the emergency operators indicate they have all the information they need.
For non-911 emergencies, contact the appropriate public utility company or trained in-house staff (Emergency Manager, Facilities Management). In all cases, notify your supervisor.
Any incident that has the potential of being a fire and life safety hazard shall be reported to Police or Fire.
Keep emergency numbers by each phone, clearly identified.

sections.heading: Evacuation

steps.step: Evacuations may be initiated by:
sub: Activation of the building fire alarm system (begin evacuation immediately without questioning whether it is a false alarm).
Public address, telephone paging system, or face-to-face directions.
Spontaneous evacuation in response to an observed emergency.
Emergency Manager or supervisor's request to evacuate.

steps.step: How to evacuate safely:
sub: Take necessary personal possessions (purse, keys, eyeglasses, medication, jacket); if time allows, turn off equipment such as tools, computers, and cooking appliances.
Calmly and quickly proceed to the nearest safe exit.
Instruct clients or visitors present to exit with you.
Use only the EXIT stairways; never use an elevator during an evacuation unless told to do so by emergency response personnel.

steps: When not to evacuate: if it is more dangerous to leave than stay (e.g., a passing toxic cloud), shut off the ventilation system, close doors and windows, and tell employees to stay inside.
Know all exits from your work area, not just the one you entered through; practice using alternate routes.
Proceed directly to the designated evacuation assembly area and remain there until told otherwise.
During evacuation, trained employees will search the building to ensure no one is left behind.

Conduct two documented evacuation drills annually: an earthquake drill in April (Earthquake Preparedness Month) and a fire drill in October (Fire Prevention Month). Contact the County Safety Office at (951) 955-3520 for drill guidance.
Complete the Evacuation Drill Report immediately after each drill, keep it in the red Emergency Response binder, and debrief staff.

sections.heading: Fire

steps.step: Fire scene priorities (in order):
sub: Rescue: remove others in immediate danger to safety.
Alert: activate the fire alarm and call 911 (you may have to dial 9 first on County phones); notify your Emergency Manager and organization managers.
Confine: close doors and windows to the room/area burning to prevent spread of fire and smoke.
Evacuate and Extinguish: remove anyone in immediate danger; attempt to extinguish the fire only if trained and it can be done safely.

steps: Extinguishing a fire is the last priority. Attempt it only if the fire is small and you know how to safely use the fire extinguisher, and only after alerting occupants and calling 911.
Stay low to the floor when evacuating to avoid breathing toxic smoke; most fire deaths are from inhaling gases and smoke.

sections.heading: Bomb Threat

steps.step: If you receive a bomb threat by phone:
sub: Use the Bomb Threat Checklist; keep a blank copy at each phone workstation.
Remain calm and keep the caller on the line as long as possible; ask to have the message repeated and record the information.
If the caller does not give the bomb's location or detonation time, ask for it.
Inform the caller that the building is occupied and detonation could kill or seriously injure many innocent people.
Note background noises and voice characteristics (sex, age, accent, speech patterns).
Immediately after the caller hangs up, report the threat to the DAS Safety Officer and your immediate supervisor; they will call the police. Wait for further instructions.

steps.step: Bomb search (occupant search begins immediately after the threat is received):
sub: Employees search their immediate work areas or as directed.
Response team members search public areas: lobbies, elevators, storage rooms, cafeteria, restrooms, and evacuation routes.
Building maintenance personnel search building-owner areas, maintenance and equipment storage, rooftop, and outside areas.
Look for anything that does not belong or cannot be explained; most bombs do not look like bombs.

steps.step: Upon locating a suspicious object:
sub: Do NOT touch, move, or disturb it in any manner.
Attempt to locate a possible owner in the immediate vicinity.
Evacuate the immediate area, leave doors and windows OPEN, and prevent re-entry.
Notify the Police with the exact location and a brief description; when feasible, shut off gas or fuel lines and remove flammables.
The Police will evaluate and determine the appropriate action.

steps.step: If evacuation is ordered (Emergency Manager's decision based on threat credibility):
sub: Follow specific evacuation directions.
Leave doors and windows open to let the blast wave escape.
Take your briefcase, purse, and lunch bag with you to minimize the search.
Conduct a quick visual check for suspicious objects as you exit and report anything unusual to the Emergency Manager outside.
The Emergency Manager will work with police to keep anyone from re-entering the building.

steps: After each area is searched, report results to the Emergency Manager or Command Center. Use normal phones for communication; radios or cellular phones can detonate a bomb.

steps.step: Before an earthquake:
sub: Check all work locations for hazards (unsecured equipment, furniture, overhead items) and have them secured or removed.

Identify safe locations to duck, cover, and hold: under desks or sturdy tables, or against inside walls (doorways are not ideal). Outside: open areas away from buildings, trees, light poles, and overhead wires.
Remove heavy items from high places, secure unstable objects, and place breakables safely.
Practice the plan; physically place yourself in safe locations.
Stock disaster supplies for home and work: enough food and water for three days; keep a kit in your vehicle or office.

steps.step: During the shaking:
sub: Remain calm.

If indoors, stay there. DO NOT RUN OUTDOORS; most deaths and injuries occur as people leave buildings. Get under a desk or table or stand in a corner of an interior wall; if you cannot get under something sturdy, duck and cover your head with your hands.
If outdoors, get into an open area away from trees, buildings, walls, and power lines.
In a high-rise, stay away from windows and outside walls; do not use elevators until inspected by service personnel.
If driving, pull over and stop, but not under overpasses or power lines; remain in the vehicle until the shaking stops.

steps.step: After the shaking:
sub: Stop and evaluate your immediate area before responding; implement this plan if necessary; turn on a radio for emergency reports.
Check for injuries and render first aid as needed; do not move seriously injured persons unless they are in danger of further injury.
Assess the situation: fires and fire hazards (especially gas leaks and damaged wiring), building cracks and overhead collapse hazards, exit usability, and available manpower and equipment.
Evacuate if appropriate; for most minor earthquakes with no signs of damage, evacuation is not needed. Watch for falling bricks and glass; report to the designated assembly area.
Prepare for aftershocks. Any building showing signs of structural damage should be evacuated until checked by a building inspector or structural engineer.

Cooperate with Emergency Managers and trained employees; all County employees become Disaster Service Workers during disasters and may not be able to go home due to emergency assignments, supervisor direction, or transportation disruption.

steps.step: Do's and don'ts:
sub: Do use portable radios for emergency broadcast information (station list varies by campus; see campus variations).
Do cooperate with fire, police, and emergency response team personnel.
Don't light matches or operate electrical equipment where gas leaks are suspected.
Don't use telephones except to report emergency information.
Don't drive unless absolutely necessary; keep streets clear for emergency vehicles.

sections.heading: Facility Maps and Utility Shut-Offs
steps: Maintain a Facility Site Plan Map showing the facility, parking lot, and adjacent buildings, with utility shut-offs (water, gas, electrical), the designated safe assembly area, and the staging area marked.
Maintain a Facility Floor Plan (Evacuation Route Map) showing evacuation routes, fire extinguishers, fire alarm pull stations, first aid kits, and disaster supplies.
Post these maps conspicuously throughout the building; the Emergency Manager must ensure evacuation maps are posted at the facility.
The Emergency Manager must know the locations of all utility shut-offs for the facility (marked on each campus site map).

documents.searchText: to establish a consistent emergency response plan for department of animal services county-owned or leased facilities. the plan provides all employees with a document outlining responses to emergencies and natural or man-made disasters to ensure their safety, and establishes trained emergency response teams at each facility. all department of animal services employees and workplaces at county-owned or leased animal services facilities: blythe animal campus, coachella valley animal campus, western riverside county/city animal shelter, and san jacinto valley animal campus. employees at each facility must be instructed in these procedures at least annually. the facility emergency manager (a supervisor with sems operations overview training at minimum) is the focal point for emergency planning, communication, and decisions at each facility, including the decision to evacuate and reoccupy. the department director approves the plan (original plans approved by robert miller, director, animal services). the plan is also activated whenever the department head or emergency manager believes it necessary. during declared disasters, all county employees become disaster service workers. emergency manager the supervisory employee who serves as facility point of contact during a disaster, major emergency, or evacuation; makes the decision to evacuate and reoccupy the facility; and administers the plan, drills, and records. appoints alternate manager(s) to act in their absence. emergency response team employees selected for emergency duties (area sweep, information runner, roll call, first aid, public/crowd control). members should be conscientious, communicate well, be physically capable, and hold positions that do not routinely take them off site. light search and rescue and bomb threat searches may be performed by trained team members only. safe assembly area a designated location as far from the building as possible, away from overhead power lines, large trees, utility mains, and vehicle traffic, where evacuees report and remain until released. staging area the location where the emergency manager operates and coordinates recovery efforts, designated on the facility site plan map. minor emergency non-life-threatening situations handled by in-house resources: power failures, interior flooding from water leaks or pipe breaks, minor smoke accumulations from non-threatening fires, minor earthquake disruptions with no damage or injuries, entrapments such as stalled elevators. moderate emergency moderate to severe and/or life-threatening emergencies, including significant property damage, involving employees plus external fire, medical, or law enforcement resources. the emergency manager is usually in charge. examples: life-threatening medical emergencies, minor fires confined to a small room, bomb threats, moderate earthquake disruptions, evacuations, minor hazardous material spills confined to the room of origin. major emergency situations that overwhelm facility resources and possibly emergency personnel, managed from an incident command post or the county emergency operations center. examples: mass triage casualties, major fire, uncontrolled hazardous material spills, explosions or bomb/chemical/biological/radiological releases, major earthquake damage with injuries. disaster service worker the status all riverside county employees assume during disasters; employees may be assigned emergency duties and may not be released to go home until dismissed by their supervisor or the disaster corps commander. plan activation and maintenance keep this plan in a red 3-ring binder labeled facility emergency response plan, accessible to all employees; the plan remains with the acting emergency manager throughout an emergency. {"step": "consider the plan officially activated when any of the following occurs:", "sub": ["serious or life-threatening injury occurs.", "fire or smoke is discovered in the facility.", "evacuation of the facility is required.", "natural events such as a storm or earthquake have damaged the facility or threatened the life or safety of its occupants.", "external sources of toxic fumes or smoke from a nearby accident or fire may enter the facility (sometimes the best course is not to evacuate but to prevent entry of smoke and fumes through mechanical means).", "whenever the department head or emergency manager believes it is necessary."]} review and update the plan annually; the emergency manager updates the site-specific section at least annually. post evacuation plan maps in strategic locations throughout the facility (lobby, conference rooms, reception areas, restrooms, etc.). instruct employees at the facility in these emergency procedures at least annually. maintain records of employee training, required evacuation drills, and actual emergency evacuations. maintain a current confidential list of employees with disabilities requiring special evacuation assistance, each with an assigned attendant. maintain a confidential employee roster (name, home phone, cell/pager) for disaster call-back and post-evacuation accountability. emergency response team responsibilities {"step": "emergency manager and alternate manager(s):", "sub": ["function as the facility point of contact during a disaster, major emergency, or evacuation.", "appoint competent alternate manager(s) to serve in their absence.", "designate a safe assembly area away from overhead power lines, large trees, utility mains, and vehicle traffic.", "be familiar with the plan, locations of fire alarms, fire extinguishers, emergency/disaster supplies, and utility shut-offs for the facility.", "ensure adequate personnel are designated and trained; maintain regular contact with employees assigned emergency duties.", "make the decision to evacuate and subsequently reoccupy the facility, normally in coordination with local fire and law officials.", "conduct a post-incident review of the plan after a drill or actual evacuation.", "coordinate with the department emergency response coordinator, local fire and police, and other agencies to facilitate training."]} {"step": "emergency response team members:", "sub": ["be familiar with the plan and the locations of fire alarms, fire extinguishers, and emergency/disaster supplies.", "report to the emergency manager for instructions during emergencies.", "maintain preparedness through recertification training (cpr and first aid), regular meetings, and drills."]} {"step": "all employees:", "sub": ["become familiar with the plan and know where a copy is kept.", "know how and to whom to report emergencies.", "become familiar with facility exits, fire extinguishers, and fire alarm pull stations.", "know the emergency assembly area location and report there upon evacuation.", "follow the instructions of the emergency manager and team members during any emergency or drill."]} {"step": "team member duty assignments during a full building evacuation or disaster:", "sub": ["emergency manager and alternate: coordinate with safety agencies; receive and send information to staff through information runners.", "area sweep assistants: sweep their floor areas to ensure all persons have evacuated, document any disabled person unable to evacuate or anyone who refuses to evacuate, and report to the emergency manager when their area is cleared.", "information runner: delivers information to and from the emergency manager and their department.", "roll call: conducts roll call of designated groups to account for all staff; relays results to the emergency manager through the information runner.", "first aid trained assistants: take a first aid kit and portable disaster supplies to the assembly area.", "public/crowd control assistant: directs the public to a safe place away from the building.", "light search and rescue and bomb threat search: trained team members only."]} {"step": "assisting employees needing special evacuation help (assigned team member; any team member in their absence):", "sub": ["visually impaired persons: tell the person the nature of the emergency and guide them to a safe area.", "hearing impaired persons: get their attention with a gesture or light tap on the arm or shoulder; write a note explaining the emergency.", "persons using crutches, canes, or walkers: if time permits, guide them to a stairwell; carrying options include a two-person arm lock or carrying the person seated in a sturdy chair.", "persons in wheelchairs: always consult the person about the number of assistants needed, how to be moved, whether to extend or bend extremities when lifting, whether to be carried forward or backward on stairs, and assistance needed after evacuation."]} reporting emergencies for any police, fire, or medical emergency, dial 9-1-1 (dial the designated number for an outside line first when applicable, e.g., 9 then 9-1-1). if 9-1-1 is down, use the facility's alternate 7-digit fire and police numbers (see campus variations). {"step": "be prepared to give emergency dispatchers:", "sub": ["the type of emergency (fire, accident, injury, etc.).", "the scope of the emergency (number of people involved, size of problem).", "the location: city, street address, building or business name, floor and room number.", "your name and the phone number you are calling from.", "any other details responders should be aware of."]} stay on the line until the emergency operators indicate they have all the information they need. for non-911 emergencies, contact the appropriate public utility company or trained in-house staff (emergency manager, facilities management). in all cases, notify your supervisor. any incident that has the potential of being a fire and life safety hazard shall be reported to police or fire. keep emergency numbers by each phone, clearly identified. evacuation {"step": "evacuations may be initiated by:", "sub": ["activation of the building fire alarm system (begin evacuation immediately without questioning whether it is a false alarm).", "public address, telephone paging system, or face-to-face directions.", "spontaneous evacuation in response to an observed emergency.", "emergency manager or supervisor's request to evacuate."]} {"step": "how to evacuate safely:", "sub": ["take necessary personal possessions (purse, keys, eyeglasses, medication, jacket); if time allows, turn off equipment such as tools, computers, and cooking appliances.", "calmly and quickly proceed to the nearest safe exit.", "instruct clients or visitors present to exit with you.", "use only the exit stairways; never use an elevator during an evacuation unless told to do so by emergency response personnel."]} when not to evacuate: if it is more dangerous to leave than stay (e.g., a passing toxic cloud), shut off the ventilation system, close doors and windows, and tell employees to stay inside. know all exits from your work area, not just the one you entered through; practice using alternate routes. proceed directly to the designated evacuation assembly area and remain there until told otherwise. during evacuation, trained employees will search the building to ensure no one is left behind. conduct two documented evacuation drills annually: an earthquake drill in april (earthquake preparedness month) and a fire drill in october (fire prevention month). contact the county safety office at (951) 955-3520 for drill guidance. complete the evacuation drill report immediately after each drill, keep it in the red emergency response binder, and debrief staff. fire {"step": "fire scene priorities (in order):", "sub": ["rescue: remove others in immediate danger to safety.", "alert: activate the fire alarm and call 911 (you may have to dial 9 first on county phones); notify your emergency manager and organization managers.", "confine: close doors and windows to the room/area burning to prevent spread of fire and smoke.", "evacuate and extinguish: remove anyone in immediate danger; attempt to extinguish the fire only if trained and it can be done safely."]} extinguishing a fire is the last priority. attempt it only if the fire is small and you know how to safely use the fire extinguisher, and only after alerting occupants and calling 911. stay low to the floor when evacuating to avoid breathing toxic smoke; most fire deaths are from inhaling gases and smoke. bomb threat {"step": "if you receive a bomb threat by phone:", "sub": ["use the bomb threat checklist; keep a blank copy at each phone workstation.", "remain calm and keep the caller on the line as long as possible; ask to have the message repeated and record the information.", "if the caller does not give the bomb's location or detonation time, ask for it.", "inform the caller that the building is occupied and detonation could kill or seriously injure many innocent people.", "note background noises and voice characteristics (sex, age, accent, speech patterns).", "immediately after the caller hangs up, report the threat to the das safety officer and your immediate supervisor; they will call the police. wait for further instructions."]} {"step": "bomb search (occupant search begins immediately after the threat is received):", "sub": ["employees search their immediate work areas or as directed.", "response team members search public areas: lobbies, elevators, storage rooms, cafeteria, restrooms, and evacuation routes.", "building maintenance personnel search building-owner areas, maintenance and equipment storage, rooftop, and outside areas.", "look for anything that does not belong or cannot be explained; most bombs do not look like bombs."]} {"step": "upon locating a suspicious object:", "sub": ["do not touch, move, or disturb it in any manner.", "attempt to locate a possible owner in the immediate vicinity.", "evacuate the immediate area, leave doors and windows open, and prevent re-entry.", "notify the police with the exact location and a brief description; when feasible, shut off gas or fuel lines and remove flammables.", "the police will evaluate and determine the appropriate action."]} {"step": "if evacuation is ordered (emergency manager's decision based on threat credibility):", "sub": ["follow specific evacuation directions.", "leave doors and windows open to let the blast wave escape.", "take your briefcase, purse, and lunch bag with you to minimize the search.", "conduct a quick visual check for suspicious objects as you exit and report anything unusual to the emergency manager outside.", "the emergency manager will work with police to keep anyone from re-entering the building."]} after each area is searched, report results to the emergency manager or command center. use normal phones for communication; radios or cellular phones can detonate a bomb. earthquake {"step": "before an earthquake:", "sub": ["check all work locations for hazards (unsecured equipment, furniture, overhead items) and have them secured or removed.", "identify safe locations to duck, cover, and hold: under desks or sturdy tables, or against inside walls (doorways are not ideal). outside: open areas away from buildings, trees, light poles, and overhead wires.", "remove heavy items from high places, secure unstable objects, and place breakables safely.", "practice the plan; physically place yourself in safe locations.", "stock disaster supplies for home and work: enough food and water for three days; keep a kit in your vehicle or office."]} {"step": "during the shaking:", "sub": ["remain calm.", "if indoors, stay there. do not run outdoors; most deaths and injuries occur as people leave buildings. get under a desk or table or stand in a corner of an interior wall; if you cannot get under something sturdy, duck and cover your head with your hands.", "if outdoors, get into an open area away from trees, buildings, walls, and power lines.", "in a high-rise, stay away from windows and outside walls; do not use elevators until inspected by service personnel.", "if driving, pull over and stop, but not under overpasses or power lines; remain in the vehicle until the shaking stops."]} {"step": "after the shaking:", "sub": ["stop and evaluate your immediate area before responding; implement this plan if necessary; turn on a radio for emergency reports.", "check for injuries and render first aid as needed; do not move seriously injured persons unless they are in danger of further injury.", "assess the situation: fires and fire hazards (especially gas leaks and damaged wiring), building cracks and overhead collapse hazards, exit usability, and available manpower and equipment.", "evacuate if appropriate; for most minor earthquakes with no signs of damage, evacuation is not needed. watch for falling bricks and glass; report to the designated assembly area.", "prepare for aftershocks. any building showing signs of structural damage should be evacuated until checked by a building inspector or structural engineer.", "cooperate with emergency managers and trained employees; all county employees become disaster service workers during disasters and may not be able to go home due to emergency assignments, supervisor direction, or transportation disruption."]} {"step": "do's and don'ts:", "sub": ["do use portable radios for emergency broadcast information (station list varies by campus; see campus variations).", "do cooperate with fire, police, and emergency response team personnel.", "don't light matches or operate electrical equipment where gas leaks are suspected.", "don't use telephones except to report emergency information.", "don't drive unless absolutely necessary; keep streets clear for emergency vehicles."]} facility maps and utility shut-offs maintain a facility site plan map showing the facility, parking lot, and adjacent buildings, with utility shut-offs (water, gas, electrical), the designated safe assembly area, and the staging area marked. maintain a facility floor plan (evacuation route map) showing evacuation routes, fire extinguishers, fire alarm pull stations, first aid kits, and disaster supplies. post these maps conspicuously throughout the building; the emergency manager must ensure evacuation maps are posted at the facility. the emergency manager must know the locations of all utility shut-offs for the facility (marked on each campus site map). california code of regulations, title 8 giso 3220 county of riverside standard safety operations manual document number 7002: general emergency plan and guidelines riverside county board of supervisors proclamation requiring two evacuation drills annually at every county-owned or leased facility state emergency management system (sems) county emergency operations center (eoc), basement of county administration center, downtown riverside; alternate eoc in basement of cac, indio

documents.title: Fiscal Requirements for Emergency Response
documents.purpose: To ensure all costs of an emergency response are captured, documented, and coded correctly so the department qualifies for emergency reimbursement, and to define how Fiscal staff support emergency operations.
documents.authority: Fiscal Services creates and administers the emergency grant code. Department supervisors are responsible for ensuring emergency time and purchases are coded and documented as required.
documents.scope: All DAS sub-departments and all personnel working in an emergency response capacity, whether directly assigned to the declared emergency or providing backfill for someone who is.
documents.supersedes: DAS Fiscal Proposed Requirements for Emergency Response (undated)
references: Riverside County Emergency Services Ordinance, Chapter 2.100
Related RCDAS Policy: 200-23 Emergency Disaster Plan
Related RCDAS Document: Emergency Purchasing Procedure (County Purchasing Manual rules)

definitions.def: A special projects grant code created by Fiscal for each emergency, used to segregate all emergency recovery expenses. Fiscal notifies all DAS sub-departments of the code to use.
An individual working indirectly in place of someone else who is working directly for the declared emergency. Backfill support may be reimbursed by emergency funding.
Time reporting code (e.g., REG, OVT, CTW, Z01). Often only specific TRCs such as OVT/CTW are billable to the emergency.

sections.heading: Expense Segregation and Time Reporting
steps: Segregate expenses for emergency recovery using the special projects grant code beginning with HS5xxxxx, created and distributed by Fiscal for each emergency.
Identify time worked for emergency response by day, rather than by pay period, since allowable response times for labor may span only a few days.
Isolate overtime, z-time (shift differential), and regular time (for backfill instances), because often only specific TRCs such as OVT/CTW are billable.
Identify situations where backfill exists, since this support may be reimbursed by emergency funding.

Send hard copies of time sheets to Fiscal Services as time sheets are submitted to HR for all personnel working in an emergency response capacity, including those working directly for the declared emergency and those working as backfill.

Ensure time sheets reflect all components of labor (TRCs) charged to the emergency grant, and that the emergency grant appears on time sheets for each applicable component of labor before keying into HRMS.

sections.heading: Supporting Documentation
steps: Provide a brief summary of work performed by each individual by date, specifying direct involvement or backfill, and the location(s) where each individual reported by date. A modified Unit Log sheet with this information may meet this need.
Forward invoices related to the emergency response to Fiscal immediately as they are received, indicating the emergency grant code in addition to the department's usual accounting string.

Provide a memo for each purchase presenting justification and the locations where goods or services are used, including the rationale for purchasing goods versus renting, in case this must be demonstrated to qualify for reimbursement.

Notify Fiscal of pending purchases or services where delay in receipt is anticipated: include the type of goods or services, the vendor if known, when they are expected to be rendered, and the location(s) where they will be used. A List of Projects form with this information may meet this need.

Provide backup documentation for rented items (times operated, location of operations, scope of work) and for contracted services (contract number for existing contracts, dates services were provided, scope of work). The Rented Equipment Summary Record and Contract Work Summary Record may meet these needs.

sections.heading: Fiscal Staff Support During an Emergency Response
steps: Assist the public by providing information or instructions, helping complete necessary forms, and translating for Spanish-speaking persons.
Record donations from the public if necessary.
Assist with counting animals, feeding animals, distributing food to evacuees, and counting stock and materials needed for the emergency.
Issue cash drawers to staff collecting money from owners redeeming their animals (return-to-owner).

related.name: Unit Log (modified for emergency time tracking)
Rented Equipment Summary Record; Contract Work Summary Record

revisions.author: Outcomes for Pets (draft)
revisions.desc: Migrated into standard RCDAS SOP template from undated 'Proposed Requirements' document. Substantive edits pending (see edit log).

documents.title: Injury and Illness Prevention Program (IIPP)
documents.flag: Unnumbered (=Protocol per rule) but is a required written program/plan - consider policy number
documents.purpose: To establish general safety guidelines and procedures for Department of Animal Services workplaces and employees. The objective is an effective safety program within each department, division, or branch, indicated by declining injury and accident rates.
documents.authority: The Director of Animal Services is responsible for the establishment, implementation, and maintenance of the IIPP under California Code of Regulations Title 8 and Board of Supervisors Policy C-12. A designated Department Safety Representative (Rita Gutierrez as of March 2013, appointed by Director Robert P. Miller) implements and maintains the program. Supervisors are responsible for implementing the program in their areas and may designate a reliable employee as a site Safety Representative.
documents.scope: All Department of Animal Services employees governed by the County of Riverside Board of Supervisors, at all four animal campuses, whether working in the field, clinic, or office.
documents.supersedes: AS-IIPP Blythe, CVAC, Riverside, and San Jacinto (March 7, 2013)
references: California Code of Regulations, Title 8, General Industry Safety Orders (including §3203, Injury and Illness Prevention Program)
County of Riverside Board of Supervisors Policy C-12
County of Riverside Board of Supervisors Policy 440 (progressive discipline)
County of Riverside Standard Safety Operation Manual (SSOM) documents and safety forms (online at safety.rc-hr.com)
SSOM Document 300 (progressive disciplinary action guidelines)
SSOM Document 501 (Safety Orientation for Supervisory Personnel)
SSOM Document 601 (safety meetings)
SSOM Documents 2001 and 2012 (Bloodborne and Airborne Pathogens)
SSOM Document 2005 (Ergonomics and Back Injury Prevention)
SSOM Document 2010 (Workplace Violence Threats and Security)
SSOM Document 4001 (Use of County Vehicles)
SSOM Document 6001 (Hazard Communication), including 6001-22, 6001-23, and 6001-25
SSOM Document 7001 (First Aid and CPR)
SSOM Document 7002 (Emergency Action Plan)

Cal/OSHA Title 8 CCR references cited in the monthly self-inspection checklist (e.g., §3220 Emergency Action Plan, §3395 Heat Illness Prevention, §5193 Bloodborne Pathogens, §5194 Hazard Communication, §14300.32 Cal/OSHA 300 Log posting)

definitions.def: Injury and Illness Prevention Program, the written safety program required at each site under Title 8 CCR §3203, kept in the Department Guide section of the County Safety Manual or other labeled binder accessible to all employees.

A product that poses a physical hazard or an acute or chronic health hazard. Physical hazards include flammable or combustible liquids, compressed gases, organic peroxides, oxidizers, and unstable or water-reactive substances. Health hazards include carcinogens, toxics, teratogens, irritants, corrosives, sensitizers, hepatotoxins, nephrotoxins, neurotoxins, agents acting on the hematopoietic system, and agents that damage the lungs, skin, eyes, or mucous membranes.
Material Safety Data Sheet, the manufacturer's hazard information sheet required for every hazardous substance used in the workplace, kept in a yellow 3-ring MSDS book with instant employee access.
Someone trained in Hazardous Materials emergency response (Hazmat); only qualified persons may contain and clean up accidental spills or releases of hazardous material.

Three-level classification for correcting reported hazards: (i) an unsafe condition, act, or procedure that results in a lost-time injury or illness; (ii) one that results in injury or illness with no lost work time; (iii) any other reported unsafe condition, act, or procedure where employee exposure exists.
County of Riverside Standard Safety Operation Manual, the set of county safety documents and forms referenced throughout this program.

sections.heading: Responsibility
steps: Assign the Director responsibility for the establishment, implementation, and maintenance of this Injury and Illness Prevention Program.

Designate a Department Safety Representative to implement and maintain the program (Attachment I, Safety Representative Appointment Letter; the appointment requires roughly 2 to 4 hours per month for program maintenance, self-inspections, safety committee meetings, and safety representative training).

steps.step: Hold Supervisors responsible for implementing this safety program in their areas.
sub: If necessary, designate a reliable employee as a site Safety Representative to implement the plan within their workplace; this person works closely with the Department Safety Representative.
Direct all questions or suggestions regarding the safety program to the Department Safety Representative.

steps: Keep a copy of this plan with each Supervisor, who should be familiar with its contents and use it as a training resource.
Ensure every employee knows an active safety plan exists to protect their health and safety, and knows the location of the plan and other safety policies in the workplace.
Keep the plan in the Department Guide section of the SSOM, Safety Manual, or other labeled binder in a central location accessible to all employees.
Evaluate the Safety Program annually in the continuing effort to reduce employee accidents and injuries.

sections.heading: Compliance
steps: Require every employee to follow established safety policies, rules, and standards.
Consider each employee's safety record in annual performance evaluations.
Recognize and reward employees for safety performance, appraised through program involvement, accident and injury rates, and other safety performance indicators.

Take appropriate disciplinary action when it is clearly determined that an employee disregards personal safety or jeopardizes the safety of co-workers, following Board of Supervisors Policy 440 and SSOM Document 300 progressive discipline guidelines. The purpose of disciplinary action is to change unacceptable practices into safe, acceptable behavior.

steps.step: Communicate health and safety matters with employees through the following methods:
sub: Monthly safety meetings.
Distribution of department-specific and county safety information on a monthly basis.
Safety information available through the County Safety Division web page.
Safety Committee meeting minutes.

sections.heading: Accident / Exposure Investigation
steps: Conduct an initial investigation of each reported occupational injury or illness by the immediate Supervisor using Safety Form 674, Immediate Supervisor's Report of Employee Injury; the purpose is to discover the root cause and take corrective action to prevent reoccurrence.

Allow the Department Safety Representative or other appointed Safety Representatives to investigate further to determine causation; determine the direct cause of each injury or illness and recommend corrective action to the immediate Supervisor.
Send a copy of Safety Form 674 by mail to the Safety Coordinator assigned to Animal Services.
Investigate every injury within 24 hours of the time of occurrence.
Report serious injuries or deaths immediately to the County Safety Office at (951) 955-3520 to meet Cal/OSHA reporting requirements and ensure a suitable investigation.

steps.step: Inspect every facility and work location to identify unsafe work conditions and practices on the following schedule:
sub: Monthly, by each Supervisor or designee for their areas of supervision, using the self-inspection checklist (Attachment IV, General Office Workplace Monthly Self-Inspection Checklist and Log).
Annually, by the assigned Safety Coordinator or County Safety Office.
Upon completion of remodeling of an existing work location or relocation to a new work location, via a pre-occupancy inspection by the assigned Safety Coordinator or County Safety Office.
Whenever a new hazardous substance, job process, or piece of equipment is introduced, or whenever a new or previously unrecognized hazard is identified and reported.

sections.heading: Hazard Correction and Reporting
steps: Take appropriate corrective action in a timely manner, based on the severity of the hazard, whenever an unsafe or unhealthy condition, practice, or procedure is observed and reported.

steps.step: Report unsafe conditions and acts immediately. Every employee has the right to report any unsafe condition, act, or procedure without fear of reprisal, verbally or in writing (SOP Form No. 401, Hazard Reporting Form), to:
sub: Their immediate Supervisor or Manager.
The assigned Safety Coordinator.
The County Safety Office, (951) 955-3520, or the 24-hour hotline, (951) 955-5868.
Cal/OSHA.

steps: Post County Hazard Report Forms with the other required Labor, Health, and Safety posters.

Correct all reported or discovered hazards in a timely manner according to hazard severity: (i) lost-time injury or illness, (ii) injury or illness with no lost work time, (iii) any other reported unsafe condition where employee exposure exists.
Inform employees of any confirmed unsafe condition or act posing an imminent danger, and notify them of interim protective measures to be taken until the hazard is corrected.
Correct unsafe conditions or acts immediately; if budgetary or time restraints prevent prompt action, initiate short-term measures to prevent accidents until the condition can be corrected.
Budget adequate funds for safety repair, improvements, and training as determined and recommended by Management, the Safety Coordinator, or the County Safety Office.
Coordinate a written response with corrective actions for reported safety hazards and those discovered through formal inspection to ensure abatement (Administration and Management).

sections.heading: Training and Instruction
steps: Ensure Supervisors receive training on the safety and health hazards to which employees under their direction may be exposed (Department Safety Representative responsibility).
Ensure employees under each Supervisor's direction receive training on general workplace safety and specific instruction on hazards unique to their job assignment.
Provide new employees and transfers a general county safety program overview in New Employee Orientation.

Provide new employees and transfers a site-specific safety orientation from their immediate Supervisor or designee upon initial assignment, documented on Attachment II (New Employee Site-Specific Safety Orientation Record) and kept in the employee file.
Require employees to attend Department and County required safety classes per safety policy, documented on Attachment III (Employee Safety Training Record).

steps.step: Provide the required safety classes, which as of the source document are:
sub: Repetitive Motion Injuries (Ergonomics in the Workplace and Back Injury Prevention), required for all County employees per SSOM 2005.
Employee Workplace Violence Awareness, required for all County employees except supervisory personnel per SSOM 2010.
Workplace Violence Training for Managers and Supervisors, required for all Managers and Supervisors per SSOM 2010 (County Safety Office only).
Driver's Training, required for all County employees who drive a vehicle on County business, or after being found at fault in a vehicle accident, per SSOM 4001.
Personal Safety and Security Awareness Training, required for all Community Service Workers and field personnel who provide public health services in the community.
First Aid and CPR sponsored by the American Red Cross; two employees at every County work location should maintain certification per SSOM 7001 (County Safety Office only).
Bloodborne and Airborne Pathogens Training, required for all employees at risk of exposure to bloodborne pathogens and tuberculosis per SSOM 2001 and 2012 (County Safety Office only).
Safety Orientation for Supervisory Personnel, required for all supervisory and designated safety personnel per SSOM 501.

steps: Schedule classes through Health Education (358-7129) or the County Safety Office (955-3520).
Provide additional training as required by specific job assignments and exposures; a list of the most common Cal/OSHA-required training is on the County Safety Office website.

sections.heading: Hazard Communication
steps: Protect employees from exposure to hazardous substances in the workplace, including laboratory chemicals, office products such as copy toners, custodial cleaning and disinfectant products, and compressed gases.
Make a Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS) available for every hazardous substance used in the workplace.
Maintain a master hazardous substance product list at each location; check new chemical products against the existing master MSDS list or database.
Obtain missing MSDS from the manufacturer (by internet or fax) and send a copy to the County Safety Office, mail stop 2170; the County Safety Office administers a searchable online MSDS database.

steps.step: Maintain an MSDS book (yellow 3-ring binder) for employee Right-to-Know, containing:
sub: SSOM Document 6001-25, Emergency Procedures Involving a Hazardous Substance.
SSOM Document 6001-23, Notice of Employee Rights.
The facility's written Hazard Communication Program, or at minimum the County's plan, SSOM Document 6001.
A current list of hazardous substances used at the workplace, updated annually (Attachment V, Hazardous Substance List).
The MSDS for every product listed.
Documentation of annual employee training (Document 6001-22) and chemical-specific training.

steps: Keep the MSDS book with the other written safety plans and manuals where employees have instant access.

steps.step: Label, handle, and store hazardous substances according to the MSDS:
sub: Affix warning labels to secondary containers identifying the substance, its known hazards, and the manufacturer's name and address.
Provide personal protective equipment, and require its use, when employees handle hazardous substances.
Store incompatible substances (acids and alkalis, oxidizers and combustibles) separately; store substances that emit harmful vapors, such as flammable liquids, in approved safety containers.
Restrict spill containment and cleanup to qualified Hazmat-trained persons; for a large spill, evacuate the area and call the local fire department.

steps.step: Train all employees on basic Hazard Communication, including reading and understanding an MSDS, proper handling of hazardous materials, proper use of PPE, and reading hazardous warning labels:
sub: Give basic Hazard Communication training at least annually through safety meetings.
Require employees who use hazardous products to receive product-specific training from their supervisor based on the MSDS; keep training records in the employee file for 1 year.
Hold employees responsible for following safe handling, use, storage, and disposal procedures.

sections.heading: Recordkeeping
steps: Keep records of self-inspections, monthly safety meetings, inspection reports, evacuation drills, hazard reports of unsafe conditions or acts, and corrective actions (Management responsibility).
Keep self-inspection records and safety meeting records on-site for a period of not less than 3 years.
Rely on the County Safety Office to keep on file annual inspections, hazard reports, and County-required safety training records.

sections.heading: Monthly Self-Inspection Checklist (Attachment IV) Coverage
steps: Verify required labor and safety postings (23 posters, including Cal/OSHA Safety and Health Protection on the Job, Workers' Compensation notices, Hazard Communication Right-to-Know, and lobby postings for ADA, weapons prohibition, and no smoking).

Complete the administrative items: current posters displayed; accessible Safety Manual and site-specific IIPP; written Emergency Action Plan with semi-annual evacuation exercises (April and October); Bloodborne Pathogens and Respiratory Protection programs if applicable; Hazard Communication Program with MSDS binder; site-specific Heat Illness Prevention Program; stocked first-aid kits; adequate First Aid/CPR/AED-trained staff; documented monthly safety meetings; documented monthly or quarterly self-inspections; Cal/OSHA 300 Log posted February 1 to April 30 and maintained 5 years; AED working with monthly documentation.

Inspect egress, electrical and lighting, fire prevention, work areas, slip-trip-fall hazards, PPE, workplace violence controls, machinery and equipment (including forklifts and pressure vessels), and flammable liquid storage, using the cited Title 8 CCR and California Fire Code references on the checklist.

campusVariations.variation: Site-specific edition covers the Blythe Animal Campus, 245 S. Carlton, Blythe, CA 92225. This address appears on the cover page and in the Attachment IV and V location fields. All other text is identical to the other campus editions.

Site-specific edition covers the Coachella Valley Animal Campus, 72-050 Pet Land Place, Thousand Palms, CA 92276. This address appears on the cover page and in the Attachment IV and V location fields. All other text is identical to the other campus editions.

Site-specific edition covers the Western Riverside County/City Animal Shelter, 6851 Van Buren Boulevard, Jurupa Valley, CA 92509. This address appears on the cover page and in the Attachment IV and V location fields. The Animal Services Library that holds the reference copy of all four plans is at this Van Buren location. All other text is identical to the other campus editions.

Site-specific edition covers the San Jacinto Valley Animal Campus, 581 S. Grand Avenue, San Jacinto, CA 92582. This address appears on the cover page and in the Attachment IV and V location fields. All other text is identical to the other campus editions.

Attachment II - New Employee Site-Specific Safety Orientation Record

Attachment V - Hazardous Substance List (Chemical Inventory and MSDS)

Heat Illness Prevention Program (per site, Title 8 CCR 3395)

Cal/OSHA Title 8 General Industry Safety Orders (dir.ca.gov)

documents.title: Adjustment of Penalties, Late Fees, and Voiding Administrative Citations
documents.purpose: To establish process consistency and accountability for revenue collections and follow-up.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services. Late fee adjustments and citation voids are authorized at the Manager level and above as the Director's designee.
documents.scope: Applicable to all Department of Animal Services (DAS) Supervisors and Managers.
documents.supersedes: Policy 000-58, effective July 1, 2014 (revised from March 6, 2014); absorbed duplicate copy of Policy 000-58 (same effective dates)
references: California Laws Handbook, 2014
California Code of Regulations, 2606.4.a (3)

definitions.def: Fee assessed when a license renewal payment is postmarked or received after the 30th day of the renewal month, based on the current Board of Supervisors approved fee schedule. The code of a late fee is "L".
Accumulated license fees owed when an expired tag type of VacCert or License exists, or when a dog over four months of age resided in the contracted licensing jurisdiction in excess of 30 days.

sections.heading: Late Fees on License Renewals
steps: Treat payment of the license renewal as due from when the previous license was issued, in compliance with California Code of Regulations, 2606.4.a (3).
Assess the applicable late fee, based on the current Board of Supervisors approved fee schedule, on payments postmarked or received after the 30th day of the renewal month.

The Director or his or her designee (Manager and above) may adjust the late fee if it is determined that the pet owner has made a reasonable effort to comply with the deadline, as allowed by State Law.

sections.heading: Retroactive (Accumulated) License Fees
steps: Collect all accumulated license fees (retroactive fees) from the pet owner when an expired tag type of VacCert or License exists, or when it is determined the dog is over four months of age and was residing in the contracted licensing jurisdiction in excess of 30 days.
Do not encourage "forgiveness" of accumulated dog license fees from year to year; accumulated fees must be collected before the dog can be currently licensed.
A Manager and above can adjust the late fee for each term but does not have the authority to change State Law by skipping years unless there is an active amnesty program.
Document the reason why late fees and penalties were adjusted in a Chameleon memo initiated by the supervisor.

sections.heading: Voiding Administrative Citations and Adjusting Penalties
steps: A Manager and above can void administrative citations and adjust penalties, provided every effort is expended to obtain compliance on citation offenses in adherence to citation processing rules.
The supervisor voiding the citation must initiate a citation Chameleon memo that documents the reason the citation was voided.

steps.step: Document in the same memo that the property owner, custodian, or owner was advised of the consequence of a repeat encounter:
sub: If a license inspector in the future encounters the dog that was claimed not to be owned or not to exist, the owner, custodian, or property owner may be issued an Admin Cite 2 and will be responsible for the previously voided citation and the current citation fees.

related.name: Memo window (documentation of adjustments and voids)
Employee Acknowledgment of Policy 000-58 (retained in personnel file and attached to employee's Person ID in Chameleon)
Board of Supervisors approved fee schedule

documents.searchText: to establish process consistency and accountability for revenue collections and follow-up. applicable to all department of animal services (das) supervisors and managers. director of animal services. late fee adjustments and citation voids are authorized at the manager level and above as the director's designee. late fee fee assessed when a license renewal payment is postmarked or received after the 30th day of the renewal month, based on the current board of supervisors approved fee schedule. the code of a late fee is "l". retroactive fees accumulated license fees owed when an expired tag type of vaccert or license exists, or when a dog over four months of age resided in the contracted licensing jurisdiction in excess of 30 days. late fees on license renewals treat payment of the license renewal as due from when the previous license was issued, in compliance with california code of regulations, 2606.4.a (3). assess the applicable late fee, based on the current board of supervisors approved fee schedule, on payments postmarked or received after the 30th day of the renewal month. the director or his or her designee (manager and above) may adjust the late fee if it is determined that the pet owner has made a reasonable effort to comply with the deadline, as allowed by state law. retroactive (accumulated) license fees collect all accumulated license fees (retroactive fees) from the pet owner when an expired tag type of vaccert or license exists, or when it is determined the dog is over four months of age and was residing in the contracted licensing jurisdiction in excess of 30 days. do not encourage "forgiveness" of accumulated dog license fees from year to year; accumulated fees must be collected before the dog can be currently licensed. a manager and above can adjust the late fee for each term but does not have the authority to change state law by skipping years unless there is an active amnesty program. document the reason why late fees and penalties were adjusted in a chameleon memo initiated by the supervisor. voiding administrative citations and adjusting penalties a manager and above can void administrative citations and adjust penalties, provided every effort is expended to obtain compliance on citation offenses in adherence to citation processing rules. the supervisor voiding the citation must initiate a citation chameleon memo that documents the reason the citation was voided. {"step": "document in the same memo that the property owner, custodian, or owner was advised of the consequence of a repeat encounter:", "sub": ["if a license inspector in the future encounters the dog that was claimed not to be owned or not to exist, the owner, custodian, or property owner may be issued an admin cite 2 and will be responsible for the previously voided citation and the current citation fees."]} california laws handbook, 2014 california code of regulations, 2606.4.a (3)

documents.title: Completion of the Chameleon Citation-Violation Window
documents.flag: Numbered (=Policy per rule) but reads as a step-by-step protocol
documents.purpose: To establish a uniform procedure for the completion of the Chameleon Citation/Violation Window for any compliance violation pertaining to State and local mandates.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services.
documents.scope: Applicable to all personnel who use the Chameleon Citation/Violation Window.
documents.supersedes: Policy 000-69, effective July 17, 2017
references: DAS Policy 000-01, General Chameleon Information
Government Code Section 53069.4
County Ordinance 630.16, Section 22, Administrative Citations and Penalties
DAS SOP - Administrative Citation Transactions
DAS SOP - Daily Input of Citations Process of License Inspectors
DAS SOP - Initiating Intent to Comply Administrative Citation
DAS SOP - Initiating State SN Violation Administrative Citation

definitions.def: Violation number auto-generated by Chameleon when the user stores (F9) a new citation record.
First administrative citation issued ($100) for the year.
Second administrative citation issued ($200) for the same offenses within the year of the ADM CITE1 violation date.
Third administrative citation issued ($500) for the same offenses within the year of the ADM CITE1 violation date.
Criminal citation issued only by PC832 trained officers.
Used when a notice to comply is issued, to be followed up after 30 days.

sections.heading: Policy
steps: In accordance with Government Code Section 53069.4 and County Ordinance 630.16 Section 22, an administrative citation may be issued for any violation of State and local ordinances.
Store a violation/citation record before adding violations/offenses. A "V" number is issued by Chameleon.

sections.heading: Violation Section Field Definitions [Alt + 1]
steps: Violation No: This field is required (red label) and searchable (yellow field). Chameleon auto-generates a violation number that starts with a V when the user stores (F9) a new citation record. The user can also enter an existing violation number to find and display information associated with a specific violation.
Violation Date and Time: Press the F5 key to show the current date and time the citation was issued. The due date is based on this field.
Type: Press the F4 key for the list of options (ADM CITE1, ADM CITE2, ADM CITE3, CITATION, WARNING as defined above).

Incident No: Contains the citation form number issued by field officers and license officers. City of Riverside citations begin with an RA; all other cities, including the unincorporated areas of Riverside County, begin with A. This field is searchable (yellow field).
Activity No and Seq: If an activity with sequence results in a CITE, enter the activity number and sequence number in these fields.
Defendant ID: Contains the identification number associated with the owner of the animal or the property owner (format P#########).

Complainant ID: Contains the identification number associated with the caller ID from the activity window. If a person has recently been stored or queried from the activity window, the computer will automatically fill in PERSON_ID with the appropriate ID number (format P#######).

steps.step: Issued By:
sub: Enter the PID of the issuing officer.
For Department citations (non-officer), enter P0287142 for DAS.

steps: Location of Violation: Press the F4 key to enter the address of the defendant or the address where the violation occurred. For RTO and S/N adoption contract citations, the location of the violation is the shelter address.
Jurisdiction: Chameleon will automatically bring the person jurisdiction over to this field. This must be changed if the follow-up enforcement is Riverside City.
Synopsis: Contains very brief phrases documenting activities. Use the memo window if longer than a phrase.

steps.step: Docket No:
sub: Enter REG MAIL for the first notice of posted citations mailed to the property owner.
Enter CERT MAIL for the final notice of posted citations mailed to the property owner.

steps: Court Date: Enter the hearing date set by the court for criminal citations issued.
Outcome Date: This field signifies the final outcome date of the citation. Requires an outcome type of CLEARED, VOID, or COLLECTION.

steps.step: Outcome Type: Press the F4 key for the list of options.
sub: Cleared: when the defendant complies with all offenses. Requires an outcome date.
Collection: when a collection billing is initiated for FTB. Requires an outcome date.
Court: when the citation is sent to court. Requires an outcome date.
Dismissed: only used for court and criminal citations. Requires an outcome date.
FTB: when collection billing is sent to the FTB. Requires an outcome date.
Hold: no action will be taken until the compliance due date. Requires a date entered in the Comply Due Date field and a brief phrase in the synopsis. NO EXCEPTIONS.
Mailed: when the citation was mailed. Requires an outcome date.
Void: when the defendant shows proof of compliance prior to the citation being issued. Requires an outcome date.

steps: LO Review Date [Extra1]: This field is specifically used by license officers and enters the future date of reviewing a citation.

Extra3: Enter the license number that is the reference number for which the citation was created. This is only applicable for one (1) animal ID. This field is automatically entered when generating auto-citations due to delinquent or expired licenses.

Comply Due Date [Extra4]: This field must contain a date when an outcome type of HOLD is entered in the citation window. A brief phrase or memo of the reason for the HOLD must be stored. NO EXCEPTIONS.

sections.heading: Offense Section [Alt + 5]
steps: Offense: Press the F4 key for the list of offenses or enter the specific code. For example, 6.08.020 for M DOG LIC.
Qty: Defaults to one (1) but can be changed. This field refers to the total count for each offense.
Animal ID: Enter the animal ID or create a new animal ID by pressing the F4 key to bring up the animal window.
Animal Description: Once the animal record is created, the description of the animal is automatically entered in this field. The officer can also enter the description of the animal manually.
Price: This is the charge incurred for a single unit based on the offense entered. The default price is $100. This must be changed if the violation type is ADM CITE2 or ADM CITE3.

steps.step: Status: Press the F4 key for applicable options.
sub: Mailed: automatically entered by Chameleon when issuing auto-citations.
Cleared: entered only when the defendant complies with a portion of the offenses.
Void: entered only when the defendant was compliant with a portion of the offenses prior to the citation.

steps: Offense box: Lists the different offenses for one animal ID.

steps.step: Offense icons: Use these icons when making changes to the offenses only.
sub: Find icon: to find an offense.
Plus icon: to add an offense.
Delete icon: to delete an offense. Contact the Chameleon administrator for deleting.
Update icon: to update over an existing offense.

Initiating Intent to Comply Administrative Citation
Initiating State SN Violation Administrative Citation
Employee Acknowledgment of Chameleon Violation/Citation Window Policy (retained in personnel file)
