documents.title: Guidelines for the Operation of Motor Vehicles
documents.purpose: To establish procedures for the operation of County vehicles assigned to Animal Services.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services. Overnight retention of County vehicles requires the written permission of the Director of Animal Services.
documents.scope: Applicable to all personnel authorized to operate assigned County vehicles.
documents.supersedes: Policy 200-01, effective February 24, 2020 (revised from October 6, 2009 and March 29, 2000)
references: County Policy D-2, Use and Purchase of County Vehicles
County Policy D-10, Overnight Retention of County Vehicles
Safety Division Operations Manual 4001, Vehicle Use and Safety Guidelines
General Form 30, Authorization to Drive Riverside County Vehicle for County Business
State of California Vehicle Code

definitions.def: A technique for operating a motor vehicle in such a way as to prevent accidents despite bad driving conditions and in spite of what other drivers on the roadway might do.
Driving while engaged in any activity that takes attention off the road, whether taking eyes off the road or hands off the wheel.
Automatic Vehicle Location unit installed in Animal Services vehicles that records location, engine run times, speed, acceleration, braking force, mileage, check engine light status, and other data.
Department Safety Representative.

sections.heading: Defensive Driving Method
steps: Apply the accident prevention formula: see the hazard, understand the defense, and act in time. Most accidents are preventable by one or both drivers involved.

Before moving the vehicle, sit for a moment and consider the driving conditions likely to be met: Are any unusual weather, road, or traffic conditions to be expected? Is the vehicle in good working condition? Am I okay physically and mentally? Do I know whether any medications I am taking might have an adverse effect on my driving?

Avoid collision with the vehicle ahead: always stay alert. Anticipate other drivers' intended moves. Watch for turn signals but do not trust them implicitly. Look beyond the vehicle immediately ahead of you. Stay as far back as practical; the usual rule is to allow one or two car lengths for each 10 mph of speed in ideal conditions, and more distance if the weather or road conditions are bad.
Avoid collision from behind: always signal your intentions well in advance. Use your turn signals and brake lights and, if necessary, arm signals. Whenever you can, stop gradually and smoothly.

Avoid head-on collisions: when you face an oncoming vehicle wholly or partially in your lane on a straight road, slow down, move as far to the right as possible, and be prepared to ease off the roadway if necessary. To avoid crashes on curves, take your foot off the gas just before entering the curve and apply power while in the curve.

Avoid intersection collisions: when approaching an intersection, take your foot off the gas pedal and cover the brake without depressing it. Always look first to the left, then to the right, and then back to the left again. Signal turns half a block away and get into the proper lane early. Check your mirror for a following vehicle and make sure the driver is aware that you are planning to turn left or right.

Avoid being hit by a passer: help the other driver pass by checking oncoming traffic and slowing down if the driver needs more time and distance. Use your mirror and turn signals before changing lanes. Take a good look before you move, wait for a break in the traffic, signal, then pull out promptly.

Avoid being hit while passing: always maintain a safe following distance. Check the traffic ahead before passing. Check to the rear, put on your left turn signal, and move to the left lane, accelerating as you do. Return to the right lane only when you can see the passed vehicle in your rear view mirror.

Avoid backing collisions: preventing the need to back a vehicle is the only positive prevention of backing collisions. Always walk around your vehicle prior to backing to ensure it is clear of all obstacles. If possible, have someone observe your backing operation to ensure the area is clear and remains clear. You are responsible for the accident if you back into someone or something.

sections.heading: Operation of County Vehicles
steps: Prior to operating any vehicle assigned to this division, physically inspect the vehicle for outward signs of damage and ensure that all lights, tires, signaling devices, and mirrors are functional and in good repair.
Complete a Unit Inspection Form (Attachment A) daily, at the beginning of each shift.
Inform your supervisor of any major discrepancies or of any unsafe or dangerous conditions prior to operating the vehicle.

Do not operate vehicles with inadequate brakes, faulty steering gear, or improper lights except to proceed to a place for repairs, and then only at such reduced speed or in such a manner as will permit safe movement. If a vehicle cannot be safely operated to get to a repair shop, request a tow truck.
Test hand and foot brakes at the start of each shift and exercise proper precautions to detect any malfunctioning during the shift.
Avoid unnecessary fast driving and sudden application of brakes, except in an emergency.
Keep the assigned County vehicle locked to avoid theft of the vehicle and/or its contents.

Assist in maintaining vehicles in a clean condition by removing personal belongings, trash, etc. after use and by washing the exterior and disinfecting the compartment boxes as needed. Where the local County garage is equipped with a carwash and vacuum, use that facility.
Do not take County vehicles home overnight without the written permission of the Director of Animal Services.
Do not transport unauthorized persons in County vehicles.
Do not get on or off any equipment while it is in motion.
Do not operate machinery and equipment in a manner that may endanger persons or property. Do not exceed safe operating speeds.

steps.step: Drive at a speed no greater than is reasonable and proper, with due regard for weather, traffic, intersections, width and character of the roadway, type of motor vehicle, and any other existing conditions. At all times, keep the vehicle under such control as to be able to bring it to a complete stop within the assured clear distance ahead. In addition:
sub: Under no conditions will the operator of any vehicle exceed posted speed limits.
No vehicle shall be driven down grade with gears in neutral or clutch disengaged.
No vehicle shall be parked on a down grade unless the fore part or aft part of the front wheels are turned into the curb or are securely chocked.
All tools and equipment shall be properly stowed and securely fastened when transported.
Under no circumstances may an employee ride outside of the cab, nor with any part of the body outside of the cab.
Operation of a County vehicle shall be in compliance with the State of California Vehicle Code.

sections.heading: Field Services Dog Truck Daily Walk Around
steps: Complete a walkaround of each dog truck prior to driving the vehicle. The walkaround will include checking all items listed on the Daily Vehicle Walk Around Checklist (Attachment A).
Notify Dispatch that the walkaround was completed when calling in starting mileage. Example: "360 is 10-08, starting mileage for vehicle 19-500 is 4,321, vehicle walk around complete".
Immediately report any damage, missing equipment, or issues to Field Services supervision.

sections.heading: Distracted Driving
steps: Do not use electronic communication devices while operating a County vehicle or a personal vehicle on County business. Operating a vehicle includes while stopped in traffic, at a stop light, or at a stop sign. Electronic communication devices include cell phones (both personal and County issued), GPS devices, tablets, and others.
Limit use of PSEC radios while driving, as you are unable to safely take notes.
If you need to make a call, check a map for directions, or send a text message, pull off the road and stop.

sections.heading: Automatic Vehicle Location Devices
steps: Animal Services vehicles have Automatic Vehicle Location (AVL) units installed. These units record the location, engine run times, speed, acceleration, braking force, mileage, check engine light status, and other data.

sections.heading: Accident Procedures
steps: First, determine if medical response is required.

If the accident occurs during business hours, notify Dispatch of the accident location, whether medical response is necessary, and whether the County vehicle will need to be towed. Dispatch will contact appropriate law enforcement, supervision, and the garage if the County vehicle requires towing.
If the accident occurs after hours, call your immediate supervisor and the appropriate law enforcement so that a report can be taken.
Stay at the accident scene until all investigations have been completed and obtain a report number.
If possible, obtain the name of the second vehicle's insurance company if the accident involves a civilian vehicle. Obtain the names, addresses, and phone numbers of any witnesses.
Complete a Vehicle Accident Report for all vehicle accidents resulting in any type of damage or injury, regardless of how minor the damage or injury.

steps.step: Gather the following information for completion of the Accident Report (Attachment B):
sub: Driver's name, driver's license number, address, and phone number.
Vehicle's make, model, year, license plate, and description of damage.
Driver's vehicle insurance company's name, policy number, and phone number.
Injured parties' names, address, phone, date of birth, sex, and description of injuries.
Location of accident.
Witness names and phone numbers.
Description of any property damage, with the name, address, and phone number of the owner.
Responding police name, badge number, report number, and address of station.
Photographs of all vehicles involved, property damage, and any other relevant pictures.

steps: Submit the Vehicle Accident Report and photos to the Animal Services Department Safety Representative (DSR) or designee within 24 hours of the accident. The DSR or designee will forward the report to the County's Safety Division.

The driver of the vehicle must fill out the Vehicle Accident Report. If the driver is not available to complete the report, it may be done by a supervisor or manager. If the driver did not complete the Accident Report, the driver must submit a written statement of the incident to their supervisor upon returning to work; the supervisor will forward the statement to the DSR or designee.

Type all reports; County Safety will not accept handwritten Accident Reports. The current version of the Vehicle Accident Report can be found as an editable PDF file on County Safety's website (https://safety.rc-hr.com/Accidents.aspx).
Do not give out any information or sign any paperwork other than that required by personnel representing the County of Riverside or law enforcement.
In the event of injury or death occurring during the accident, notify the Safety Director's office immediately.
If the vehicle can be driven, take it directly to a County garage for damage inspection and schedule repairs. As soon as possible, complete the required County of Riverside Accident Report.

related.name: Daily Vehicle Walk Around Checklist (Attachment A)
County of Riverside Confidential Vehicle Accidents/Incidents Report, Form No. 942.6 (Attachment B)
County Safety Division, 3403 10th Street, Suite 501, Riverside, CA 92501, Mail Stop 2170, phone 951.955.3520

Appendix B: County of Riverside Confidential Vehicle Accidents/Incidents Report (Form No. 942.6, revised January 11, 2017) (attached separately)

documents.title: Training New Officers
documents.purpose: To effectively and properly train all new Animal Control Officers.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services. The Operations Chief and the Director must first approve all steps before any disciplinary actions are taken against the trainee/officer.
documents.scope: All new Animal Control Officers with the County of Riverside.
documents.supersedes: Policy 200-03, effective October 5, 2009 (revised from September 11, 2000 and June 19, 2000)
references: POST (Peace Officer Standards and Training) program model
DAS Policies and Procedures Manual
County Ordinances and Municipal Codes
State Humane Manual (State Penal, Food and Agriculture, and Health and Safety Codes)

definitions.def: Peace Officer Standards and Training, the training program model found in most Southern California law enforcement agencies, based on required learning domains with a required amount of time spent on each block.

sections.heading: Book Training
steps: Review the Policies and Procedures Manual: all new officers and/or trainees will review the Policies and Procedures Manual with their Senior Officer. All questions will be answered by the Senior Officer and/or Supervising Animal Control Officer.

Review County Ordinances or Municipal Codes: all new officers and/or trainees will review the County Ordinances with their Senior Officer and/or Supervisor. All questions will be answered by the Senior Officer and/or Supervising Animal Control Officer.

Review the State Humane Manual: all new officers and/or trainees will review the State Humane Manual with their Senior Officer to confirm they have a basic understanding of the relevant State Penal, Food and Agriculture, and Health and Safety Codes directly related to their duties. All questions will be answered by the Senior Officer and/or Supervising Animal Control Officer.
Administer a written test to all new officers and/or trainees on Departmental Policies and Procedures, County Ordinances, and State Laws.

sections.heading: In-House Training
steps: Provide all new officers and/or trainees hands-on training in the euthanasia room with the appropriate Training Officer. A euthanasia test will be given at the end of the training, which the officer in training must pass.
Provide all new officers and/or trainees hands-on training in handling aggressive animals using control sticks, leashes, and muzzles for officer safety.

sections.heading: Field Training
steps: The new ACO/trainee will ride along with their Senior ACO, or designee, for a period of no less than two (2) weeks in order to observe and learn ACO skills. During this time, the trainee will be given daily and weekly evaluations.
When the Senior ACO feels the new trainee/officer is able to competently do his or her job on their own, the Senior ACO will sign off for the trainee/officer to be released for non-supervised work.

The Senior ACO and/or Supervising Animal Control Officer will be responsible for talking with the trainee/officer on a daily basis to answer any questions and to get an overview of the trainee/officer's progress.
The Senior ACO will complete weekly evaluations based on daily reports and paperwork turned in by the trainee/officer.

Give the trainee/officer a final evaluation at five (5) months to determine whether he or she should be held back for another six (6) months of daily supervised training or advanced to the title of Animal Control Officer.
The Operations Chief and the Director will first approve all steps before any disciplinary actions are taken against the trainee/officer.

documents.title: Preliminary Investigation and Obtaining a Written Search Warrant
documents.purpose: To provide a procedure on how to effectively obtain a search warrant, including the preliminary investigation and written report that support it.
documents.authority: The Operations Chief and Director of Animal Services review the case and determine whether authorization for a search warrant will be given. The District Attorney reviews and signs the affidavit and search warrant; the Court Magistrate signs the warrant.
documents.scope: Applicable to all Field Services personnel who investigate cruelty cases and must inspect properties without owner consent.
documents.supersedes: Policy 200-05, effective October 7, 2009 (revised from July 6, 2000)
references: Penal Code Section 1524
Penal Code Section 599a
Penal Code Section 1536

definitions.def: Under the "reasonable man" doctrine: facts, or apparent facts, viewed through the eyes of the experienced officer, which would generate a reasonable belief that a crime has been or is about to be committed.
Circumstances such as severely injured animals under which no search warrant is needed.

sections.heading: Preliminary Investigation and Written Report
steps: Locate and identify the owner, the animal(s), and any witnesses.

steps.step: Conduct the initial investigation and collect all known information pertaining to the case:
sub: Allegations of the case.
Surrounding circumstances.
Incidents pertaining to the case (previous or current).
Names, addresses, and phone numbers of people involved.
Who brought in the animals?
How long have the animals been on the property?
Were all of the animals kept on the property? If not, where were they kept?
Did the owner or caretaker take the animal(s) to a veterinarian to be examined?
Are the animal(s) severely injured? NOTE: no search warrant is needed when there are exigent circumstances.
If the animal(s) are not seriously injured, what is their condition? (For example: stable, not in need of immediate veterinary care; will it live long enough to obtain a search warrant?)
What is the condition of the property?
Are the animal(s) to remain on the property until a search warrant can be obtained?
Does the animal have food, shelter, and water? Is the lack of any of these items the cause of the problem?

steps: If allowed, take photographs of the animal(s) and the property.
If not allowed to take photographs, write down a clear, concise description of the property and animals. NOTE: this information will be needed at the time you complete the actual search warrant.

steps.step: Once all facts have been gathered, complete a written Preliminary Field Report (Attachment A), noting the following information:
sub: Condition of the animal(s).
Condition of the property.
Dates, times, and people involved when any warnings or recommendations were given by the Animal Control Officer to the owner/caretaker to correct the problem.
Dates, times, and steps taken by the Animal Control Officer to correct the problem.
Dates, times, and reasons given by the owner/caretaker as to why the problem was not corrected.
All statements made by the owner/caretaker.

Parcel number and owner information of the property in question. This mandatory information can be obtained by requesting a printout from the County Assessor's Office (Attachment B) and is required in order to request the search warrant.

steps: Present the written Preliminary Field Report, photographs, and all additional case information to the Senior Animal Control Officer for review and discussion. Make changes or corrections, if any, at this time.

Present the written Preliminary Field Report, photographs, and all additional case information to the Operations Chief and Director of Animal Services, who will review and determine if authorization for a search warrant will be given.

sections.heading: Affidavit for Search Warrant
steps: Complete the affidavit (Attachment C), provided by the District Attorney's office. The affidavit is a condensed version of the Preliminary Field Report and contains all supporting information for the search warrant, including probable cause(s) for a search.

sections.heading: Search Warrant
steps: Complete the Search Warrant (Attachment D), which is provided by the District Attorney's Office.
Present the Search Warrant and all additional case information to the Senior Animal Control Officer for review.
Present the Search Warrant and all additional case information to the Supervisor or a member of the Management team for final review.

sections.heading: Presentation to the District Attorney

steps.step: Present the following information to the District Attorney for review and signature of the affidavit and search warrant:
sub: Preliminary Field Report.
Photographs.
Affidavit for Search Warrant.
Search Warrant.

steps: The District Attorney may request that corrections be made. If so, make corrections as requested at that time.

sections.heading: Presentation to the Court Magistrate
steps: Present the affidavit and search warrant to the Court Magistrate for review. NOTE: the Court Magistrate may request that corrections be made to either document. If so, make corrections as requested at that time.

The Court Magistrate will ask the Animal Control Officer to raise their right hand and swear that the information contained in the documents is true. Once the Animal Control Officer is sworn in, the Court Magistrate will sign the Search Warrant. NOTE: some Court Magistrates may have a clerk rubber stamp the warrant with the Magistrate's signature instead of an original signature.

Present the Search Warrant to the filing office, where it will be logged in and assigned a warrant number. At this time, the formal Search Warrant (Attachment E) will be given to the Animal Control Officer by the clerk. This is the official document that will be given to the person being served. Keep it in a sealed envelope until the time of the search.
In addition to the original, obtain copies of the Search Warrant containing the Court seal from the clerk, to be given to each person on the property at the time of the search.
Serve the Search Warrant and return it to the Court within 10 days from the issue date (Attachment F).

related.name: Preliminary Field Report (Animal Welfare Investigation Report)
Affidavit for Search Warrant (provided by District Attorney's office)
Search Warrant (provided by District Attorney's office)

appendices: Appendix A: Preliminary Field Report (Animal Welfare Investigation Report) (attached separately)
Appendix B: County Assessor's Office parcel printout sample (attached separately)
Appendix C: Sample Affidavit for Search Warrant (attached separately)
Appendix D: Sample Search Warrant (attached separately)
Appendix E: Sample formal Search Warrant (attached separately)
Appendix F: Sample Return to Search Warrant (attached separately)

animal control officer for review and discussion. make changes or corrections, if any, at this time. present the written preliminary field report, photographs, and all additional case information to the operations chief and director of animal services, who will review and determine if authorization for a search warrant will be given. affidavit for search warrant complete the affidavit (attachment c), provided by the district attorney's office. the affidavit is a condensed version of the preliminary field report and contains all supporting information for the search warrant, including probable cause(s) for a search. search warrant complete the search warrant (attachment d), which is provided by the district attorney's office. present the search warrant and all additional case information to the senior animal control officer for review. present the search warrant and all additional case information to the supervisor or a member of the management team for final review. presentation to the district attorney {"step": "present the following information to the district attorney for review and signature of the affidavit and search warrant:", "sub": ["preliminary field report.", "photographs.", "affidavit for search warrant.", "search warrant."]} the district attorney may request that corrections be made. if so, make corrections as requested at that time. presentation to the court magistrate present the affidavit and search warrant to the court magistrate for review. note: the court magistrate may request that corrections be made to either document. if so, make corrections as requested at that time. the court magistrate will ask the animal control officer to raise their right hand and swear that the information contained in the documents is true. once the animal control officer is sworn in, the court magistrate will sign the search warrant. note: some court magistrates may have a clerk rubber stamp the warrant with the magistrate's signature instead of an original signature. present the search warrant to the filing office, where it will be logged in and assigned a warrant number. at this time, the formal search warrant (attachment e) will be given to the animal control officer by the clerk. this is the official document that will be given to the person being served. keep it in a sealed envelope until the time of the search. in addition to the original, obtain copies of the search warrant containing the court seal from the clerk, to be given to each person on the property at the time of the search. serve the search warrant and return it to the court within 10 days from the issue date (attachment f). penal code section 1524 penal code section 599a penal code section 1536

documents.title: Planning a Search
documents.purpose: To provide a procedure on how to effectively serve a search warrant.
documents.authority: The Coordinator of the raid directs planning and briefing; supervision must be notified with the latest update before execution.
documents.scope: Applicable to all Field Services personnel who investigate cruelty cases and must inspect properties without owner consent.
documents.supersedes: Policy 200-06, effective October 7, 2009 (revised from July 6, 2000)
references: DAS Policy 200-05, Preliminary Investigation and Obtaining a Written Search Warrant

definitions.def: A briefing conducted at a central location, with everyone present who will participate in the raid, just before the search is executed.
A drawing of the property to be searched showing structures, gates, entrances, and animal locations.

steps.step: Complete the planning checklist prior to conducting a search:
sub: Do you have the correct address?
Is the description of the house correct?
Do you know the layout of the interior of the home?
Do you have a drawing of the property?
Where is the best point to enter the home?
How many occupants (male, female, children) are believed to be in the house?
Do you expect possible resistance from occupants of the property?
Do you know if the occupants have a criminal history and/or outstanding warrants?
Is the property secure to a point that the occupants will not be able to escape?
Do the occupants have access to transportation that might be used to escape off of the property? If so, where is it located on the property?
What is the number of animals on the property?
Are the animals loose or restrained on the property? If they are loose, is there a concern for the safety of the officers entering the property?
Is there a history of animal bites on the property? A history of rabies? (Check with Rabies Control.)
Are there any other animal concerns that will need to be addressed?

sections.heading: Verification of Information: Personnel Needs Checklist

steps.step: Complete the personnel needs checklist prior to conducting a search:
sub: How many entry team members will be needed?
How many officers will be needed to man the perimeter of the property?
How many animal transport units will be needed?
How many trailers will be needed?
Is support needed from other agencies?
Will a veterinarian need to be present or on stand-by?
How many Sheriff Deputies are required?
Do we need the Riverside Sheriff's Posse?

steps.step: Complete the equipment checklist prior to conducting a search:
sub: Search Warrant (Attachment A).
Bolt cutter.
Crowbar.
Radios.
Video camera(s).
35mm camera and ID board.
Animal transports.
Animal identification materials.
Animal capture equipment (tranquilizer guns and traps as needed).
Evidence Log(s)/Property Receipts (Attachment B).

sections.heading: Pre-Raid Briefing
steps: Conduct a pre-raid briefing at a central location, with everyone present who will be participating in the raid, just before the search is to be executed.

steps.step: The Coordinator of the raid must complete the following at or before the pre-raid briefing:
sub: Notify supervision with the latest update.
Notify Dispatch of which officers are participating in the raid and will not be available for regular calls until further notice.
Verify that everyone involved in the raid has seen the Search Warrant and the Plot Plan (Attachment C).
Verify that everyone involved in the raid is aware of what is expected of him or her and what the raid objective is.

steps.step: Cover the following information at the pre-raid briefing; it must be thoroughly understood by everyone present prior to going to the search site:
sub: Announcement of supervisory protocol (identify the Investigating Officer, team leaders, and other leadership roles for the raid).
Announcement of team assignments.
Communicate all known possible problems that may arise during the course of the raid.
Discuss what to do if someone is injured.
Discuss the procedures to take if an arrest is made (for example, who will make the arrest, who will detain the person(s) if not arrested).
Discuss who will be in charge of the evidence logging of the animals and other items. Identify that person to all personnel present.
Identify who will be videotaping and taking pictures via the 35mm camera. Discuss the conversations that should and should not take place in view or earshot of the cameras.
Discuss what to do if a shooting takes place (for example, where the safest place will be to get cover).
Discuss all safety procedures.
Check all equipment to make sure that it is accessible and properly working.
Create a list of names, addresses, and organizations of all personnel who are present at the pre-raid briefing and will be coming and going off of the raid property.
Announce that a written report will be required from everyone present, at the end of the raid, stating the specifics of the job that they performed during the raid.
Discuss the Plot Plan.

steps: If all of the above issues have been covered during the pre-raid briefing and all present are satisfactorily aware and confident in their assigned roles, the warrant may then be executed.

related.name: Evidence Log/Property Receipt (Riverside County Property Report, Form No. 409)
DAS Policy 200-05, Preliminary Investigation and Obtaining a Written Search Warrant

appendices: Appendix A: Sample Search Warrant (attached separately)
Appendix B: Evidence Log/Property Receipt (Property Report, Form No. 409) (attached separately)
Appendix C: Sample Plot Plan (attached separately)

documents.title: Completion of Potentially Dangerous Animal Restraint Order
documents.flag: Numbered (=Policy per rule) but reads as a step-by-step protocol
documents.purpose: To establish a uniform procedure for controlling potentially dangerous dog complaints.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: Applicable to all personnel entrusted to enforce Ordinance 771. To be used by all Animal Services personnel when receiving, investigating, and following up on a Potentially Dangerous complaint.
documents.supersedes: Policy 200-10, Completion of Potentially/Dangerous Animal Restraint Order, effective October 2009 (revised from August 23, 2000)
references: Riverside County Ordinance 771, Section 3a

definitions.def: A substantial impairment of the physical condition of a person or animal which requires professional medical treatment, including, but not limited to, loss of consciousness; concussion; bone fracture; protracted loss or impairment of function of any bodily member or organ; muscle tears; disfiguring lacerations; punctures or a wound requiring multiple sutures; or any injury requiring corrective or cosmetic surgery.

sections.heading: When to Use
steps: Initiate a Potentially Dangerous and/or Dangerous Animal Restraint Order when Animal Control is conducting an investigation in regard to a Potentially Dangerous Animal complaint.

sections.heading: How to Complete the Restraint Order
steps: Case Number: Enter the investigating Animal Control Officer's report number. (The case number is the date that the Animal Control Officer responds to the call.)
Animal Control Number: Enter the control number only if the animal will be impounded at the Shelter.
Issued By: Enter the officer's last name.
Issued To: Enter the animal owner/custodian's full name.
Date: Enter the date that the Potentially Dangerous Animal Restraint Order was completed for the owner/custodian.
Address: Enter the exact address of where the animal(s) resides.
Phone (Home): Enter the home phone number of the owner/custodian.
Phone (Work): Enter the work phone number of the owner/custodian.
This Animal is Described As: Enter a thorough description of the animal(s), including breed, color, sex, and any noticeable markings or scars.
Circumstances: Enter the reason that the Potentially Dangerous Restraint Order is being issued.
"Beware of vicious ___": Enter the type of animal (i.e., dog, cat, horse, etc.).
"I also agree to keep the ___": Enter the type of animal (dog, cat, horse, etc.).
Signature of Owner/Keeper: Enter the owner/custodian's signature.
Date: Enter the date that the owner/custodian signed the Potentially Dangerous Restraint Order.

sections.heading: Hearing Notice
steps: Give a Request for Potentially Dangerous Dog Hearing (Appendix C) to the owner/caregiver, along with the Potentially Dangerous Animal Restraint Order (Appendix A) and the Dangerous Animal Restraint Order (Appendix B).
If the dog is impounded at the shelter, the Animal Control Officer will need to write the Control Card number on the Hearing Notice.

related.name: Request for Potentially Dangerous Dog Hearing

appendices: Appendix A: Potentially Dangerous Animal Restraint Order (form, attached separately)
Appendix B: Dangerous Animal Restraint Order (form, attached separately)
Appendix C: Request for Potentially Dangerous Dog Hearing (form, attached separately)

documents.title: Issuance of Notice to Appear in Court (Citation PM31)
documents.purpose: To establish a uniform procedure for completing and distributing the Notice to Appear in Court citation (PM31). (The original policy contains no Purpose statement; this sentence is drawn from the policy's content.)
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: Applicable to all personnel authorized to issue citations.
documents.supersedes: Policy 200-11, Issuance of Notice to Appear in Court (Citation PM31), effective October 7, 2009 (revised from July 27, 2000)
references: Riverside County Ordinance 630
Riverside City Municipal Code Title 8

sections.heading: When to Use
steps: Use a Notice to Appear only after the authorized personnel have personally observed a violation of the Riverside County Ordinance.

steps.step: When completing a citation, the authorized personnel shall:
sub: Use a black ballpoint pen.
Block print in all sections except the issuing officer and violator's signatures.

Place the hard cover of the citation book under the last copy of the citation being written in order to prevent marking other blank citations, as they are made with NCR (non-carbon reproduction) paper.

sections.heading: How to Complete the Citation
steps: Date: Enter the date that the citation is written showing the month, day, and year in number form, separated by dashes (i.e., 4-23-99).
Time: Enter the time that the violation was observed using conventional standard time references (i.e., 9:15 a.m.).
Day of Week: Enter the day of the week that the violation was observed using the abbreviations Mon, Tues, Wed, Thur, Fri, Sat, or Sun.
Identification of Violator: Enter the full name and address of the violating subject. Enter the subject's phone number on the line marked "business address."

steps.step: Personal Identification of Violator: Enter the following.
sub: Sex (m or f)
Hair color
Eye color
Height
Weight
Race (W = white, B = black, H = Hispanic, O = other)
Date of birth (i.e., 7-12-39)
Subject's identification number: California Driver's License (CDL) or California Identification (CID)

steps: Violation of Riverside County Ordinance Number: If this is a County violation, enter "630." If this is a City violation, put a line through "Violation of Riverside County Ordinance Number" and write "Riverside City Municipal Code Title 8."

Enter the section number and descriptive title (for example, Section 8.12.010, failure to license; Section 8.04.050, leash law violation). NOTE: Only one (1) violation may be entered on each citation, although it may pertain to multiple animals that have the same owner.
Enter the description of all animals involved (i.e., breed, color, sex, etc.).
Location of Violation(s): Enter the exact location(s) where the violation(s) occurred.
Issuing Officer: The issuing officer will sign his or her name (first, middle initial, and last) and enter his or her badge number.

Signature: The subject in violation will sign his or her name, after having the citation explained to him or her and clarifying that signing the citation is a promise to appear in court, but not an admission of guilt.
Municipal: Enter an "X" in the appropriate box to indicate the court in which the subject's case will be heard.
Court: Enter the correct name of the court and the address where the subject's case will be heard.

steps.step: Court Appearance Date: Enter the day, month, and time that the subject will be required to appear in court (for example, on the 12th day of Jan, 1999 at 1:30 PM).
sub: The violator shall be given at least six (6) weeks, but not more than seven (7) weeks, before being required to appear for arraignment.
Should the violator refuse to sign the citation, the unsigned citation is to be submitted to the ACO Supervisor for review and a possible criminal complaint being filed.

sections.heading: Form Disposition

steps.step: Distribute the copies of the citation form as follows:
sub: White copy: court
Yellow copy: subject in violation
Pink copy: filed at Animal Services in the alphabetical filing system
Golden rod copy: to be retained by the issuing officer for his or her own records and to be kept as long as the officer sees fit.

steps: A voided citation must have the reason that it was voided written on the citation. Citations are pre-numbered and must be accounted for, whether issued or voided.

documents.title: Stand-By Duties
documents.purpose: To establish a uniform procedure for personnel assigned stand-by duty. Stand-by time is assigned to provide emergency services after hours and on holidays. Stand-by will be rotated on the work schedule, although assignments may be given on short notice due to injuries, illnesses, natural disasters, etc.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: All County of Riverside Animal Control Officers.
documents.supersedes: Policy 200-15, Stand-By Duties, effective September 6, 2013 (revised from October 7, 2009)

sections.heading: Contact
steps: Each employee assigned to stand-by duty shall provide the Department with a home phone number or cell phone number. The Department will provide the employee with a cell phone for this duty.
While on stand-by duty, the officer will be available for contact from the Sheriff's Department, Police Department, supervision, other officers, and the answering service.

sections.heading: Responding to Calls
steps: If the officer receives a call, text, or e-mail, the officer shall ensure the call is within their assigned jurisdiction and reply to the answering service that they have received the call.
Make a phone call to the reporting party without delay.
Screen all requests for service received after hours by asking appropriate questions to insure the request is of an emergent nature, and verify all information prior to responding.
After verification, respond to the call expeditiously or make a call for an officer to respond the following business day.

In the event a call back number was not afforded the officer or attempts made by the officer are inconclusive, the officer must use their best judgment and common sense in determining if a response is needed or not. DAS would encourage all officers to be overly cautious in these situations.

sections.heading: Compensation
steps: Each officer on stand-by will be compensated with stand-by pay (Z07), which is equal to one (1) hour of regular pay for every eight (8) hours on stand-by.

In the event the officer must respond to a request, he or she will be compensated time and a half from the time the officer receives the call until the officer returns to the location where the stand-by duty originated, with a minimum of 2 hours pay.
If the officer does not respond, he or she shall be paid for time spent returning phone calls, collecting, and verifying information.

steps.step: Each employee must apply common sense and good judgment when determining whether a call is an emergency needing immediate response. The following are guidelines of types of calls that need to be responded to while on stand-by duty:
sub: Stray bite in which ownership of the animal cannot be established
Injured stray animals
Sick stray animals
Loose livestock
Requested assistance by other law enforcement agencies
Dog attacking livestock in progress
Exotic animal at large
Vicious or guard dog at large
Severe dog bite
Confirmed extreme cruelty or neglect case
Calls where the reporting party is insistent on service and it is in the best interest of DAS to handle the call

Pick up of an animal that was either brought in by a "Good Samaritan" or dropped off earlier by yourself or another officer, that requires the animal to be picked up from an overnight emergency veterinarian the following morning before closing

sections.heading: How to Evaluate a Stand-By Request: Emergency Calls
steps: During non-field service hours, the answering service will receive Animal Services calls. When the answering service or law enforcement agency contacts the officer on stand-by, the officer shall attempt to get the call-back telephone number of the person reporting the incident. This may not always occur (i.e., injured dog in an intersection reported by a person driving by the scene; dog bite victim was taken to the hospital, etc.); however, depending on the situation, it may still be mandatory that the call be responded to.

If a peace officer (Sheriff or Police) is in the area and is available to confirm the call, the Animal Control Officer is relieved from responding to the call until the peace officer confirms the emergency and verifies a response is necessary.

When a telephone number of the reporting party is available, call the reporting party before responding. The few minutes that elapse between the call and the call back could change the status of the request (i.e., an injured animal died just before the return call; a stray sick dog was picked up by its owner; loose livestock is captured by the owner and confined, etc.).

Ask relevant questions in order to get all of the facts to insure that the proper action is taken. Responding to a non-emergency could cause undue delay to a real emergency; however, refusing an insistent reporting party by not responding to a questionable call may have far greater consequences by exposing the public to danger, causing an animal unnecessary suffering, or damaging public relations.

sections.heading: How to Evaluate a Stand-By Request: Non-Emergency Calls
steps: Record each request of a non-emergent nature on the officer's overtime trip sheet just as if the stand-by officer would have responded. This will ensure the officer received the request and the reason for non-response was noted.

If follow-up is needed, the stand-by officer will create a Chameleon activity and appropriate memo, and inform their Sergeant of the status along with the nature of what needs to be done, on the next business day.

sections.heading: Returning to Shelters
steps: In the event the officer must return to the shelter to drop off animals or equipment, the officer must be aware of possible security and safety issues. The officer should observe the front of the building for anyone loitering, abandoned animals, or indications of a break-in.
Whether the shelter has an automatic gate or manually operated gate, officers must pull through the gate and either close the gate or wait until the automatic gate closes before proceeding.

sections.heading: Paperwork, Computer Work, and Return of Equipment
steps: Fill out trip sheets for all calls received, whether they were responded to or not. Include the name, address, and phone number of the reporting party and the time the call was received.
Indicate whether the call was handled by phone call only and the reason the officer did not respond.
For those calls completed, create an activity, disposition, and appropriate memos and working times.
For those calls not responded to that require follow-up, complete an activity and appropriate memos for response the next business day.

To insure the equipment is available for use the following day, officers who are off the weekday after a stand-by are required to return their vehicle, stand-by phone, rifle, immobilization gun, or any other equipment by 8:30 a.m. The only exception is with prior approval from supervision.

Officers turning over stand-by to another officer are required to return all equipment at 7:30 a.m. An exception is if the officers agree on an alternate time and the change is approved by the supervisor.

In the event the stand-by officer has to pick up an animal dropped off or left overnight at any of the emergency pet clinics, the officer is required to pick up the animal early enough to make it back to their respective shelter or office by the time their shift begins at 7:30 a.m.

sections.heading: Contacting Supervision
steps: There are several instances where an officer may question whether responding to a call is truly necessary. Each employee must apply common sense and good judgment when determining whether a call is an emergency. Remember, responding to a non-emergency could cause undue delay to a real emergency; however, refusing an insistent reporting party by not responding to a questionable call may have far greater consequences by exposing the public to danger, causing an animal unnecessary suffering, or damaging public relations.
Contact supervision regarding high level complaints, when additional officers other than those on call need to respond, or when an extreme case has been confirmed.

documents.searchText: to establish a uniform procedure for personnel assigned stand-by duty. stand-by time is assigned to provide emergency services after hours and on holidays. stand-by will be rotated on the work schedule, although assignments may be given on short notice due to injuries, illnesses, natural disasters, etc. all county of riverside animal control officers. director of animal services contact each employee assigned to stand-by duty shall provide the department with a home phone number or cell phone number. the department will provide the employee with a cell phone for this duty. while on stand-by duty, the officer will be available for contact from the sheriff's department, police department, supervision, other officers, and the answering service. responding to calls if the officer receives a call, text, or e-mail, the officer shall ensure the call is within their assigned jurisdiction and reply to the answering service that they have received the call. make a phone call to the reporting party without delay. screen all requests for service received after hours by asking appropriate questions to insure the request is of an emergent nature, and verify all information prior to responding. after verification, respond to the call expeditiously or make a call for an officer to respond the following business day. in the event a call back number was not afforded the officer or attempts made by the officer are inconclusive, the officer must use their best judgment and common sense in determining if a response is needed or not. das would encourage all officers to be overly cautious in these situations. compensation each officer on stand-by will be compensated with stand-by pay (z07), which is equal to one (1) hour of regular pay for every eight (8) hours on stand-by. in the event the officer must respond to a request, he or she will be compensated time and a half from the time the officer receives the call until the officer returns to the location where the stand-by duty originated, with a minimum of 2 hours pay. if the officer does not respond, he or she shall be paid for time spent returning phone calls, collecting, and verifying information. requests for service {"step": "each employee must apply common sense and good judgment when determining whether a call is an emergency needing immediate response. the following are guidelines of types of calls that need to be responded to while on stand-by duty:", "sub": ["stray bite in which ownership of the animal cannot be established", "injured stray animals", "sick stray animals", "loose livestock", "requested assistance by other law enforcement agencies", "dog attacking livestock in progress", "exotic animal at large", "vicious or guard dog at large", "severe dog bite", "confirmed extreme cruelty or neglect case", "calls where the reporting party is insistent on service and it is in the best interest of das to handle the call", "pick up of an animal that was either brought in by a \"good samaritan\" or dropped off earlier by yourself or another officer, that requires the animal to be picked up from an overnight emergency veterinarian the following morning before closing"]} how to evaluate a stand-by request: emergency calls during non-field service hours, the answering service will receive animal services calls. when the answering service or law enforcement agency contacts the officer on stand-by, the officer shall attempt to get the call-back telephone number of the person reporting the incident. this may not always occur (i.e., injured dog in an intersection reported by a person driving by the scene; dog bite victim was taken to the hospital, etc.); however, depending on the situation, it may still be mandatory that the call be responded to. if a peace officer (sheriff or police) is in the area and is available to confirm the call, the animal control officer is relieved from responding to the call until the peace officer confirms the emergency and verifies a response is necessary. when a telephone number of the reporting party is available, call the reporting party before responding. the few minutes that elapse between the call and the call back could change the status of the request (i.e., an injured animal died just before the return call; a stray sick dog was picked up by its owner; loose livestock is captured by the owner and confined, etc.). ask relevant questions in order to get all of the facts to insure that the proper action is taken. responding to a non-emergency could cause undue delay to a real emergency; however, refusing an insistent reporting party by not responding to a questionable call may have far greater consequences by exposing the public to danger, causing an animal unnecessary suffering, or damaging public relations. how to evaluate a stand-by request: non-emergency calls record each request of a non-emergent nature on the officer's overtime trip sheet just as if the stand-by officer would have responded. this will ensure the officer received the request and the reason for non-response was noted. if follow-up is needed, the stand-by officer will create a chameleon activity and appropriate memo, and inform their sergeant of the status along with the nature of what needs to be done, on the next business day. returning to shelters in the event the officer must return to the shelter to drop off animals or equipment, the officer must be aware of possible security and safety issues. the officer should observe the front of the building for anyone loitering, abandoned animals, or indications of a break-in. whether the shelter has an automatic gate or manually operated gate, officers must pull through the gate and either close the gate or wait until the automatic gate closes before proceeding. paperwork, computer work, and return of equipment fill out trip sheets for all calls received, whether they were responded to or not. include the name, address, and phone number of the reporting party and the time the call was received. indicate whether the call was handled by phone call only and the reason the officer did not respond. for those calls completed, create an activity, disposition, and appropriate memos and working times. for those calls not responded to that require follow-up, complete an activity and appropriate memos for response the next business day. to insure the equipment is available for use the following day, officers who are off the weekday after a stand-by are required to return their vehicle, stand-by phone, rifle, immobilization gun, or any other equipment by 8:30 a.m. the only exception is with prior approval from supervision. officers turning over stand-by to another officer are required to return all equipment at 7:30 a.m. an exception is if the officers agree on an alternate time and the change is approved by the supervisor. in the event the stand-by officer has to pick up an animal dropped off or left overnight at any of the emergency pet clinics, the officer is required to pick up the animal early enough to make it back to their respective shelter or office by the time their shift begins at 7:30 a.m. contacting supervision there are several instances where an officer may question whether responding to a call is truly necessary. each employee must apply common sense and good judgment when determining whether a call is an emergency. remember, responding to a non-emergency could cause undue delay to a real emergency; however, refusing an insistent reporting party by not responding to a questionable call may have far greater consequences by exposing the public to danger, causing an animal unnecessary suffering, or damaging public relations. contact supervision regarding high level complaints, when additional officers other than those on call need to respond, or when an extreme case has been confirmed.

documents.title: Field Operations, Duties and Procedures
documents.purpose: To establish a uniform procedure and set guidelines for Department of Animal Services (DAS) employees working within the ACO series. Safety is the responsibility of all DAS employees, including you, your supervisor, and other department management. If you determine that a member of the public possibly presents a threat to your personal safety, immediately remove yourself from the situation, if possible, and consult with your supervisor for further direction.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: Applicable to all Field staff linked with the Animal Control Officer (ACO) promotional ladder, including ACO I and II, Sergeant, Lieutenant, and Captain, and is related to their duties in the field.
documents.supersedes: Policy 200-16, Field Operations, Duties and Procedures, effective March 19, 2020 (revised from March 12, 2014; revised from October 7, 2009)
references: DAS Policy 200-01
DAS Policy 200-22
DAS Policy 200-30
DAS Policy 200-36

sections.heading: Method
steps: The individual ACO in the field is the first line representative of DAS in dealing with the public. The importance of achieving a favorable first impression with a citizen cannot be over stressed. It is at this initial contact point where the credibility and reputation of DAS and the County of Riverside are most critical. The ACO must be capable of performing his or her duties in a confident, professional manner. The following procedural rules and regulations are intended to assist the ACO to achieve the confidence and professional sureness the job demands.

sections.heading: Work Requirements
steps: Briefings: All Officers will be ready to start their tour of duty promptly at the scheduled time and in complete uniform, including possession of all personal communication equipment issued by the Department. Any field personnel anticipating arriving late to briefing must notify their Sergeant (Lead) or Lieutenant (Supervisor) (both considered supervision) before the start of their shift via phone. Personnel will be attentive to the instructions given and will refrain from unproductive behavior in questioning assignments.
Schedule: All shift assignments will be made by the Lieutenant in accordance with current operational needs and staffing.

Work Assignments: Vehicle and area assignments will be made by the Sergeant and/or Lieutenant. No changes will be made without approval of the person making the assignments. Areas will be rotated when possible to enable field personnel to gain knowledge of the entire area. Officers are not to leave their assigned geographic work area at any time during their shift without permission of the supervisor or designee.

Preparation of Forms: Each employee is expected to have all necessary blank forms available and to properly complete each form used, giving all correct and pertinent information relating to the subject matter of the particular form.

Responsibility for Equipment: Every employee must care for and keep in good condition the equipment, tools, and supplies issued to him or her by the Department. All battery-operated equipment must also be charged prior to their shift. Careless use of property, equipment, and tools resulting in loss or unnecessary repair costs may result in disciplinary action. The Officer should inventory all equipment, supplies, and paperwork before leaving for their assigned area, thus eliminating the need to return to the shelter unnecessarily during their shift. This may also eliminate safety issues, such as discovering they are not carrying a control stick after arriving to a call of a vicious dog.

steps.step: Responsibility for Assigned Vehicles:
sub: All personnel will perform a visual inspection of their assigned vehicle prior to operating the County vehicle. Any loss, damage, or unserviceable condition noticed by the employee will be reported to supervision immediately.
Accidents, however slight, must be reported immediately to supervision and the proper law enforcement agency for report purposes.
No County vehicle will be operated outside the County of Riverside except in pursuit of an animal involved in a bite case or by request of, or permission from, Supervision.
No one, not even an employee, is permitted to ride in a DAS vehicle without first having properly executed a Ride Along request form pursuant to DAS Policy 200-30.

The employee assigned to a particular vehicle will wash the exterior on a regular basis, or when necessary, at the shelter or County car wash. The employee assigned to a vehicle will wash and disinfect the cages at the end of each shift and verify the cab area is clean. Field Supervision will periodically inspect the interior and exterior of the vehicles for compliance.

Vehicles will be fueled on a daily basis, preferably before proceeding to assigned areas. When an Officer is assigned standby duty, his or her vehicle will be fueled at the end of the normal working day in preparation for standby duties.

All DAS vehicles must be parked legally and locked whenever possible to prevent any unwanted activity. When returning a vehicle to the shelter, the vehicle must be parked in its assigned parking area. The employee assigned to the vehicle shall insure that a set of keys, including a compartment key, is hung up in the key box, or specified area, no later than the end of their shift. This will allow use of the vehicle by other employees as needed.
Animal Control vehicles shall be operated in compliance with all State vehicle laws and pursuant to DAS Policy 200-01.

steps: Vehicle Maintenance Due: Each employee assigned to a vehicle is responsible for filling out a Service Request form (Appendix B) and turning the vehicle in for servicing or repairs. Employees picking up such vehicles will be responsible for verifying that the service has been completed.

Meals: Meal breaks are assigned by area and are to be taken as close as possible to the scheduled time. Each Officer is to notify the dispatcher of their lunch location via the radio. Meals will be taken within their assigned areas unless authorized by supervision to eat elsewhere. Two daily breaks, 15 minutes each in length, are also to be taken within the assigned working area.

Inspection and Supervision in the Field: All employees are subject to inspection and supervision at any time or place during their routine tour of duty. Supervision may include contacting complaining citizens to evaluate performance of the Officer in the field.

Citations and Warnings: Citations and warnings are important law enforcement tools. Citations are to be issued by the Officer on the scene. Officer discretion for individual citations is allowed with previous approval of a Supervisor. Warnings should be used when an officer feels it will help move the situation into compliance.

Request for Peace Officer Back-up: If the Officer feels that he or she or any other person is in jeopardy, they will request a Peace Officer back-up. Such requests will be reserved for serious instances only, involving real danger, and will be kept to a minimum in order to avoid reducing the urgent impact of such assistance. Assistance from a Peace Officer may be requested when a subject refuses to present identification or accept a citation. In this case, the Officer is to contact their supervision for direction. Peace Officer back-ups will not be requested to assist during animal impoundments. DAS is committed to a policy of cooperation with other law enforcement agencies to the extent of available manpower and equipment.

Impounding of Animals: The humane care and treatment of any animal encountered in the field or shelter is the principal concern and responsibility of all DAS employees. One of the main functions of an Animal Control Officer is to protect animals and the public by impounding stray, confined, and unwanted animals. The physical requirements, along with knowledge and skills to humanely and safely handle a variety of animal species, are essential for the completion of the ACO duties. Officers should possess the ability to lift heavy animals and equipment and use proper lifting techniques pursuant to Policy 200-22.

Care of Animals While in the Unit: Care will be taken not to overload a unit with animals. Permission must be requested to return to the shelter for off-loading when necessary. Injured animals will be brought to the shelter or a veterinarian as soon as possible. When carrying animals, vehicles will be parked in the shade whenever possible and compartment air conditioners will be used when appropriate to avoid unnecessary discomfort to the animals.

Off-Loading Units: All animals will be unloaded by the Animal Control Officer or assigned DAS staff. All required paperwork or computer work is to be completed by the ACO at the time of off-loading. Extreme care must be taken to ensure that paperwork and computer entries are done correctly, and all pertinent information is recorded.

Wild Animals: The regulation of wild animals is the responsibility of the California Department of Fish and Wildlife. However, if there is an immediate threat to life or property, report of injury, or if another official agency requests assistance, such calls will be answered by DAS. Depending on work assignment, DAS may assist the public with calls relating to nuisance wild animals. Any questions about wild animal calls should be directed to supervision for direction.
Extra Enforcement: Officers will patrol their geographical areas when not handling specific complaints, taking appropriate action when violations are observed.

Euthanasia Solution/Controlled Substance: All Animal Control Officers will receive a course of training by the Department prior to being authorized to use Sodium Pentobarbital euthanasia solution. All Officers will carry euthanasia solution with them at all times while on duty and working in the field. It is the responsibility of the Officer to have an adequate supply at the beginning of each shift. Euthanasia solution is a controlled substance, and the bottle which holds the solution must be labeled to identify the contents. The euthanasia solution must be kept in a secured, locked vehicle or locked in a storage cabinet at the shelter. Euthanasia solution will not be left overnight inside the County vehicle except in the case where the Officer is on standby duty. Euthanasia solution should never be left unsecured and unattended. Euthanasia solution must be kept in a locked "euth lock box" while in the vehicle. The euth lock box must be chained and locked to a cab structure to reduce the possibility of easy theft. Euthanasia solution must be accompanied by a euthanasia log, which reflects the user name, date issued, amount, and bottle number from which the solution originated (see Policy 200-36).
Negligence or willfully failing to abide by the guidelines set forth in this policy may result in disciplinary action.

sections.heading: Relationship with Supervision
steps: Lieutenant of Field Services: The Lieutenant of Field Services is considered a Supervisor and shall be responsible for the discipline, morale, training, and effective performance of duties of the Animal Control Officers. He or she reports directly to the Captain of Field Services and/or Commander of Field Services.

Sergeant of Field Services: The Sergeant of Field Services reports directly to the Lieutenant of Field Services and assists the Lieutenant with his or her duties. Sergeants will be present at briefings and will inspect Officers for proper uniforms and equipment. Sergeants will assign vehicles and areas and check and assist those under their supervision while in the field. They will take a personal interest in the problems and welfare of their subordinates, guiding and counseling them as required. Sergeants are responsible for giving appropriate professional advice and instruction to the Animal Control Officers for the development of administrative and supervisory skills. When disciplinary or commendatory action is indicated, a Sergeant will make recommendations in writing to the Lieutenant. Supporting documentation shall be included and/or attached to written reports.

related.name: Service Request Form / Vehicle Maintenance Request

appendices: Appendix A: Acknowledgment of County of Riverside Department of Animal Services Policy for Field Operations, Duties and Procedures (form, attached separately)
Appendix B: Vehicle Maintenance Request (form, attached separately)

documents.title: Completion of Controlled Substance Log
documents.flag: Numbered (=Policy per rule) but reads as a step-by-step protocol
documents.purpose: To provide a uniform procedure for recording the amount of controlled substance used each time an animal is chemically immobilized in the field. (The original policy contains no Purpose statement; this sentence is drawn from the policy's content.)
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: All County of Riverside Animal Control Officers who use Telazol, Ketamine, Ketamine/Xylazine, or Butorphanol/Xylazine in the field.
documents.supersedes: Policy 200-18, Completion of Controlled Substance Log, effective October 7, 2009 (revised from July 1, 2006)
references: DAS Policy 309 (as cited in the original; legacy policy number)
DAS Policy 200-36 (referenced in related field policies for controlled substance logging)

sections.heading: When to Use
steps: Use the Controlled Substance Log (Appendix A) to record the amount of the controlled substance used each time an animal is chemically immobilized.
Each bottle is individually identified and has been assigned to the officer.

sections.heading: Chemical Bottle
steps: Write on the bottle the date that the chemical was first used or reconstituted and the concentration (i.e., mg per ml).

sections.heading: Controlled Substance Log
steps: Drug: Use the appropriate form which was assigned with each vial.
Bottle #: The pre-assigned bottle number must match the form.
Date: Enter the date of administration.

Animal Identification: Enter the Chameleon animal ID number of the animal which was immobilized. If the animal was not captured and impounded, an ID must still be created and attached to the Activity with a memo detailing the event.
Amount Used: Enter the amount of chemical that was dispensed. Include and record the drug volume which may have been lost if the target was not hit.

Amount Remaining: Enter the amount of chemical remaining in the bottle. (Subtract the amount used from the beginning amount. This total should be the same as the amount physically remaining in the bottle.)

Should there be a need to discard any remaining reconstituted Telazol or any other controlled substance chemical, this information MUST be logged on the Controlled Substance Log. There must be two (2) sets of initials on the Controlled Substance Log when documenting any discarded chemical.

sections.heading: Control Card
steps: When completing the Animal Control Card (PM-002) (Appendix B) on any tranquilized animal, enter the word "Tranquilized" in bold letters on the front of the Control Card in the open space next to the animal information (see Policy 309, step 4).

steps.step: On the back of the Control Card, under "Additional Information" (see Policy 309, step 51), note the following:
sub: Type of chemical used (i.e., Ketamine/Rompun, etc.)
Amount or dosage in milliliters
Concentration of milligrams per milliliter

steps.step: Enter the following information on the Immobilization Report Form (Appendix C):
sub: Type of chemical used
Amount of chemical used
Concentration of chemical used

sections.heading: General Information
steps: Do not use any unused controlled substance chemical with a past due expiration date stamped on the bottle.
Return unused or expired preparations to the shelter Veterinarian for disposal and reconciliation before a refill will be dispensed.

related.name: Controlled Substance Logs CS8, CS8a, CS8b
Animal ID and Activity (memo attached when animal not captured)

appendices: Appendix A: Controlled Substance Log CS8/CS8a/CS8b (form, attached separately)
Appendix B: Animal Control Card PM-002 (form, attached separately)
Appendix C: Immobilization Report Form (form, attached separately)

documents.searchText: to provide a uniform procedure for recording the amount of controlled substance used each time an animal is chemically immobilized in the field. (the original policy contains no purpose statement; this sentence is drawn from the policy's content.) all county of riverside animal control officers who use telazol, ketamine, ketamine/xylazine, or butorphanol/xylazine in the field. director of animal services when to use use the controlled substance log (appendix a) to record the amount of the controlled substance used each time an animal is chemically immobilized. each bottle is individually identified and has been assigned to the officer. chemical bottle write on the bottle the date that the chemical was first used or reconstituted and the concentration (i.e., mg per ml). controlled substance log drug: use the appropriate form which was assigned with each vial. bottle #: the pre-assigned bottle number must match the form. date: enter the date of administration. animal identification: enter the chameleon animal id number of the animal which was immobilized. if the animal was not captured and impounded, an id must still be created and attached to the activity with a memo detailing the event. amount used: enter the amount of chemical that was dispensed. include and record the drug volume which may have been lost if the target was not hit. amount remaining: enter the amount of chemical remaining in the bottle. (subtract the amount used from the beginning amount. this total should be the same as the amount physically remaining in the bottle.) should there be a need to discard any remaining reconstituted telazol or any other controlled substance chemical, this information must be logged on the controlled substance log. there must be two (2) sets of initials on the controlled substance log when documenting any discarded chemical. control card when completing the animal control card (pm-002) (appendix b) on any tranquilized animal, enter the word "tranquilized" in bold letters on the front of the control card in the open space next to the animal information (see policy 309, step 4). {"step": "on the back of the control card, under \"additional information\" (see policy 309, step 51), note the following:", "sub": ["type of chemical used (i.e., ketamine/rompun, etc.)", "amount or dosage in milliliters", "concentration of milligrams per milliliter"]} immobilization report form {"step": "enter the following information on the immobilization report form (appendix c):", "sub": ["type of chemical used", "amount of chemical used", "concentration of chemical used"]} general information do not use any unused controlled substance chemical with a past due expiration date stamped on the bottle. return unused or expired preparations to the shelter veterinarian for disposal and reconciliation before a refill will be dispensed. das policy 309 (as cited in the original; legacy policy number) das policy 200-36 (referenced in related field policies for controlled substance logging)

documents.title: Animal Loading Ramp Procedure
documents.purpose: To ensure that all employees know how to operate the loading ramps safely and correctly.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: All Field Services employees.
documents.supersedes: Policy 200-21, effective October 7, 2009 (revised from August 20, 2001)

sections.heading: When to Use
steps: Use ramps, when necessary, to load domestic animals estimated to weigh over 50 pounds and not more than 200 pounds.
Use the ramps for all small livestock that will fit in the large compartment of the Animal Control truck only.

sections.heading: How to Use
steps: Use caution at all times when deploying the ramp.
Remove the retaining pin located inside the large animal compartment on the driver side of the Animal Control truck.

From the ramp's resting location under the large animal compartment, gently lift the ramp up using the handles and pull the ramp out slowly. The ramp should deploy to either side of the unit with very little effort. Pull the ramp all of the way out until it stops and guide the ramp to the ground while bending knees.

steps.step: Use basic lifting rules.
sub: Bend knees and keep back straight when lowering the ramp to the ground. Bend knees and size up the load.
Keep the ramp close to the body and use legs to lift and push while keeping the back straight.

steps.step: Once the animal is safely loaded onto the Animal Control truck and the cage door is closed, load the ramp as follows.
sub: Line the ramp up with the ramp opening.
Slide the ramp back in carefully, making sure not to twist your back or knees.
Lift the ramp up over the ramp resting lip and reinstall the retaining pin inside the large compartment.

Do not drop the ramp on the ground. If the ramp is dropped, it will make a very loud noise and could cause the ramp to bend, making it difficult to slide back in, as well as scaring the animals on the vehicle. NOTE: If the ramp is dropped, the Animal Services employee must notify supervision immediately.
Do not walk on the ramp. The ramp is designed for animals weighing 50 to 200 pounds. If an employee is unable to load an animal without walking on the ramp, they are to call supervision for help.

revisions.author: Outcomes for Pets (draft)
revisions.desc: Migrated into standard RCDAS SOP template. Content unchanged from 2009 version; substantive edits pending (see edit log).

documents.title: Animal Lifting
documents.purpose: To ensure that all employees who lift animals do so in the proper manner so as to not hurt themselves or the animal which they are lifting.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: All Animal Services employees.
documents.supersedes: Policy 200-22, effective October 7, 2009 (revised from August 20, 2001)

sections.heading: When to Use
steps: Use the following procedure any time an animal is being lifted off of the ground, placed in a cage, animal carrying unit, or on a table without the use of other aids or assistance.

sections.heading: Lifting an Animal Off the Ground and Placing in a Cage or Carrying Unit
steps: Muzzle all animals before lifting the animal off of the ground and placing it in a cage or animal carrying unit.

steps.step: Follow basic lifting rules.
sub: Bend knees and size up the load.
Keep the animal close to one's body and lift with legs.
Keep one's back straight.
Position the animal inside the vehicle, or in a cage, being careful not to twist one's back or knees.
Do not release the grip on the animal's muzzle or leash until the cage door can be safely shut.
Shut the cage door with one hand, keeping the animal secure with the other hand.
Use one's body to block the cage opening.

steps: See photos (Attachment A).

sections.heading: Lifting an Animal Off the Ground and onto a Table
steps: Muzzle all animals before lifting the animal off of the ground and placing it onto a table.

steps.step: Follow basic lifting rules.
sub: Bend knees and size up the load.
Keep the animal close to one's body and lift with legs.
Keep one's back straight.
Position the animal onto a table, being careful not to twist one's back or knees.
Do not release the grip on the animal's muzzle or leash until one knows that they can safely release it.

steps: See photos (Attachment A).

appendices: Appendix A: Photos demonstrating proper lifting (attached separately)
