documents.title: Notice of Seizure of Animals
documents.purpose: To establish a uniform procedure for seizing and impounding animals found to be in a condition of neglect or that appear to have been cruelly treated and left without proper care.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: All Animal Services field staff that investigate animal cruelty cases and impound animals.
documents.supersedes: Policy 200-24, effective October 7, 2009 (revised from August 14, 2002)
references: California Penal Code 597.1
Riverside County Code 6.12.030 and 6.12.040
Riverside County Ordinance No. 716
Riverside City Title 6, Section 12.030

sections.heading: Policy Statement
steps: Riverside Animal Services is committed to the preservation of the health and welfare of all animals within the County of Riverside. In doing so, the Department of Animal Services will enforce the provisions of the State of California Penal Code 597.1 and the Riverside County Code 6.12.030 and 6.12.040.

sections.heading: When to Use
steps: Conduct an animal welfare investigation to determine if probable cause exists to believe that an animal is being kept in violation of said state law and county code.

If it is found, based upon investigation, that impoundment is necessary to save an animal's life, issue a notice of seizure of animals to the owner or person authorized to keep the animal(s) or his or her agent.
If no known owner is located, as in the case of abandoned animals at real property, post notice at the premises.
Issue the notice of seizure of animals based on the exigency of the circumstances; it will be either a pre-notice of seizure or a post-notice of seizure.

sections.heading: Notice of Pre-Seizure of Animals (Hearing Prior to Seizure)
steps: Where the need for immediate seizure is not present and prior to the commencement of any criminal proceedings, provide the owner or keeper of the animals, if known or ascertainable after reasonable investigation, with the opportunity for a hearing prior to any seizure or impoundment of the animal. This generally should occur after the animal owner or keeper was issued a notice of violation and failed to comply with the officer's directions by the end of the correction deadline.

steps.step: Complete the notice of pre-seizure as follows.
sub: Enter the date.
Enter the name of the owner or caretaker if known. Enter the mailing address of the owner or caretaker if known, including city, state, and zip code.
Enter the description of animal(s) including name, type of animal and breed, sex, and any identifying marks.
Enter the reason for the seizure (i.e., found in neglected condition, appears to have been cruelly treated).
Enter the name and address of the shelter the animals will be housed at.
Enter the name of the officer serving the notice.

Enter the date the declaration of ownership must be returned by. In the case of the pre-seizure notice, proof of ownership must be received two (2) days from the date of the notice, excluding weekends and holidays.
Enter the name of the officer investigating the case.
Enter again the date the notice is to be received by Animal Services: two (2) days from the date of notice, excluding weekends and holidays.

steps: Hand deliver the notice to the owner or keeper of the animal or post it on the premises if necessary. Also mail the notice by certified letter to the owner or keeper's address if known.

sections.heading: Notice of Seizure of Animals (Post-Seizure Hearing)
steps: Whenever an Animal Control Officer seizes or impounds an animal based on a reasonable belief that prompt action is required to protect the health or safety of the animal or the health and safety of others, the officer shall, prior to the commencement of any criminal proceedings, provide the owner or keeper of the animals, if known or ascertained after reasonable investigation, with the opportunity for a post-seizure hearing to determine the validity of the seizure or impoundment or both.

steps.step: Complete the notice of seizure as follows.
sub: Enter the animal owner or keeper's name, address, city, state, and zip code.
Enter the date of impoundment.
Enter the address from where the animals were impounded.
Enter the name of the shelter and the address where the animals were impounded.
Enter the description of the animals including breed or type of animal, color, age, and name, if known.
Enter the name and title of the Operations Chief or Supervising Animal Control Officer to whom the animal owner or keeper should return the declaration of ownership/request for post-seizure hearing.
Enter the name and title of the investigating officer.

steps: Hand deliver the notice to the owner or keeper of the animal or post it on the premises if necessary. Also mail the notice by certified letter to the owner or keeper's address if known.

The owner or keeper must request the post-seizure hearing by signing and returning the Declaration of Ownership/Request for Post-Seizure Hearing within 10 days, including weekends and holidays, of the date of the notice.

related.name: Notice of Pre-Seizure of Animals (Attachment A)
Notice of Seizure of Animals, Post-Seizure Hearing (Attachment B)
Declaration of Ownership/Request for Pre-Seizure Hearing
Declaration of Ownership/Request for Post-Seizure Hearing

appendices: Appendix A: Notice of Pre-Seizure of Animal(s) letter with Declaration of Ownership/Request for Pre-Seizure Hearing (attached separately)
Appendix B: Notice of Seizure of Animal(s) letter with Declaration of Ownership/Request for Post-Seizure Hearing (attached separately)

documents.title: Chemical Capture
documents.purpose: To provide a basic understanding of the chemical equipment available, deciding when and where to use it, calculating drug dosages, legal requirements, and public and personnel safety issues. Chemical capture is dangerous, difficult, and expensive. It should be used only after careful consideration of all aspects of the case, and only by personnel that have been trained and only with appropriate equipment.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services. Field use requires Supervisor or Senior Officer approval; officers must be qualified by a Senior Animal Control Officer or Supervisor before using immobilization drugs and equipment.
documents.scope: Applicable to all staff who must immobilize an animal.
documents.supersedes: Policy 200-27, effective October 2009 (revised from December 8, 2003)
references: California Penal Code 832 (Firearms Certification)
Department of Animal Services Policy 323, Completion of Controlled Substance Log

definitions.def: The use of remotely injected immobilization drugs to capture an animal that cannot be confined by other means.
The time between injection and immobilization of the animal.

sections.heading: General Procedure
steps: Consider chemical immobilization as a last resort. Careful consideration must be given to whether or not chemical capture is the best method to resolve the situation.

First attempt to confine the animal using additional personnel or animal traps. When these attempts have failed, or if exigent circumstances exist (i.e., dog bite), immobilization may be used with the Supervisor or Senior Officer's approval.
Make every possible effort to safeguard the health and welfare of the animal. The person who administers the drug must accept the moral and legal responsibility for the life of that animal.

Use only well-muscled areas of the neck, shoulder, or hindquarters as sites for remote injection. This will reduce the possibility of traumatic injury and increase the rate of the drug's absorption and tranquilization.

sections.heading: Firearm Safety Rules
steps: All personnel must be Penal Code 832 Firearms Certified, as immobilization guns are considered firearms.
Observe firearms safety rules at all times. The darts can be propelled at speeds up to 700 feet per second (477 miles per hour), causing serious damage to anybody or anything that is struck.

A Senior Animal Control Officer or Supervisor must qualify all officers before using the immobilization drugs and equipment. The officer must demonstrate an understanding of the drugs, calculations, equipment, and maintaining records, and must accurately and safely hit the target.
The officer and Senior/Supervisor will fill out a Chemical Immobilization Qualifying Report (Attachment A).

sections.heading: Legal Issues
steps: All drugs currently recommended for animal immobilization must be obtained by a licensed veterinarian and used under veterinary supervision.

A veterinarian must keep records of all non-narcotic scheduled drugs received or dispensed. The records must be kept for a period of three years and are subject to inspection by the Drug Enforcement Administration.
Use of immobilization drugs by a layperson without instruction, consultation, or veterinary supervision is illegal.

Fill out Controlled Substance Logs (Attachment B) when any drugs are used or discarded. Use Immobilization Reports (Attachment C) for the specific animal each time the immobilization gun is used (see Policy 323, Completion of Controlled Substance Log).

Do not leave equipment unattended. When not in use, it must be kept under lock in a secure room or vehicle. Take great care to protect the drugs and equipment from theft or misuse. It should not be possible for unauthorized personnel or the public to have access to the equipment or drugs.

sections.heading: Equipment
steps: Three (3) different projectors (guns) are available in the western Riverside County office: two (2) rifles and one pistol-type, all of which are propelled by the use of a CO2 (carbon dioxide) cartridge.

Use single-use disposable darts, which come in assorted volume sizes. The most common will be 2 cc and 3 cc darts. Some reusable darts are available as back-ups; these darts require some practice to assemble correctly.
After discharge, clean the projector and restock the supplies for the next user.
Discard all used darts in a sharps container.
Report any broken, malfunctioning, or missing equipment to supervision.

sections.heading: Public Safety
steps: Conduct chemical immobilization in public areas with great care. Officers may want to explain their intentions to the citizens so as not to alarm them. Ask citizens to leave the area.

Be aware that a dart missing the target animal may hit a bystander or cause damage to property such as a broken window or punctured car tire. The liability for any damages or injuries incurred will be the responsibility of the shooter and the County of Riverside.
Recover and properly discard all expended darts, used drug bottles, needles, and syringes to avoid injury or poisoning.

sections.heading: Officer Safety
steps: Be familiar with basic CPR and first aid and have kits available.

Wear disposable gloves to prevent absorption of drugs through the skin. All immobilization drugs are potentially dangerous to humans. Accidental injection of these drugs may cause disorientation, excitation, personality modifications, respiratory and circulatory depression, coma, cardiac muscle toxicity, and death.

Report any accidental drug poisoning to supervision immediately. Take the patient to a medical facility as soon as possible. It is recommended that the facility be notified of the patient and of the drug used, and that the product literature or drug bottle accompany the patient to the facility, as most human medical institutions have limited knowledge of the pharmacology and effects of the different drugs used in animal immobilization.
Report all injuries to supervision immediately.

sections.heading: Drug: Ketamine/Xylazine
steps: Mix the Ketamine/Xylazine combination (cocktail) at 2 cc of 100 mg/cc Xylazine (Rompun) added to 10 cc of Ketamine. The Ketamine bottle must be labeled after the 2 cc of Xylazine has been mixed in with the Ketamine.
This commonly used cocktail is relatively safe, produces dissociative effects, unconsciousness, and relaxation, and has a long shelf life.

Dose a domestic dog at .6 cc of the cocktail per 10 pounds (example: 40 lb. dog, 40/10 = 4, 4 x .6 cc = 2.4 cc). In this case, use a 3 cc dart and add .6 cc of sterile water to fill the dart and keep it balanced during flight.

sections.heading: Drug: Telazol
steps: Telazol is a dissociative anesthetic and a tranquilizer with FDA approval for both dogs and cats. This tranquilizer has a high capture success rate, a high safety margin, and is effective in many species of animals. It is also more expensive than other available drugs.

Telazol is packaged in a 5 cc bottle containing 500 mg of powder. Reconstitute the drug with sterile water using a syringe and hypodermic needle. The usual concentration is made by adding 5 cc of water to the entire bottle of dry drug, resulting in a strength of 100 mg Telazol per 1 cc of solution.
Telazol can be reconstituted to yield a higher mg per cc solution. If 2.5 cc of water is added to the bottle, a 250 mg per cc concentration is created. This feature allows smaller darts to be used.

The field dosage for a dog using an intramuscular injection of Telazol is 6 mg per pound. Using a 100 mg per cc strength, a 60 lb. dog would require 360 mg of drug, or 3.6 cc. Using a 250 mg per cc strength, the same dog would still require 360 mg but only 1.8 cc of solution.
Calculate drug dosage with the formula: WEIGHT (LB.) x RECOMMENDED DOSAGE (MG/LB) divided by CONCENTRATION (MG/CC) = total drug in cc. Example: 60 lb x 6 mg/lb = 360 mg; 360 mg / 100 mg/cc = 3.6 cc.
The shelf life for Telazol is approximately four (4) days without refrigeration and up to two (2) weeks refrigerated. Write the date of the reconstitution and the concentration on the Telazol bottle.

The induction time (knock-down time) using the intramuscular route (IM) is between 2 and 15 minutes. When given in appropriate dosages, the period of immobilization lasts 30 to 45 minutes, and recovery is usually clean and steady. Increased respiration and salivation are common side effects of Telazol.

sections.heading: Shooter Responsibilities
steps: The shooter is designated as the lead officer in the operation. He or she will stage with the other officers (chasers) to conduct a briefing and create a plan.
Estimate the animal's weight. Measure drugs and load darts.
The area officer will make suggestions as to the habits of the animal and its territory. An evaluation of the capture site will help prepare and plan for an effective capture.
Attempt to set up the shot. Assign tasks such as blocking off escape routes, notifying the public, watching traffic, and positioning officers in vehicles or on foot.
Discuss and implement safety precautions for the officers, the public, and the animal.

sections.heading: Capture
steps: When the dart hits the target animal, remain quiet and preferably out of sight.
Recover the dart. Examine the dart to see if it has discharged and then safely dispose of it in the sharps container.

Watch the path of the target animal. Observe the animal for signs of being under the influence, such as spontaneous urination or rubber legs. If there are no signs after 30 minutes, evaluate the alternatives. The shooter will make a decision on whether or not to re-inject the animal.

If the animal shows signs of being under the influence, the chasers should observe the animal without approaching and causing more stress. One of the secrets of successful darting is not to reveal to the animal from which direction it was darted. If an animal does not know from which direction it is threatened, it will be reluctant to run in any direction and may stay in the area during induction time.

Avoid pursuing or stressing the animal. If the animal is pursued or subjected to stress, it will respond accordingly: the dog may run into traffic, jump off a cliff, fall into water, or harm itself in some other way. Another potentially harmful effect of stress is an increase in body temperature.
When the animal goes down, wait a few minutes and quietly approach. Secure the animal with a control stick.
After it is determined that the animal is anesthetized, apply gentle restraint while ensuring an open airway. Safely and humanely load the animal in the unit.

sections.heading: Post Capture and Animal Care
steps: Examine the animal once it has been loaded onto the vehicle. Note the location of the injection site.
If the injection site is bleeding profusely, apply a pressure bandage if necessary. If the dart has struck an organ or the bleeding cannot be controlled, seek veterinary attention.
Place dogs on their sides with legs extended. Make sure the dog has an open airway.

If the animal is overheated and panting excessively, attempt to cool the animal down and seek medical attention or return to the shelter as soon as possible. Use fans and coolers to help cool the animal. A stressed animal does not cope well with extreme temperatures, and many drugs inhibit the animal's ability to regulate its own temperature. Hyperthermia or heatstroke can be fatal.

related.name: Policy 323, Completion of Controlled Substance Log

appendices: Appendix A: Chemical Immobilization Qualifying Report (attached separately)
Appendix B: Controlled Substance Log (attached separately)
Appendix C: Riverside County Animal Control Immobilization Report (attached separately)

officers before using the immobilization drugs and equipment. the officer must demonstrate an understanding of the drugs, calculations, equipment, and maintaining records, and must accurately and immobilization must be obtained by a licensed veterinarian and used under veterinary supervision. a veterinarian must keep records of all non-narcotic scheduled drugs received or dispensed. the records must be kept for a period of three years and are subject to inspection by the drug enforcement administration. use of immobilization drugs by a layperson without instruction, consultation, or veterinary supervision is illegal. fill out controlled substance logs (attachment b) when any drugs are used or discarded. use immobilization reports (attachment c) for the specific animal each time the immobilization gun is used (see policy 323, completion of controlled substance log). do not leave equipment unattended. when not in use, it must be kept under lock in a secure room or vehicle. take great care to protect the drugs and equipment from theft or misuse. it should not be possible for unauthorized personnel or the public to have access to the equipment or drugs. equipment three (3) different projectors (guns) are available in the western riverside county office: two (2) rifles and one pistol-type, all of which are propelled by the use of a co2 (carbon dioxide) cartridge. use single-use disposable darts, which come in assorted volume sizes. the most common will be 2 cc and 3 cc darts. some reusable darts are available as back-ups; these darts require some practice to assemble correctly. after discharge, clean the projector and restock the supplies for the next user. discard all used darts in a sharps container. report any broken, malfunctioning, or missing equipment to supervision. public safety conduct chemical immobilization in public areas with great care. officers may want to explain their intentions to the citizens so as not to alarm them. ask citizens to leave the area. be aware that a dart missing the target animal may hit a bystander or cause damage to property such as a broken window or punctured car tire. the liability for any damages or injuries incurred will be the responsibility of the shooter and the county of riverside. recover and properly discard all expended darts, used drug bottles, needles, and syringes to avoid injury or poisoning. officer safety be familiar with basic cpr and first aid and have kits available. wear disposable gloves to prevent absorption of drugs through the skin. all immobilization drugs are potentially dangerous to humans. accidental injection of these drugs may cause disorientation, excitation, personality modifications, respiratory and circulatory depression, coma, cardiac muscle toxicity, and death. report any accidental drug poisoning to supervision immediately. take the patient to a medical facility as soon as possible. it is recommended that the facility be notified of the patient and of the drug used, and that the product literature or drug bottle accompany the patient to the facility, as most human medical institutions have limited knowledge of the pharmacology and effects of the different drugs used in animal immobilization. report all injuries to supervision immediately. drug: ketamine/xylazine mix the ketamine/xylazine combination (cocktail) at 2 cc of 100 mg/cc xylazine (rompun) added to 10 cc of ketamine. the ketamine bottle must be labeled after the 2 cc of xylazine has been mixed in with the ketamine. this commonly used cocktail is relatively safe, produces dissociative effects, unconsciousness, and relaxation, and has a long shelf life. dose a domestic dog at .6 cc of the cocktail per 10 pounds (example: 40 lb. dog, 40/10 = 4, 4 x .6 cc = 2.4 cc). in this case, use a 3 cc dart and add .6 cc of sterile water to fill the dart and keep it balanced during flight. drug: telazol telazol is a dissociative anesthetic and a tranquilizer with fda approval for both dogs and cats. this tranquilizer has a high capture success rate, a high safety margin, and is effective in many species of animals. it is also more expensive than other available drugs. telazol is packaged in a 5 cc bottle containing 500 mg of powder. reconstitute the drug with sterile water using a syringe and hypodermic needle. the usual concentration is made by adding 5 cc of water to the entire bottle of dry drug, resulting in a strength of 100 mg telazol per 1 cc of solution. telazol can be reconstituted to yield a higher mg per cc solution. if 2.5 cc of water is added to the bottle, a 250 mg per cc concentration is created. this feature allows smaller darts to be used. the field dosage for a dog using an intramuscular injection of telazol is 6 mg per pound. using a 100 mg per cc strength, a 60 lb. dog would require 360 mg of drug, or 3.6 cc. using a 250 mg per cc strength, the same dog would still require 360 mg but only 1.8 cc of solution. calculate drug dosage with the formula: weight (lb.) x recommended dosage (mg/lb) divided by concentration (mg/cc) = total drug in cc. example: 60 lb x 6 mg/lb = 360 mg; 360 mg / 100 mg/cc = 3.6 cc. the shelf life for telazol is approximately four (4) days without refrigeration and up to two (2) weeks refrigerated. write the date of the reconstitution and the concentration on the telazol bottle. the induction time (knock-down time) using the intramuscular route (im) is between 2 and 15 minutes. when given in appropriate dosages, the period of immobilization lasts 30 to 45 minutes, and recovery is usually clean and steady. increased respiration and salivation are common side effects of telazol. shooter responsibilities the shooter is designated as the lead officer in the operation. he or she will stage with the other officers (chasers) to conduct a briefing and create a plan. estimate the animal's weight. measure drugs and load darts. the area officer will make suggestions as to the habits of the animal and its territory. an evaluation of the capture site will help prepare and plan for an effective capture. attempt to set up the shot. assign tasks such as blocking off escape routes, notifying the public, watching traffic, and positioning officers in vehicles or on foot. discuss and implement safety precautions for the officers, the public, and the animal. capture when the dart hits the target animal, remain quiet and preferably out of sight. recover the dart. examine the dart to see if it has discharged and then safely dispose of it in the sharps container. watch the path of the target animal. observe the animal for signs of being under the influence, such as spontaneous urination or rubber legs. if there are no signs after 30 minutes, evaluate the alternatives. the shooter will make a decision on whether or not to re-inject the animal. if the animal shows signs of being under the influence, the chasers should observe the animal without approaching and causing more stress. one of the secrets of successful darting is not to reveal to the animal from which direction it was darted. if an animal does not know from which direction it is threatened, it will be reluctant to run in any direction and may stay in the area during induction time. avoid pursuing or stressing the animal. if the animal is pursued or subjected to stress, it will respond accordingly: the dog may run into traffic, jump off a cliff, fall into water, or harm itself in some other way. another potentially harmful effect of stress is an increase in body temperature. when the animal goes down, wait a few minutes and quietly approach. secure the animal with a control stick. after it is determined that the animal is anesthetized, apply gentle restraint while ensuring an open airway. safely and humanely load the animal in the unit. post capture and animal care examine the animal once it has been loaded onto the vehicle. note the location of the injection site. if the injection site is bleeding profusely, apply a pressure bandage if necessary. if the dart has struck an organ or the bleeding cannot be controlled, seek veterinary attention. place dogs on their sides with legs extended. make sure the dog has an open airway. if the animal is overheated and panting excessively, attempt to cool the animal down and seek medical attention or return to the shelter as soon as possible. use fans and coolers to help cool the animal. a stressed animal does not cope well with extreme temperatures, and many drugs inhibit the animal's ability to regulate its own temperature. hyperthermia or heatstroke can be fatal. california penal code 832 (firearms certification) department of animal services policy 323, completion of controlled substance log

documents.title: Heat Related Illnesses in Animals
documents.purpose: The humane care and treatment of any animal encountered in the field or shelter is the principal concern and responsibility of all Department of Animal Services employees. It is important for all personnel to understand the causes, signs, prevention, and first aid treatment of heat related illnesses, or hyperthermia.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: Applicable to all Department of Animal Services employees and volunteers.
documents.supersedes: Policy 200-28, effective October 2009 (revised from February 4, 2005)

definitions.def: The rise in body temperature above normal value for that species. Prolonged increases in temperature may cause temporary or permanent damage, including death.

sections.heading: Causes of Heat Related Illnesses (Hyperthermia)
steps: Understand that, unlike humans, dogs and cats cannot cool themselves by sweating through their skin. A dog's and cat's most efficient method of cooling is by panting, which takes place in the nose where there are many folds of tissue that provide a large surface area for cooling. This tissue actually perspires and has a large blood supply, thereby acting much like the radiator in a car. Dogs' and cats' only other sweat glands are located in the pads of their feet.

Heat related illnesses occur when the animal gets severely overheated, most commonly due to overexertion, excitation, high air temperatures, lack of access to water, or a combination. Other factors predisposing certain animals are age, weight, breed, and some diseases.

sections.heading: Signs and Symptoms
steps: Recognize the symptoms of heat stress in dogs and cats: heavy panting, difficulty breathing, glazed eyes, rapid pulse, unsteady or staggering gait, bright red gum color, rectal temperatures above 103.5 degrees Fahrenheit, excessive salivation, vomiting, or a deep red or purple swollen tongue.

Untreated, heat stress develops into heatstroke or exhaustion. The signs of heat stroke may include body temperatures above 105 degrees F, convulsions, labored breathing, collapse, shock, seizures, coma, and death.

sections.heading: Prevention
steps: Any employee, while on duty, noticing an animal in distress or with the potential to become heat stressed, such as being left unattended in a vehicle, has an obligation to attempt to resolve the problem. It is imperative that the employee notify an Animal Control Officer or supervisor with their concerns immediately.

All employees driving an animal carrying vehicle are to check their truck's air conditioning system as part of their daily vehicle inspection to ensure that the cooling unit is working properly. If, upon inspection, the unit is not properly functioning, the employee is to bring it to their immediate supervisor's attention to seek direction on how to proceed.

Start the prevention process by determining options and choosing the most effective and safest method of impounding the animal for the sake of the public, the officer, and the animal. Pursuing a frightened animal and placing it in an enclosed vehicle on a warm day can quickly cause it to suffer brain damage or die from heat stroke in a short amount of time.

After the animal has been confined, evaluate its condition to determine if veterinary treatment, returning to the shelter, or carrying the animal in the truck is the appropriate action. Any Animal Control Officer who believes returning to the shelter and unloading is in the best interest of their animals should do so after notifying the Senior Animal Control Officer or designee.

Operate compartment air conditioners when the ambient temperature in the compartments or outdoors exceeds 80 degrees or when any animal appears stressed from overheating. A good rule of thumb is that if you are using the air conditioner in the cab, you should have it on for the animals.

Monitor animals under your control at least every 30 minutes when transporting animals in a county vehicle, and make every attempt to park in the shade whenever possible. Personnel that are required to leave animals unattended in the units for extended periods of time may choose to leave the engine running, air conditioner on, and doors locked. If this method is chosen, the truck's hood must be kept open to help dissipate the heat and the driver must carry two (2) sets of keys.

sections.heading: Treatment
steps: Any front counter employee noticing an animal in distress is to notify kennel staff as soon as possible. Kennel staff will transport the animal to the receiving area and take the necessary precautions to reduce the trauma to the animal.

Provide animals in the receiving area showing signs of heat stress with water immediately. Kennel Attendants or Animal Control Officers who determine that an animal is in severe distress are to notify their supervisor or the veterinary staff immediately. Kennel Attendants and Animal Control Officers will start first aid to treat the hyperthermia.

steps.step: Administer first aid treatment of hyperthermia in animals as follows.
sub: Take the animal's initial body temperature.
Move the animal to a cooler location.
Provide drinking water.
Spray down or bathe in cool water.
Turn on a fan.
Apply ice packs to head, neck, feet, chest, or abdomen for short periods of time.
Monitor body temperature and stop cooling treatment when temperature reaches 104 degrees F.

steps: If available, the veterinary staff will decide how best to treat the animal. In addition to the first aid steps mentioned above, treatment with intravenous fluids, medications, or cold water enemas may be necessary.

The veterinary staff will take the animal's rectal temperature to determine if the animal is suffering from heat stress or stroke and to provide an initial starting point to monitor the animal's progression through treatment. The periodic monitoring of the temperature helps to determine when to stop applying agents to reduce the temperature and ensure the temperature does not drop below normal.
If the veterinary staff is not available, or if they decide that an outside veterinarian is more appropriate, transport the animal, after first aid has been initiated, to the nearest veterinarian.

sections.heading: Summary
steps: The Department of Animal Services' employees are charged with protecting animals in their care from stress and danger. In the event of hyperthermia, the shelter and field staff will notify supervision, administer first aid, and seek veterinary care when appropriate.

revisions.author: Outcomes for Pets (draft)
revisions.desc: Migrated into standard RCDAS SOP template. Content unchanged from 2009 version; substantive edits pending (see edit log).

documents.title: Use of Bite Sticks
documents.purpose: To create a uniform policy of why, when, and how to use the bite stick.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: This procedure is applicable to all Animal Services employees trained to carry a bite stick.
documents.supersedes: Policy 200-32, effective October 7, 2009 (revised from April 1, 2007)

sections.heading: Method
steps: Use bite sticks as a defensive tool to provide an Animal Control Officer with non-lethal force for dealing with aggressive animals.

Animal Control Officers and License Inspectors frequently encounter hostile or aggressive animals with little or no notice of an impending attack. If the officer is faced with a charging or aggressive animal and other options are not available, the bite stick may be deployed.
No Animal Control Officer or License Inspector is permitted to carry or use a bite stick without proper animal related training.

Wear the bite stick in its holster on the opposite hip of the officer's strong side (left hip if right-handed). When the stick is drawn from the holster in a quick motion across the body, the stick extends and locks in place with a loud noise.

sections.heading: Progressive Escalation of Defense
steps: Often, the noise from the deployment of the bite stick is enough to startle the dog and stop the attack, allowing time to retreat.
The dog can be kept at a distance by holding the stick between the officer and the dog, allowing an opportunity for retreat.
The dog can bite the stick, allowing an opportunity for retreat.

In rare instances, the dog may pass by the stick to get to the officer. In this situation, the officer may strike the dog in the sensitive muzzle area to defend him/herself in order to retreat and to call for assistance without placing their life in imminent danger.
The bite stick is a defensive tool and is never to be used to punish the animal.

sections.heading: Reporting
steps: If an officer strikes an animal with their bite stick and the animal is impounded, the officer must have the animal examined by a veterinarian.
Complete a Use of Bite Stick report and notify the senior/supervisor.

related.name: Use of Bite Stick report (Attachment A)

appendices: Appendix A: Use of Bite Stick report

documents.title: Field Euthanasia Policy
documents.purpose: To assist staff in determining if euthanasia is the proper course of action when impounding animals in need.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services. Field euthanasia under Penal Code 597f(d) requires the approval of the officer's immediate superior.
documents.scope: Applicable to all Department of Animal Services (DAS) staff that may be responsible for performing euthanasia in the field.
documents.supersedes: Policy 200-33, effective October 7, 2009 (revised from September 1, 2009)
references: California Code of Regulations, Title 16, 2039, Sodium Pentobarbital/Euthanasia Training
California Business and Professions Code 4827(d), Practice Exceptions
California Penal Code 597f(d), Permitting Animals to Go Without Care
California Food and Agricultural Code 17005, State Euthanasia Policy
California Food and Agricultural Code 17006, Irremediable Serious Illness or Injury; Newborn Needing Maternal Care
Department of Animal Services Policy 402, Euthanasia Policy

sections.heading: Policy
steps: Complete the state mandated course as defined in the California Code of Regulations, Title 16, 2039, Sodium Pentobarbital/Euthanasia Training. All DAS field staff involved in the euthanasia process are required to complete this training. With this training, California's Business and Professions Code 4827, Practice Exceptions, (d) allows for the administering of sodium pentobarbital for euthanasia by an Animal Services employee without the presence of a veterinarian.

Animal Control Officers are allowed to perform euthanasia as noted under California Penal Code 597f, Permitting Animals to Go Without Care, (d), which states: "Notwithstanding any other provision of this section, any officer of a pound or animal regulation department or humane society, or any officer of a police or sheriff's department may, with the approval of his or her immediate superior, humanely destroy any abandoned animal in the field in any case where the animal is too severely injured to move or where a veterinarian is not available and it would be more humane to dispose of the animal."
Adhere to any other applicable Federal and State laws, specifically Food and Agricultural Code 17005, State Euthanasia Policy, which in part states, "No adoptable animal should be euthanized."
Reference and abide by the Department of Animal Services Policy 402, Euthanasia Policy, when determining whether euthanasia should be performed.

steps.step: Seriously ill or severe injury.
sub: California Food and Agriculture Code 17006, Irremediable Serious Illness or Injury; Newborn Needing Maternal Care, states in part, "Animals that are irremediably suffering from a serious illness or severe injury shall not be held for owner redemption or adoption."
The Animal Control Officer must determine if the animal meets these requirements before euthanasia or transport to a DAS shelter with a veterinarian on premises or to a local veterinary hospital.

steps.step: Too young.
sub: California Food and Agriculture Code 17006 states in part, "Newborn animals that need maternal care and have been impounded without their mothers may be euthanized without being held for owner redemption or adoption." Also, Food and Agriculture Code 17005, State Euthanasia Policy, (a), states in part, "Adoptable animals include only those animals eight weeks of age."

The Animal Control Officer must determine the age of the animal prior to contemplating euthanasia. If the officer cannot determine age, the animal should be assessed by a DAS staff member who may be more experienced or comfortable aging the animal.

steps.step: Owner request for impound and euthanasia.
sub: Owners may sign a written request to have their animal humanely euthanized. There is a fee for this service and those animals shall be euthanized immediately.

All animals accepted under this service must have a medical or behavioral condition. Such requests that involve questionable circumstances, an inadequate reason, or an adoptable animal will be referred to a private veterinarian (Policy 402).

Food and Agriculture Code 17005, State Euthanasia Policy, (a), states in part, "Adoptable animals include only those animals eight weeks of age or older that, at or subsequent to the time the animals is impounded or otherwise taken into possession, have manifested no sign of behavioral or temperamental defect" or "have manifested no sign of disease, injury, or congenital or hereditary condition."
The Animal Control Officer must determine the animal to be too young, ill, or have a behavioral problem before advising the owner that euthanasia may be performed.

documents.title: Use of Force - General
documents.purpose: The use of force by law enforcement personnel is a matter of critical concern to the public and the law enforcement community. Animal Control Officers are in constant contact with aggressive and injured animals, loose livestock, and members of the public who can react violently and unpredictably to situations involving their animals. This policy establishes the limitations on officer authority and the careful balancing of the rights of animals and human beings required when force is used to protect the public welfare.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: Applicable to all Department of Animal Services Animal Control Officers and staff that use Department approved weapons.
documents.supersedes: Policy 200-04 (replaced in its entirety). The Use of Force Report form in this policy replaces the report forms used in Policies 200-02, 200-27, 200-31, and 200-32. Effective date of source: November 12, 2009.
references: California Penal Code Section 830.9 (Animal Control Officer arrest and warrant powers)
California Penal Code Section 832 (training course)
California Penal Code Section 836 (arrest authority)
California Penal Code Sections 1523 and 1530 (search warrants)
California Penal Code Section 834a (duty to refrain from resisting arrest)
Fourth Amendment, United States Constitution
DAS Policy 200-02 (Chemical Irritant)
DAS Policy 200-27 (Lethal Force/Firearms)
DAS Policy 200-31 (Electrical Control Devices)
DAS Policy 200-32 (Intermediate Weapons)

definitions.def: Includes capture guns, blowguns, carbon dioxide operated rifles and pistols, handguns, rifles, and shotguns.
The use of force is of three types: (1) mental, psychological, or verbal; (2) physical; (3) deadly or lethal.

sections.heading: Policy
steps: Comply with applicable California and federal law in all uses of force. California Penal Code Section 830.9 states that Animal Control Officers are not Peace Officers but may exercise the powers of arrest of a Peace Officer specified in Section 836 and the power to serve warrants as specified in Sections 1523 and 1530 during the course and within the scope of their employment, if those officers successfully complete a course in the exercise of those powers pursuant to Section 832. The part of the training course specified in Section 832 pertaining to the carry and use of firearms shall not be required for any Animal Control Officer whose employing agency prohibits the use of firearms.
Treat the use of force or firearms/weapons as defensive in nature, to be used only when necessary and justified.

Exercise good judgment at all times when force is necessary. Each situation explicitly requires the use of force to be reasonable, and only that force which reasonably appears to be necessary may be used to gain control or resist attack.

Do not use physical force based solely on verbal threats of violence, verbal abuse, or hesitancy by the person in following commands. These do not, in and of themselves, justify the use of physical force without additional facts or circumstances which, taken together, pose a threat of harm to the Animal Control Officer or others.

Understand that unreasonable application of physical force on a person is a violation of California and federal law which may result in criminal prosecution and/or civil liability for the Officer. Unreasonable application of physical force on an animal is a violation of California Humane Law which may result in criminal prosecution and/or civil liability for the Officer. A violation of the Department's use of force policy may also subject the Animal Control Officer to Departmental discipline.

Apply the standard for determining whether force was reasonable: that conduct which a reasonable Animal Control Officer would exercise based upon the information the Officer had when the conduct occurred.

Pay careful attention to the facts and circumstances of each particular case, including the severity of the issue and whether the animal or person poses an immediate threat to the safety of the Officers or others. Use the most appropriate force option given the circumstances, taking into account the situation facing the Officer as well as his/her training and experience.

sections.heading: Escalation and De-Escalation of Force
steps: Attempt to control an animal or person through advice, warning, or persuasion, but be prepared for the use of physical force. The primary objective is to ensure control with such force as is objectively reasonable under the circumstances.
Escalate or de-escalate to the amount of force which reasonably appears to be necessary to overcome the resistance and to gain control when physical force is applied.

steps.step: Apply the three key points regarding escalation and de-escalation of physical force:
sub: Physical force is used to control the animal or person.

Whenever force is used, the Animal Control Officer's defensive reactions must be in response to the animal's or person's actions. Note: this does not mean an Officer has to wait until attacked. Based on the circumstances, an Animal Control Officer may be justified in using reasonable force to prevent an attack.
An Animal Control Officer may use only the amount of force which reasonably appears to be necessary to control the animal or person.

steps: Act within the range of conduct identified as reasonable. The Fourth Amendment requires that officers use only such force as is objectively reasonable under the circumstances; officers need not avail themselves of the least intrusive means of responding to an exigent situation.

sections.heading: Use of Force Techniques: Five Approved Levels

steps.step: Level 1, Presence: Rely on the mere presence of a uniformed Animal Control Officer, coupled with good verbal communication, to gain willful submission and avoid further escalation of force.
sub: California Penal Code Section 834a states that if a person has knowledge, or by the exercise of reasonable care should have knowledge, that they are being arrested by a Peace Officer, it is the duty of such person to refrain from using force or any weapon to resist such arrest.

steps.step: Level 2, Verbalization: Use advising, warning, or persuading, the most commonly used technique to effect the arrest of a person. Techniques include:
sub: Explaining any actions about to be taken.
Allowing a person to save face in front of his/her peers.
Recognizing a person's remarks are not a personal attack against the Officer, and allowing a person to retain dignity whenever possible.
Attempt to de-escalate confrontations by utilizing verbalization techniques prior to, during, and after any use of physical force.

steps.step: Level 3, Chemical Irritant/Electrical Control Devices (see DAS Policies 200-02 and 200-31): Use chemical irritant to overcome and control an animal or person's aggressive actions when verbalization is unsuccessful.
sub: Verbal threats of violence by a person do not alone justify the use of chemical irritants.

Chemical irritant may be used if the Animal Control Officer reasonably believes that it would be unsafe to approach and control the animal or person, or when it is tactically unwise to entangle with the animal or person and it is desirous to maintain a distance.

The only departmentally approved Electrical Control Device is the Taser, a non-lethal control device which may be used to control aggressive animals or potentially violent people when the Officer reasonably believes that deadly force does not appear to be justifiable and/or necessary, and there is a reasonable expectation that it will be unsafe for Officers to approach and place themselves within range of the animal or person.

steps.step: Level 4, Intermediate Weapons (see DAS Policy 200-32): Use impact weapons such as the expandable baton or flashlight to immediately impede the threatening actions of an aggressive animal or person.
sub: The baton may be appropriately displayed as a show of force if verbalization techniques appear ineffective. A decision to draw or exhibit a baton must be based on the tactical situation, for example when entering a location of prior aggressive animal or caution person calls, or where there is an escalating risk to Officer safety.
Use the baton for striking movements and blocks if the situation continues to escalate.

The flashlight is an alternative impact weapon. While not preferred over the baton in most situations, it is usually readily available, especially at night, and may be appropriate when the baton is not accessible or too cumbersome.
Use caution to avoid striking areas such as the head, throat, neck, spine, or groin, which may cause serious injury to the person.

steps.step: Level 5, Lethal Force (see DAS Policy 200-27): Escalate to lethal force only as a last resort dictated by the actions of an animal or person where the Officer has reasonable cause to believe the animal or person poses a significant threat of death or serious physical injury to the Officer or others.
sub: The weapon of choice is generally one of the various departmentally approved firearms; however, Officers may use any reasonable means to protect themselves or other persons from an immediate and significant threat of death or serious physical injury.
Where the Officer has reasonable cause to believe the animal or person poses a threat of serious physical harm to the Officer or others, it is reasonable to prevent escape by using lethal force.

The use of less lethal munitions is neither encouraged nor discouraged in deadly force situations. Evaluate each situation by the facts and circumstances. Less lethal force should not be considered a substitute for deadly force in lethal situations.

sections.heading: Use of Firearms
steps: Use firearms only when an Animal Control Officer believes his/her life or the life of another is in imminent danger, or in danger of great bodily harm, or when all other reasonable means of apprehension have failed to prevent the escape of a dangerous animal or felony suspect whom the Officer has reason to believe presents a serious danger to others, where the felonious conduct includes the use or threatened use of deadly force.
Draw a firearm only when there is likelihood of danger to the Officer or other persons.

steps.step: Do not discharge a firearm or use any other type of deadly force in the performance of duties except under the following circumstances:
sub: In the necessary defense of himself/herself or any other person who is in imminent danger of death or great bodily harm.
Where the officer has reasonable cause to believe that the person poses a threat of serious physical harm, either to the officer or to others.
To kill a dangerous animal that is attacking the officer or another person or persons, or which if allowed to escape presents a danger to the public.
To immobilize an animal that poses a threat to public safety.
When humanity requires the destruction of an animal to save it from further suffering, and other disposition is not possible.
For target practice at an approved range or in unrestricted areas.
To give an alarm or call assistance for an important purpose when no other means are available.

steps.step: Observe the following prohibitions on display and discharge of firearms:
sub: Do not display or draw firearms in any public place except for inspection or use, and do not handle weapons in a careless manner which could result in an accidental discharge.
Do not discharge a firearm as a warning shot.
Generally, do not discharge a firearm at or from a moving vehicle unless in the necessary defense of himself/herself or any other person who is in imminent danger of death or great bodily harm.

sections.heading: Overview of Techniques
steps: Act in self defense using one or more of the approved control techniques within approved use of force standards when an animal or person physically attacks an Officer.

De-escalate as resistance declines. For example, if a person assumes a clenched-fists fighting stance and the Officer counters by drawing the baton as a show of force, and the person then drops his hands and resumes a normal posture, the Officer should proceed with extreme caution, maintain an advantageous position, ensure no additional threat exists, and de-escalate all the way back to verbalization: advising, warning, and persuading.

Escalate the degree of force needed to maintain control of the situation when the amount of force used by a person increases. An Officer is permitted by law not only to use the level of force used by the suspect but to use reasonable force to overcome the resistance.
Recognize that the reasonable amount of force needed to control an animal or person may vary from one Officer to another.

sections.heading: Situation-Based Use of Force Continuum
steps: Apply the continuum with flexibility. The standard for evaluating an Officer's use of force is reasonableness under the facts and circumstances known to the Officer at the time. The Department recognizes Officers are expected to make split-second decisions, and the amount of time available to evaluate and respond may impact those decisions.

steps.step: Consider factors that may affect force option selection, including but not limited to:
sub: Officer/subject factors (age, size, relative strength, skill level, injury/exhaustion, number of Officers versus number of animals or people).
Influence of drugs or alcohol.
Proximity to weapons.
Availability of other options.
Seriousness of the offense in question.
Other exigent circumstances.

steps: Understand that an Officer need not attempt to gain control by use of the lowest level of force on the continuum when reason dictates and the Officer can articulate that a higher level of force is reasonable. Skipping steps may be appropriate given the resistance encountered. The continuum should be viewed as an elevator, not a ladder: an Officer may go directly to any level provided the force selected is reasonable.

sections.heading: Mental Attitude
steps: Recognize that emotional involvement is a factor in the escalation or de-escalation of force. In order to react to every situation with the reasonable amount of force, an Officer must be in good physical condition, possess self defense and verbalization skills, and have a mature, professional attitude.
Maintain self confidence in training and the ability to control the situation.

steps.step: Complete the following whenever involved in a reportable use of force incident or after discharging a firearm, Taser, or chemical irritant control device for any reason other than an approved training exercise:
sub: Summon medical aid, as needed.
Immediately notify a supervisor of involvement in a use of force incident.
Call the Police or Sheriff's Department immediately if the force used falls within Level 5 and/or results in death or serious likelihood of death to a person.
Use a Department Use of Force form to report the full details of the use of force incident.

sections.heading: Supervisor Responsibilities
steps: Confirm medical aid has been summoned, as needed.
Respond to the scene, independently investigate the use of force, and make a report of the incident.
Notify the Police/Sheriff and Director immediately if the force used falls within Level 5 and/or results in death or serious likelihood of death to a person.

Take photographs in all reportable use of force incidents that result in an injury or a complaint of injury. Take care to protect the subject's personal privacy interests; discuss any possible concerns with a field supervisor prior to taking the photographs.

steps.step: Report the incident as follows:
sub: Ensure a Use of Force Report form is completed by the Animal Control Officer and checked by the supervisor within twenty four (24) hours and forwarded to the Director/Deputy Director/Commander.

The Use of Force Report form is sufficient documentation when the force used did not result in an injury or complaint of injury. A simple complaint of pain, without evidence of underlying injury, may properly be documented on the Use of Force Report form.
Complete a separate Use of Force Report form for each subject or animal upon whom force was used. Each report shall include the force levels used by each officer involved in the incident.

steps.step: Complete a Use of Force Investigation report within ten (10) days to supplement the Use of Force Report form and forward it to the Director/Deputy Director when:
sub: The force used was the direct cause of injury or complaint of injury, beyond a simple complaint of pain, on a person.
The force used involved the application of chemical irritant, electrical control device, baton, or similar control technique/device on a person.
The force used falls within Level 5, with the exceptions of items listed in Level 5, Lethal Force, subsection 2, (d) and (e).

steps: The Commander/Captain shall prepare all administrative reports of incidents wherein the force used falls within Level 5 and/or death or serious likelihood of death results. Sergeants/Lieutenants shall not prepare any administrative reports of such incidents unless directed by the Director/Deputy Director/Commander.

sections.heading: Conclusion
steps: The decision to use physical force places a tremendous responsibility on the Animal Control Officer. Ideally, all situations would require only verbalization. While the control of an aggressive animal or person through advice, warning, or persuasion is preferable, the use of physical force is sometimes unavoidable. Officers must be able to escalate or de-escalate the amount of force which reasonably appears to be necessary to control a situation as the resistance increases or decreases. Force should only be used as a reasonable means to secure control.

appendices: Appendix: Use of Force Report form (attached separately; source PDF includes form pages, revised 12/7/2009)

revisions.author: RCDAS
revisions.desc: Original policy. Replaced Policy 200-04 in its entirety; Use of Force Report form replaced report forms in Policies 200-02, 200-27, 200-31, and 200-32.

revisions.author: Outcomes for Pets (draft)
revisions.desc: Migrated into standard RCDAS SOP template. Content unchanged from 2009 version; substantive edits pending (see edit log).

documents.title: Issuance of Sentry-Guard-Attack Dog Business Permit
documents.purpose: To establish a uniform procedure for accepting applications and issuing Sentry/Guard/Attack Dog Business Permits as required by California Health and Safety Code Section 121916.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: Applies to all staff involved in the investigation and issuance of the Sentry/Guard/Attack Dog Business Permits.
documents.supersedes: None identified. Effective date of source: July 20, 2010.
references: California Health and Safety Code Section 121916 (permit requirement)
California Health and Safety Code Sections 121880, 121881, 121885, 121890 (definitions)
California Health and Safety Code Section 121917 (application procedure)
California Health and Safety Code Section 121918 (standards)
California Health and Safety Code Section 121919 (suspension and revocation)
California Health and Safety Code Sections 121920, 121921, 121925, 121930, 121935 (requirements)
California Health and Safety Code Sections 121940, 121945 (violations and penalties)
The Dog Act of 1969, Health and Safety Code Sections 121875 et seq.
Business and Professions Code Chapter 11.5 (commencing with Section 7580) of Division 3 (private patrol operators)

definitions.def: Any dog trained to work without supervision in a fenced facility and to deter or detain unauthorized persons found within the facility.

Any dog trained to guard, protect, patrol, or defend any premises or area, or any dog trained as a sentry or to protect, defend, or guard any person or property, or any dog such as a schutzhund or any similar classification.
A dog trained to locate narcotics by scent.
A dog trained to work with a handler in searching facilities for burglary suspects and other intruders.

sections.heading: Findings (H&S 121916)
steps: Any person or owner of an attack, guard, or sentry dog that operates or maintains a business to sell, rent, or train an attack, guard, or sentry dog shall obtain a permit from the local public agency or private society or pound contracting with the local public agency for animal care or protection services.

Each local agency shall adopt and implement a permit program for the administration of subdivision (a). A local agency may charge a fee for the issuance or renewal of a permit required under this section. The fee shall not exceed the actual costs for the implementation of the permit program.
For purposes of this section, local public agency means a city, county, or city and county.

sections.heading: Application Procedure (H&S 121917)
steps: Require the applicant to furnish the Department of Animal Services (DAS) with a list of the types of animals to be kept or used for any purpose, with the estimated maximum number of animals to be kept.
Require the applicant to furnish DAS with the name and telephone number of a responsible person who has access to the animals and who can be reached during an emergency.
Require the applicant to notify DAS when any animal for which a permit is required is kept or maintained.
DAS may establish the maximum number of animals to be kept or maintained on the premises.
Require any permittee to report in writing any change in address, ownership, or management to DAS at least 15 days prior to any change.

Require any permittee to maintain a register of the name and address of any person from whom any animal is received and to whom any animal is sold, traded, or given. This list shall be available to a DAS representative upon demand.

sections.heading: Requirements the Reviewing DAS Staff Member Must Ensure Are Met

steps.step: Identification (H&S 121920): Verify that the owner or trainer of any attack, guard, or sentry dog has ensured the dog is microchipped and the owner's identification has been entered into a local or national registry. Each dog subject to this chapter shall at all times wear an identification tag provided by the attack, guard, or sentry dog company furnishing the dog for hire. The identification tag shall contain, but not be limited to, the following information:
sub: The name of the dog.
The name, address, and telephone number of the attack, guard, or sentry dog company furnishing the dog for hire.
Any telephone number so provided shall be to a telephone that is manned by a person 24 hours per day every day of the year so that calls from the public may be received and answered.
The identification tag required by this section shall be in addition to any tag required or issued by any agency of government to show that a dog has been immunized or inoculated against disease.

steps.step: Vaccinations (H&S 121921): Verify that no person sells, gives away, or lets for hire any guard, attack, or sentry dog unless the following requirements have been met:
sub: The dog has been immunized against distemper and rabies.
A certificate of rabies vaccination has been issued by a licensed veterinarian and is current and valid.

steps: Humane Transportation (H&S 121925): Verify that whenever a dog subject to this chapter is being transported anywhere, it is well secured in a humane manner as will reasonably prevent its possible escape.

Visit by Dog Handler (H&S 121930): Verify that each dog subject to this chapter, wherever and whenever the dog is kept when on actual duty, is visited by a dog handler at least once every 12 hours to insure that the dog's physical condition, its surroundings, and its food and water supply are adequate, and if inadequate, the dog handler shall do whatever may be necessary to correct or remedy the situation. The dog handler shall be either the owner of, or be employed by or under contract to, the sentry dog company that placed the dog on assignment.

steps.step: Keeping of Sentry Dogs, Guard Dogs, Attack Dogs, or Trackers (H&S 121935):
sub: No person shall take a sentry dog or a tracker or attack dog into, or keep one in, any portion of any business establishment that is open to the general public, unless the dog is accompanied or kept by a dog handler.

No person shall keep any sentry dog or tracker or attack dog in any business establishment or any other place open to the general public at any time unless there is posted at every entrance a sign of sufficient size and design to warn persons that such a dog is used at the business establishment or place.

This section does not apply to dogs used and accompanied by peace officers or uniformed employees of private patrol operators and operators of a private patrol service who are licensed pursuant to Chapter 11.5 (commencing with Section 7580) of Division 3 of the Business and Professions Code, while employees are acting within the course and scope of their employment as private patrolmen.
This section does not apply to any dog handler or his or her dog while training the dog or another dog handler.

sections.heading: Violations (H&S 121940)
steps: Except as otherwise specified in this chapter, any person violating any provision of this chapter, other than Section 121945, shall be subject to a civil penalty of up to one thousand dollars ($1,000) per violation. The action may be prosecuted in the name of the people of the State of California by the district attorney for the county in which the violation occurred, or by the city attorney in the city in which the violation occurred, in the appropriate court.

sections.heading: Penalties (H&S 121945)
steps: In lieu of the civil penalties imposed pursuant to Section 121940, any person or owner who violates The Dog Act of 1969 (Health and Safety Code Sections 121875 et seq.) shall be subject to a civil penalty of up to one thousand dollars ($1,000), or shall be prohibited from selling, renting, leasing, or training any attack, guard, or sentry dog for up to 30 days, or both.

For a second offense, the person or owner shall be subject to a civil penalty of up to two thousand five hundred dollars ($2,500), or a prohibition from selling, renting, leasing, or training any attack, guard, or sentry dog for up to 90 days, or both.

For a third offense, the person or owner shall be subject to a civil penalty of up to five thousand dollars ($5,000), or a prohibition from selling, renting, leasing, or training any attack, guard, or sentry dog for up to six months, or both.

For a fourth or any subsequent offense, the person or owner shall be subject to a civil penalty of up to ten thousand dollars ($10,000), or a prohibition from selling, renting, leasing, or training any attack, guard, or sentry dog for up to one year, or both.

For purposes of this section, a violation that occurred over five years prior to the most recent violation shall not be considered. An action for recovery of the civil penalty and for a court order enjoining a person or owner from engaging in the business for the period set forth in this section may be prosecuted by the district attorney for the county where the violation occurred, or the city attorney for the city where the violation occurred, in the appropriate court.

steps.step: DAS may suspend or revoke a permit issued under this chapter if DAS determines that the permittee has done any of the following:
sub: Made any false statement or given any false information in connection with an application for a license or a renewal or reinstatement thereof.
Violated any provisions of this chapter.
Violated any rule of an ordinance adopted pursuant to the authority contained in this chapter.
Committed any other act that would be grounds for denial of a license.

documents.title: Completion of the Euthanasia Log
documents.flag: Numbered (=Policy per rule) but reads as a step-by-step protocol
documents.purpose: To establish the procedure for completing the Field Euthanasia Log, which accounts for euthanasia solution (Sodium Pentobarbital) from the point at which it is issued to the Animal Control Officer to the time, location, and amount administered.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services. The Chief Veterinarian is responsible for every vial of euthanasia solution possessed by the Department of Animal Services.
documents.scope: All County of Riverside Animal Control Officers who use Sodium Pentobarbital for euthanasia purposes.
documents.supersedes: Prior version of Policy 200-36 effective July 1, 2006 (prepared May 2006 by the Chief Veterinarian). Current source effective September 23, 2013.
references: Drug Enforcement Agency (DEA) controlled substance requirements, as cited in the source document
Field Euthanasia Log (CS9), Attachment A
Controlled Substance Flow Chart, Attachment B

definitions.def: Sodium Pentobarbital (dispensed as Fatal-Plus), a controlled substance under DEA jurisdiction. The DEA maintains a system of monitoring from production through storage and use, as well as punishment for non-compliance.

sections.heading: When to Use
steps: Issue a Field Euthanasia Log (Attachment A) each time an Animal Control Officer receives a bottle of euthanasia solution (Fatal-Plus), regardless of the amount of solution.
Ensure the Euthanasia Log reflects the bottle number from which the solution was dispensed as well as the starting volume.

sections.heading: How to Complete the Euthanasia Log
steps: Issued to: Enter the name of the employee to whom the solution is issued.
Date Issued: Enter the date that the solution was issued to the employee.
Bottle #: Enter the unique number assigned to the vial of euthanasia solution.
Issued By: Be certain that the name of the issuing Veterinarian or RVT is present.
Beginning Amount: Enter the volume of solution which is present at the time it is issued.
Amount Used: Enter the total volume in cc's which was administered to the animal.
Ending Amount: Enter the ending amount of solution. This number should be the difference between the starting amount and the quantity used.
Date: Enter the date that the specific euthanasia was performed.
Time: Enter the time of day the euthanasia solution was used.
Impound #: Enter the Chameleon animal ID of the euthanized animal.
Description: Note the age, breed, and sex of the animal(s).
Condition: Identify, briefly, the reason for euthanasia, i.e. HBC, newborn without dam, etc.
Date and Amount Returned: Complete when the vial is returned to the Veterinarian.

sections.heading: Discarded or Lost Solution
steps: Log the information on the Euthanasia Log by following items 5 through 12 above should there be a need to discard any solution or if solution is lost. ALL SOLUTION MUST BE ACCOUNTED FOR.
Obtain the initials of a witness to document and verify any discarded solution.

related.name: Animal ID (impound number of euthanized animal)

appendices: Appendix: 200-36 Attachment A Field Euthanasia Log (attached separately)
Appendix: 200-36 Attachment B Controlled Substance Flow Chart (attached separately)

documents.title: Fence Fixing Program
documents.purpose: To establish a procedure to provide resources and record support provided through fence fixing initiatives.
documents.authority: Not stated in source draft.
documents.scope: DAS shelter and field staff who provide fence-fixing supplies to the public (implied by source; not explicitly stated).
documents.supersedes: None. Drafted for future consideration; program not currently implemented.
references: References not completed in source draft (placeholder: insert relevant policies or codes)

sections.heading: Offering Fence-Fixing Support
steps: Offer solutions to resolve the issue to all individuals who have lost an animal due to a fence-related issue and all individuals who directly request fence-fixing support from DAS staff. Solutions offered may vary by DAS location and available resources.
Provide individuals with supplies.

sections.heading: Supplies

steps.step: Supply list:
sub: Dig defenders
Tie-out trolleys (two securing ends with a connecting cable/rope)
Post anchors
Paracord
Zip ties
Other

steps: Store supplies at individual shelter locations and, as space allows, in field vehicles.
Provide supplies on a case-by-case basis as supplies allow. Specific supplies cannot be guaranteed to the public at specific locations or times.
Order supplies when specific items run low. (Source draft leaves the responsible team/supervisor as a placeholder.)

steps.step: In-shelter:
sub: Fill out the Pet Support form indicating fence repair supplies were provided to an individual.
Include the client's phone number.
Include how many animals they own that are impacted by providing this service (i.e. will not be able to escape the fence or get lost).

steps.step: In the field:
sub: Not resolved in source draft. The draft notes: Field Services does not currently fill out Pet Support forms, and asks whether this should become part of their process for consistency.
