documents.title: Duties of the Shelter Veterinarian
documents.purpose: It is the duty of the Shelter Veterinarian to take all steps necessary (diagnostics and treatment) to maintain the health of all animals housed at the Riverside Department of Animal Services (DAS).
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: This procedure is applicable to all personnel involved in the observation, monitoring, and recording of the health of all animals impounded at DAS.
documents.supersedes: Duties of the Shelter Veterinarian, effective May 28, 2007
references: California Penal Code Section 597 (animal cruelty)

sections.heading: Morning Confiscate Evaluation and Quarantine Observation and Release
steps: At the beginning of each shift, evaluate all animals requiring observation in the quarantine section using the corresponding Bite/Scratch Reports found on the Quarantine clipboard (located in the clinic).
For animals acting and behaving normally, put the date and your initials on line 41 of the bite report.

Bring any clinical abnormalities that are or may be consistent with neurological disease, including rabies, immediately to the attention of the Shelter Veterinarian. The Bite Report can be used as a reference to clinical signs encountered in rabid dogs and cats. Direct attention also to the development of other medical issues or body functions (eating, drinking, and urination).

To release an animal from Quarantine Observation, check the box labeled 'upon inspection' and write the current date and your signature on the Bite Report (line 43). Make a notation in the Chameleon memo with regard to the date released from quarantine and initial it.

sections.heading: Daily Treatments and Examinations

steps.step: Conduct veterinary examinations using the Animal Without Treatment Record report.
sub: Treat identifiable medical problems appropriately within the course and scope of licensure.
Supervise the RVTs during the vaccination and treatment of impounded animals.
Update the treatment records within Chameleon, as well as the To Do List, accordingly.
As state law permits, any animal deemed to be irremediably suffering may be euthanized independent of legal holding length requirements.

steps: Review the treatment records of all animals under treatment and continue or change treatments as appropriate.
Remove from treatment those animals whose medical problems are resolved.
Examine all special attention animals and address their medical needs as appropriate.
Advise the Supervising RVT of any newly impounded Special Attention animals for the determination relative to services and treatments concerning the impounds.

steps.step: Accept inquiries from the general public concerning animals they have adopted or questions they may have concerning animal diseases, etc.
sub: Handle all inquiries professionally and make every attempt to supply the customer with as much information as needed.
If the caller requests to discuss the matter with a supervisor, refer them to the Clinic Manager or designee.

steps: Consult with prospective adopters or owners as necessary.

steps.step: Supervise RVTs in the performance of their duties as detailed in the Duties of a RVT policy and/or as directed.
sub: Maintain and utilize the proper chain of command in resolving personnel and policy issues or concerns.
Instruct RVTs in current medical theory and practice.
Oversee inventory control as maintained by the RVT.

steps.step: Perform other duties as prescribed by the Clinic Manager or designee, including:
sub: Field livestock examinations and animal cruelty cases.
Working with the Clinic Manager or designee in formulating and updating section policies and procedures.
Performing morning rounds and providing input for animals that have reached their retention period.
Providing input for Performance Evaluations.
Providing spay/neuter surgery coverage as needed.

sections.heading: Morning Processing
steps: All personnel using sedatives and/or euthanasia solution are required to obtain and read the specific Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS). A booklet entitled 'Reading and Understanding Material Safety Data Sheets' is found in the MSDS binder.
Processing begins at approximately 8:30 am.
The euthanasia review list is reviewed and completed by shelter leadership and veterinary staff through daily population rounds.
The euthanasia procedure is handled by the Adoption Counselors (ACT).
On a daily basis, undertake the Dose Report and a reconciliation of the euthanasia solution.
Refer to the Policy and Procedure, Methods and Techniques for Euthanizing Dogs and Cats.

sections.heading: Cruelty Investigations
steps: Per the Policy and Procedure, Inhumane Investigations, the impounding Animal Control Officer will notify the Shelter Veterinarian of a possible Cruelty Investigation (PC 597) upon intake.

steps.step: A detailed and comprehensive Supplemental Investigation Report (SIR) will be completed by the end of the officer's shift unless otherwise authorized by the Watch Commander (WC).
sub: Submit along with the SIR a copy of the Chameleon Activity Report, Activity memo, kennel window, and kennel memo.
It is the responsibility of the WC to make sure the report and attachments are submitted.

steps: If the Clinic Veterinarian is not on duty (after hours), transport the animal to an outside contract emergency veterinary hospital. Advise the emergency clinic of the PC 597 circumstance of impound. Any blood work, x-rays, etc. can be approved by the WC.

The examining veterinarian will record their initial findings in the Description Box under the Treatment Window and will also submit an email with the findings to the Supervising Animal Control Officer, Director, and Chief Veterinarian within 24 hours.

In cases where no lab work is required, submit a comprehensive report within 72 hours of the examining veterinarian's next regular scheduled workday. Where lab work is required and results are pending, submit a comprehensive report 10 days from the original date of examination.

possible cruelty investigation (pc 597) upon intake. {"step": "a detailed and comprehensive supplemental investigation report (sir) will be completed by the end of the officer's shift unless otherwise authorized by the watch commander (wc).", "sub": ["submit along with the sir a copy of the chameleon activity report, activity memo, kennel window, and kennel memo.", "it is the responsibility of the wc to make sure the report and attachments are submitted."]} if the clinic veterinarian is not on duty (after hours), transport the animal to an outside contract emergency veterinary hospital. advise the emergency clinic of the pc 597 circumstance of impound. any blood work, x-rays, etc. can be approved by the wc. the examining veterinarian will record their initial findings in the description box under the treatment window and will also submit an email with the findings to the supervising animal control officer, director, and chief veterinarian within 24 hours. in cases where no lab work is required, submit a comprehensive report within 72 hours of the examining veterinarian's next regular scheduled workday. where lab work is required and results are pending, submit a comprehensive report 10 days from the original date of examination. california penal code section 597 (animal cruelty)

documents.title: Completion and Routing of Government Quarantines
documents.purpose: To ensure compliance with government regulations regarding importation of dogs from outside the United States and, in special situations, from other states experiencing rabies outbreaks.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: This procedure is utilized by the Rabies Control Desk.
documents.supersedes: Completion and Routing of the Rabies Control Investigation Report for Government Quarantines, effective May 28, 2007
references: Public Health Service Foreign Quarantine Regulations, 42 CFR Section 71.51
Notice to Owners and Importers of Dogs (COC 75.37)

definitions.def: Restriction of an animal by the owner or his agent to a building or other enclosure in isolation from other animals and from persons except for contact necessary for its care, or, if it is allowed out of such enclosure, muzzling the animal and keeping it on leash. (42 CFR 71.51)

sections.heading: Policy
steps: When a dog has been brought into the United States from another country, the Department of Health and Human Services, Atlanta, Georgia will issue to the owner a Notice to Owners and Importers of Dogs advising them of the quarantine requirements.
A copy of this notice is sent to Rabies Control in the county where the dog will reside.
When this copy is received, initiate a Rabies Control Investigation Report and place the dog under Government quarantine.

sections.heading: Completion of the Rabies Control Investigation Report for Government Quarantine
steps: Enter 'Government Quarantine' on line 2a.
Enter 'Dept. of Health, Atlanta, GA' or other source of information on line 9.
Enter the name of the person completing the report and the current date on line 10.
Enter the date of entry on line 12a.
Enter the owner's information and the dog's information on lines 32a through 35.
Enter the map reference for the owner's address.
Enter the country the dog came from under line 38.

sections.heading: Distribution of Reports
steps: Pull the yellow copy of the Rabies Control Investigation Report and attach the Notice to Owners and Importers of Dogs. File both the yellow copy and the Notice to Owners and Importers of Dogs in the active government file.
Give the Rabies Control Investigation Report to the Dispatcher for field officers to quarantine the dog.
After the dog has been placed under quarantine, file the original Rabies Control Investigation Report in the active file by the date of the next action (visit or quarantine release).

sections.heading: Length of Quarantine
steps: Dogs under four (4) months of age are placed under quarantine until they are rabies vaccinated at four months of age and then quarantined for an additional 30 days from the date of vaccination.

Dogs over four (4) months of age not rabies vaccinated are placed under quarantine and must be rabies vaccinated within 48 hours. The dogs remain under quarantine for 30 days from the date of vaccination.

Dogs that have been rabies vaccinated for at least 30 days prior to entry into California with a vaccine approved by the USDA and California Department of Health Services (excludes almost all foreign vaccinations) are placed under quarantine and released at the same time, providing they are healthy. Notify the County Veterinarian or Rabies Control clerk immediately if the animal is found not to be healthy.

Dogs that have been rabies vaccinated for less than 30 days prior to importation into California with a vaccine approved by the USDA and California Department of Health Services (excludes almost all foreign vaccinations) are placed under quarantine for 30 days from the date of vaccination.

sections.heading: Unable to Locate Dog or Dog Has Moved Out of the Country
steps: Fill out a Memorandum of Return of Notice of Importation, checking the appropriate boxes.
Mail to: Veterinary Public Health Unit, Centers for Disease Control, 1600 Clifton Road Northeast, Atlanta, Georgia, 30333.

sections.heading: Quarantine Release
steps: Pull all copies of the Rabies Control Investigation Report from the active file. Discard all copies, keeping only the original and supplemental parts of the report.
Attach the Notice to Owners and Importers of Dogs to the original Rabies Control Investigation Report.

steps.step: Enter the information on the Rabies Control Investigation Report into the Chameleon Bite Window using the following field entries:
sub: BITE NO.: the computer will generate a number when the screen is stored.
TYPE: enter GOV for government quarantine.
BITE DATE: enter the numerical date of when the animal entered the country.

ACTIVITY NO.: used for ACOs only. Input the activity number from the top right-hand corner of the Rabies Control Investigation Report. Press F4 to go to the activity screen (animal and, if applicable, owner PID are to be completed by the ACO). Press the Enter key and the owner and animal ID will transfer to the bite screen.
PRICE: not applicable.
RECEIPT NO.: not applicable.
ANIMAL ID: enter the animal ID stored by the ACO. Verify that the bite box is marked with a Y; this creates a bite icon for the animal. Write the animal ID number on line 1a of the bite report.
OWNER ID: create an owner ID if not found in Chameleon. Verify that the information on the bite report is the same as in Chameleon.
VICTIM ID: enter P511742. This person ID is only used by the Rabies Control Desk.
AGE: not applicable.
RELATION: do not use.
LOCATION: not applicable.
SEVERITY: not applicable.
CIRCUMSTANCE: not applicable.
TREATMENT BY: not applicable.
DATE: not applicable.
DESC: not applicable.
QUARANTINE BEGIN DATE: the date the quarantine is imposed. See line 40 of the bite report.
BY: DAS Chameleon ID, last name, or agency abbreviation (DAS Field Officer or Clinic Veterinarian).
LOCATION: enter SHELTER if the quarantined animal is impounded at a DAS shelter; enter HOME if the quarantine is imposed by DAS at home.
END DATE: the numerical date the quarantine was ended.
ABATED BY: same convention as the BY field above.
LAB SPECIMEN NO.: not applicable.
HEAD RESULT: not applicable.
EXAM DATE: not applicable.
DISPOSITION: not applicable.
HEAD DATE: not applicable.
CONTROLLED BY: not applicable.

sections.heading: Filing
steps: File the original Rabies Control Investigation Report and the Notice to Owners and Importers of Dogs in the inactive or completed file by date under Government Quarantine.

related.name: Rabies Control Investigation Report (Animal Bite/Scratch Report)
Notice to Owners and Importers of Dogs (COC 75.37)
Memorandum of Return of Notice of Importation
Veterinary Public Health Unit, Centers for Disease Control, 1600 Clifton Road NE, Atlanta, GA 30333

appendices: Appendix A: Notice to Owners and Importers of Dogs (CDC form, attached separately)
Appendix B: Sample completed Rabies Control Investigation Report for Government Quarantine (attached separately)
Appendix C: Memorandum of Return of Notice of Importation (attached separately)
Appendix D: Government Quarantine process flowchart (attached separately)

revisions.desc: Migrated into standard RCDAS SOP template. Content unchanged from 2009 version; substantive edits pending (see edit log). Named staff in the Chameleon BY field replaced with role title.

documents.title: Rabies Control Investigation Report for Survey Only
documents.purpose: To establish procedures for the initiation and completion of the Rabies Control Investigation Report (Animal Bite/Scratch Report) for a rabies virus survey.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: Applicable to DAS personnel required to complete this report for a rabies virus survey.
documents.supersedes: Completion and Routing of the Rabies Control Investigation Report for Survey Only, revisions of October 8, 2009 and May 28, 2007

sections.heading: Policy
steps: In the interest of public health and safety, the DAS Chief Veterinarian or Rabies Control Clerk (Office Assistant III) may require testing on a rabies suspect wild animal, despite the fact that there has been no human or domestic animal exposure for this animal.

steps.step: Initiate a bat vs. human bite report (not Survey Only) in the following circumstances:
sub: A person who wakes up in a room and sees a bat either flying around or roosting by the wall will be deemed an exposure whether there is a bite or not.
A person touches the bat with bare hands, whether or not they state the bat bit them.
A bat lands in an occupied pool filled with people.

steps.step: Initiate a bat vs. domestic pet bite report (not Survey Only) in the following circumstances:
sub: A person who wakes up in a room, with a pet sleeping with the person, and sees a bat either flying around or roosting by the wall will be deemed an exposure whether there is a bite or not.
The pet is playing with, or carrying, the bat in its mouth.
The family owns a pet and does not know if the bat came in contact with the pet, whether there is a bite or not.

sections.heading: When to Initiate a Survey Only Report
steps: If it has been determined that no direct exposure has occurred, the Animal Control Officer (ACO) will impound the rabies suspect animal and initiate a Survey Only Bite/Scratch Report.

steps.step: Circumstances requiring a Survey Only Bite/Scratch Report include, but are not limited to, the following:
sub: Humans and/or domestic animals currently near or having been near the presence of a bat that has positive confirmation of no physical contact other than with thick gloves or a shovel.
A bat found laying on the ground with no pet running around near the area.
A person finds a bat but picks it up with thick gloves or another resource without touching the bat.
Skunks, raccoons, and foxes appearing sick or behaving abnormally (under direction of supervision).
Any animal impounded by direction of the DAS Chief Veterinarian or Rabies Control Clerk.

steps: Once the Survey Only report is completed by the ACO, exposure will be determined by the DAS Chief Veterinarian or Rabies Control Office Clerk.

sections.heading: Distribution of Copies
steps: Refer to the Completion of Rabies Control Investigation Report Animal vs. Human and the Completion of Rabies Control Investigation Domestic vs. Wild Animal procedures.

sections.heading: Completion of Report
steps: It is imperative in Survey Only reports to determine that there was no exposure to any human or animal. If there was human exposure, complete the report as for animal vs. human. If there was animal exposure, complete the report as for domestic vs. wild animal.

steps.step: Complete the Survey Only report using the numbered spaces on the form as follows:
sub: 1a. Imp #: Animal ID generated by Chameleon.
1b. Case #: no change.
2a. Name: enter 'No human/animal exposure'.
2b. Victim (last name): enter 'Survey Only'.
3. Sex: N/A.
4. Age: N/A.
5. Telephone: home and work numbers of the person for whom you impounded the animal.
6. Address: address from where the animal was impounded.
7. Parent/Guardian: last name first, first name, middle initial of the person for whom you impounded the animal.
8. Address: where the person listed in #7 lives, if different from #6.
9. Source: mark the 'other' box and list the person and his/her telephone number. If the source is the person for whom you impounded the animal, list '#7' and list 'same' for the telephone number.
10. Report Received By: no change.
11. Place of Attack: although there is no attack, list the place where the animal was discovered. Be specific.
12a. Attack Date/Time: line out 'attack'. Enter the date and time the animal was impounded.
12b. Date of Bite: enter the date of impound.
13. Circumstances: enter 'see attached supplemental,' where a detailed report will be written. 'Through material?' will not apply.
14. Wound Location: N/A.
15. Skin Break: N/A.
16. Wound Treatment: N/A.
17. Treated By: N/A.
18. Anti-Rabies Treatment: N/A.
19-26. Veterinary Review, Case Closed, Laboratory Exam items: no change.
27. Case Closed: no change.
28. Notification: N/A.
29. QV Date: N/A.
30. QR Date: N/A.
31. Miscellaneous: no change. Generally used to complete line 13 and line 37.
32a. Owner (Custodian): of the rabies suspect animal. Generally enter 'stray' because there is usually no owner. If there is an owner, enter last name first, first name, then middle initial.
32b. Owner: of the rabies suspect animal. Enter only if there is a known owner; otherwise the Rabies Clerk will complete.
33. Address: of the rabies suspect animal owner. Leave blank if there is no owner.
34. Animal: enter the information applicable to the rabies suspect animal.
35. Description: no change.
36. License Number: N/A.
37. Behavior: mark the appropriate box. Make sure the specifics are described in line 13 or 31.
38. Travels: N/A.
39. Vaccination: N/A.
43. Release: N/A.
44. Cause of Death: complete whenever the rabies suspect animal dies or is euthanized. Mark the appropriate box and enter the date that death occurred.
45. Veterinarian/Symptoms: if a veterinarian observed the animal before it was impounded, mark the appropriate box. If not, leave blank.
Signature: N/A.
Non-numbered items: Map Ref.: N/A. QT Fee: N/A.

sections.heading: Supplemental Report
steps: Mark the Supplemental box and write a detailed report on the attached supplemental page.

steps.step: For all species, the report must include, but is not limited to, the following:
sub: Describe the exact manner in which the ACO impounded the rabies suspect animal. Include any safety equipment used during the impoundment of the animal, i.e., latex gloves, leather gloves, four (4) foot Ketch-All, net with four (4) foot pole, coffee can, etc.
Provide the length of time from when the reporting party first noticed the bat or rabies suspect animal to when the animal was impounded.

steps.step: For bats, the report must also include:
sub: The full names, addresses, telephone numbers, and ages of any individuals that were within fifty (50) feet of the bat, including an approximation of the closest distance each may have come to the bat.
A full description of any domestic mammals that were within fifty (50) feet of the bat, including an approximation of the closest distance each may have come to the bat.

related.name: Rabies Control Investigation Report (Animal Bite/Scratch Report)
Completion of Rabies Control Investigation Report Animal vs. Human
Completion of Rabies Control Investigation Report Domestic vs. Wild Animal

appendices: Appendix A: Sample completed Survey Only Rabies Control Investigation Report with supplemental page (attached separately)
Appendix B: Survey Only process flowchart (attached separately)

documents.title: Controlled Substances Chain of Custody
documents.purpose: To define inventory control, tracking, safekeeping, reconciliation, and review/audit of all pharmaceuticals used by the Department of Animal Services.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: This procedure applies to all Veterinarians and Registered Veterinary Technicians.
documents.supersedes: Controlled Substances Chain of Custody, effective May 28, 2007
references: Title 21, CFR Section 1301.71(a) (DEA security requirements for controlled substances)

sections.heading: Policy
steps: Title 21, CFR Section 1301.71(a) requires that all registrants provide effective controls and procedures to guard against theft and diversion of controlled substances.

steps.step: A list of factors is used to determine the adequacy of these security controls. Factors affecting practitioners include:
sub: The location of the premises and the relationship such location bears on security needs.
The type of building and office construction.
The type and quantity of controlled substances stored on the premises.
The type of storage medium (safe, vault, or steel cabinet).
The control of public access to the facility.
The adequacy of the registrant's monitoring system (alarms and detection systems).
The availability of local police protection.

steps: Maintain inventories and records of controlled substances listed in Schedules I and II separately from all other records maintained by the registrant.

Maintain inventories and records of controlled substances in Schedules III, IV, and V separately or in such a form that they are readily retrievable from the ordinary business records of the practitioner.
Maintain all records related to controlled substances and keep them available for inspection for a minimum of two years.

sections.heading: Ordering
steps: The Supervising Registered Veterinary Technician (SRVT) completes the DEA form accurately.
The form must be signed by the approved DEA license holder.
Except for the last page of the form, the rest of the copies are sealed and mailed to the vendor/supplier.

sections.heading: Receipting of Controlled Substance Supplies
steps: Once supplies are received, the SRVT verifies the count of supplies received.
Number the bottles and place them in a locked safe.

steps.step: Enter the bottles into the Controlled Substance Log with the following information:
sub: Date logged
Beginning amount
Lot number
Ending amount
Bottle numbers
Signature of designated receiver

steps: Only limited designated staff members have access to the safe code.

sections.heading: Dispensing of Controlled Substances: Authorized Dispenser
steps: Designated staff members who have the safe combination code dispense to authorized staff based on the controlled substance used.
The dispenser logs the number of bottles, the bottle numbers, and the name of the person the bottles were dispensed to on the controlled substance log. The log is returned to the safe.
Keep the safe locked.

steps.step: Once the authorized recipient returns the empty bottle along with the completed individual controlled substance log:
sub: Verify and reconcile the bottle to the log. Ensure the dosage balances with the log.
If there is some amount left inside the bottle, enter the amount into the original controlled substance log to be dispensed at a later date.
File the individual controlled substance log.
Render the empty bottle unusable.

sections.heading: Dispensing of Controlled Substances: Authorized Recipient
steps: Secure the controlled substance locked in a box with minimum access, behind locked doors or in a locked truck (for Field Officers).

steps.step: Maintain and complete the individual controlled substance log in a timely manner, which includes:
sub: Date logged
Beginning amount
Ending amount
Bottle numbers
Amount used
Signature of designated receiver

steps: For authorized Animal Care Technicians (ACT), enter use of the bottle into Chameleon and print the usage log once the bottle is completed.
Veterinary staff using controlled substances enter the dose into Chameleon and print the usage log once the bottle is completed.
Once the bottle is completed, return the empty bottle and individual controlled substance log to the SRVT or authorized dispenser in a timely manner.

sections.heading: Review and Audit of Controlled Drugs

steps.step: The Clinic Manager performs surprise audits in all shelter locations by:
sub: Performing inventory on drugs locked in the safe against the controlled substance log.
Verifying storage compliance of controlled drugs and accessibility.

documents.searchText: to define inventory control, tracking, safekeeping, reconciliation, and review/audit of all pharmaceuticals used by the department of animal services. this procedure applies to all veterinarians and registered veterinary technicians. director of animal services policy title 21, cfr section 1301.71(a) requires that all registrants provide effective controls and procedures to guard against theft and diversion of controlled substances. {"step": "a list of factors is used to determine the adequacy of these security controls. factors affecting practitioners include:", "sub": ["the location of the premises and the relationship such location bears on security needs.", "the type of building and office construction.", "the type and quantity of controlled substances stored on the premises.", "the type of storage medium (safe, vault, or steel cabinet).", "the control of public access to the facility.", "the adequacy of the registrant's monitoring system (alarms and detection systems).", "the availability of local police protection."]} maintain inventories and records of controlled substances listed in schedules i and ii separately from all other records maintained by the registrant. maintain inventories and records of controlled substances in schedules iii, iv, and v separately or in such a form that they are readily retrievable from the ordinary business records of the practitioner. maintain all records related to controlled substances and keep them available for inspection for a minimum of two years. ordering the supervising registered veterinary technician (srvt) completes the dea form accurately. the form must be signed by the approved dea license holder. except for the last page of the form, the rest of the copies are sealed and mailed to the vendor/supplier. receipting of controlled substance supplies once supplies are received, the srvt verifies the count of supplies received. number the bottles and place them in a locked safe. {"step": "enter the bottles into the controlled substance log with the following information:", "sub": ["date logged", "beginning amount", "lot number", "ending amount", "bottle numbers", "signature of designated receiver"]} only limited designated staff members have access to the safe code. dispensing of controlled substances: authorized dispenser designated staff members who have the safe combination code dispense to authorized staff based on the controlled substance used. the dispenser logs the number of bottles, the bottle numbers, and the name of the person the bottles were dispensed to on the controlled substance log. the log is returned to the safe. keep the safe locked. {"step": "once the authorized recipient returns the empty bottle along with the completed individual controlled substance log:", "sub": ["verify and reconcile the bottle to the log. ensure the dosage balances with the log.", "if there is some amount left inside the bottle, enter the amount into the original controlled substance log to be dispensed at a later date.", "file the individual controlled substance log.", "render the empty bottle unusable."]} dispensing of controlled substances: authorized recipient secure the controlled substance locked in a box with minimum access, behind locked doors or in a locked truck (for field officers). {"step": "maintain and complete the individual controlled substance log in a timely manner, which includes:", "sub": ["date logged", "beginning amount", "ending amount", "bottle numbers", "amount used", "signature of designated receiver"]} for authorized animal care technicians (act), enter use of the bottle into chameleon and print the usage log once the bottle is completed. veterinary staff using controlled substances enter the dose into chameleon and print the usage log once the bottle is completed. once the bottle is completed, return the empty bottle and individual controlled substance log to the srvt or authorized dispenser in a timely manner. review and audit of controlled drugs {"step": "the clinic manager performs surprise audits in all shelter locations by:", "sub": ["performing inventory on drugs locked in the safe against the controlled substance log.", "verifying storage compliance of controlled drugs and accessibility."]} title 21, cfr section 1301.71(a) (dea security requirements for controlled substances)

documents.title: Pet Licensing
documents.purpose: To establish a consistent process for issuing dog licenses as required by the State of California.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services
documents.scope: Applicable to all Department of Animal Services (DAS) staff responsible for issuing a dog license.
documents.supersedes: Pet Licensing, effective March 8, 2009
references: California Code of Regulations, Title 17, 2606.4(a.1), Licensing and Vaccination Procedure
Riverside County Codified Ordinance Title 6, Chapter 6.08.020, Mandatory Dog License and Vaccination

sections.heading: Policy
steps: Require residents of contracted licensing cities and the unincorporated areas in the County of Riverside who own dogs four (4) months of age or older to have a current license and a current rabies vaccination certificate, in compliance with California Code of Regulations, Title 17, 2606.4(a.1), Licensing and Vaccination Procedure, and Riverside County Codified Ordinance Title 6, Chapter 6.08.020, Mandatory Dog License and Vaccination.

appendices: Appendix A: Rabies Certificate (attached separately)

documents.title: Telephone Credit Card Transaction
documents.purpose: To establish consistent procedures when handling credit card transactions at the request of an Adoption Partner Organization (APO) via the telephone.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services (approved the original policy April 15, 2009)
documents.scope: Applicable to all DAS staff who are responsible for coordinating and arranging adoptions and/or transfers to Adoption Partner Organizations (APO), otherwise known as rescue groups.
documents.supersedes: None. Original policy effective April 15, 2009.
references: RCDAS Policies 205, 206, and 207 (Cash Handling)

definitions.def: A rescue group approved to adopt or transfer animals from the Department of Animal Services under an Adoption Partner Agreement.

sections.heading: Policy
steps: Adoption Partner Organizations (APO) have the option of completing an adoption or transfer using a credit card transaction by telephone.
DAS staff shall process credit card payments using a designated telephone credit card terminal for telephone payments located at a DAS shelter or clinic front counter.
DAS staff shall be responsible for ensuring that all credit card transactions are accurate and for bringing any discrepancies to the attention of supervision.

sections.heading: Security Requirements
steps: Require each APO to complete a Credit Card Authorization Form with a listing of authorized persons who may verbally request the use of the card.
Keep current driver's license numbers and expiration dates of all authorized users on record with DAS using Chameleon.
Make a list of authorized users available to applicable DAS Adoption Partner staff.

steps.step: Rescue Coordinator Chief:
sub: The Rescue Coordinator Chief and the APC will maintain completed Credit Card Authorization Forms and the list of authorized credit card users of the APO.

steps.step: Adoption Partner Coordinator (APC):
sub: The APC will maintain completed Credit Card Authorization Forms and the list of authorized credit card users of the APO.
The APC will provide the Office Assistant the information of the animals to be processed for transfer with the corresponding Animal ID and the Person ID of the group.
The APC will obtain the time of pick up and the name of the person who will be accepting the transferred animal for the group, and place a Rescue Memo attached to each Animal ID in Chameleon.
The APC or designee prepares the adoption/transfer packet upon completion of the services and the Chameleon transaction.

steps.step: Office Assistant:
sub: Contact the APO representative to obtain verbal authorization and credit card information for any fees incurred with the transfer/adoption of a DAS animal.
Ensure the transaction for the animal transferred is processed through Chameleon (refer to Policies 205, 206, and 207, Cash Handling).

Ensure the animal is outcomed in Chameleon appropriately, i.e., Transfer-Surgwait for those animals requiring sterilization surgery or Transfer-Resc-Cont for those already sterilized or to be released without surgery.

Ensure three Chameleon receipts are printed: the first receipt is attached to the unsigned original first copy (merchant copy) credit card receipt and deposited the day of the transaction; the second receipt is emailed to the card holder and copied to both the Rescue Coordinator and Chief; the third receipt is placed in the Rescue Pending Pick-Up File.
Ensure original receipts for all transactions are maintained by the DAS fiscal office.
If the credit card transaction purchase is approved, print two credit card receipts (the original and duplicate receipts).
If the purchase is not approved, a message will appear on the credit card machine, at which time the credit card holder is notified to provide another credit card or the transaction is terminated.

appendices: Appendix: 100-10 Credit Card Auth Form (attached separately)

documents.title: Duties of the Rabies Control Desk
documents.purpose: To establish a uniform procedure for the management and prioritization of the Rabies Control Desk.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services. The Chief Veterinarian or designee completes quarantine dispositions and reviews owner-released quarantines.
documents.scope: Applicable to the Office Assistant III assigned to the Rabies Control Desk.
documents.supersedes: Policy 100-11, effective May 28, 2007; revised December 7, 2015; revised October 26, 2016; current version effective January 5, 2017.
references: RCDAS Policy 000-03, Completion of the Chameleon Kennel Window
State bite report statistics provided by the Chameleon administrator

definitions.def: The date the quarantine period ends: 10 days from the date of bite for dogs, cats, and ferrets; 14 days for livestock.
A visit to a home-quarantined biting animal without current rabies vaccination, conducted on the 5th day of quarantine (7th day for livestock).
Unable to Quarantine.
Fluorescent rabies antibody test (head test) performed by the laboratory on a specimen.
Potentially Dangerous Report.

sections.heading: Policy
steps: Process, follow through, and maintain all incoming and outgoing bite reports on a daily basis for the determination of post-exposure rabies treatment to victims of a bite or scratch in a timely manner. This duty belongs to the Rabies Control clerk or designee.

steps.step: Sort mail and faxes from hospitals, doctors, or victims according to where the biting animal lives:
sub: If the biting animal lives in our jurisdiction, create an activity (see procedure).
If the biting animal lives in Riverside County but is out of our jurisdiction, send the fax with a cover letter to the appropriate animal control agency and place the sent fax in the Faxes folder.

If the biting animal lives out of the county and the victim resides in Riverside County: write up the bite report, send (mail/fax) information to the outside agency, file in the active bite reports file based on date of bite, and follow up on the QTR date with the jurisdiction for disposition of quarantine.
If both the animal and victim live in another jurisdiction, send to the correct agency.

steps.step: Process mail and faxes from other animal control agencies:
sub: Sort as with mail and faxes from hospitals, doctors, or victims.
If a yellow copy was sent, file it in the active file according to the date of the bite and the victim's last name.
Follow up on disposition of quarantine or fax the disposition to the agency.

steps.step: Active bite reports:
sub: Complete the Quarantine Release (QR) date, 10 days from the date of bite for dogs, cats, and ferrets; 14 days for livestock.
If the biting animal is quarantined at home and does not have a current rabies shot, it is to have a Quarantine Visit (QV) on the 5th day of quarantine (7 days for livestock). Complete the QV box.
Enter the bite report into Chameleon (see procedures for entering data in the computer).
File the yellow copy/scan of the report by date and victim's last name.

steps.step: Active bite reports for animals quarantined at the shelter:
sub: The Veterinarian/RVT will retain all copies of the bite report except for the yellow copy.
Process reports as with active bite reports above.
Put active shelter quarantine bite reports on the bite report clipboard in the clinic.
Owner Released Quarantine animals are to be reviewed by the clinic veterinarian or his or her designee for quarantine disposition.
For bite reports with the animal in kennel number Morgue, see Head Test Procedures.

steps.step: Field quarantine release reports:
sub: Check to see that line 40 is signed and dated by an ACO.
Make sure that the owner or custodian signature, date of quarantine, and California Driver's License (CDL) lines are completed.
Check to see that line 43 is signed and dated by an ACO.
Make sure the quarantine period has been fulfilled.
Pull the yellow copy from the active file.
Dispose of copies as well as the supplemental report if it is blank.
Send a notification letter to the victim. Do not send a letter if the victim has the same address as the owner or the victim is a pet (pet vs. wildlife).
Complete line 28.
File the completed report in the file cabinet by owner's last name, month, and year of bite. If the animal is a stray, file by victim's last name in the stray folder.

steps.step: Shelter quarantine release reports:
sub: Check to see that line 40 is signed and dated by an ACO/RVT/Veterinarian.
Check to see that line 43 is signed and dated by an RVT/Veterinarian.
Verify that all impounded/observed dates are noted on line 41.
Pull the yellow copy from the active file.
Dispose of the copy.
Retain the original and supplemental report if needed.
Send a notification letter. Do not send a letter if the victim has the same address as the owner.
Complete line 28.
File the completed report in the file cabinet by owner's last name, month, and year of bite. If the animal is a stray, file by victim's last name in the stray folder.

steps.step: Unable to Quarantine reports:
sub: Read the bite report in its entirety.
Make sure the patrol check is noted throughout the quarantine period.
Verify all patrol checks are entered on line 42.
Enter the report into Chameleon (see procedures for entering into the computer).
Dispose of all copies.
Retain the original and supplemental report.
Have the Chief Veterinarian or designee complete line 27.
Send the UTQ notification letter.
Complete line 28. Do not send a letter if the victim has the same address as the owner.
File the completed report in the file cabinet by owner's last name, month, and year of bite. If the animal is a stray, file by victim's last name in the stray folder.

steps.step: Pet vs. wildlife reports:
sub: If the wildlife involved was impounded, a head test will be initiated on the animal. Follow Head Test Procedures to continue.

If the pet animal was currently rabies vaccinated prior to contact with a wild animal, that pet is to be re-vaccinated for rabies within 48 hours and quarantined for 30 days from the date of re-vaccination. Exception: FRA test (see Head Test Procedure).

If the pet animal was not rabies vaccinated at the time of contact, the pet animal is to be vaccinated within 48 hours and quarantined for six months from the date of vaccination. Exception: FRA test results.

If the pet animal was re-vaccinated less than 30 days ago and came in contact with a wild animal, the pet does not need to be re-vaccinated but is quarantined for 30 days from the day of the incident. Exception: FRA test result (see procedures).
Email a brief summary of the circumstances of possible exposure to the designated Department of Public Health contacts, the County Veterinarian, the Chief, and the Supervising Office Assistant.
The ACO will retain all copies of the bite report except for the yellow and goldenrod copies.
Enter the bite report into Chameleon (see procedures).
Verify/complete the QV and QR boxes.
File the yellow copy by date of bite and pet owner's last name.
Wait for head test results if applicable. When results show negative, have the pet released from quarantine.
Process the bite report for release (see Quarantine Release Procedures).
When head test results come back positive, notify the Chief Veterinarian immediately.

steps.step: Outgoing:
sub: Bite reports need to have a Kennel Record and Bite Window completed.
Confirm the Veterinarian/RVT has completed lines 19 and 20.
Confirm the goldenrod copy of the original paperwork is attached to the specimen.
Dispose of all the extra paperwork except for the original bite report and yellow copy.

Exception for pet vs. wildlife reports: the goldenrod copy of the bite report is attached to the specimen; the yellow copy is given to the Rabies Control desk; the remaining copies, which include the original, will be retained by the ACO for follow-up on the notice to re-vaccinate and the 30-day or 6-month quarantine; the victim is the pet.
Enter the bite report into Chameleon (see procedure).
File the yellow copy in the active file by date of bite and victim's last name.
At the Rabies desk, create an activity for heads to be delivered to the lab for the next business day.
Email a brief summary of the circumstances of possible exposure to the designated Department of Public Health contacts, the County Veterinarian, the Chief, and the Supervising Office Assistant.

steps.step: Incoming, if the head test comes back negative:
sub: Update or enter the bite report in Chameleon (see Chameleon procedures).
Enter the results in the Chameleon Bite Window with the date, result, and laboratory test number.
Attach the original bite report to the lab report.
Send out the FRA Negative notification letter. Do not send a letter if the victim has the same address as the owner or the victim is a pet (pet vs. wildlife).
Complete line 28.
Pull the yellow copy from the active file and dispose.
If pet vs. wildlife, notify the Supervising Field Services Sergeant or Lieutenant by electronic mail or telephone to have the pet released from quarantine.

File the report in the file by year, month, and owner's last name. If the animal is a stray, file by victim's last name in the stray folder. If survey only, file by victim's last name in the survey-only folder.

steps.step: Incoming, if the head test comes back positive (human vs. animal):
sub: Follow the incoming (results) procedures for updating Chameleon, entering results in the Bite Window, and attaching the original report to the lab report.
Make a copy of the original report and fax to Epidemiology for review.
Inform the Chief Veterinarian or designee.
Pull the yellow copy from the active file and dispose.
File a copy of the report in the positive file folder in the designated file drawer.

steps.step: Incoming, if the head test comes back positive (pet vs. wildlife):
sub: Find the yellow copy of the bite report and attach the lab paperwork.
Make a copy of the report and give it to the Chief Veterinarian.
File the yellow or copy of the bite report in the quarantine release file by QV or QR date.
The pet goes through the whole quarantine period, 30 days or 6 months.

sections.heading: Ferret Bite Procedures
steps: Quarantine owned ferrets for 10 days from the date of bite.
Ferret owners will pay all fees incurred for quarantines.
See Chameleon procedures for how to enter ferret bites.

sections.heading: Audit and Reconciliation
steps: Review the bite report for accuracy and completeness in compliance with state law.

steps.step: The Rabies desk Office Assistant III will review the bite report for the following:
sub: The report is written in black ink only. Colored ink fades and becomes difficult to read when copies are made or when faxed.
The report is legibly printed in block letters. Reports may be forwarded to other agencies, and it is important that they be able to read the information.
Do not refer to notes entered into the activity.
Verify the quarantine release date (line 30).

Verify the kennel window is correct. All animals impounded with a bite report should have subtype Bite on their kennel record. If sent for FRA testing, the outcome subtype should always be rabies test. Refer to Policy 000-03, Completion of the Chameleon Kennel Window.

steps.step: Pet vs. wildlife report review:
sub: A bite report is initiated for each animal.
The bite victim is written as the pet dog or cat and the owner's last name.
The victim's address is the address where the pet dog or cat resides.
The full name and telephone number of the owner is written in the source of information.
In line 31 of the bite report, write the Animal ID of the pet animal, a full description, and the last time the animal was vaccinated, or note no current vaccination.
Line 34 contains the type of wildlife the pet had possible contact with.
A 48-hour mandatory vaccination is imposed.
The yellow copy is at the Rabies desk.
Corrections: a bite report is a legal document. Make corrections by drawing a line through the incorrect information and writing the correct information above it.

steps.step: Human vs. animal (including wildlife) report review:
sub: The age of the victim must be written on the report.
If the victim is a minor, the full name and telephone number of the guardian or parent must be written in line 7 (name of parent/guardian).
Verify the date and time the report was initiated.
The circumstances of attack must contain a brief description of how the bite or scratch happened.
Include a brief description of the type of bite, such as puncture, scratch, laceration, or tear.

If the animal is currently vaccinated, make sure to write the kind of rabies vaccination given, date vaccinated, vaccination expiration, and name of clinic or veterinarian (state law requires owners to show proof).
The yellow copy is at the Rabies desk.

steps.step: Out of jurisdiction report review:
sub: Fax, email, or mail the rabies control cover sheet to follow up on the disposition of the quarantine for bite reports that were forwarded to another animal control agency.
Document on line 31 of the bite report the date when the agency was contacted.
Make three attempts to obtain the quarantine disposition.

If the disposition is not obtained, submit for the reviewer to sign, send the UTQ Out of Jurisdiction notification letter, complete line 28 (do not send a letter if the victim has the same address as the owner), and file the completed report in the file cabinet by owner's last name, month, and year of bite. If the animal is a stray, file by victim's last name in the stray folder.

If the disposition is obtained, complete line 40 with the date of the quarantine, name of the investigator, and agency city; complete line 43 with the quarantine release date, investigator, and agency city; complete line 28 (do not send a letter if the victim has the same address as the owner); and file the completed report in the file cabinet by owner's last name, month, and year of bite. If the animal is a stray, file by victim's last name in the stray folder.

steps.step: Run the Bite Overview Audit Report on a monthly basis, based on last month's bite dates:
sub: Counts of the types of bite reports must balance with the state stats provided by the Chameleon administrator.
Send email to the respective divisions and carbon copy the immediate supervisor for bite reports flagged with a status of YELLOW.
Refresh the report on the 12th day.

related.name: Notification Letter for FRA Test Result
Out of Jurisdiction Quarantine Notification Letter
Policy 000-03, Completion of the Chameleon Kennel Window

documents.title: Assistance Dog (Service, Signal, and Guide Dog) Procedure
documents.purpose: To establish a uniform guideline for processing dog tags issued to assistance dogs.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services (approved the original policy). The Chief of Veterinary Services approves or denies assistance dog tag applications.
documents.scope: Applicable to all Department of Animal Services staff responsible for issuing assistance dog tags.
documents.supersedes: None. Original policy effective July 22, 2014.
references: California Food and Agricultural Code 30850, Application - Assistance Dogs
California Food and Agricultural Code 30851 (owners of assistance dogs shall comply with all state and local ordinances regarding health and licensure requirements for dogs)
California Food and Agricultural Code 30852 (the tag identifying a dog as an assistance dog shall be used only by a person with a disability or a trainer of an assistance dog)
California Civil Code 54.1(c) (definitions of guide dog, signal dog, and service dog)

ADA regulations effective March 15, 2011 (a service animal is any dog individually trained to do work or perform tasks for the benefit of an individual with a disability; animals that merely provide emotional support are excluded)

definitions.def: Any dog trained by a person licensed as defined in Title III of the Americans with Disabilities Act of 1990 (Civil Code 54.1(c)).
Any dog trained to alert an individual who is deaf or hearing impaired to intruders or sounds (Civil Code 54.1(c)).

Any dog individually trained to the requirements of the individual with a disability, including, but not limited to, minimal protection work, rescue work, pulling a wheelchair, or fetching dropped items (Civil Code 54.1(c)).

steps.step: Issue a copy of the approved assistance dog tag form and a permanent assistance dog tag when the following requirements have been met:
sub: The dog is four months of age.
The dog resides within the City of Riverside or in the unincorporated Riverside County jurisdiction.
The dog is vaccinated against rabies with a vaccine approved by the State of California, and the period of immunization covers the desired licensing period.
The Assistance Dog Identification Application is completed.
The applicant provides documentation of a microchip.
The applicant provides a copy of an identification card or driver's license.
The owner's signature is affixed to the affidavit.
A certificate issued by a licensed trainer is provided.

sections.heading: New Assistance Dog License Tag Window
steps: Begin at the tag window.
Create and store a Person ID and Animal ID to issue an application. Query to see if the owner exists in Chameleon (see 000-01, General Chameleon Information).
If the Person ID or Animal ID does not exist, proceed to create both the Animal and Person ID.
In the tag type field, enter LIC SERV or press F4 to bring up the option.
Enter MAIL or COUNTER in the subtype field, or press F4 and choose.
Make sure that the person jurisdiction matches the tag jurisdiction.
Enter TEMPORARY in the tag_status field.
In the problem code field, enter or choose APR PNDING.
Enter the rabies vaccination information in the appropriate fields of the tag window.
Store the record.

sections.heading: Report
steps: With the LIC SERV record displayed, press F3 to view the list of reports in Chameleon.
Scroll down and choose SERVICE TAG NEW APPLICATION.
Enter the password.
Print the report and mail, fax, or email it to the applicant.

sections.heading: Application Review
steps: Once an application is received, submit all documents, including proof of rabies vaccination and microchip, to the Chief of Veterinary Services for review.
The Chief of Veterinary Services will either deny or approve the application.
If the application is approved, proceed to issuing an assistance dog tag.

steps.step: If the application is denied:
sub: Bring up the tag record with tag type LIC SERV and status TEMPORARY.
Change the problem code to DENIED and update the tag window.
Bring up the reports menu.
Choose SERVICE TAG DENIAL LETTER.
Enter the password and last name of the applicant.
Print the letter and mail it.

sections.heading: Issuing an Assistance Dog Tag
steps: In the tag window, pop up (F4) the PERSONID to find the applicant.
Perform a search based on the PID in the tag window.
Place the cursor in the Tag No. box and enter L followed by the last two digits of the current year (for example, tag number 330 in year 2012 is entered as L12-330).
Update the information (F8); the person and animal will be linked under the tag window as one record.

sections.heading: Create a Receipt
steps: Pop up (F4) in the receipt window.
Confirm the Person ID and Animal ID are correct.
In the item code field, LIC SERV, LIC SERV 2YR, or LIC SERV 3YR will appear.
The license number, Animal ID, fee type, and price will be displayed.
Enter the total amount of monies ($0.00) and store (F9). A window will appear giving the option to print the receipt. Make a selection.

related.name: Guidelines for Assistance Dog Tag Application
Tag window, Receipt window, Reports menu (Service Tag New Application, Service Tag Denial Letter)

documents.title: Microchip Scanning
documents.purpose: To establish a consistent process for scanning animals for microchips at intake and at each status change. Microchip scanning supports owner identification and reunification in compliance with California Food and Agricultural Code sections 31108 and 31752, reduces unnecessary length of stay for owned animals, and ensures RCDAS makes reasonable efforts to contact owners before any disposition decision is made.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services. All RCDAS staff involved in animal impoundment, intake processing, kennel management, or disposition are responsible for performing and documenting microchip scans at the points required by this procedure. Supervisors are responsible for monitoring compliance and ensuring all staff have access to functioning universal microchip scanners.
documents.scope: This procedure applies to all RCDAS staff at all shelter locations who handle animals entering RCDAS custody, including stray impounds, owner surrenders, field seizures, and transfers. It applies to all species for which microchipping is standard practice, including dogs, cats, and rabbits.
documents.supersedes: Policy 100-14, Microchip Scanning, effective August 20, 2014 (original one-page policy statement, absorbed into this expanded draft revision).
references: California Food and Agricultural Code sections 31108 and 31752 (Impound Holding Period for Dogs and Cats)
California Food and Agricultural Code section 30850 (cited in the draft source for microchip implantation requirements; verify citation)
RCDAS SOP Owner Notification Procedure

definitions.def: A microchip reader capable of detecting all ISO-compliant (134.2 kHz) and legacy (125 kHz) microchip frequencies, including 125 kHz, 128 kHz, and 134.2 kHz chips.
Detection of a microchip number by the scanner, confirmed by a second pass to verify the number is read consistently.
A search of one or more microchip registries to identify the registered owner associated with a detected chip number.
The RCDAS shelter management software used to document all animal records, including microchip scan results and owner contact attempts.

steps.step: Scan every animal for a microchip at each of the following points, without exception:
sub: At intake, before the animal is kenneled.
At each status change, including transfer to a different housing area, medical hold placement, foster placement, and pre-adoption processing.
Prior to any disposition, including adoption, transfer to a partner organization, euthanasia, or return to owner.

sections.heading: Scanning Technique
steps: Use only a universal scanner. Verify the scanner is functioning before beginning each intake session by testing against a known chip or the scanner's self-test function.

Hold the scanner approximately one inch from the animal's body. Begin at the left shoulder blade, which is the standard implant site, and move the scanner slowly in a figure-eight pattern across the back of the neck and both shoulder blades.
Continue scanning down the midline of the back and along both sides of the neck. Chips migrate over time and may be found anywhere along the dorsal surface.
If the scanner detects a number, perform a second pass over the same area to confirm the number reads consistently before recording it.

sections.heading: Positive Scan: Owner Lookup and Notification
steps: Record the chip number in Chameleon immediately upon detection.

Search a minimum of two microchip registries to identify the registered owner. The draft source leaves the designated registries to be specified (for example, the AAHA Universal Pet Microchip Lookup at lookup.aaha.org). Document each registry searched and the result in Chameleon.

If owner contact information is found, initiate owner notification per the RCDAS Owner Notification Procedure and document all contact attempts, including date, time, method, and outcome, in Chameleon.
If registry lookup returns no owner information, document the chip number and the registries searched in Chameleon. Continue standard stray hold procedures.

sections.heading: Negative Scan
steps: If no chip is detected after a thorough scan, document the negative result in Chameleon.
For owner-surrendered animals, inform the owner that no chip was detected and offer microchipping at intake if available, or advise the owner to request microchipping before finalizing the surrender.
For stray animals, a negative scan does not preclude owner reunification. Continue standard lost-and-found procedures regardless of scan result.

sections.heading: Scanner Maintenance
steps: Notify your supervisor immediately if a scanner malfunctions, produces inconsistent reads, or fails a self-test.
Supervisors ensure a functioning replacement scanner is available before intake operations continue.
Clean scanners per manufacturer instructions and store them securely between uses.

related.name: California Food and Agricultural Code sections 31108, 31752, 30850

documents.title: Use of Animals in Research, Testing, and Education
documents.purpose: Pursuant to the potential use of impounded animals for public health education, testing, and research, the department acknowledges the guidance provided by the American Veterinary Medical Association (AVMA).
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services (approved the original policy December 21, 2016).
documents.scope: This policy applies to all animals within the service area of the Riverside County Department of Animal Services, whether impounded alive or dead and independent of intake type.
documents.supersedes: None. Original policy effective December 21, 2016.
references: American Veterinary Medical Association (AVMA) policy on the use of animals in research, testing, and education
Russell and Burch (1959), the 3 Rs tenet (replacement, reduction, refinement)

definitions.def: Principles of Russell and Burch (1959): replacement of animals with non-animal methods wherever feasible; reduction of the number of animals consistent with sound experimental design; and refinement of experimental methods to eliminate or reduce animal pain and distress.

sections.heading: Policy

steps.step: The department acknowledges the AVMA policy, which states:
sub: The AVMA recognizes that animals have an important role in research, testing, and education for continued improvement of human and animal health and welfare. The AVMA also recognizes that humane care of animals used in research, testing, and education is an integral part of those activities.

In keeping with these concerns, the AVMA endorses the principles embodied in the 3 Rs tenet of Russell and Burch (1959): replacement of animals with non-animal methods wherever feasible; reduction of the number of animals consistent with sound experimental design; and refinement of experimental methods to eliminate or reduce animal pain and distress.

The use of animals in research, testing, and education is a privilege carrying with it unique professional, scientific, and moral obligations, and ethical responsibilities. The AVMA encourages proper stewardship of all animals and supports the judicious use of animals in meaningful research, testing, and education programs. Third-party review of the welfare of all animals is essential for all facilities.

The AVMA condemns all acts of violence, vandalism, or intimidation directed toward individuals, facilities, or tertiary organizations affiliated with the use of animals in research, testing, or education.

steps: In addition, the department recognizes that animals should only be used in biomedical research when it is clear that their use is required to achieve an important outcome and where no other feasible method is available.

related.name: AVMA policy: Use of Animals in Research, Testing, and Education

documents.title: Reconciliation and Disposition of Expired Drugs
documents.purpose: It is the expectation of the Department of Animal Services that all drugs be ordered in a time frame and volume commensurate with usage, to minimize returns and redistribution of drugs (controlled or non-controlled). Controlled substances that are expired, excess, or unwanted must be disposed of according to DEA regulations.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services (approved the original policy January 9, 2018). The SRVT manages expired drug receptacles and disposition; the Unit Registrant documents losses.
documents.scope: Applicable to Department of Animal Services staff who handle, reconcile, or dispose of expired, excess, or unwanted drugs, controlled or non-controlled.
documents.supersedes: None. Original policy effective January 9, 2018.
references: 21 U.S.C. 827, Records and Reports of Registrants
21 C.F.R. 1307, Disposal of Controlled Substances (as cited in the original; the attached DEA fact sheet notes disposal regulations moved to 21 C.F.R. Part 1317 effective October 9, 2014)
U.S. Drug Enforcement Administration, www.dea.gov
Form DEA-41, Registrant Record of Controlled Substances Destroyed
DEA Disposal Regulations: Registrant Fact Sheet

definitions.def: The supervising registered veterinary technician role responsible for drug receptacles, returns, and destruction documentation at each shelter (abbreviation as used in the original; not expanded in the source).
The DEA registrant responsible for the unit's controlled substances, including documenting losses on Form DEA-41.

steps.step: Non-controlled:
sub: Remove the drug from inventory.
Place it in the blue receptacle clearly labeled Expired Non-Controlled Drugs, maintained by the SRVT for each shelter.

steps.step: Controlled:
sub: Personally deliver the drug to the SRVT with all necessary paperwork, following the chain of custody.
The SRVT will confirm whether the expired controlled substance can be returned to the manufacturer.

If returned to the manufacturer: the manufacturer will send a shipping label and return invoice as proof of delivery and transfer of accountability; the SRVT will make a copy after signing the return invoice; the invoice is placed in the log book by the SRVT.

If not returned to the manufacturer: follow DEA guidelines by completing Form DEA-41; the SRVT must have two witnesses; the expired drug is dumped in used kitty litter; the witnesses sign Section D of Form DEA-41; the completed form is placed in the log book by the SRVT.

steps.step: Non-controlled:
sub: If returned to the manufacturer, the manufacturer will send a shipping label and return invoice as proof of delivery and transfer of accountability.

steps.step: Controlled:
sub: If returned to the manufacturer, the manufacturer will send a shipping label and return invoice as proof of delivery and transfer of accountability.
The SRVT will make a copy after signing the return invoice.
The invoice is placed in the log book by the SRVT.

steps.step: Non-controlled:
sub: If returned to the manufacturer, the manufacturer will send a shipping label and return invoice as proof of delivery and transfer of accountability.

steps.step: Controlled:
sub: If returned to the manufacturer, the manufacturer will send a shipping label and return invoice as proof of delivery and transfer of accountability.
The SRVT will make a copy after signing the return invoice.
The invoice is placed in the log book by the SRVT.

sections.heading: Reporting a Loss
steps: If a container of a controlled substance is broken, the Unit Registrant will document the loss in the disposition record, including the date of the incident and the signature of a witness, if available.
The Unit Registrant must complete DEA Form 41 for the amount of the substance lost and write unintentional destruction on the form.
The Unit Registrant must then obtain the signatures of the person who broke the bottle and the witness (if possible) and sign the Form 41 as required.
This form must be kept as a record of destruction and be available by the registrant for at least two years.

documents.title: Quarantine Area Safety and Animal Handling Guidelines
documents.purpose: This SOP establishes safety and handling procedures for the quarantine area at all RCDAS shelter campuses. Animals held in quarantine may include bite-quarantine animals under rabies observation, animals impounded as potentially dangerous, dangerous, or vicious under Riverside County ordinance, and animals held as evidence in cruelty or legal cases. These procedures protect employee safety, public safety, and the integrity of the quarantine observation period as required by California law.
documents.authority: The RCDAS Rabies Control program, under the direction of the Chief Veterinarian and in coordination with the Riverside County Department of Public Health, has authority over quarantine placement, duration, observation, and release decisions. Shelter supervisors are responsible for enforcing quarantine area access controls and safety procedures at each campus.
documents.scope: This procedure applies to all RCDAS employees, volunteers, and authorized visitors at all four campuses (Jurupa Valley, San Jacinto, Coachella Valley, and Blythe). It also applies to members of the public who may be authorized to enter the quarantine area for animal identification purposes.
documents.supersedes: RCDAS Policy 300-03, Quarantine Guidelines (originally November 26, 2001; revised October 7, 2009).
references: California Health and Safety Code, Sections 121575-121700 (Rabies Control)
California Code of Regulations, Title 17, Section 2606 (Quarantine and Isolation of Bite Animals)
California Compendium of Rabies Control and Prevention
California Food and Agricultural Code
Riverside County Ordinance No. 630
RCDAS Policy 300-03 (revised from October 7, 2009; originally November 26, 2001)

definitions.def: A mandatory observation period, typically 10 days from the date of the bite, during which a dog or cat that has bitten a person is confined and observed for clinical signs of rabies. California law requires quarantine for all domestic mammals that have bitten a person or been exposed to a rabid or suspected rabid animal.
A quarantine served at the animal owner's private residence under conditions approved by the local health officer, as an alternative to shelter quarantine when circumstances allow.

definitions.term: Potentially Dangerous, Dangerous, or Vicious Animal
definitions.def: An animal classified under Riverside County Ordinance No. 630 based on documented behavior. These animals may be held in quarantine housing for public safety and legal proceedings.
The process by which an owner reclaims an impounded animal after meeting all legal and financial requirements.

sections.heading: Quarantine Area Access Control
steps: Keep all doors leading into the quarantine area secured and locked at all times to prevent unauthorized entry.

Limit access to authorized personnel: RCDAS employees assigned to quarantine duties, veterinary staff, Animal Control Officers, and supervisors. Volunteers are permitted in the quarantine area only with supervisor approval and direct staff supervision.

Allow members of the public to enter the quarantine area only for the purpose of identifying an animal for return to owner. Public access requires a verified RTO form and staff escort at all times. Members of the public are never left unattended in the quarantine area.
Post signage at all quarantine area entry points indicating that the area is restricted and that unauthorized entry is prohibited.

sections.heading: Animal Handling and Contact Restrictions
steps: Avoid all direct physical contact with dogs and cats under bite quarantine, including handling, petting, bathing, and walking. Animals under bite quarantine remain in their assigned kennels for the duration of the observation period.

Use caution when cleaning and feeding to maintain physical separation from the animal. Use tools and techniques that allow feeding and cleaning without entering the animal's immediate space when possible.
Do not permit animals under bite quarantine outside of their assigned kennel for exercise, enrichment, or any other purpose unless specifically authorized by veterinary staff for medical treatment.

When space limitations require quarantine animals to be housed in kennels within general population areas, clearly mark the kennel with quarantine signage and apply the same handling restrictions as in the dedicated quarantine area.

sections.heading: Employee Bite or Scratch Reporting
steps: Report any bite, scratch, or saliva exposure from a quarantine animal to a supervisor immediately. This reporting requirement has no exceptions, because animals in bite quarantine are under observation specifically to determine rabies status, and rabies is transmitted through saliva.
The supervisor initiates the employee injury reporting process in accordance with county workers' compensation procedures and ensures the incident is documented in Chameleon.
Notify veterinary staff of any contact incident so that the animal's observation record can be updated.

sections.heading: Food and Items from the Public
steps: Do not give treats, food, toys, bedding, or any other items provided by the public or the animal's owner to any animal in quarantine. All quarantine animals receive only RCDAS-supplied food, water, and bedding.
If an owner brings items for their quarantine animal, politely explain the restriction and offer to hold the items for return at the time of release.

sections.heading: Quarantine Observation and Documentation
steps: Observe each quarantine animal daily for clinical signs of illness, changes in behavior, neurological symptoms, or any other abnormalities. Document observations in Chameleon.

Report any signs of illness, behavioral changes, aggression changes, or neurological symptoms (disorientation, excessive salivation, paralysis, seizures, sudden temperament change) to veterinary staff and the Rabies Control program immediately.

If a quarantine animal dies during the observation period, notify the Rabies Control program and veterinary staff immediately. The animal's remains may need to be submitted for rabies testing as directed by the Rabies Control program and the local health officer.

sections.heading: Quarantine Release
steps: Release a quarantine animal only upon written authorization from the Rabies Control program or the local health officer. The standard bite quarantine period for dogs and cats is 10 days from the date of the bite.
Verify that all fees, licensing requirements, and any conditions imposed by the Rabies Control program or Animal Control Officers have been met before releasing the animal to its owner.
Document the quarantine release date and any release conditions in Chameleon.

related.name: RCDAS Bite Report Call Procedures (SOP CC-013)
Riverside County Ordinance No. 630 (Potentially Dangerous, Dangerous, and Vicious Animals)
California Health and Safety Code, Chapter 1: Rabies Control
California Compendium of Rabies Control and Prevention
County of Riverside workers' compensation injury reporting procedures

documents.title: Veterinary Care for Animals Held at Shelter
documents.purpose: To ensure that all animals in need of veterinary care while being housed at the shelter are provided with such care, and that Animal Services personnel have access to medication for the animals under care.
documents.authority: Director of Animal Services (approved the original policy). Kennel staff may humanely destroy a severely injured stray or abandoned dog or cat only with the approval of his or her immediate superior (RCC 6.12.110).
documents.scope: All Animal Services employees authorized to handle animals.
documents.supersedes: Policy 300-05, effective March 8, 2002; revised October 7, 2009. Absorbs duplicate copy 300-05-407, Veterinary Care for Animals Held at Riverside Shelter (identical content, same effective dates).
references: Riverside County Code (RCC) 6.12.090
Riverside County Code (RCC) 6.12.100
Riverside County Code (RCC) 6.12.110

sections.heading: Introduction
steps: Although the County of Riverside Department of Animal Services employs a full-time veterinary staff, there are many cases where the veterinary staff is unavailable for several days or a week at a time. The following guidelines are designed to help staff in providing veterinary care for animals under their care both when the Department of Animal Services veterinary staff is available and when they are unavailable.

sections.heading: Responsibilities
steps: The veterinary staff at the Riverside Animal Shelter will provide veterinary care for animals in need of such care when they are available. In the absence of said veterinary staff, the services of an outside veterinarian will be provided.
Animal Control Officers are responsible for ensuring that any animal under their care, and in need of veterinary care, is provided with such care in a timely manner.

When an animal at the shelter is in need of veterinary care and the veterinary staff is unavailable, call an available Animal Control Officer for transportation of the animal to a local veterinary clinic (RCC 6.12.100). When no Animal Control Officer is available, a member of the kennel staff will be utilized to transport the injured or sick animal.
If the animal brought to the shelter is other than a dog or cat and no owner is known, the kennel staff may humanely destroy the animal (RCC 6.12.090).

If the animal brought to the shelter is a dog or cat, the kennel staff may, with the approval of his or her immediate superior, humanely destroy any stray or abandoned animal in any case where the animal is too severely injured to move or where a veterinarian is not available and it would be more humane to dispose of the animal (RCC 6.12.110).
Any staff member transporting an animal must have a current Riverside County Authorization to Drive form (Attachment A) on file with Fleet Services and the County Safety Department.

sections.heading: When the Veterinary Staff is Available
steps: Animal Control Officers may bring sick or injured animals to the shelter for veterinary care when the veterinary staff is available.
Place the animal in a cage in the Receiving Area.

The Animal Control Officer will fill out the control card and place it, along with a plastic card marked vet check, in the cardholder in the front of the cage. The Animal Control Officer must write the injury/illness specifics on the impound card, in the upper right hand corner just below the impound number.

Take severely injured or sick animals needing immediate veterinary care to the nearest veterinarian for assistance; do not bring them to the shelter. When an animal is too injured or sick for humane transportation, it may be humanely euthanized in the field by the Animal Control Officer.

Place animals with what may appear to be a contagious disease in one of the cages located outside of the Receiving Area at the shelter. In this case, place the impound card in the plastic cardholder located on the wall between the roll-up door and the standard door in Receiving.

sections.heading: When the Veterinary Staff is Unavailable
steps: Take animals needing veterinary care to an outside veterinary clinic for service when the shelter veterinary staff is unavailable. Animal Control Officers are to take any sick or injured animals to the nearest veterinarian for care.

When an outside veterinary service has prescribed medications for the animal and the County of Riverside veterinary staff is unavailable, and will not be available the following day, the Animal Control Officer is to obtain medications from the veterinarian by whom the animal was seen.

related.name: Riverside County Authorization to Drive form (Attachment A)

appendices: Appendix A: Riverside County Authorization to Drive form (attached separately)
